topics.title: Basic Components: Transmitters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Components: Transmitters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** and classical **Data Communication System (DCS)** model, the **Transmitter** is the functional subsystem responsible for converting the original information (generated by the *Source*) into a form that is **electrically, spectrally, and logically compatible** with the physical transmission medium. It performs the first three critical transformations: **Source Encoding → Channel Encoding → Modulation (or Line Coding)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The transmitter is the device that *originates* the data and converts it into electromagnetic/optical signals suitable for propagation through a guided or unguided medium. It is one of the **five basic components** of any data communication system: **Source → Transmitter → Transmission System (Channel) → Receiver → Destination.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a **handwritten letter** to a friend in another country:

| Letter Process | Transmitter Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Writing your thoughts on paper | **Source** (raw data, e.g., voice, text) |
| Translating to the friend's language | **Source Encoder** (digitization, compression) |
| Writing the message in a tamper-proof sealed envelope | **Channel Encoder** (error-control coding) |
| Choosing a courier (air/sea) based on speed and cost | **Modulator / Line Coder** (signal conditioning) |
| Handing it to the postal service | **Channel Adapter** (matching to physical medium) |

The *Transmitter* is essentially the **"entire assembly line"** that prepares your raw message so it can travel safely, efficiently, and accurately over the channel.

### 1.3 Position in the Communication Chain

> [!NOTE]
> A **complete data communication system** has 5 sequential blocks:
> 1. **Source** → produces the information
> 2. **Transmitter** → transforms and conditions the signal *(our topic)*
> 3. **Transmission System / Channel** → carries the signal
> 4. **Receiver** → reconstructs the original message
> 5. **Destination** → consumes the message

The transmitter is the **only intelligent, active sub-system** between the source and the physical medium; therefore its design directly determines the *throughput, reliability, spectral efficiency, and reach* of the entire system.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Data Rate (R)** — measured in **bits per second (bps)**; symbols per second = **Baud**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — unitless, expressed in **decibels (dB)** as $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** — dimensionless ratio, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-9}$.
- **Bandwidth (B)** — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Spectral Efficiency (η)** — measured in **bps/Hz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Transmitter

A generic digital transmitter (as taught under **Forouzan's "Data Communications and Networking"** and **Stallings' "Data and Computer Communications"** — the two prescribed references for PECCT751) is decomposed into **four sequential sub-blocks**:

**Step-by-Step Logic Flow:**

1. **Source Encoder** — Converts analog information (voice, video) into a digital bit stream using **PCM / DM / DPCM / ADPCM**; for digital sources, it performs **data compression** (lossless: Huffman, LZW; lossy: JPEG, MP3).
   - *Why?* Computers and digital channels only understand bits; compression saves bandwidth.

2. **Channel Encoder** — Adds **redundant bits** to the data stream to detect/correct errors introduced by the noisy channel. Techniques: **Parity check, Hamming code, CRC, Convolutional, Turbo, LDPC**.
   - *Why?* Real channels add noise, fading, ISI; redundancy enables the receiver to detect/correct errors.

3. **Modulator / Line Encoder** — Maps the bit stream onto an analog carrier (for **bandpass transmission** — wireless, optical, coax) or into a baseband pulse waveform (for **baseband transmission** — copper pairs, baseband LANs).
   - *Why?* Baseband digital pulses often contain DC, low-frequency energy that cannot pass through transformers or wireless antennas; modulation shifts the spectrum to a suitable band.

4. **Channel Adapter / Line Driver** — Performs **amplification, impedance matching, filtering, and electrical isolation** to drive the physical medium without reflections or distortion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In some textbooks the **Channel Encoder + Modulator** is collectively called the *"Digital Modem"*, and the **Channel Adapter** is part of the *Transmission System*. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats all four blocks as part of the transmitter.

### 2.2 Baseband vs. Bandpass Transmission

| Feature | Baseband Transmission | Bandpass Transmission (Carrier Modulation) |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | Digital pulses (NRZ, RZ, Manchester) | Analog modulated carrier |
| Medium | Copper twisted pair, baseband coax | Wireless, optical fiber, broadband coax |
| Coding scheme | Line coding | ASK, FSK, PSK, QAM |
| Spectral location | Near DC to $f_{max}$ | Shifted to $f_c \pm B$ |
| Use case | Ethernet, RS-232, USB | Wi-Fi, cellular, satellite, FM radio |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam relevant** (Module 1, 2-mark and 14-mark derivations).

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity  $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $S/N$ = SNR | Upper bound on transmitter data rate |
| 2 | Nyquist Maximum Data Rate (noiseless)  $C = 2B \log_{2}M$ | $M$ = signal levels, $B$ = bandwidth | Bandwidth vs. signaling efficiency |
| 3 | Bit Rate of Line Coder  $R_b = N \cdot f_{baud}$ | $N$ = bits per symbol | Calculating throughput |
| 4 | Spectral Efficiency  $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | bps/Hz | Compare line codes |
| 5 | Signal Power after Modulation  $S_{out} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ for ASK/PSK | $A_c$ = carrier amplitude | Power budget problems |
| 6 | Minimum Bandwidth (Nyquist)  $B_{min} = \dfrac{R}{2\log_{2}M}$ | $R$ = bit rate | For Min-BW design problems |
| 7 | Redundancy (Channel Coding)  $r = \dfrac{n - k}{n}$ | $k$ = info bits, $n$ = coded bits | Coding efficiency |
| 8 | Coding Efficiency  $\eta_c = \dfrac{k}{n}$ | dimensionless | Compare codes (Hamming, CRC) |

> **Note on Table Markup:** Absolute values are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ in math mode; e.g., $\vert S/N \vert$ — never with the raw `|` character that would break markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** GSM transmitters use **GMSK modulation** with **convolutional channel coding** (rate 1/2) — textbook example of source-channel coded transmitter.
- **Optical Fiber:** Modern **100 Gbps / 400 Gbps** transponders use **DP-QPSK / 16-QAM** with **FEC (SD-FEC, HD-FEC)** — applied transmitter engineering.
- **IoT & LPWAN:** **LoRa transmitters** use **CSS (Chirp Spread Spectrum)** modulation with **Hamming FEC** for long-range, low-power transmission.
- **Satellite (DVB-S2):** **QPSK / 8-PSK / 16-APSK** with **LDPC (rate 1/2 to 9/10)** and **BCH outer code** — gold-standard transmitter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Transmission Bandwidth of a Transmitter Using M-ary Signaling

**Statement:** For a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, an M-ary PCM/line-coded transmitter must satisfy Nyquist's first criterion to avoid ISI.

**Given:**
- Bit rate at the transmitter output: $R$ bits/second
- Number of distinct signal levels: $M$
- Maximum usable bandwidth: $B$ Hz (in Hz)

**Step 1 — Relationship between bit rate and symbol rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Each signal level carries } \log_{2}M \text{ bits.} \\
\text{Therefore, the symbol (baud) rate } D \text{ is:} \\
D &= \frac{R}{\log_{2}M} \quad \text{[symbols/second = Baud]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist Minimum Bandwidth Criterion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nyquist states that the minimum bandwidth required to} \\
\text{transmit } D \text{ symbols/second without ISI is:} \\
B_{min} &= \frac{D}{2} \quad \text{[Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $D$ from Step 1 into Step 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{min} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{R}{\log_{2}M} \\
\therefore B_{min} &= \frac{R}{2\log_{2}M} \quad \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- Doubling $M$ (e.g., from 2 to 4 levels) **halves the bandwidth** for the same bit rate — this is the fundamental trade-off exploited by **M-ary PSK / QAM** in modern wireless transmitters.
- Cost: at fixed power, the **Euclidean distance** between signal points shrinks as $\sqrt{M}$, so **BER rises**. Hence Shannon's law caps the maximum $M$ allowed at a given SNR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check with Nyquist Capacity:** Rearranging the result, $R = 2B \log_{2}M$, which is exactly the Nyquist noiseless capacity formula in §2.3 — proof of internal consistency.

### 3.2 Derivation: Spectral Efficiency of an M-ary Bandpass Transmitter

**Step 1 — Definition of spectral efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} \quad \text{[bps/Hz]}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Nyquist minimum bandwidth $B_{min} = R_b / (2\log_{2}M)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{R_b}{\dfrac{R_b}{2\log_{2}M}} \\
\eta &= 2\log_{2}M \quad \text{[bps/Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (a 16-QAM transmitter, $M = 16$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= 2 \log_{2}(16) \\
&= 2 \times 4 \\
&= 8 \text{ bps/Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with BPSK ($M = 2$):**

$$
\eta_{BPSK} = 2 \log_{2}(2) = 2 \times 1 = 2 \text{ bps/Hz}
$$

> Thus a 16-QAM transmitter achieves **4× the spectral efficiency** of BPSK, but at the cost of higher SNR requirement (≈ 10.5 dB extra for BER $= 10^{-5}$).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Channel Coding on Transmitter Bit Rate

| Parameter | Before Channel Encoding | After Channel Encoding | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Information bits per block | $k$ | $k$ (unchanged) | Source data preserved |
| Coded bits per block | — | $n > k$ | Adds redundancy |
| Transmitter bit rate | $R_s$ | $R_c = R_s \cdot \dfrac{n}{k}$ | Increases by factor $n/k$ |
| Bandwidth required | $B$ | $B \cdot \dfrac{n}{k}$ | Slightly increases |
| BER (after decoding) | $P_e$ | $P_e^{'} < P_e$ | Drops sharply |
| Coding gain | — | $G_c$ (dB) | Equivalent SNR savings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **standard KTU 14-mark answer framework** for any question that asks *"Explain the role of the channel encoder in a transmitter"*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Digital Transmitter

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Source] --> srcEnc[Source Encoder]
    srcEnc --> chEnc[Channel Encoder]
    chEnc --> mod[Modulator / Line Encoder]
    mod --> adp[Channel Adapter / Line Driver]
    adp --> chan[Transmission Channel]

    classDef block fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1F4E8C,color:#000,stroke-width:1.5px
    class src,srcEnc,chEnc,mod,adp,chan block
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each block performs a *distinct, non-overlapping* transformation.
- Information flows strictly **left → right**; **no feedback** in a one-way transmitter.
- The output of `adp` is the **transmitted signal $s(t)$** injected into the channel.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Detailed Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph StageA[Stage 1 Source Encoding]
        A1[Analog Input] --> A2[Sampler]
        A2 --> A3[Quantizer]
        A3 --> A4[Binary Coder]
        A4 --> A5[Compressor]
    end

    subgraph StageB[Stage 2 Channel Encoding]
        B1[Source Bits k] --> B2[Parity / Hamming / CRC Encoder]
        B2 --> B3[Coded Bits n]
    end

    subgraph StageC[Stage 3 Modulation]
        C1[Coded Bits] --> C2[Bit to Symbol Mapper]
        C2 --> C3[Carrier Modulator ASK FSK PSK QAM]
        C3 --> C4[Bandpass Signal s t]
    end

    subgraph StageD[Stage 4 Channel Adapter]
        D1[Bandpass Signal] --> D2[Power Amplifier]
        D2 --> D3[Impedance Matching Network]
        D3 --> D4[Antenna or Cable]
    end

    A5 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    C4 --> D1
```

**Reading guide for students:**
- `StageA` is the **Source Encoder** — implements PCM for analog sources.
- `StageB` is the **Channel Encoder** — error-control redundancy.
- `StageC` is the **Modulator** — maps bits to analog waveform.
- `StageD` is the **Channel Adapter** — final electrical/optical interface.

### 4.3 Block-Level Flow — Information Transformations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Message m of t] -->|ADC| DM[Digital Bit Stream b i]
    DM -->|Compression| CB[Compressed Bits c i]
    CB -->|ECC Encoding| EB[Encoded Bits e i]
    EB -->|Mapping| SYM[Complex Symbols s k]
    SYM -->|Pulse Shaping| PS[Baseband Waveform x t]
    PS -->|Upconversion| RF[Passband Signal s t]
    RF --> PA[Power Amplifier] --> ANT[Antenna / Fiber / Cable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **information-flow schematic** is what examiners expect in a 7-mark *"With a neat block diagram, explain the transmitter of a digital communication system"* answer.

### 4.4 State Diagram — Line Coding Selection (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need DC Balance?} -->|Yes| Q2{Need Clock Recovery?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Use Manchester or Biphase]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4[Use 8B 10B or 64B 66B]
    Q1 -->|No| Q5{Bandwidth Limited?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Multilevel PAM or M ary NRZ]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use NRZ L or NRZ I]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision tree mirrors the **Forouzan "Line Coding" section** — frequently asked in KTU Module 1 short-answer questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a transmitter in a data communication system. List its main functional blocks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **transmitter** is the subsystem of a data communication system that converts the source-generated information into a signal suitable for transmission over the physical channel.
>
> **Main functional blocks:**
> 1. **Source Encoder** — performs A/D conversion and/or data compression.
> 2. **Channel Encoder** — adds redundancy for error detection/correction.
> 3. **Modulator / Line Encoder** — converts bits to transmission waveform.
> 4. **Channel Adapter** — provides amplification and impedance matching.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing with one-line purpose = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between baseband and bandpass transmission. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Baseband | Bandpass |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency content | From 0 to $f_{max}$ | Centered on carrier $f_c$ |
| Coding | Line coding (NRZ, Manchester) | Modulation (ASK, FSK, PSK) |
| Example | Ethernet (10BASE-T) | Wi-Fi (802.11 g/n using OFDM) |

*(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks; tabular format preferred by KTU valuators.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]*

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a digital communication **transmitter**. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Nyquist minimum bandwidth** required for an M-ary line-coded transmitter. A transmitter sends data at **9600 bps** using **16-level signaling** over a noiseless channel. Calculate the **minimum bandwidth** required. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the 4-stage block diagram from §4.2 above. Label Source Encoder, Channel Encoder, Modulator, Channel Adapter.
2. **Source Encoder (1.5 marks):**
   - Converts analog → digital (PCM: Sampling → Quantization → Encoding).
   - Compresses redundant data (Huffman/LZW).
   - Output: efficient bit stream.
3. **Channel Encoder (1.5 marks):**
   - Adds controlled redundancy using block codes (Hamming, CRC) or convolutional codes.
   - Enables receiver to detect/correct bit errors.
4. **Modulator (1 mark):**
   - Maps bits to symbols; performs ASK/FSK/PSK/QAM.
   - Shifts spectrum to a band suitable for the channel.
5. **Channel Adapter (1 mark):**
   - Power amplification + impedance matching + filtering.
   - Drives antenna or cable without reflection loss.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 mark):**

$$
B_{min} = \frac{R}{2\log_{2}M}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (1 mark):**

- $R = 9600$ bps
- $M = 16 \Rightarrow \log_{2}M = 4$

**Step 3 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
B_{min} = \frac{9600}{2 \times 4} = \frac{9600}{8} = 1200 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Baud rate and verification (2 marks):**

- Symbol rate $D = R/\log_{2}M = 9600/4 = 2400$ Baud.
- Using Nyquist: $B_{min} = D/2 = 1200$ Hz. ✓

**Step 5 — Spectral efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{R}{B_{min}} = \frac{9600}{1200} = 8 \text{ bps/Hz}
$$

(Equivalent to $2\log_{2}M = 8$ bps/Hz — confirms derivation.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

> **(a)** Explain the **Shannon–Hartley theorem** and discuss its implications on the design of a transmitter. A channel has $B = 4$ kHz and $S/N = 30$ dB. Find the **maximum achievable data rate**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the following **line coding schemes** used in transmitters: **NRZ-L, NRZ-I, Manchester, and AMI**. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Shannon–Hartley Capacity (2 marks):**

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps}
$$

**Implications for transmitter design (2 marks):**
- The transmitter can never exceed $C$ bps, no matter how many signal levels $M$ are used.
- $C$ grows only **logarithmically** with SNR; doubling SNR does not double capacity.
- $C$ grows **linearly** with bandwidth — hence wideband systems (UWB, OFDM, mmWave) are preferred for high-rate transmitters.

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

- Convert SNR from dB: $S/N = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$.
- Substitute:

$$
C = 4000 \times \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = 4000 \times \log_{2}(1001)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(1001) \approx 9.967
$$

$$
\therefore C \approx 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39{,}869 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

| Code | Encoding Rule | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** | 0 → Low, 1 → High (or vice-versa) | Simple, low bandwidth | No DC balance, poor clock recovery |
| **NRZ-I** | 1 = transition, 0 = no transition | Better sync than NRZ-L | Still has DC component |
| **Manchester** | 0 = HL transition, 1 = LH transition (mid-bit) | Self-clocking, DC balanced | Doubles bandwidth |
| **AMI** | Alternate Mark Inversion: 1 alternates ±V, 0 = 0 V | DC balanced, easy error detection | Long runs of 0s lose clock |

*(Award 1.5 marks per row for advantage + disadvantage, plus 1 mark for conclusion/selection table.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "transmitter" with "source"** — Source is the *originator of data* (a person, a sensor, a computer); Transmitter is the *electronic/electrical subsystem* that conditions the source output. Do NOT interchange them.
> 2. **Mixing up "Source Encoder" with "Channel Encoder"** — Source encoding = compression / A/D; Channel encoding = error-control. This is a **favourite trick question** worth 2–3 marks in Part A.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers — bandwidth in **Hz**, rate in **bps**, spectral efficiency in **bps/Hz**. Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 14-mark answers** — a neat, labelled diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself, even before any explanation.
> 5. **dB to linear conversion error** — $S/N$(dB) $= 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$, so $30$ dB $= 1000$, **not** $30$. Mistake here cascades to wrong capacity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transmitter = Source Encoder + Channel Encoder + Modulator + Channel Adapter.** Remember the order — many KTU questions are reverse-order traps.
- **Source encoding = efficiency** (compress); **Channel encoding = reliability** (redundancy). Don't swap these in answers.
- **Baseband** uses **line coding**; **Bandpass** uses **modulation**. Choose the right block in the right system.
- **Nyquist (noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_{2}M$ → governs M-ary design.
- **Shannon (noisy):** $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ → ultimate upper bound.
- Always state **units** in numerical answers: Hz, bps, bps/Hz, dB.
- A **block diagram** is *mandatory* in 14-mark transmitter questions — never skip.
- Common **SNR conversion:** $+3$ dB ≈ double power, $+10$ dB = $10\times$ power.
- For **M-ary line coding**, $B_{min} = R/(2\log_{2}M)$ and $\eta = 2\log_{2}M$.
- **M-ary trade-off:** larger $M$ ⇒ smaller bandwidth + higher spectral efficiency + lower noise immunity.
- Real transmitters (GSM, Wi-Fi, DVB) all use the same 4-block structure — just with different specific algorithms.
- Know the **five basic components** of any DCS by heart: Source, Transmitter, Channel, Receiver, Destination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receivers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Receivers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data and Computer Communication** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1), a **Receiver** is the terminal subsystem of a digital communication link whose function is to recover the original transmitted bit sequence from a corrupted, noisy, and possibly distorted version of the transmitted waveform arriving at the channel output. Formally, the receiver executes two principal operations:

1. **Signal Demodulation / Detection:** Maps the continuous-time, continuous-amplitude received waveform $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ (where $s_i(t)$ is one of $M$ possible transmitted signals and $n(t)$ is Additive White Gaussian Noise – AWGN) into a discrete set of $M$ decision regions in signal space.
2. **Decision / Estimation:** Selects the transmitted symbol $\hat{m}$ that maximizes the **a posteriori probability** $P(s_i \mid r)$ (Maximum A Posteriori – MAP criterion) or, equivalently under equally likely symbols, the **likelihood** $f(r \mid s_i)$ (Maximum Likelihood – ML criterion).

The optimal receiver in the presence of AWGN is the **Matched Filter Receiver** (or its time-domain dual, the **Correlator Receiver**), which maximizes the output signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the sampling instant and minimizes the bit error probability $P_e$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Receivers):** The scope of this topic covers the **optimum receiver for binary signals in AWGN**, the **matched filter**, the **correlator receiver**, the **ML decision rule**, the **signal space representation**, and the derivation of the **probability of error $P_e$** for coherent binary modulation schemes (BPSK, BFSK, and coherently detected BFSK).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are in a noisy, crowded railway station (the **channel**), and your friend (the **transmitter**) is shouting one of two words — "YES" or "NO" — from a distance. By the time the words reach you, they are buried in station noise, train horns, and echoes. Your task as a **receiver** is to decide which word was spoken.

How do you do it? You don't analyze every single millisecond of the sound wave. Instead, you:

1. **Listen for the entire duration** of the word (this is the *integration window*).
2. **Mentally "subtract"** what you *expected* each word to sound like, and pick the one that is "closest" to what you actually heard (this is the *correlation / matched filter* operation).
3. **Make a decision** — the word whose expected pattern matches best is your guess (this is the *ML decision*).

The engineering version of this intuitive process is precisely the **Correlator Receiver** or **Matched Filter Receiver**, and the *closeness measure* is the **Euclidean distance** in the **signal space** (a coordinate system where each possible transmitted signal is a single point).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Receiver Design:**
> - **SNR at decision point:** $\gamma = \frac{E_b}{N_0}$ (Energy per bit to noise spectral density ratio), unitless.
> - **Bit Error Rate (BER):** $P_e$, dimensionless probability.
> - **Noise Power Spectral Density:** $N_0/2$ (for two-sided AWGN), in **Watts/Hz**.
> - **Boltzmann's constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (used in thermal noise calculations).

---

### 1.3 Role of the Receiver in the Communication System Block

The receiver is the second half of a communication system. The complete chain is:

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Encoder} \rightarrow \text{Modulator} \rightarrow \text{Channel} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Receiver}} \rightarrow \text{Decoder} \rightarrow \text{Sink}
$$

Within the **Receiver** block, the canonical sub-blocks are:

1. **Front-end / Bandpass Filter + LNA:** Selects the band, suppresses out-of-band noise, and amplifies.
2. **Demodulator / Matched Filter or Correlator:** Translates the waveform into a soft decision variable.
3. **Sampler:** Samples the matched-filter output at the optimal instant $t = T_b$.
4. **Decision Device (Threshold Comparator):** Compares the sample against a threshold $\lambda$ and outputs $\hat{m} \in \{0, 1\}$.
5. **Synchronization (Carrier, Symbol, Frame):** Provides the timing references (assumed ideal in KTU analytical problems).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model of the Received Signal

The received signal over a bit interval $[0, T_b]$ for binary signaling is modeled as:

$$
r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b, \quad i \in \{1, 2\}
$$

where:
- $s_1(t), s_2(t)$ are the two candidate transmitted signals (e.g., for BPSK: $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$, $s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\phi_1(t)$).
- $n(t)$ is a zero-mean AWGN random process with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.
- $\phi_1(t)$ is the orthonormal basis function (energy-normalized: $\int_0^{T_b} \phi_1^2(t)\,dt = 1$).

---

### 2.2 Steps in Optimum Receiver Design (The "Why" and "How")

**Step 1 — Project the continuous signal onto an orthonormal basis.**
The received waveform is an infinite-dimensional object, but all information relevant to detection is captured by projecting it onto the **signal subspace** spanned by $\{s_1(t), s_2(t)\}$. This gives the **received vector** $\mathbf{r} = [r_1, r_2]$ where $r_i = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_i(t)\,dt$.

**Step 2 — Compute the likelihood of each hypothesis.**
Because $n(t)$ is Gaussian, the projected noise components $n_i$ are i.i.d. Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. The conditional PDF of $\mathbf{r}$ given hypothesis $H_i$ is:

$$
f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_i) = \frac{1}{(\pi N_0)^{N/2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2}{N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Maximum Likelihood (ML) decision rule.**
For equiprobable symbols, ML = MAP. The receiver decides $\hat{H}_1$ if:

$$
f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_1) > f(\mathbf{r} \mid H_2)
$$

Taking the natural log and dropping common constants yields the **minimum-distance rule**:

$$
\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_1\|^2 < \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_2\|^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{decide } s_1
$$

**Step 4 — Realize the rule using a correlator or matched filter.**
The inner product $\int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_i(t)\,dt$ is realized in hardware by either:
- **Correlator:** Multiply $r(t)$ by a locally generated replica $\phi_i(t)$, integrate over $[0, T_b]$.
- **Matched Filter:** A linear filter with impulse response $h(t) = \phi_i(T_b - t)$ whose output is sampled at $t = T_b$.

Both are mathematically equivalent because matched filtering is *time-reversed correlation*.

**Step 5 — Compute the probability of error.**
The final analytical step is to find the probability that noise pushes $\mathbf{r}$ across the decision boundary, which yields the standard closed-form BER expressions.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Note |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Received signal model | $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ | $n(t)$: zero-mean AWGN, PSD $N_0/2$ |
| 2 | Orthonormal basis energy | $\int_0^{T_b} \phi_k^2(t)\,dt = 1$ | Required for normalization |
| 3 | Correlator output | $r_i = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_i(t)\,dt$ | Continuous inner product |
| 4 | Matched filter impulse response | $h(t) = s_i(T_b - t)$ | Time-reversed, shifted copy of $s_i(t)$ |
| 5 | Matched filter output SNR | $\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}$ | Maximum SNR for a given $E_b, N_0$ |
| 6 | ML decision metric | $D_i = 2\int_0^{T_b} r(t)s_i(t)\,dt - E_i$ | Pick the largest $D_i$ |
| 7 | BPSK BER (coherent) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Antipodal signaling |
| 8 | Coherent BFSK BER | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Orthogonal signaling |
| 9 | Q-function definition | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$ | Use $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$ |
| 10 | Bit energy vs symbol energy | $E_b = E_s / \log_2 M$ | For $M$-ary schemes |
| 11 | Noise variance per basis | $\sigma_n^2 = N_0/2$ | Per dimension in signal space |
| 12 | Euclidean distance (BPSK) | $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Distance between signal points |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** For **antipodal** signals (BPSK), the argument of $Q$ is $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$. For **orthogonal** signals (BFSK), it is $\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ — exactly **$\sqrt{2}$ worse** because orthogonal signaling uses the available energy less efficiently.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The matched filter is **the fundamental building block of every digital modem**:
- **5G NR / 4G LTE baseband receivers** use matched-filter-style correlators with OFDM pilot symbols for channel estimation.
- **GPS receivers** use a bank of 32 matched filters (one per satellite PRN code) running in parallel.
- **Optical fiber receivers** (EDFA + PIN photodiode) implement a "modified" matched filter via the integrate-and-dump front-end.
- **Radar and sonar** systems use matched filters to detect echoes buried in noise — the same mathematics.
- **UART / SPI / I2C digital logic** at the PCB level can be viewed as a binary matched filter on a 2-level signal.

> [!TIP]
> The **matched filter is not a heuristic** — it is the *unique* linear filter that maximizes output SNR, a result known as the **North Matched Filter Theorem**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Matched Filter Output and Maximum SNR

**Problem Setup:** Find the impulse response $h(t)$, $0 \le t \le T_b$, that maximizes the output SNR at $t = T_b$ when the input is $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, where $s(t)$ is known and $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 1 — Write the output of the linear filter.**
The filter output at sampling instant $t_0$ is:

$$
y(t_0) = \int_0^{t_0} r(\tau)\,h(t_0 - \tau)\,d\tau
$$

The signal component is $y_s(t_0) = \int_0^{t_0} s(\tau)\,h(t_0 - \tau)\,d\tau$.

The noise component has zero mean and variance (using the PSD $N_0/2$):

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \int_0^{t_0} \int_0^{t_0} \frac{N_0}{2}\,\delta(u-v)\,h(t_0-u)\,h(t_0-v)\,du\,dv = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{t_0} h^2(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Form the SNR functional.**

$$
\mathrm{SNR}(t_0) = \frac{y_s^2(t_0)}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{\left[\int_0^{t_0} s(\tau)\,h(t_0-\tau)\,d\tau\right]^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{t_0} h^2(\tau)\,d\tau}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.**
Cauchy–Schwarz states: $\left[\int f(\tau)g(\tau)\,d\tau\right]^2 \le \int f^2(\tau)\,d\tau \cdot \int g^2(\tau)\,d\tau$, with equality iff $f(\tau) = c\,g(\tau)$.

Choose $f(\tau) = s(\tau)$ and $g(\tau) = h(t_0 - \tau)$. Equality holds when:

$$
h(t_0 - \tau) = c\,s(\tau) \quad \Rightarrow \quad h(t) = c\,s(t_0 - t)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to obtain the maximum SNR.**

$$
\mathrm{SNR}_{\max} = \frac{\int_0^{t_0} s^2(\tau)\,d\tau}{N_0/2} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}
$$

For a bit with energy $E_b$, the maximum SNR at the decision point is $\boxed{2E_b/N_0}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: BER for Coherent BPSK

**Step 1 — Signal definitions.** Let the orthonormal basis be $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over $[0, T_b]$ with $f_c \gg 1/T_b$.

- $s_1(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ represents bit "0"
- $s_2(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi(t)$ represents bit "1"

**Step 2 — Decision variable at the correlator output.**

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\phi(t)\,dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_0
$$

where $n_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ because the noise projected onto $\phi(t)$ is Gaussian with variance $N_0/2$.

**Step 3 — Decision rule.** Threshold $\lambda = 0$. Decide "0" if $r > 0$, else "1".

**Step 4 — Probability of error.** Given "0" was sent, $r \sim \mathcal{N}(+\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$. The error occurs if $r < 0$:

$$
P(e \mid 0) = P(r < 0 \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

By symmetry $P(e \mid 1) = P(e \mid 0)$, and since both hypotheses are equiprobable:

$$
\boxed{P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: BER for Coherent BFSK (Orthogonal Signaling)

**Step 1 — Two orthonormal basis functions.** $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$, $\phi_2(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_2 t)$, with $f_1 - f_2 = n/T_b$ for integer $n$, ensuring orthogonality.

**Step 2 — Correlator outputs.**

$$
r_1 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1, \quad r_2 = n_2, \quad \text{when bit "0" sent}
$$

$$
r_1 = n_1, \quad r_2 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_2, \quad \text{when bit "1" sent}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision rule.** Decide "0" if $r_1 > r_2$, else "1".

**Step 4 — Error analysis.** Let $X = r_1 - r_2$. Under $H_0$: $X = \sqrt{E_b} + (n_1 - n_2)$. Since $n_1, n_2$ are independent $\mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$, their difference has variance $N_0$.

$$
P_e = P(X < 0 \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}}\right)
$$

The final BER for coherent BFSK is:

$$
\boxed{P_e^{\text{BFSK,coh}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (BPSK vs BFSK vs DPSK)

| Modulation | $P_e$ Expression | $E_b/N_0$ for $P_e = 10^{-5}$ | Power Penalty vs BPSK | Signaling Class |
|------------|-------------------|--------------------------------|-----------------------|-----------------|
| BPSK (coherent) | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **9.6 dB** | 0 dB (reference) | Antipodal |
| BFSK (coherent) | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **12.6 dB** | 3 dB | Orthogonal |
| DPSK (differential) | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | **9.3 + 1 ≈ 9.9 dB** | ~3 dB at high SNR | Phase comparison |
| BFSK (non-coherent) | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ | **12.6 + 0.4 ≈ 13.0 dB** | 3.4 dB | Non-orthogonal envelope |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** To achieve the same BER of $10^{-5}$, BPSK needs $9.6$ dB of $E_b/N_0$, while coherent BFSK needs $3$ dB more. This $3$ dB is the "orthogonality penalty."

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Matched Filter for a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Given a transmitted signal $s(t) = A\,\Pi\!\left(\dfrac{t - T_b/2}{T_b}\right)$ (a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and width $T_b$), find the matched filter impulse response and output.

**Step 1 — Apply the definition $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$.**

$$
h(t) = A\,\Pi\!\left(\frac{T_b - t - T_b/2}{T_b}\right) = A\,\Pi\!\left(\frac{T_b/2 - t}{T_b}\right)
$$

This is a rectangle from $t = 0$ to $t = T_b$, i.e., a **time-reversed** version of $s(t)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the convolution output $y(t) = s(t) * h(t)$.**

$$
y(t) = \int_0^{T_b} s(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau
$$

For $0 \le t \le T_b$:

$$
y(t) = \int_0^{t} A \cdot A\,d\tau = A^2 t
$$

For $T_b \le t \le 2T_b$:

$$
y(t) = \int_{t - T_b}^{T_b} A^2\,d\tau = A^2(2T_b - t)
$$

At the peak $t = T_b$: $y(T_b) = A^2 T_b = E_b$ — the matched filter **concentrates all signal energy at one point**.

---

### 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Question:** A BPSK receiver operates over AWGN with $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. The bit energy is $E_b = 8 \times 10^{-18}$ J. Find the probability of bit error.

**Solution:**

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 400
$$

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 400}\right) = Q(\sqrt{800}) = Q(28.28)
$$

Using the Q-table or approximation $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$ for large $x$:

$$
P_e \approx \frac{1}{28.28 \times 2.507} e^{-400} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-174} \approx 0
$$

**Conclusion:** With this $E_b/N_0 = 26$ dB, the link is essentially error-free.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: General Binary Digital Receiver

The following Mermaid flowchart shows the canonical structure of an optimum binary receiver in AWGN, mapping every functional block and the signal at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received Waveform r(t) Equals s_i(t) plus n(t)] --> B[Bandpass Filter and LNA]
    B --> C[Multiplier 1: r(t) times phi_1(t)]
    B --> D[Multiplier 2: r(t) times phi_2(t)]
    C --> E[Integrator over 0 to T_b]
    D --> F[Integrator over 0 to T_b]
    E --> G[Sampler at t equals T_b: r1]
    F --> H[Sampler at t equals T_b: r2]
    G --> I[Decision Device and Threshold Comparator]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Output Bit m hat]
    I --> K[Bit Error Probability P_e]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two parallel branches correspond to the two orthonormal basis functions $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$. The integrator outputs are the projections $r_1, r_2$ onto the signal space. The decision device compares $r_1$ and $r_2$ and emits the most likely transmitted bit.

---

### 4.2 Equivalent Realization Using Matched Filters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input r(t)] --> B[Matched Filter 1: h1(t) equals s1 T_b minus t]
    A --> C[Matched Filter 2: h2(t) equals s2 T_b minus t]
    B --> D[Sampler at t equals T_b]
    C --> E[Sampler at t equals T_b]
    D --> F[Comparator]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is r1 greater than r2?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Decide Bit 0]
    G -- No  --> I[Decide Bit 1]
```

---

### 4.3 Signal Space Constellation Diagram (Conceptual via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BPSK_Constellation
        P0["s1 at plus sqrt_Eb on axis 1"]
        P1["s2 at minus sqrt_Eb on axis 1"]
        V[(Decision boundary at origin)]
        P0 --- V
        V --- P1
    end
    P0 -. distance d equals 2 sqrt_Eb .-> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The two signal points are placed symmetrically about the origin. The decision boundary (the perpendicular bisector) is the locus where $\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_1\| = \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_2\|$. Any received vector $\mathbf{r}$ falling on the right of this boundary is decoded as "0"; on the left, as "1".

---

### 4.4 Functional State Machine: Matched Filter Output Over Time

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoisePhase: t less than 0
    NoisePhase --> RisingEdge: t equals 0
    RisingEdge --> Peak: t equals T_b divided by 2
    Peak --> SamplingInstant: t equals T_b
    SamplingInstant --> Compare: Decision device
    Compare --> OutputBit0: r greater than threshold
    Compare --> OutputBit1: r less than threshold
    OutputBit0 --> [*]
    OutputBit1 --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This state machine mirrors the triangular shape of the matched filter output derived in Section 3.5. The sampling instant $t = T_b$ is the only time at which the SNR is maximized.

---

### 4.5 Receiver in the End-to-End Communication Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Bits] --> ENC[Channel Encoder]
    ENC --> MOD[Modulator: Maps Bits to Waveform s_i t]
    MOD --> CH[(AWGN Channel: Adds Noise n t)]
    CH --> RX_BPF[Receiver Front End: BPF and LNA]
    RX_BPF --> RX_MF[Matched Filter or Correlator]
    RX_MF --> RX_SAMP[Sampler at t equals T_b]
    RX_SAMP --> RX_DEC[Decision Device]
    RX_DEC --> DEC[Channel Decoder]
    DEC --> SNK[Destination Sink]
    RX_DEC -. computes .-> PEOUT[P_e]
```
