## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Define a **matched filter**. State and prove the property that gives it the maximum output SNR.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A matched filter is a linear time-invariant filter whose impulse response is the **time-reversed, time-shifted replica** of the known signal: $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$. **Property (North's theorem, 1 mark):** It maximizes the output signal-to-noise ratio at the sampling instant. **Proof (2 marks):** Using the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality on the SNR functional:

$$
\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{\left[\int_0^{T_b} s(\tau)h(T_b - \tau)d\tau\right]^2}{\frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b}h^2(\tau)d\tau} \le \frac{\int_0^{T_b}s^2(\tau)d\tau}{N_0/2} = \frac{2E_s}{N_0}
$$

Equality holds only when $h(t) = c\,s(T_b - t)$, which is the matched filter condition.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** What is the **maximum likelihood decision rule** for binary signaling in AWGN? Express it as a minimum-distance rule.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For equiprobable binary signals $s_1(t), s_2(t)$ corrupted by AWGN, the ML rule (1 mark) is:

$$
\text{Decide } s_1 \iff f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_1) > f(\mathbf{r} \mid s_2)
$$

Since the conditional PDF is Gaussian, taking the log and dropping common terms (1 mark) gives the **minimum-distance rule** (1 mark):

$$
\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_1\|^2 < \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_2\|^2 \iff \text{Decide } s_1
$$

The receiver thus picks the signal point closest to the received vector in Euclidean signal space.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **optimum receiver for binary signals in AWGN** using the **correlator** realization. Derive the probability of error expression for **coherent BPSK**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the matched filter impulse response for the signal $s(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ transmitted over $[0, T_b]$. Compute the matched filter output at the sampling instant. Compare the BER of **coherent BPSK and BFSK** at high SNR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Part (a) Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** The receiver consists of two parallel correlators (multiplier + integrator), one for each basis function, followed by a sampler and a decision device. The threshold is set at zero for equiprobable BPSK.

**Mathematical Derivation (4 marks):** Let the orthonormal basis be $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The received signal is $r(t) = \pm\sqrt{E_b}\phi(t) + n(t)$.

The correlator output is:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b}r(t)\phi(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + \int_0^{T_b}n(t)\phi(t)\,dt
$$

The noise integral is Gaussian: $n_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. Under $H_0$ (bit "0"), $r \sim \mathcal{N}(+\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$. Threshold $\lambda = 0$:

$$
P_e = P(r < 0 \mid H_0) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

---

**(b) — Part (b) Solution:**

**Matched Filter for Cosine (3 marks):** $h(t) = s(T_b - t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c(T_b - t))$. Because $f_cT_b = n$ (integer number of cycles) and cosine is even, $h(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over $[0, T_b]$. Output at $t = T_b$:

$$
y(T_b) = \int_0^{T_b}A^2\cos^2(2\pi f_c\tau)\,d\tau = A^2 \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} = E_b
$$

**BER Comparison (4 marks):**
- BPSK: $P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$
- BFSK: $P_e^{\text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$

Using the asymptotic approximation $Q(x) \approx \frac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$:

$$
\frac{P_e^{\text{BFSK}}}{P_e^{\text{BPSK}}} \approx \sqrt{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)
$$

For large $E_b/N_0$, BPSK outperforms BFSK by approximately **3 dB** in required $E_b/N_0$ for a given $P_e$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the **signal space representation** of binary signals. Show that the BPSK and BFSK signal constellations are equivalent to 1-D and 2-D geometric problems. Derive the minimum-distance decision rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital communication system uses **coherent BPSK** over AWGN with $E_b/N_0 = 12$ dB. Compute the probability of bit error. Repeat for **coherent BFSK** at the same SNR. If the system requires $P_e \le 10^{-6}$, find the additional $E_b/N_0$ in dB needed for BFSK. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Part (a) Solution:**

**Signal Space (3 marks):** A set of $M$ deterministic signals can be represented as points in an $N$-dimensional vector space ($N \le M$). A basis $\{\phi_k(t)\}$ of orthonormal functions is chosen, and each signal is represented by the vector $\mathbf{s}_i = [s_{i1}, s_{i2}, \ldots, s_{iN}]$ where $s_{ik} = \int s_i(t)\phi_k(t)\,dt$.

- **BPSK:** $N = 1$, $\mathbf{s}_1 = +\sqrt{E_b}$, $\mathbf{s}_2 = -\sqrt{E_b}$.
- **BFSK:** $N = 2$, $\mathbf{s}_1 = [\sqrt{E_b}, 0]$, $\mathbf{s}_2 = [0, \sqrt{E_b}]$.

**Minimum-Distance Rule (4 marks):** For AWGN, the log-likelihood is proportional to $-\|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|^2 / N_0$. The ML rule is:

$$
\hat{i} = \arg\min_i \|\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s}_i\|
$$

Geometrically, the decision region for $\mathbf{s}_i$ is the **Voronoi cell** — the set of points closer to $\mathbf{s}_i$ than to any other $\mathbf{s}_j$.

---

**(b) — Part (b) Solution:**

**Convert dB to linear:** $E_b/N_0 = 10^{12/10} = 15.85$.

**BPSK BER (2 marks):**

$$
P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 15.85}\right) = Q(\sqrt{31.70}) = Q(5.63) \approx 9.1 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**BFSK BER (2 marks):**

$$
P_e^{\text{BFSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{15.85}\right) = Q(3.98) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Additional SNR for BFSK to reach $10^{-6}$ (3 marks):**
Set $Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right) = 10^{-6}$, hence $\sqrt{E_b/N_0} = Q^{-1}(10^{-6}) \approx 4.75$, giving $E_b/N_0 \approx 22.6$ (linear) = **13.5 dB**.
Extra SNR needed = $13.5 - 12 = $ **1.5 dB**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in BPSK's $Q$ argument:** A common mistake is writing $P_e^{\text{BPSK}} = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ instead of $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. This yields a 3 dB error in the BER.
> 2. **Mixing up $N_0$ and $N_0/2$:** The "two-sided" PSD is $N_0/2$, but the noise power per dimension is $N_0/2$. Always be explicit about which convention is being used.
> 3. **Setting threshold at $\lambda = 0$ for unequal priors:** If $P(H_1) \neq P(H_2)$, the threshold shifts to $\lambda = \frac{N_0}{2\sqrt{E_b}} \ln\!\left(\frac{P(H_2)}{P(H_1)}\right)$. KTU frequently tests this case.
> 4. **Skipping the signal space basis justification:** Marks are reserved for explicitly defining $\phi_k(t)$ and showing the projection integrals, not just quoting the result.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Receivers" — Module 1:**

- **The receiver is an ML / MAP decision device** operating in signal space, not on raw waveforms.
- **Matched filter impulse response:** $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$ — time-reversed and shifted copy of the signal.
- **Correlator = matched filter** (mathematical dual); both produce the same decision variable at $t = T_b$.
- **Maximum output SNR** of a matched filter is $2E_b/N_0$, derived via the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.
- **Minimum-distance rule:** Decide the signal point closest (in Euclidean sense) to the received vector.
- **BPSK BER:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ — antipodal, 1-D constellation.
- **BFSK BER (coherent):** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$ — orthogonal, 2-D constellation, 3 dB worse than BPSK.
- **DPSK BER:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ — non-coherent, 3 dB worse at high SNR.
- **Threshold for equiprobable binary signals:** $\lambda = 0$ (symmetric constellation).
- **Q-function identities:** $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$, $Q(0) = 0.5$, and $Q(x) \to 0$ exponentially as $x \to \infty$.
- **Noise projection variance:** Each basis projection $n_i$ has variance $N_0/2$ (two-sided PSD convention).
- **Decision region geometry:** Voronoi cell bounded by perpendicular bisector of the signal pair.
- **Receiver sub-blocks (in order):** BPF + LNA $\rightarrow$ Demodulator (Matched Filter / Correlator) $\rightarrow$ Sampler $\rightarrow$ Decision Device $\rightarrow$ Decoder.
- **The matched filter is optimum only under:** known signal, AWGN, and linear time-invariant filter assumption.
- **Real-world examples to cite in answers:** 5G NR baseband, GPS PRN code acquisition, optical fiber integrate-and-dump, radar echo detection, and UART bit sampling.
- **Numerical safety:** Always convert dB to linear before applying the $Q$ function: $E_b/N_0|_{\text{lin}} = 10^{E_b/N_0|_{\text{dB}}/10}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmission Medium (Transmission Channel / Link)**
> A *transmission medium* is the physical path over which an electromagnetic signal propagates from a transmitter to a receiver. In the OSI and TCP/IP layered architectures, the transmission medium constitutes the **Layer 0 (Physical Layer substrate)** — the foundation upon which the Physical Layer (Layer 1) places bits on the link.

The data communication literature partitions all transmission media into two super-classes:

| Super-Class | Alternate Names | Physical Confinement |
|---|---|---|
| **Guided Media** | Wired / Bounded / Line Media | Signal confined inside a solid conductor (copper, glass/plastic) |
| **Unguided Media** | Wireless / Unbounded / Free-Space Media | Signal radiates through atmosphere, vacuum, or water |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine two cities that need to exchange goods (data):**
>
> 1. **Guided Media = A Toll Road (NH-44).** Trucks (signals) are physically locked to a fixed paved surface. Bandwidth is limited by lane count, attenuation by road condition, and noise by traffic density. You can predict and engineer the path.
>
> 2. **Unguided Media = An Air Corridor.** Goods are air-dropped from a plane (transmitter) and must be caught by another plane (receiver). Range is limited by atmosphere (attenuation/weather), bandwidth by frequency allocation, and the path is unpredictable.

A student should remember: **the medium does NOT create information — it only carries, attenuates, distorts, and delays it.**

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Memorize)

The KTU 2024 Scheme paper uses the following universally accepted metrics:

- **Attenuation (A)**: measured in **decibels (dB)**, where $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$
- **Bandwidth (B)**: measured in **Hertz (Hz)** for analog, **bits per second (bps)** for digital
- **Propagation Speed**: $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper, $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum
- **Propagation Delay ($\tau_p$)**: time for a single bit to traverse the link — **seconds (s)**
- **Noise Floor**: ambient power — measured in **dBm (decibels relative to one milliwatt)**
- **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)**: dimensionless — often expressed in **dB**

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Classification Snapshot (Memorize This Tree)

```
Transmission Media
├── 1. Guided (Wired)
│     ├── 1.1 Twisted-Pair Copper
│     │       ├── Unshielded Twisted Pair (UTP) — CAT5e, CAT6, CAT6a, CAT7, CAT8
│     │       └── Shielded Twisted Pair (STP)
│     ├── 1.2 Coaxial Cable
│     │       ├── Baseband (50 Ω) — 10BASE2, 10BASE5
│     │       └── Broadband (75 Ω) — Cable TV, DOCSIS
│     └── 1.3 Optical Fiber Cable
│             ├── Single-Mode Fiber (SMF) — 9/125 µm
│             ├── Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF) — 50/125 µm, 62.5/125 µm
│             └── Plastic Optical Fiber (POF)
└── 2. Unguided (Wireless)
      ├── 2.1 Terrestrial Microwave (2–40 GHz)
      ├── 2.2 Satellite Microwave (C, Ku, Ka bands)
      ├── 2.3 Radio Broadcast (3 kHz–300 MHz)
      ├── 2.4 Infrared (300 GHz–400 THz) — Line-of-Sight
      └── 2.5 Millimeter Wave (30–300 GHz) — 5G FR2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Takeaway:** Every media question begins with the phrase *"Classify and describe…"* — your answer should always start by stating **Guided vs Unguided**, then sub-classify.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Guided Media — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Twisted-Pair Copper Cable

**Operational Concept:** Two insulated copper conductors (~22–26 AWG) are *helically twisted* around each other. The twist is the genius of the design: any external **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** induces *equal and opposite* currents in adjacent twists, which cancel at the receiver (a principle called **common-mode rejection**).

- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** – no metallic foil; relies *purely* on twist-rate to reject noise.
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)** – adds a metallic foil/braid. Used in **Token Ring (IBM Type 1)** and industrial EMI-heavy environments.

| Property | Value (Typical) |
|---|---|
| Characteristic Impedance | $100 \pm 15$ **Ω** |
| Attenuation @ 100 MHz, 100 m | $\approx 22$ dB (CAT6) |
| Bandwidth | Up to **2 GHz** (CAT8) |
| Max Segment Length | **100 m** (Ethernet rule) |
| Cost | Lowest |

#### 2.1.2 Coaxial Cable

**Operational Concept:** A center conductor, a dielectric insulator, an outer cylindrical shield (braid/foil), and a plastic jacket. The geometry creates a perfectly controlled cylindrical waveguide field, **eliminating external radiation** and **confining the EM field** between center and shield.

- **Baseband (50 Ω)** — used in legacy 10BASE5 ("Thicknet") and 10BASE2 ("Thinnet") Ethernet.
- **Broadband (75 Ω)** — used in cable television and DOCSIS cable-modem internet.

| Property | Value (Typical) |
|---|---|
| Characteristic Impedance | **50 Ω** (baseband), **75 Ω** (broadband) |
| Bandwidth | Up to **~1 GHz** practical |
| Max Segment Length | 500 m (10BASE5), 185 m (10BASE2) |
| Attenuation @ 100 MHz, 100 m | $\approx 7$ dB (RG-8) |

#### 2.1.3 Optical Fiber Cable

**Operational Concept:** An optical fiber is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide. It consists of a **core** (high refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by a **cladding** (lower refractive index $n_2$). Light is guided by **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core-cladding boundary, provided the angle of incidence at the core-cladding interface exceeds the **critical angle** $\theta_c$.

- **SMF (Single-Mode Fiber)** – 9 µm core, propagates only the *fundamental* mode. Used for long-haul (>40 km) telecom.
- **MMF (Multi-Mode Fiber)** – 50/62.5 µm core, supports hundreds of modes. Used in LANs and data centers (<2 km).

| Property | SMF (1310 nm) | MMF (850 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| Core Diameter | **9 µm** | **50/62.5 µm** |
| Bandwidth-Distance | Practically **unlimited** | 500 MHz·km (OM1) – 4700 MHz·km (OM4) |
| Typical Attenuation | **0.35 dB/km** | 3.0 dB/km |
| Light Source | Laser (DFB) | LED / VCSEL |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

### 2.2 Unguided Media — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Terrestrial & Satellite Microwave

**Operational Concept:** Highly directional beams at frequencies **2–40 GHz** propagate in straight lines. Above ~3 GHz, *line-of-sight* is required and signals are blocked by solid obstacles.

- **Parabolic Dish Antennas** focus the beam — gain $G = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^2$ (where $A_e$ is the effective aperture).
- **Satellites** sit in Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO, 35,786 km), Medium Earth Orbit (MEO, 2,000–35,786 km), or Low Earth Orbit (LEO, 160–2,000 km — e.g., Starlink).

#### 2.2.2 Radio Waves & Infrared

- **Radio (3 kHz–300 MHz):** Omni-directional, follows Earth's curvature, used in AM/FM broadcast, Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz), Bluetooth.
- **Infrared (300 GHz–400 THz):** Line-of-sight, does *not* penetrate walls; used in TV remotes, IrDA, and indoor VLC (Visible Light Communication).

### 2.3 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following six equations cover ~80% of numerical questions asked in KTU ESE under this topic.** Memorize them as a unified toolkit.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attenuation (Power Loss) | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **dB** |
| 2 | Total Link Attenuation | $A_{total} = \alpha L + A_{connector} \cdot N$ | $\alpha$ = dB/km, $L$ = km |
| 3 | Free-Space Path Loss (Friis) | $L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$ | **dB**; $d$ = meters |
| 4 | Wavelength-Frequency | $\lambda = c / f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 5 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **bps** |
| 6 | Nyquist Max Bit Rate | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | **bps**; $M$ = signal levels |
| 7 | Critical Angle (Fiber) | $\sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | requires $n_1 > n_2$ |
| 8 | Numerical Aperture (Fiber) | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_0 \sin \theta_a$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Propagation Delay | $\tau_p = \dfrac{L}{v_p}$ where $v_p = c / n$ | **seconds** |
| 10 | Refractive Index of Medium | $n = c / v_p$ | dimensionless, $n \ge 1$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UTP + RJ-45** dominate enterprise LANs because of cost, ease of termination, and PoE capability.
- **Coaxial** remains in cable-broadband last-mile because of its 75 Ω standard and shield effectiveness.
- **Fiber** powers the **internet backbone** (undersea cables), **5G fronthaul**, and **data-center spine-leaf** architectures — its near-zero attenuation and immunity to EMI make it irreplaceable at scale.
- **Microwave/satellite** bridges rural areas, ships, and aircraft where fiber is uneconomical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attenuation, Power Loss, and dB Mathematics

**Statement:** When a signal of input power $P_{in}$ travels through a medium of length $L$ km and emerges with output power $P_{out}$, define attenuation and the standard dB formula.

**Step 1 — Definition of Power Loss Ratio.**

The power loss (a dimensionless ratio) is defined as the input power divided by the output power:

$$
R = \frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to a logarithmic scale using the bel.**

A "bel" is $\log_{10}$ of the power ratio. Thus:

$$
L_{bel} = \log_{10}(R) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Scale to decibels (1 bel = 10 decibels).**

$$
L_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the unknown output power.**

Exponentiating both sides by $1/10$:

$$
\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}} = 10^{L_{dB}/10}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-L_{dB}/10}
$$

**Step 5 — Special cases for KTU numericals.**

- A 3 dB loss $\Rightarrow P_{out} = P_{in}/2$ (half-power point).
- A 10 dB loss $\Rightarrow P_{out} = P_{in}/10$ (10× loss).
- A 20 dB loss $\Rightarrow P_{out} = P_{in}/100$ (100× loss).
- A 30 dB loss $\Rightarrow P_{out} = P_{in}/1000$ (1000× loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A doubling of distance does NOT mean double the dB loss — the dB value scales *linearly* with distance, but the *power* scales *exponentially*.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation)

**Statement:** Derive the path loss experienced by an isotropic antenna radiating uniformly in free space.

**Step 1 — Isotropic Power Density at Distance $d$.**

If a transmitter radiates total power $P_t$ uniformly, the power density (W/m²) at distance $d$ is the radiated power divided by the surface area of the sphere of radius $d$:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Received Power by an Aperture of Effective Area $A_e$.**

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot A_e}{4\pi d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the antenna-effective area relation.**

For an isotropic radiator, $A_e = \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the path loss as a ratio.**

$$
L_{fs} = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels (the form KTU uses).**

$$
L_{fs\,(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

Equivalently, in the form most useful in KTU numericals:

$$
L_{fs\,(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44
$$

where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**, the 32.44 coming from $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ with appropriate unit normalization.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Angle in Optical Fiber (TIR Condition)

**Statement:** Find the maximum angle of incidence at the core-cladding interface for which light will be totally internally reflected.

**Step 1 — Snell's Law at the core-cladding interface.**

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2
$$

where $\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence (from the core normal) and $\theta_2$ is the refracted angle into the cladding.

**Step 2 — TIR begins at $\theta_2 = 90°$.**

Total internal reflection occurs when the refracted ray grazes the interface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$, which means $\sin \theta_2 = 1$:

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \cdot 1
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical angle.**

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

Since $n_1 > n_2$, this ratio is $<1$, so $\theta_c$ is well-defined.

**Step 4 — Acceptance Angle and Numerical Aperture.**

A ray entering the fiber from air ($n_0 = 1$) at external angle $\theta_a$ refracts to $\theta_1$ at the core face. Applying Snell's law at the air-core boundary and substituting, the acceptance condition is:

$$
\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = NA
$$

The quantity $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ is the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** — a dimensionless figure of merit for a fiber. Typical telecom fiber: $NA \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

> **Problem:** A signal of 100 mW is launched into a fiber of length 50 km with an attenuation coefficient of 0.4 dB/km. There are two splices, each introducing 0.5 dB loss. Connector loss is 1 dB at each end. Find the received power.
>
> **Solution:**
> 1. Fiber loss: $0.4 \times 50 = 20$ dB
> 2. Splice loss: $2 \times 0.5 = 1$ dB
> 3. Connector loss: $2 \times 1 = 2$ dB
> 4. **Total loss:** $20 + 1 + 2 = 23$ dB
> 5. **Output power:** $P_{out} = 100 \text{ mW} \times 10^{-23/10} = 100 \times 10^{-2.3} = 100 \times 5.01 \times 10^{-3} = 0.501 \text{ mW} = 501\ \mu\text{W}$

---

### 3.5 KTU Comparison Matrix — Choose the Right Medium

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix as a "cheat sheet" during exam practice.** The "best" medium is *always* problem-specific.

| Criterion | UTP | Coaxial | SMF | MMF | Microwave | Satellite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | Medium (GHz) | Medium | Very High | High | High | High |
| Attenuation | High (dB/100m) | Medium | Very Low (dB/km) | Low | Free-space | Free-space + rain |
| EMI Immunity | Low | Medium | **Perfect** | Perfect | Low | Low |
| Security | Low | Medium | **Very High** | Very High | Low | Low |
| Cost / meter | **Lowest** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | **Highest** |
| Typical Use | LAN, Phone | Cable TV | Backbone, FTTH | Data center | Urban links | Rural, Maritime |
| Installation | Easy | Medium | Hard | Hard | Hard | Very Hard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Transmission Media

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transmission Media] --> B[Guided Wired]
    A --> C[Unguided Wireless]

    B --> B1[Twisted Pair]
    B --> B2[Coaxial Cable]
    B --> B3[Optical Fiber]

    B1 --> B1a[UTP CAT5e to CAT8]
    B1 --> B1b[STP]

    B2 --> B2a[Baseband 50 ohm]
    B2 --> B2b[Broadband 75 ohm]

    B3 --> B3a[SMF 9 by 125]
    B3 --> B3b[MMF 50 or 62.5 by 125]
    B3 --> B3c[Plastic Optical Fiber]

    C --> C1[Terrestrial Microwave 2 to 40 GHz]
    C --> C2[Satellite Microwave GEO MEO LEO]
    C --> C3[Radio Broadcast 3 kHz to 300 MHz]
    C --> C4[Infrared 300 GHz to 400 THz]
    C --> C5[Millimeter Wave 30 to 300 GHz]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fiber Optic Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Source Data Bits] --> encA[Line Encoder NRZ or Manchester]
    encA --> elecA[E O Converter LED or LASER]
    elecA --> fibA[Optical Fiber Core n1 and Cladding n2]
    fibA --> spaA[Splices and Connectors]
    spaA --> recA[Photodetector PIN or APD]
    recA --> ampA[Regenerator and Amplifier]
    ampA --> decA[Line Decoder]
    decA --> dstA[Destination Data Bits]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Wireless Terrestrial Microwave Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    tx1[Transmitter Modem] --> mux1[Multiplexer TDM or FDM]
    mux1 --> upm1[Upconverter RF Carrier]
    upm1 --> hpa1[High Power Amplifier HPA]
    hpa1 --> dish1[Parabolic Dish Antenna Tx]
    dish1 -. Line of Sight .-> dish2[Parabolic Dish Antenna Rx]
    dish2 --> lna1[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    lna1 --> dwn1[Downconverter to IF]
    dwn1 --> dem1[Demultiplexer]
    dem1 --> rx1[Receiver Modem]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — End-to-End Communication Through a Medium

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Transmitter] --> ENC1[Encoder]
    ENC1 --> MOD1[Modulator]
    MOD1 --> TXA1[Tx Antenna or Coupler]
    TXA1 -. Guided or Free Space .-> RXA1[Rx Antenna or Coupler]
    RXA1 --> DEM1[Demodulator]
    DEM1 --> DEC1[Decoder]
    DEC1 --> R1[Receiver]
    NOISE1[Noise and Attenuation injected at medium] -.- TXA1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The *dashed line* between TXA1 and RXA1 represents the medium — the same component whether the path is a copper wire, glass fiber, or free space. The *blocks* before and after are identical for any medium; only the *coupling* device changes (RJ-45 vs BNC vs SC/LC connector vs dish).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify transmission media. Differentiate between guided and unguided media with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Transmission media are classified into **two** types based on signal propagation:

**1. Guided (Wired/Bounded) Media** — The signal is confined inside a solid physical path.
- *Examples:* Twisted-pair copper cable (UTP), Coaxial cable, Optical fiber.

**2. Unguided (Wireless/Unbounded) Media** — The signal radiates through free space (air/vacuum).
- *Examples:* Terrestrial microwave link, Satellite communication, Radio broadcast, Infrared.

| Parameter | Guided | Unguided |
|---|---|---|
| Signal path | Fixed | Variable (radiates) |
| Capacity | Up to Tbps (fiber) | Up to Gbps (5G mmWave) |
| Security | Higher | Lower |
| Installation | Per-meter cost high in fiber | High capex (towers) |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for differentiation table.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define attenuation. A signal of 50 mW is transmitted; after traversing 10 km of UTP the received power is 0.5 mW. Calculate the total attenuation in dB.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Attenuation is the gradual loss of signal power as it propagates through a medium, expressed in decibels (dB).

**Calculation:**

$$
A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

$$
A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{0.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Per-km attenuation:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{A}{L} = \frac{20}{10} = 2 \text{ dB/km}
$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for correct value.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **Question Choice:** Students answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** *Compare the three guided media — twisted pair, coaxial, and optical fiber — in terms of construction, bandwidth, attenuation, EMI immunity, and typical applications.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A satellite link operates at 6 GHz with a distance of 36,000 km. Calculate the free-space path loss. If a transmitter power of 10 W is used with antenna gains of 40 dB (Tx) and 50 dB (Rx), compute the received power. Take $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a): 7 marks**

| Property | Twisted Pair | Coaxial Cable | Optical Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Two insulated copper wires twisted helically | Center conductor, dielectric, cylindrical shield, jacket | Glass core, glass cladding, buffer, jacket |
| **Bandwidth** | Up to 2 GHz (CAT8) | Up to 1 GHz | > 60 THz (theoretical) |
| **Attenuation** | High (~20 dB/100 m @ 100 MHz) | Moderate (~7 dB/100 m @ 100 MHz) | Very low (0.35 dB/km @ 1310 nm) |
| **EMI Immunity** | Low (twist helps; STP better) | Medium (shielded) | **Immune** (uses photons, not electrons) |
| **Typical Use** | LAN, telephone | Cable TV, broadband | Backbone, FTTH, undersea cables |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Credit:** Award 1 mark per *valid comparison* with a justified example. Mere copy-paste of features without contrast = 0.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b): 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute free-space path loss.**

$$
L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

$$
L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4 \pi \times 36{,}000{,}000}{0.05}\right)
$$

$$
= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4.524 \times 10^9}{1}\right)
$$

$$
= 20 \times 9.6556 = 193.11 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert transmitter power to dBm.**

$$
P_{t\,(dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^4) = 40 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Friis transmission equation with antenna gains.**

$$
P_r \text{ (dBm)} = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs}
$$

$$
P_r = 40 + 40 + 50 - 193.11 = -63.11 \text{ dBm}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to watts.**

$$
P_r = 10^{(-63.11/10)} \text{ mW} = 10^{-6.311} \text{ mW} \approx 4.88 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mW} = 0.488 \text{ pW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 1:** Forgetting to convert $P_t$ to dBm before applying gain subtraction. If you stay in linear units, the computation collapses to numerical overflow. Always use dB.
>
> **Common Pitfall 2:** Using $c = 3 \times 10^8$ but plugging in GHz directly as Hz ($6 \times 10^9$ Hz) — students often write $6 \times 10^6$ Hz, off by 3 orders of magnitude. The numerical answer then looks plausible but is wrong by **30 dB**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *With a neat diagram, explain the principle of total internal reflection in optical fiber. Derive the expression for the numerical aperture.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ and $n_2 = 1.46$. Find: (i) the critical angle at the core-cladding interface, (ii) the numerical aperture, (iii) the acceptance angle, and (iv) the maximum bit rate possible over a 5 km link if the bandwidth-distance product is 500 MHz·km.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a): 7 marks**

**Principle of TIR (3 marks):**
When a light ray travels from a medium of higher refractive index ($n_1$, the core) to one of lower refractive index ($n_2$, the cladding), the refracted ray bends *away* from the normal. Beyond a certain incidence angle (the **critical angle** $\theta_c$), the refracted ray grazes the interface — at and beyond this angle, no refraction occurs; the ray is **totally internally reflected**, and light is *trapped* within the core.

**Diagram (Block-level Functional — since physics ray-tracing is non-trivial in Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    AA[Light Source] --> AB[Core n1]
    AB -. Ray path zigzag .-> AC[Cladding n2]
    AC -. TIR at boundary .-> AB
    AB --> AD[Far-end Output]
```

**Derivation of NA (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Snell's law at air-core boundary:**

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1 \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Snell's law at core-cladding boundary (TIR condition):**

$$
n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° \Rightarrow \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}
$$

But $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_c$, so $\sin \theta_1 = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - (n_2/n_1)^2}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into (1):**

$$
n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - (n_2/n_1)^2} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Numerical Aperture (with $n_0 = 1$ for air):**

$$
NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b): 7 marks**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, length $L = 5$ km, $B \cdot L = 500$ MHz·km.

**(i) Critical angle:**

$$
\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.46}{1.48} = 0.9865
$$

$$
\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.46°
$$

**(ii) Numerical Aperture:**

$$
NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425
$$

**(iii) Acceptance angle:**

$$
\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.04°
$$

**(iv) Maximum bit rate (using $B \cdot L$):**

The bandwidth of the 5 km link:

$$
B = \frac{500 \text{ MHz·km}}{5 \text{ km}} = 100 \text{ MHz}
$$

Assuming **NRZ encoding**, the maximum bit rate $\approx 2B$ (Nyquist, binary):

$$
C \approx 2 \times 100 \text{ MHz} = 200 \text{ Mbps}
$$

(Or, depending on the engineering convention the examiner expects, $C = B$ for a baseband pass, yielding 100 Mbps. State your assumption.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall 3 — B·L units:** A bandwidth-distance product of 500 MHz·km does **not** mean 500 MHz at 1 km. It means 500 MHz·km total — divide by the actual link length. Many students leave it as 500 MHz and lose 1–2 marks.
>
> **Common Pitfall 4 — Snell’s law in fiber:** Students frequently write $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ with $\theta_2 = 0$ thinking that's "TIR" — it's not. TIR corresponds to the refracted ray *just grazing* the interface, i.e., $\theta_2 = 90°$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Transmission Media**

- **Classification first, always.** Every answer begins: *"Media are of two types — guided and unguided…"*
- **Attenuation is in dB** (NOT linear). Formula: $A = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$.
- **dB rules of thumb:** 3 dB = half power; 10 dB = 10× loss; 20 dB = 100× loss.
- **Friis Free-Space Loss:** $L_{fs} = 20 \log(4\pi d / \lambda)$; with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, the constant is **32.44**.
- **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$. Always convert $f$ to Hz before dividing.
- **Shannon capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the *theoretical* upper bound; **Nyquist** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ — the *symbol-rate* bound for noiseless channels.
- **Twisted pair's** secret is the **twist** (common-mode noise rejection).
- **Coaxial's** secret is the **cylindrical geometry** (eliminates external radiation loss).
- **Fiber's** secret is **TIR** at $n_1/n_2$ interface — critical angle: $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$.
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sin\theta_a$.
- **Microwave = Line-of-Sight**; cannot bend around obstacles (unlike lower-frequency radio).
- **Satellite propagation delay** (GEO) $\approx 250$ ms one-way; this matters for VoIP, gaming, TCP.
- **5G mmWave (FR2, 24–52 GHz)** has high bandwidth but very short range and poor wall penetration — drives the need for **small cells**.
- **Most common KTU exam trap:** confusing $P_{in}/P_{out}$ with $P_{out}/P_{in}$ in the dB formula. Always verify the sign: positive dB = loss.
- **Attenuation grows linearly with length (dB/km)** but power decays **exponentially** (10^(−L/10)).
- **SMF vs MMF:** SMF is for distance (long-haul, single wavelength laser), MMF is for cost (short-reach, LED/VCSEL).

> **End of Module 1 — Transmission Media Notes.**
> **Next Recommended Topic:** *Switching Techniques & Multiplexing (Module 1 continuation).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Flow Types: Simplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Flow Types: Simplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Data Communication**, the direction in which data travels between two communicating devices is known as the **Data Flow Mode** (or Transmission Mode). According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *Data & Computer Communication*, data flow across a transmission medium is classified into three primary categories:

1. **Simplex**
2. **Half-Duplex**
3. **Full-Duplex**

**Simplex transmission** is the most restrictive mode of data communication, in which the communication between a sender (transmitter) and a receiver is **strictly unidirectional**. The signal travels in **one and only one direction**, and the receiver has no capability to send back any acknowledgment, control signal, or data to the sender.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** *Simplex communication is a one-way data transmission system in which information flows from the sender to the receiver, but the receiver cannot transmit any data back to the sender over the same channel.*

The channel capacity (bandwidth) is entirely allocated to the sender, since the reverse path either does not exist physically or is permanently disabled.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogies (Intuitive Understanding)

To understand simplex intuitively, picture the following real-life situations:

- **A Public Address (PA) System** at a railway station: The announcer speaks through the microphone, and passengers only listen through the loudspeakers. Passengers cannot reply through the same system.
- **A Television (TV) Broadcast:** The TV transmitter sends audio and video signals to your television set. Your TV cannot send anything back to the broadcasting station.
- **FM Radio Transmission:** The radio station transmits music; the listener only receives it.
- **A Keyboard to Computer:** The keyboard sends keystroke data to the CPU, but the CPU does not send signals back through the keyboard cable.
- **A One-Way Street:** Traffic can only move in one direction; there is no provision for oncoming vehicles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook:** Simplex = "Mono-directional Street." Once data leaves the source, it can only arrive at the destination — never return.

## 1.3 Key Design Metrics

- **Channel Bandwidth Allocation:** 100% reserved for the forward direction.
- **Reverse Path:** Physically absent or logically disabled.
- **Acknowledgment (ACK):** Not possible; therefore **no error-control feedback** from the receiver.
- **Typical Use Case:** Broadcasting, telemetry, sensor-to-base-station reporting, public alerting systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture

A simplex communication system is built using three fundamental building blocks:

1. **Source / Transmitter (Tx):** Originates and pushes the data signal onto the medium. Only capable of *transmitting*.
2. **Transmission Channel (Medium):** The physical path — copper wire, optical fiber, or wireless spectrum — that carries the signal in **one direction only**.
3. **Destination / Receiver (Rx):** Accepts and interprets the signal. Only capable of *receiving*; cannot reverse the flow.

```
[ Source (Tx) ]  --->  [ Channel ]  --->  [ Destination (Rx) ]
```

## 2.2 Why Simplex? — Engineering Rationale

- **Why used:** It is the **simplest, cheapest, and most bandwidth-efficient** topology because the entire channel bandwidth is dedicated to forward traffic. No reverse channel hardware, no collision-detection logic, and no acknowledgment mechanism are required.
- **How it works:** The transmitter modulates data onto a carrier and continuously pumps it through the medium. The receiver demodulates and processes it in real-time.
- **Trade-off:** The total absence of feedback means that **error correction and flow control cannot be implemented at the data-link layer** for the reverse direction.

## 2.3 Comprehensive Comparison Table (Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex)

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the **most frequently asked 3-mark questions** in KTU Module 1. Memorize the table verbatim.

| Parameter | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Data Flow** | One direction only | Both directions, **one at a time** | Both directions, **simultaneously** |
| **Sender Capability** | Send only | Send OR Receive | Send AND Receive at the same time |
| **Receiver Capability** | Receive only | Receive OR Send | Receive AND Send simultaneously |
| **Channel Utilization** | $100\%$ forward path | $100\%$, shared by time | $100\%$ forward + $100\%$ reverse |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Acknowledgment (ACK)** | Not possible | Possible (sequential) | Possible (parallel) |
| **Typical Examples** | TV, Radio, Keyboard, PA System | Walkie-Talkie, CB Radio | Telephone, Mobile Networks |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | Maximum (for one-way) | Moderate | Maximum (overall) |

## 2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Simplex

**Advantages:**
- Very simple circuit design — minimal hardware required.
- Maximum bandwidth available for the forward channel.
- No need for collision-detection or arbitration protocols.
- Inexpensive — ideal for mass-broadcast systems.

**Disadvantages:**
- No two-way interaction (no ACK possible).
- Error detection feedback is impossible; relies entirely on forward error correction (FEC) if used.
- Limited applicability — cannot be used for conversational or interactive systems.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Broadcasting Industry:** Radio and Television networks rely entirely on simplex.
- **Telemetry & IoT:** A sensor node (e.g., weather station) transmits readings to a central server with no return traffic.
- **Industrial Automation:** Conveyor-belt sensors report status to a control unit.
- **Aerospace & Defense:** Satellite-to-ground downlink of imagery (the uplink uses a separate channel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Logical Flow of a Simplex Transaction

Because the topic is conceptual rather than numerical, the "derivation" here is a rigorous **operational sequence** showing exactly how a simplex system processes information step by step.

**Step 1 — Source Data Generation:**
The originating device (e.g., a TV camera at the studio) generates raw analog or digital information $D(t)$ as a function of time $t$.

$$
D(t) \quad \text{where } t \in [0, T]
$$

**Step 2 — Encoding and Modulation at the Transmitter:**
The source data is encoded (e.g., MPEG-4 for video) and modulated onto a carrier wave of frequency $f_c$. The modulated signal $S_{tx}(t)$ can be written as:

$$
S_{tx}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2 \pi f_c t)
$$

where:
- $A_c$ = amplitude of the carrier
- $m(t)$ = message signal (containing the source data)
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency

**Step 3 — Propagation through the Channel:**
The signal travels through the physical medium (e.g., air for TV broadcast). The channel introduces attenuation and noise. The received signal $S_{rx}(t)$ is:

$$
S_{rx}(t) = S_{tx}(t) \cdot h(t) + n(t)
$$

where:
- $h(t)$ = channel impulse response (attenuation factor)
- $n(t)$ = additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN)

**Step 4 — Reception and Demodulation:**
The receiver (your TV antenna + tuner) demodulates $S_{rx}(t)$ to recover the message $m(t)$. Since the system is **simplex**, this is the **terminal step** of the chain — no data is ever transmitted back.

**Step 5 — Logical Conclusion (Why no ACK is sent):**
The physical path $P_{reverse}$ is either:
- Physically absent: $\exists \, P_{forward} \mid \nexists \, P_{reverse}$, or
- Logically disabled: $P_{reverse} = \emptyset$

$$
\therefore \quad \text{ACK signal} = \text{NULL}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose Simplex

| Engineering Requirement | Simplex Suitable? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Broadcast to millions of receivers | **Yes** | One sender, many receivers, no return traffic |
| Sensor reporting (IoT) | **Yes (uplink only)** | Sensor just pushes data; base station may reply via different channel |
| Conversational voice call | **No** | Requires bidirectional feedback |
| File download (one-way) | **Yes** | Server streams, client only receives |
| Web browsing | **No** | Requires request-response (HTTP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, if a question lists multiple scenarios, students often incorrectly mark web-browsing as simplex. **Remember: HTTP requires a request from the client, hence it is NOT simplex.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Simplex Data Flow Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrating the unidirectional nature of simplex transmission, including the explicit absence of a reverse channel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Source / Transmitter Tx] -->|Unidirectional Data Flow| medA[Transmission Channel Medium]
    medA -->|Forward Signal Only| dstA[Destination / Receiver Rx]
    dstA -.->|NO reverse path| srcA

    style srcA fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style medA fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style dstA fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison with Half-Duplex and Full-Duplex

This expanded schematic contrasts simplex with the other two data-flow modes so students can visualize the architectural difference.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph simplexBlock[Simplex Mode]
        sTx[Tx Only] ==> sCh[Single Channel] ==> sRx[Rx Only]
        sRx -.->|No Return Path| sTx
    end

    subgraph halfDuplexBlock[Half-Duplex Mode]
        hDev1[Device A Tx or Rx] <==> hCh[Shared Channel Time-Divided] <==> hDev2[Device B Rx or Tx]
    end

    subgraph fullDuplexBlock[Full-Duplex Mode]
        fDev1[Device A Tx and Rx Simultaneously] <--> fCh[Two-Way Channel] <--> fDev2[Device B Tx and Rx Simultaneously]
    end

    style sTx fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style sRx fill:#d62728,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style hDev1 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style hDev2 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style fDev1 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    style fDev2 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows (`==>` or `<==>`)** indicate the allowed direction(s) of data flow.
- **Dashed arrows (`-.->`)** explicitly represent **blocked or non-existent** paths — the defining feature of simplex.
- **Color coding** distinguishes the modes: **blue/red** (Simplex), **purple** (Half-Duplex), **brown** (Full-Duplex).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1: Define Simplex mode of communication. Give two examples. [3 Marks]**
*[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer:**

Simplex communication is a unidirectional mode of data transmission in which signals travel from the sender (transmitter) to the receiver in **one direction only**. The receiver cannot transmit any data, control signal, or acknowledgment back to the sender over the same channel.

The two defining features are:
1. Communication is **one-way** (monodirectional).
2. The **entire bandwidth** of the channel is used for forward transmission only.

**Examples:**
1. Television broadcasting (TV transmitter → TV receiver).
2. FM radio transmission (radio station → listener's radio set).

*(Additional valid examples: PA system, keyboard-to-CPU link, satellite telemetry downlink.)*

---

### **Question 2: Why is simplex mode unsuitable for interactive applications such as video conferencing? [3 Marks]**
*[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**Model Answer:**

Simplex mode is **unsuitable for video conferencing** because video conferencing is fundamentally a **bidirectional, real-time interactive application** that requires:

1. **Simultaneous two-way data flow:** Both participants must send and receive audio/video packets at the same time. Simplex permits only one-way transmission, breaking this requirement.
2. **Acknowledgment and feedback:** Protocols like RTP/RTCP require continuous feedback (jitter, packet loss, synchronization) from the receiver. Simplex has **no reverse channel**, so ACK/NACK signals cannot be exchanged.
3. **Error recovery:** Interactive sessions need retransmission of lost packets, which demands a return path.

Hence, **Full-Duplex** (or at minimum Half-Duplex) is mandatory for video conferencing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A: Data Flow Types in Communication [14 Marks]**
*[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**A (a)** With neat diagrams, explain the three modes of data flow in communication systems. Mention at least one real-world example for each. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The three modes of data flow define the direction in which signals travel between two communicating devices:

**1. Simplex Mode:**
- Communication is **strictly one-way**.
- The transmitter sends; the receiver only receives. No return path exists.
- *Diagram:* `Source (Tx) →→→ Medium →→→ Destination (Rx)` with a dashed (blocked) reverse arrow.
- *Example:* Television broadcasting, FM radio.

**2. Half-Duplex Mode:**
- Communication is **two-way, but only one direction at a time**.
- Both devices can transmit and receive, but they must take **turns** sharing the same channel.
- *Diagram:* `Device A ⇄ (Channel shared by time) ⇄ Device B`.
- *Example:* Walkie-Talkie, Citizen Band (CB) radio.

**3. Full-Duplex Mode:**
- Communication is **two-way and simultaneous**.
- Both devices can transmit and receive **at the same time**, equivalent to having two independent simplex channels (one in each direction).
- *Diagram:* `Device A ⇆ Channel ⇆ Device B` (dual arrows).
- *Example:* Telephone conversation, mobile (4G/5G) voice call.

---

**A (b)** Compare Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex modes based on directionality, bandwidth efficiency, hardware complexity, and two practical examples. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Directionality** | One-way only | Two-way, alternating | Two-way, simultaneous |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | 100% for forward path | Shared by time-division | 100% forward + 100% reverse |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest (single path) | Moderate (switching logic) | Highest (dual paths / echo cancellation) |
| **Example 1** | TV broadcast | Walkie-Talkie | Mobile phone call |
| **Example 2** | FM Radio | Police dispatcher radio | Landline telephone |
| **ACK Possible?** | No | Yes (sequentially) | Yes (parallel) |

**Conclusion:** The choice of mode depends on the application. Simplex is preferred for broadcasting (cost + simplicity), Half-Duplex for occasional two-way voice, and Full-Duplex for real-time interactive communication.

---

### **Question B: Simplex Communication — Deep Dive [14 Marks]**
*[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**B (a)** Explain the architecture and operation of a Simplex communication system with a block diagram. Why is the reverse channel absent in simplex? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of a Simplex System (Block Diagram):**

```
[ Source / Tx ]  →→→  [ Encoder & Modulator ]  →→→  [ Channel ]  →→→  [ Demodulator & Decoder ]  →→→  [ Destination / Rx ]
```

**Step-by-step Operation:**

1. **Source:** Generates the information signal (voice, video, sensor data).
2. **Encoder + Modulator:** Converts source data into a transmission-friendly form and modulates it onto a carrier wave of frequency $f_c$.
3. **Channel:** The physical medium (air, cable, fiber) propagates the modulated signal in the forward direction.
4. **Demodulator + Decoder:** Recovers the original information from the received signal.
5. **Destination:** Consumes/presents the data (e.g., TV screen, loudspeaker).

**Why is the reverse channel absent?**
- Simplex is **designed** for one-way information delivery (broadcasts, telemetry).
- Providing a reverse path would require additional hardware, doubling cost — defeating the purpose of a low-cost broadcast system.
- The application does not **need** feedback (e.g., a TV viewer does not need to send data back to the broadcaster).
- Therefore, the reverse path is **intentionally omitted** in both physical topology and protocol design.

---

**B (b)** Discuss the advantages and limitations of Simplex communication. State two engineering scenarios where simplex is the **only** correct choice and justify. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Simplicity:** Minimal hardware; no switching, arbitration, or duplexing logic needed.
2. **Cost-Effective:** Cheapest to deploy — ideal for mass-market consumer devices (radios, TVs).
3. **Maximum Bandwidth Utilization:** The full channel capacity is dedicated to forward traffic.
4. **No Collision Risk:** Since only one device ever transmits, CSMA/CD or token-passing is unnecessary.

**Limitations:**
1. **No Acknowledgment:** The receiver cannot confirm receipt; lost packets go undetected.
2. **No Error Feedback:** Cannot request retransmission; relies on forward error correction (FEC) if needed.
3. **Not Interactive:** Cannot be used for conversational, request-response, or transactional systems.
4. **No Flow Control:** The sender has no idea if the receiver is overwhelmed.

**Engineering Scenarios where Simplex is the ONLY Correct Choice:**

1. **FM/AM Radio and Television Broadcasting:**
   - *Justification:* A single tower transmits to millions of receivers. Providing a reverse channel from every TV/radio back to the station is physically and economically impossible. The application requires no return traffic.

2. **Satellite Telemetry / Remote Sensing (e.g., weather satellites):**
   - *Justification:* The satellite continuously downlinks scientific data (temperature, imagery) to a ground station. The satellite's sensors do not require instructions on the same channel; if control is needed, a **separate** uplink frequency is used (which is a separate simplex channel). Hence, the downlink itself is strictly simplex.

*(Additional valid scenarios: Public Address (PA) systems, IoT sensor push-only nodes, conveyor-belt status reporting.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "unidirectional" with "unidirectional over time."** Simplex is **permanently** one-way, not "one-way per session."
> 2. **Writing "simplex uses half the bandwidth."** Wrong — simplex uses **100% of the channel bandwidth** for the forward direction; it simply lacks a reverse path.
> 3. **Calling web-browsing or video calls simplex.** These are **Full-Duplex** because both client and server exchange data.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the diagram.** In 7-mark sub-questions, **a neat block diagram is mandatory**; skipping it can cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Walkie-Talkie (Half-Duplex) with Mobile Phone (Full-Duplex).** This is a classic trap question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simplex = One-Way Only.** Data flows from sender → receiver with **no possibility of return traffic**.
- The **reverse channel is physically or logically absent**, so ACK, NACK, and retransmission requests are impossible.
- **100% of the channel bandwidth** is dedicated to the forward direction (not "half").
- **Key examples to memorize:** Television broadcast, FM radio, PA system, keyboard-to-CPU, satellite telemetry downlink.
- **Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex** is a guaranteed KTU Module-1 question — memorize the comparison table (direction, examples, ACK, bandwidth).
- **Walkie-Talkie = Half-Duplex** (most common trap). **Telephone = Full-Duplex.**
- Simplex systems are **cheapest, simplest, and most bandwidth-efficient** for their specific use case, but **cannot support interactive or feedback-dependent applications**.
- In a 7-mark answer, **always include a labeled block diagram** — examiners reward it explicitly.
- The formula form for a modulated simplex signal is: $S_{tx}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- For KTU, if asked "which mode is used by X," first check if **any return data** is involved. If yes → **not** simplex.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half-Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Half-Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Half-Duplex Transmission Mode** is a data communication configuration in which **both** communicating stations (DTEs — Data Terminal Equipment) are *capable* of transmitting and receiving signals, but they **cannot do so simultaneously** over the same physical channel. At any given instant, one station acts as the **transmitter** while the other acts as the **receiver**; the roles are then *reversed* when required.

Formally, half-duplex is denoted as a **two-way alternate (TWA)** communication mode by standards bodies such as the **ITU-T** and **IEEE**. The Physical Layer guarantees that the medium is *logically shared* in time, even though the physical medium may be the same wire, fiber, or radio frequency band.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag — PECCT751 / Module 1:** This topic falls under the *Data Transmission / Transmission Modes* section. Students are expected to (i) classify transmission modes, (ii) state where half-duplex is deployed, and (iii) justify it relative to simplex and full-duplex modes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **narrow, one-lane wooden bridge** connecting two villages:
- Cars can travel in **both directions** across the bridge.
- However, only **one car at a time** can cross.
- A **traffic warden** at each end ensures that a car from village A enters only when no car from village B is on the bridge.
- If both cars arrive at the same time, one must **wait** (back-off), and the other crosses first.

That bridge is the **half-duplex channel**.

### 🎤 Another Daily-Life Analogy — The Walkie-Talkie
- A walkie-talkie has a **"Press-to-Talk" (PTT)** button.
- You *push* to speak, *release* to listen.
- You **cannot** speak and listen on the same radio frequency *at the same instant*.
- This is the quintessential real-world example of half-duplex.

| Real-World Example | Mode |
|---|---|
| FM Radio broadcast | **Simplex** (one-way only) |
| Walkie-Talkie / CB Radio | **Half-Duplex** |
| Mobile Phone Call (voice) | **Full-Duplex** |
| Telephone line (modern PSTN) | **Full-Duplex** |

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Direction Capability:** **2 (Bidirectional)**
> - **Simultaneity Allowed:** **No (Alternate only)**
> - **Channel Count (logical):** **1 shared logical channel**, time-shared between directions
> - **Reference ITU-T Notation:** **Two-Way Alternate (TWA)**
> - **Typical Reference Frequency Bands (CB / Walkie-Talkie):** **27 MHz (CB)**, **462–467 MHz (FRS/GMRS)**
> - **Typical Line Bit-Rate Penalty vs Full-Duplex:** Up to **~50%** throughput reduction when traffic is symmetric.

---

## 1.4 Why Half-Duplex Exists — Engineering Motivation

Half-duplex is a **deliberate engineering trade-off**. It exists because:
1. **Cost Reduction:** A single physical wire/antenna/fiber core can serve both directions (no need for separate TX/RX paths).
2. **Spectrum Conservation:** In radio, half-duplex uses **one frequency** (or one paired channel with fast switching) instead of two.
3. **Power Efficiency:** Mobile/handheld devices save power by not maintaining simultaneous TX and RX oscillators.
4. **Legacy Compatibility:** Older Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2) and certain industrial bus systems were natively half-duplex.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

A half-duplex communication session unfolds in five well-defined phases:

1. **Channel Acquisition (Sense):**
   The station that *wants* to transmit first checks whether the shared medium is idle. In wired Ethernet (legacy), this is the **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** procedure.
2. **Direction Lock:**
   Once the medium is idle, the transmitting station "locks" the channel in its direction. A control signal — *Request to Send (RTS)* in RS-232, or the **PTT line** in radio — asserts transmitter control.
3. **Data Transmission:**
   Frames/packets flow from sender → receiver in one direction only. The receiver does **not** transmit simultaneously (no echo cancellation needed in classic half-duplex).
4. **Direction Turn-Around (Channel Reversal):**
   When the first transmission completes, a *guard time* (typically **10–50 ms** in radio, **microseconds** in wired Ethernet) is inserted before the reverse direction is enabled. This prevents overlap and echoes.
5. **Reverse Transmission & Channel Release:**
   The previously-receiving station now becomes the transmitter. After it finishes, the channel is released to idle.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| Carrier Sense | Avoid collisions when both stations want to send simultaneously |
| Direction Lock | Prevent mid-frame role reversal (which would corrupt the frame) |
| Guard Time | Allow analog circuitry (AGC, PLL, squelch) to settle after direction change |
| Channel Release | Free the medium so the other station can compete fairly for access |

## 2.3 Direction Control Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield)

| Mechanism | Where Used | Control Signal / Method |
|---|---|---|
| **RTS / CTS** (Request/Clear to Send) | RS-232, RS-485, RS-422 half-duplex | Hardware handshake lines |
| **PTT** (Press-to-Talk) | Walkie-Talkies, CB, Ham radio | Pushbutton grounds the TX enable line |
| **CSMA/CD** | Legacy 10BASE5 / 10BASE2 Ethernet | Carrier Sense + Collision Detect |
| **TDMA slot allocation** | Half-duplex radio links (e.g., TETRA) | Time-slot arbitration by base station |
| **Token passing** | ARCNET, Token Ring (logical) | Possession of token = TX right |
| **HDLC / XMODEM protocol** | Synchronous serial links | Alternating poll/select frames |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** When asked *"How does half-duplex differ from simplex?"*, examiners want you to mention **bi-directional capability** *plus* **non-simultaneous constraint**, not just "two-way."

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield formulas** for half-duplex throughput and channel efficiency. Memorize them — they appear in 3-mark and 14-mark derivations.

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Legend |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{HD} = \dfrac{T_{f}}{T_{f} + T_{ta}}$ | **Channel Efficiency** of half-duplex link. $T_f$ = frame transmission time, $T_{ta}$ = turnaround time |
| 2 | $T_f = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Frame transmission time. $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = data rate (bps) |
| 3 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{L}{T_{f} + T_{ta}}$ | Effective useful data rate delivered per cycle |
| 4 | $U_{HD} = \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + T_{ta}} = \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{ta}}$ | Channel Utilization (0 to 1) for alternating traffic |
| 5 | $U_{max} = \lim_{L \to \infty} U_{HD} = 1$ | As frame length grows, turnaround penalty vanishes (theoretical) |
| 6 | $\rho_{HD} = \dfrac{T_{ta}}{T_{f}}$ | **Turnaround Ratio** — used to decide if half-duplex is acceptable |
| 7 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_{prop}}$ for CSMA/CD | Maximum frames/sec in legacy half-duplex Ethernet to detect collisions |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing $\eta_{HD}$ (efficiency of a *single* alternating exchange) with full-link utilization. Always state assumptions: *symmetric traffic*, *single shared channel*, *no retransmissions*.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Half-duplex is not an obsolete concept — it survives in modern systems because:
- **Industrial IoT & Fieldbus (RS-485):** Half-duplex RS-485 uses a *single twisted pair* for thousands of sensors in factories — full-duplex would double cabling cost.
- **Aeronautical Communications:** ACARS and VHF Data Link Mode 2 operate half-duplex to maximize spectrum reuse.
- **Satellite & HF Radio:** Long propagation delays (HF: 5–30 ms round-trip) make full-duplex impractical — half-duplex with PTT remains standard.
- **Walkie-Talkies (PMR446, FRS):** Regulators in EU/US mandate half-duplex on license-free bands to maximize user count.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Channel Utilization of a Half-Duplex Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 for the 14-mark questions. Master it.

### Setup
Consider two stations, **A** and **B**, communicating over a half-duplex link of bit-rate $R$ bits/sec.
- Station A sends one frame of $L$ bits to B.
- Station B immediately sends one frame of $L$ bits back to A (a "ping-pong" cycle).
- Let $T_{ta}$ be the fixed **turn-around time** required to reverse the channel direction.

### Step 1 — Compute the transmission time of one frame

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \dfrac{\text{Bits in frame}}{\text{Bit rate}} \\
    &= \dfrac{L}{R} \quad \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** A frame of $L$ bits sent at rate $R$ bps requires $L/R$ seconds to leave the transmitter fully.

### Step 2 — Compute total cycle time for one A→B and one B→A exchange

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= \underbrace{T_f}_{\text{A} \to \text{B}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{ta}}_{\text{reverse 1}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_f}_{\text{B} \to \text{A}} \;+\; \underbrace{T_{ta}}_{\text{reverse 2}} \\
         &= 2\,T_f \;+\; 2\,T_{ta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Two frames are transmitted (one each way), and the channel reverses direction twice — once after A→B, once after B→A. Each reversal costs $T_{ta}$ seconds of dead time.

### Step 3 — Useful data delivered per cycle

Total useful data per cycle $= 2L$ bits (the payload in both directions, ignoring overhead).

### Step 4 — Effective Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{eff} &= \dfrac{\text{Useful bits per cycle}}{\text{Cycle time}} \\
        &= \dfrac{2L}{2 T_f + 2 T_{ta}} \\
        &= \dfrac{L}{T_f + T_{ta}} \\
        &= \dfrac{L}{L/R + T_{ta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Channel Utilization (Efficiency)

Define $U$ as the fraction of time the channel is *actually* carrying useful user bits (ignoring $T_{ta}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{HD} &= \dfrac{2 T_f}{2 T_f + 2 T_{ta}} \\
       &= \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + T_{ta}} \\
       &= \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + T_{ta}} \\
       &= \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{ta}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $U_{HD}$ lies in the closed interval $[0,1]$.
- As $L \to \infty$: $U_{HD} \to 1$ (long frames amortize the turnaround cost).
- As $T_{ta} \to 0$: $U_{HD} \to 1$ (instant direction switching — the ideal limit).
- As $L \to 0$ or $R \cdot T_{ta} \to \infty$: $U_{HD} \to 0$ (turnaround overhead dominates).

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Practice numeric (often asked in 3-mark questions):**

Given $R = 9600$ bps, $L = 1200$ bits, $T_{ta} = 0.1$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \dfrac{1200}{9600} = 0.125 \text{ s} \\
U_{HD} &= \dfrac{0.125}{0.125 + 0.1} = \dfrac{0.125}{0.225} \approx 0.556 \\
S_{eff} &= \dfrac{2 \times 1200}{2 \times 0.125 + 2 \times 0.1} = \dfrac{2400}{0.45} \approx 5333 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** Effective throughput (5333 bps) is less than the raw line rate (9600 bps) because of turnaround loss — exactly as expected for half-duplex.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Turn-Around Ratio Decision Rule

Engineers often need a quick criterion: *"Is this link efficient enough as half-duplex, or do we need full-duplex?"*

Define the **turnaround ratio** $\rho$:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{T_{ta}}{T_f}
$$

- If $\rho \ll 1$ (e.g., $\rho < 0.05$): half-duplex is *efficient*; minimal penalty.
- If $\rho \gg 1$: half-duplex is *wasteful*; full-duplex or longer frames are recommended.

Rewrite utilization in terms of $\rho$:

$$
U_{HD} = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + T_{ta}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \rho}
$$

**Engineering insight:** To reach $U_{HD} \ge 0.9$, we need $\rho \le \tfrac{1}{9} \approx 0.111$. So $T_f$ must be at least **9 times** larger than $T_{ta}$ — a useful design rule.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Half-Duplex vs Simplex vs Full-Duplex)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks this as a 14-mark comparison question.** Memorize the table.

| Parameter | **Simplex** | **Half-Duplex** | **Full-Duplex** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction of data flow | One direction only | Both, but **not simultaneously** | Both, **simultaneously** |
| ITU-T designation | One-Way (OW) | Two-Way Alternate (TWA) | Two-Way Simultaneous (TWS) |
| Logical channels needed | 1 (one-way) | 1 (time-shared) | 2 (or echo-cancelled 1) |
| Cable / spectrum cost | **Lowest** | **Medium** | **Highest** |
| Typical examples | TV broadcast, pager | Walkie-talkie, RS-485 bus, legacy Ethernet, CB radio | Mobile phone, modern Ethernet (100BASE-TX full), fiber backbone |
| Turnaround overhead | None | **Yes** ($T_{ta}$) | None |
| Collisions possible? | No | **Yes** (if both transmit simultaneously — mitigated by CSMA/CD or PTT) | No |
| Echo canceller needed? | No | No | **Yes** (if 2-wire full-duplex, e.g., 1000BASE-T) |
| Throughput efficiency | $\le 100\%$ (one way only) | $\dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{ta}}$ (per direction) | $\approx 100\%$ (with echo cancellation) |
| Hardware complexity | **Lowest** | Medium (direction control logic) | **Highest** |
| Power consumption | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

## 3.4 Channel Allocation Strategies in Half-Duplex

When **N** stations share a half-duplex channel, an arbitration mechanism is required. KTU commonly tests three:

1. **Polling:** A central controller asks each station *"Do you have data?"* in turn. Used in legacy IBM SNA networks.
2. **CSMA/CD:** Stations listen to the carrier; if idle, transmit; if collision detected, back off and retry. Used in classic Ethernet.
3. **Token Passing:** Only the station holding the token may transmit. Used in ARCNET, Token Ring, and some industrial buses.

$$
\text{CSMA/CD efficiency:} \quad U_{CSMA/CD} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.4 \cdot a}
\quad \text{where} \quad a = \dfrac{T_{prop}}{T_{trans}}
$$

This formula shows that **as propagation delay grows** (longer cables, satellite hops), efficiency drops — another reason half-duplex is preferred on long-latency media.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Half-Duplex Communication Flow (Functional Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the **state transitions** of a half-duplex channel during a typical A↔B exchange, including the **direction-reversal guard time** $T_{ta}$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IdleState
    IdleState: Channel Idle (both A and B silent)
    AtoB: A transmits to B
    Reverse1: Turnaround 1 (guard time Tta)
    BtoA: B transmits to A
    Reverse2: Turnaround 2 (guard time Tta)
    Collision: Simultaneous TX detected (CSMA/CD back-off)

    IdleState --> AtoB : Station A asserts PTT / RTS
    AtoB --> Reverse1 : Frame complete (Tta begins)
    Reverse1 --> BtoA : Channel re-armed for B
    BtoA --> Reverse2 : B frame complete
    Reverse2 --> IdleState : Channel released
    IdleState --> Collision : Both A and B transmit concurrently
    Collision --> IdleState : Back-off timer expires (retry)
```

---

## 4.2 Half-Duplex System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StationA[Station A]
        DataA[User Data]
        TXA[Transmitter Modulator]
        RXA[Receiver Demodulator]
        SwA[Direction Switch / PTT Control]
    end
    subgraph SharedMedium[Shared Half-Duplex Medium]
        CH[(Single Channel: Wire or RF Link)]
    end
    subgraph StationB[Station B]
        SwB[Direction Switch / PTT Control]
        TXB[Transmitter Modulator]
        RXB[Receiver Demodulator]
        DataB[User Data]
    end

    DataA --> TXA --> SwA
    SwA -- TX enabled A to B --> CH
    CH -- RX path B from A --> SwB
    SwB --> RXB --> DataB

    DataB --> TXB --> SwB
    SwB -- TX enabled B to A --> CH
    CH -- RX path A from B --> SwA
    SwA --> RXA --> DataA

    classDef tx fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#dff5e1,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#e8e8ff,stroke:#34495e,color:#000
    class TXA,TXB tx
    class RXA,RXB rx
    class SwA,SwB ctrl
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU-style narration):**
- The **single shared channel** (CH) in the middle physically carries signals in only *one* direction at any instant.
- The **Direction Switch / PTT Control** blocks (SwA, SwB) ensure that the transmitter and receiver of a station are never both connected to the channel at the same time.
- When SwA is set to TX (red path), SwA simultaneously routes the incoming channel to RXA — but SwB does the opposite (TX off, RX on).
- After the frame completes, both switches flip (this flip takes $T_{ta}$ seconds, the guard time).

---

## 4.3 Timing Diagram — Direction Reversal on the Wire

```
Time → ───────────────────────────────────────────────────►

       A TX              ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓     idle
                        │ A's frame  │ ↑Tta│ idle          ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓
       Channel ON-DIR:  ◄─────────► │◄──►│◄──────────────►│ B's frame     │
                                       │                                 │
Direction (A→B / B→A):  ─── A→B ─────►│◄── B→A ──────────────────────────►
                                       │ ↑
                                     guard time Tta
                                       │
                              both directions idle
```

**Reading guide:**
- From $t = 0$ to $t = T_f$: A transmits, B receives (channel direction = A→B).
- From $t = T_f$ to $t = T_f + T_{ta}$: **dead time** — no station transmits; analog circuitry settles.
- From $t = T_f + T_{ta}$ to $t = 2 T_f + T_{ta}$: B transmits, A receives (channel direction = B→A).
- The shaded idle band is the **turnaround penalty** unique to half-duplex.

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define half-duplex transmission. State any two examples.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Model Answer (board-key style):**
> *Half-duplex transmission is a mode of data communication in which both stations can transmit and receive, but not simultaneously. At any given time, the channel carries data in only one direction; the direction is alternated as required. This is also called **two-way alternate (TWA)** communication.*
>
> **Examples:** (i) Walkie-talkie / Citizens Band (CB) radio, (ii) Legacy 10BASE2 Ethernet using CSMA/CD, (iii) Half-duplex RS-485 industrial bus.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write *"half-duplex is one-way"* — that is the definition of **simplex**, not half-duplex. You lose **all 3 marks** if this confusion is shown. Always clarify the *non-simultaneous* bidirectional capability.

### Q2. List any four differences between half-duplex and full-duplex transmission.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
> | S.No | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | Both directions possible, but **not at the same time** | Both directions **simultaneously** |
> | 2 | Uses **one** physical channel (time-shared) | Uses **two** physical or logically-separated channels |
> | 3 | No echo-canceller required | **Echo canceller required** for 2-wire full-duplex |
> | 4 | Example: walkie-talkie | Example: mobile phone, modern switched Ethernet |
