## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme):** Answer **one** full question (14 marks), which contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
Explain the concept of half-duplex transmission with a neat block diagram. Compare it with simplex and full-duplex modes (any 5 points).

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
A half-duplex link has a data rate of $R = 64$ kbps and a turnaround time of $T_{ta} = 50$ ms. If the frame size is $L = 128$ bytes (1024 bits), compute:
(i) Frame transmission time $T_f$
(ii) Channel utilization $U_{HD}$
(iii) Effective throughput $S_{eff}$
(iv) Comment on whether this half-duplex link is efficient.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**
*(Definition already given in Section 1 — repeat concisely here.)*

Half-duplex transmission allows **bidirectional** communication between two devices, but the channel can carry data in **only one direction at a time**. A direction-control mechanism (PTT, RTS/CTS, CSMA/CD, or token) determines which station is currently the transmitter. The channel is logically *time-shared* between the two directions, with a guard time $T_{ta}$ inserted during reversal to prevent collisions and allow circuit settling.

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 for the system architecture.)*

**Comparison Table (5 points):**

| # | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-way only | Two-way, **not simultaneous** | Two-way, **simultaneous** |
| 2 | 1 channel | 1 time-shared channel | 2 separate channels (or 1 with echo-cancellation) |
| 3 | Lowest cost | Medium cost | Highest cost |
| 4 | Example: TV broadcast | Walkie-talkie | Mobile phone |
| 5 | No turnaround | Turnaround delay $T_{ta}$ | No turnaround |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R = 64{,}000$ bps, $T_{ta} = 0.05$ s, $L = 128 \times 8 = 1024$ bits.

**(i) Frame transmission time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1024}{64000} = 0.016 \text{ s} = 16 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Channel utilization:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{HD} &= \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + T_{ta}} = \dfrac{0.016}{0.016 + 0.05} = \dfrac{0.016}{0.066} \\
       &\approx 0.2424 \;\;(24.24\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Effective throughput for one full A↔B cycle:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{eff} &= \dfrac{2L}{2 T_f + 2 T_{ta}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1024}{2 \times 0.016 + 2 \times 0.05} \\
        &= \dfrac{2048}{0.132} \\
        &\approx 15{,}515 \text{ bps} \;\approx\; 15.15 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Comment:**
The utilization is only **~24%**, meaning the channel is idle for **~76% of the time** due to turnaround overhead. The turnaround ratio is $\rho = T_{ta}/T_f = 0.05 / 0.016 \approx 3.125$, which is *much greater* than 1. Therefore, **this half-duplex configuration is inefficient** — the frame size should be increased, $T_{ta}$ reduced, or full-duplex used.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
With the help of a timing diagram, explain the direction-reversal mechanism in half-duplex communication. Define *turnaround time* and *guard time*.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
A half-duplex radio channel operating at 9.6 kbps has a turnaround time of 250 ms. Determine:
(i) The minimum frame size required to achieve channel utilization $U_{HD} \ge 0.8$.
(ii) The effective throughput at that frame size.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Half-duplex communication involves two stations (A and B) sharing a single channel. When A has finished transmitting, the channel cannot be immediately reused by B — a small fixed interval called the **guard time** or **turnaround time** ($T_{ta}$) must elapse before B can begin transmitting.

The turnaround time is required because:
- The receiver's AGC (Automatic Gain Control) and PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) must relock on the new incoming signal.
- The transmitter's carrier oscillator must stabilize at the new frequency (in FDD radios).
- Propagation echoes on the medium must dissipate.

*(Refer to the timing diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R = 9600$ bps, $T_{ta} = 0.25$ s. We require $U_{HD} \ge 0.8$.

**(i) Minimum frame size $L_{min}$:**

Using the channel utilization formula:
$$
U_{HD} = \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{ta}} \ge 0.8
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{L}{L + R T_{ta}} &\ge 0.8 \\
L &\ge 0.8\,(L + R T_{ta}) \\
L &\ge 0.8 L + 0.8 R T_{ta} \\
0.2 L &\ge 0.8 R T_{ta} \\
L &\ge 4 R T_{ta} \\
L &\ge 4 \times 9600 \times 0.25 \\
L &\ge 9600 \text{ bits} = 1200 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum frame size = 9600 bits (≈ 1200 bytes).**

**(ii) Effective throughput at $L = 9600$ bits:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{9600}{9600} = 1 \text{ s} \\
U_{HD} &= \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + T_{ta}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.25} = 0.8 \\
S_{eff} &= U_{HD} \times R = 0.8 \times 9600 = 7680 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective throughput = 7680 bps.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Half-Duplex Numericals:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $T_{ta}$ to seconds** when it is given in ms (e.g., 250 ms → 0.25 s). Loses ~2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up units of $L$:** Some questions give frame size in *bytes* — convert to bits by multiplying by 8 before using $L/R$.
> 3. **Using raw $R$ as throughput:** Effective throughput is *always less* than $R$ for half-duplex unless $T_{ta} = 0$. Writing $S = 9600$ bps when $T_{ta} > 0$ loses the full 7 marks in part (b).
> 4. **Confusing "channel utilization" with "channel capacity":** $U_{HD}$ is a *fraction* (0 to 1), not a bit-rate.
> 5. **Not stating direction-control mechanism:** In descriptive answers, always mention *how* the channel reversal is achieved (PTT, RTS/CTS, CSMA/CD, token). Examiners explicitly award marks for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Half-Duplex (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Bidirectional but **non-simultaneous** communication; channel direction alternates in time.
- ⭐ **ITU-T Name:** **Two-Way Alternate (TWA)**.
- ⭐ **Canonical Examples:** Walkie-talkie, CB radio, half-duplex RS-485 bus, legacy 10BASE2/10BASE5 Ethernet.
- ⭐ **Key Formula (most-tested):**
$$U_{HD} = \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + T_{ta}} = \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{ta}}$$
- ⭐ **Turnaround Ratio:** $\rho = T_{ta}/T_f$. For $U_{HD} \ge 0.9$, need $\rho \le 1/9$.
- ⭐ **Direction Control Methods:** PTT, RTS/CTS, CSMA/CD, Token Passing, TDMA slot allocation, HDLC polling.
- ⭐ **Turn-Around Time $T_{ta}$:** Time required to reverse channel direction — needed for AGC relock, PLL re-acquisition, and echo decay.
- ⭐ **Advantages:** Cost-effective (single channel), spectrum-efficient, power-efficient, simple hardware.
- ⭐ **Disadvantages:** Throughput penalty, collisions possible if control fails, not suitable for symmetric real-time traffic (e.g., voice).
- ⭐ **Comparison Anchors:** Simplex = one-way only; Full-Duplex = simultaneous two-way with echo cancellation.
- ⭐ **Modern Relevance:** Still dominant in **RS-485 industrial buses**, **VHF aeronautical links**, **HF ham radio**, and **license-free PMR446 walkie-talkies**.
- ⭐ **Golden Rule:** As $L \to \infty$, $U_{HD} \to 1$; as $T_{ta} \to \infty$ (or $L \to 0$), $U_{HD} \to 0$.
- ⭐ **Examiner Triggers:** Always include (i) a labeled diagram, (ii) the efficiency formula, (iii) at least one real-world example, (iv) explicit comparison with simplex and full-duplex.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full-Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full-Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Full-Duplex (FDX / Full-Duplex Transmission)** is a bidirectional communication mode in which **both** communicating stations can transmit and receive data **simultaneously** over the same link or channel. The physical or logical channel must therefore support two independent data paths (or one path with sufficient capacity to be time-shared) such that a signal sent by Station A reaches Station B at the **exact same instant** that a signal from Station B reaches Station A.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to **Course Outcome CO1**: *Understand the fundamentals of data communication and signal transmission*), Full-Duplex is contrasted against:

- **Simplex (SX):** One-way only (e.g., TV broadcast, keyboard → CPU).
- **Half-Duplex (HDX):** Two-way, but **not** simultaneous (e.g., walkie-talkie; one must "press to talk").

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Full-Duplex is grouped under "Transmission Modes" in Module 1 of PECCT751. Examiners commonly pair it with Simplex and Half-Duplex to test the candidate's ability to *classify* and *justify* the choice of mode for a given application scenario.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two friends talking on a **mobile phone**:
- They can both speak **at the same time** and still understand each other.
- The voice from Friend A travels to Friend B, while the voice from Friend B travels to Friend A, in the **same instant**.

This is **Full-Duplex**. 

Now compare:
- A **walkie-talkie** (push-to-talk): only one person speaks at a time → **Half-Duplex**.
- A **television broadcast** from a TV tower to your home: the tower transmits, your TV only receives → **Simplex**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **"S.H.F."** — **S**implex (Single direction), **H**alf (Half at a time), **F**ull (Full simultaneous).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Channel Capacity (C):** Measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Bandwidth (B):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Propagation Delay (t_p):** Time taken for a signal to traverse the medium.
- **Transmission Delay (t_t):** Time to push all bits of a packet onto the link.
- **Effective Throughput in Full-Duplex:** **2 × C** (when two independent physical channels exist).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Full-Duplex Works

For Full-Duplex operation, the system must resolve one fundamental problem: **How do we keep the "outgoing" signal from drowning out the "incoming" signal at each end?**

The standard engineering solutions are:

1. **Two Separate Physical Channels (4-Wire / Twin Cable):**
   - Channel-1 carries A → B
   - Channel-2 carries B → A
   - This is the method used in **traditional landline telephony** and **Full-Duplex Ethernet over fibre**.

2. **Frequency Division (Frequency-Division Duplex — FDD):**
   - The available bandwidth B is split into two non-overlapping sub-bands.
   - **Lower sub-band** = uplink (e.g., 890–915 MHz in GSM uplink).
   - **Upper sub-band** = downlink (e.g., 935–960 MHz in GSM downlink).
   - A **duplexer** (band-pass filter pair) at each radio isolates Tx from Rx.

3. **Time Division (Time-Division Duplex — TDD):**
   - The same frequency is used, but the time axis is split into alternating Tx and Rx slots.
   - Requires very tight **synchronization** and **guard periods** to avoid collision.
   - Used in **5G NR TDD mode, Wi-Fi (with CSMA/CA + ACK)** and **half-duplex radios emulating FDX**.

4. **Echo Cancellation (EC):**
   - The station transmits and receives on the **same** physical wire.
   - An **echo canceller** digitally subtracts the locally transmitted signal from the received mixture.
   - Used in **G.168 echo cancellers, modern 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet** (uses hybrid circuits + DSP echo cancellation over a single twisted pair).

### 2.2 Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneous? | Channel Requirement | Throughput (vs. raw B) | Classic Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Simplex | One-way | N/A | 1 channel | $C$ | TV broadcast, Keyboard |
| Half-Duplex | Two-way | No | 1 shared channel | $\leq C$ | Walkie-talkie, legacy Hub |
| **Full-Duplex** | **Two-way** | **Yes** | **2 channels (or 1 with FDD/TDD/EC)** | $\mathbf{\leq 2C}$ | **Telephone, Switched Ethernet, 5G** |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas govern the *capacity* and *timing* of a Full-Duplex link. These are tested both directly (numerical) and indirectly (justification type) in KTU examinations.

| # | Formula | Name | Variables & Units | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | **Nyquist Capacity** (noiseless) | $C$ in bps, $B$ in Hz, $M$ = signal levels | Max bit rate per **single** direction |
| 2 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** (noisy) | $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (dimensionless) | Theoretical upper bound per direction |
| 3 | $C_{\text{FDX}} = C_{\text{A→B}} + C_{\text{B→A}}$ | **Full-Duplex Aggregate** | bps | Total link throughput |
| 4 | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{v}$ | **Propagation Delay** | seconds, $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in cable | One-way trip time |
| 5 | $t_{t} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | **Transmission Delay** | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = rate (bps) | Time to push packet onto wire |
| 6 | $\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{t_{t}}{t_{t} + 2 t_{p}}$ | **Stop-and-Wait Efficiency** | dimensionless | (For context: explains why FDX shines) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the Shannon formula above, the absolute-value / fraction is enclosed inside `\left( \right)` and uses `\dfrac` only inside math display. When reproducing this in a markdown table, **do not** use the pipe symbol `\vert x \vert`; always use `\mid` or the macro `\lvert \rvert` to avoid breaking the table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** The entire **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** and **4G/5G** cellular systems are Full-Duplex. It enables a natural conversational experience.
- **Data Centres:** **Full-Duplex Switched Ethernet (10/100/1000 BASE-T)** replaced half-duplex shared-medium Ethernet; aggregate switch fabric throughput equals the sum of all port rates running concurrently.
- **Satellite Comms:** Modern **VSAT** and **LEO satellite** constellations (e.g., Starlink) use FDX or near-FDX with advanced echo cancellation to maximise spectral efficiency.
- **Industrial Control:** **Full-duplex RS-485** with 4-wire configuration is used in factory automation for deterministic bidirectional PLC chatter.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks a *numerical* question comparing Simplex / Half-Duplex / Full-Duplex capacity, or a *derivation* of Full-Duplex aggregate throughput. Below are the **exhaustive, non-truncated** analytical procedures.

### 3.1 Derivation: Aggregate Full-Duplex Throughput

We begin with the **Nyquist theorem** for a noiseless baseband channel of bandwidth $B$ Hertz using $M$ discrete signal levels. The maximum data rate in **one direction** is:

$$
C_{\text{one-way}} = 2 B \log_{2}(M) \;\; \text{bits/second}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the two independent directions.**
In a Full-Duplex link, direction A → B and direction B → A are logically (or physically) independent. If both directions use the **same** bandwidth $B$ and the **same** signal level $M$, then:

$$
C_{\text{A→B}} = 2 B \log_{2}(M)
$$

$$
C_{\text{B→A}} = 2 B \log_{2}(M)
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the two independent capacities.**
Because the two directions transmit simultaneously, the **aggregate** capacity of the Full-Duplex link is the sum:

$$
C_{\text{FDX}} = C_{\text{A→B}} + C_{\text{B→A}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the identical expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 B \log_{2}(M) + 2 B \log_{2}(M) \\
&= 2 \times 2 B \log_{2}(M) \\
&= 4 B \log_{2}(M)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — State the engineering conclusion.**
A Full-Duplex link over bandwidth $B$ with $M$ signal levels can carry up to **$4B \log_{2}(M)$ bps** in aggregate — exactly **double** a Half-Duplex link of the same physical parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "double"?** Half-Duplex must time-share the channel, so its one-way peak is $2B \log_{2}(M)$ but the *useful* aggregate over a long period is still only $2B \log_{2}(M)$ (because while A is sending, B is idle). Full-Duplex allows both to *use* the channel at the same instant.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]:**
A Full-Duplex link uses a bandwidth of **$B = 4$ kHz** and **$M = 16$** signal levels. Compute:
- (i) the maximum data rate in **one direction**,
- (ii) the **aggregate** Full-Duplex throughput,
- (iii) the time required to transmit a **$1$ MB** file in Full-Duplex.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — One-way rate (Nyquist):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{one-way}} &= 2 B \log_{2}(M) \\
&= 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(16) \\
&= 2 \times 4000 \times 4 \\
&= 32\,000 \;\text{bps} \;=\; \mathbf{32 \; kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Aggregate Full-Duplex throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{FDX}} &= 2 \times C_{\text{one-way}} \\
&= 2 \times 32\,000 \\
&= \mathbf{64\;kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Time to send a 1 MB file (Full-Duplex, one direction at 32 kbps):**

Convert $1$ MB to bits: $1\;\text{MB} = 8 \times 10^{6}$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{\text{Size}}{\text{Rate}} \\
&= \dfrac{8 \times 10^{6}\;\text{bits}}{32 \times 10^{3}\;\text{bps}} \\
&= 250\;\text{seconds} \;=\; \mathbf{4\;\text{minutes}\;10\;\text{seconds}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** Notice the file is sent in *one direction*. In Full-Duplex, the reverse channel is *simultaneously free* to carry an ACK — this is precisely why Full-Duplex TCP flows outperform Half-Duplex flows even when application data is one-way.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "When to choose Full-Duplex?"

| Application Scenario | Recommended Mode | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Two-way voice conversation | **Full-Duplex** | Naturalness requires simultaneous talk. |
| File download from a server | Simplex (or Asymmetric FDX) | Server sends, client rarely uploads. |
| Walkie-Talkie (Push-to-Talk) | Half-Duplex | Hardware simplicity, single frequency. |
| Keyboard-to-CPU | Simplex | Data flow is one-way. |
| Modern 5G smartphone | **Full-Duplex (or near-FDX)** | Bidirectional voice + data. |
| Old coaxial shared Ethernet (10BASE-5) | Half-Duplex | CSMA/CD collision detection needs shared medium. |
| Switched 1000BASE-T link | **Full-Duplex** | Each direction uses its own pair + echo cancellation. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Full-Duplex Channel Architecture (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[STATION A TX] -->|Signal A to B| link1[CHANNEL 1 FDD UPLINK]
    link1 --> nodeBRx[STATION B RX]
    nodeBTx[STATION B TX] -->|Signal B to A| link2[CHANNEL 2 FDD DOWNLINK]
    link2 --> nodeARx[STATION A RX]
    nodeA -.->|Echo Canceller DSP| nodeARx
    nodeB -.->|Echo Canceller DSP| nodeBRx
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed lines depict the **digital echo-cancellation path** — the locally generated Tx signal is subtracted from the Rx mixture to recover the far-end signal cleanly on a *single* physical medium. This is the technique that allows **1000BASE-T** to achieve Full-Duplex on a single Cat-5e cable.

### 4.2 Full-Duplex Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STATION_A[STATION A END]
        srcA[Source Data A] --> modA[Modulator]
        modA --> dupA[Duplexer Filter]
    end
    subgraph MEDIUM[TRANSMISSION MEDIUM]
        dupA -->|f1 Carrier| channelAB[Channel A to B]
        channelAB --> dupB2[Duplexer Filter]
        dupB1[Duplexer Filter] -->|f2 Carrier| channelBA[Channel B to A]
        channelBA --> dupA2[Duplexer Filter]
    end
    subgraph STATION_B[STATION B END]
        dupB2 --> demodB[Demodulator]
        demodB --> sinkB[Destination Data B]
        srcB[Source Data B] --> modB[Modulator]
        modB --> dupB1
        dupA2 --> demodA[Demodulator]
        demodA --> sinkA[Destination Data A]
    end
```

### 4.3 Comparison Timeline — Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex

```mermaid
gantt
    title Transmission Mode Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Simplex
    A to B Only   :done, s1, 0, 10
    section Half-Duplex
    A to B Phase  :done, h1, 0, 5
    B to A Phase  :active, h2, 5, 10
    section Full-Duplex
    A to B Stream :done, f1, 0, 10
    B to A Stream :done, f2, 0, 10
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Gantt chart:** In the **Simplex** row, only one stream exists. In **Half-Duplex**, two streams alternate (notice the gap). In **Full-Duplex**, **both streams run in parallel for the full duration** — this is the visual essence of simultaneous bidirectional communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Full-Duplex transmission mode. How does it differ from Half-Duplex?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Full-Duplex transmission** is a mode of communication in which **both stations can transmit and receive data simultaneously** over the link. 
>
> **Difference from Half-Duplex:**
> - *Half-Duplex* allows two-way communication, **but not at the same time** — only one station transmits while the other receives; they must take turns.
> - *Full-Duplex* allows **simultaneous** two-way communication, effectively **doubling the link throughput** for the same physical bandwidth.
>
> **Examples:** Telephone conversation is Full-Duplex; a walkie-talkie is Half-Duplex. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contrast, 1 mark for example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Mention any **three** physical techniques** used to implement Full-Duplex communication on a single medium.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD):** Allocating two distinct, non-overlapping frequency bands for the two directions.
2. **Time Division Duplex (TDD):** Alternating transmit and receive time slots on the same frequency, synchronised between stations.
3. **Echo Cancellation:** Digitally subtracting the locally transmitted signal from the received signal mixture on a single wire (used in 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet). *(1 mark each.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Full-Duplex communication system**. Discuss the role of a **duplexer**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A noiseless Full-Duplex channel has a bandwidth of **$B = 6$ kHz** and uses **$M = 8$** signal levels. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum one-way data rate.
- (ii) Aggregate Full-Duplex throughput.
- (iii) Time to transmit a **$500$ KB** file in one direction. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** Full-Duplex communication is a bidirectional mode where both stations can **transmit and receive simultaneously** on the same link.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Source A] → [Mod-A] → [Duplexer-A] ⇄ Medium ⇄ [Duplexer-B] → [Demod-B] → [Sink B]
[Source B] → [Mod-B] → [Duplexer-B]    ⇄        ⇄ [Duplexer-A] → [Demod-A] → [Sink A]
```

**Role of the Duplexer (2 marks):**
The **duplexer** is a pair of **band-pass filters** (or a circulator) that allows a transceiver to transmit and receive on the **same antenna** (or the same physical medium) by separating the two directions in the **frequency domain**. For FDD systems, the duplexer prevents the strong local Tx signal from saturating the sensitive Rx front-end.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) One-way rate (Nyquist):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{one-way}} &= 2 B \log_{2}(M) \\
&= 2 \times 6000 \times \log_{2}(8) \\
&= 2 \times 6000 \times 3 \\
&= 36\,000\;\text{bps} = \mathbf{36\;kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Aggregate Full-Duplex throughput:**

$$
C_{\text{FDX}} = 2 \times 36\,000 = \mathbf{72\;kbps}
$$

**(iii) Time to send 500 KB in one direction:**
$500\;\text{KB} = 500 \times 1024 \times 8 = 4\,096\,000$ bits.

$$
t = \dfrac{4\,096\,000}{36\,000} \approx \mathbf{113.8\;\text{seconds}}
$$

*(Distribution: 3 + 2 + 2 marks across the three sub-parts.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **Simplex, Half-Duplex and Full-Duplex** transmission modes in terms of direction, simultaneity, channel requirement and give **one real-world example** for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain any **two techniques** to achieve Full-Duplex on a single physical medium, with suitable diagrams. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneous? | Channel Requirement | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Simplex | One-way | N/A | 1 channel | TV broadcast |
| Half-Duplex | Two-way | No | 1 shared channel | Walkie-talkie |
| Full-Duplex | Two-way | Yes | 2 channels (or 1 with FDD/TDD/EC) | Telephone |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Full-Duplex provides the highest channel utilisation but at the cost of greater hardware complexity (duplexers, echo cancellers, more spectrum).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Technique 1 — Frequency Division Duplex (FDD) (3.5 marks):**
The total bandwidth $B$ is split into two non-overlapping sub-bands: $B_{\text{up}}$ (uplink) and $B_{\text{down}}$ (downlink). A **duplexer** at each end uses band-pass filtering to isolate Tx from Rx. Example: GSM cellular uplink (890–915 MHz) and downlink (935–960 MHz).

**Technique 2 — Echo Cancellation (3.5 marks):**
Both ends transmit and receive on the **same** wire. A **digital signal processor (DSP)** models the local Tx signal's "echo" path (impedance mismatch, hybrid circuit leakage) and **subtracts** it from the Rx mixture, recovering the far-end signal. This is what makes **1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet** Full-Duplex over a single Cat-5e cable (using all 4 pairs bidirectionally with hybrid + DSP).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing "Half-Duplex = one-way"** — it is *two-way but not simultaneous*. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Full-Duplex aggregate** — a common error is reporting $C_{\text{FDX}} = 2B \log_2(M)$ instead of $4B \log_2(M)$.
> 3. **Confusing FDD with TDD** — FDD = frequency split; TDD = time split. Examiners **do** test this distinction.
> 4. **Not stating the units (bps, kHz, seconds)** — KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly penalises missing units.
> 5. **In numericals, forgetting $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 10^6$ bits vs. $1 \text{ MiB} = 8 \times 2^{20}$ bits** — read the question carefully; use the convention it specifies.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts** — KTU valuation keys explicitly allocate **2–3 marks** for a labelled diagram in transmission-mode questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full-Duplex = simultaneous two-way communication.** Always state the word *simultaneous* in your answer to be safe.
- **Key formulas to memorise:**
  - $C = 2B \log_2 M$ (Nyquist, one direction)
  - $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ (Shannon, one direction)
  - $C_{\text{FDX}} = 2 \times C_{\text{one-way}}$ (aggregate)
- **Three implementation techniques:** FDD (frequency split), TDD (time split), Echo Cancellation (DSP subtraction).
- **Duplexer** is the hardware enabler of FDD; **echo canceller** is the enabler of single-wire FDX.
- **Real-world examples** to quote in exams: **telephone network, switched Ethernet, 5G NR, Starlink satellites**.
- **Numerical pitfall:** A Full-Duplex channel of bandwidth $B$ supports **twice the aggregate data rate** of a Half-Duplex channel of the same $B$.
- **Memory anchor:** **"S.H.F."** — Simplex, Half, Full — in increasing order of channel utilisation and hardware complexity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Media: Guided Media: Twisted Pair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transmission Media: Guided Media: Twisted Pair ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Twisted Pair (TP):** A guided transmission medium consisting of **two separately insulated copper conductors**, typically of 22–26 AWG (American Wire Gauge), helically twisted around each other at a defined lay length (twists per metre) for the purpose of carrying electrical signals while minimising susceptibility to external electromagnetic interference (EMI) and internal crosstalk between adjacent pairs. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the twisted pair belongs to the class of **guided (bounded) media**, meaning the signal energy is physically confined and directed along a physical path, as opposed to **unguided (unbounded/wireless) media** where propagation occurs through free space. The fundamental engineering purpose of *twisting* the two conductors is to ensure that any **external electromagnetic interference** induces equal and opposite noise voltages in successive twists, which then cancel out at the receiver differential input. This principle is known as **common-mode rejection** through symmetry. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Parallel Walkers:** Imagine two friends walking down a long, narrow corridor holding the ends of a long rope between them. If a strong gust of wind blows from the left, the rope will be pushed right. If the two friends are linked, they can compensate. Now imagine **twisting the rope** so that every metre the left and right sides swap positions. Any sustained wind (interference) will now hit each side **equally** over distance, and the differential signal at the destination remains clean. The *twist* is what makes the pair **noise-cancelling**. A simpler analogy: think of a **braided rope**. Each strand crosses over and under the other regularly. Whatever external tug affects one strand, the next moment affects the other — averaging out. That is exactly what a twisted pair does for electrical signals. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Conductor diameter (typical):** 0.4 mm – 0.8 mm - **Characteristic Impedance (Z₀):** **100 Ω** (Ethernet) or **120 Ω** (ISDN, older telephony) - **Nominal propagation velocity:** **≈ 0.6 c – 0.77 c** (where c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s) - **Operating bandwidth:** **100 kHz – 500 MHz** (depending on category) - **Standard maximum segment length:** **100 m** (for Ethernet 10BASE-T and above) - **Twists per metre (typical):** 2 – 24, varying per category and per pair within a cable ### 1.4 Classification Overview Twisted pairs are classified on two orthogonal axes: **Axis 1 — Shielding:** - **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair):** No metallic shield around pairs; relies solely on twisting and balanced transmission. - **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair):** Each pair (or all pairs) wrapped in a metallic foil/braid shield, reducing EMI and crosstalk further. **Axis 2 — Application Category (TIA/EIA-568 standard):** Categories define performance grades — primarily usable bandwidth and maximum data rate. | Category | Bandwidth | Typical Use | |----------|-----------|-------------| | Cat 1 | < 1 MHz | Voice / Telephone | | Cat 3 | 16 MHz | 10BASE-T Ethernet | | Cat 5 | 100 MHz | 100BASE-TX | | Cat 5e | 100 MHz | 1000BASE-T | | Cat 6 | 250 MHz | 10GBASE-T (≤ 55 m) | | Cat 6a | 500 MHz | 10GBASE-T (≤ 100 m) | | Cat 7 / 7a | 600 / 1000 MHz | 10GBASE-T, datacentres | | Cat 8 | 2000 MHz (2 GHz) | 25GBASE-T / 40GBASE-T | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Construction & Geometric Operating Principle A twisted pair cable is essentially a **balanced transmission line**, which is a two-conductor configuration in which neither conductor is tied to ground. The signal is transmitted as the **voltage difference** between the two wires. The operating principle relies on three physics-based facts: 1. **Twist cancellation of inductive pickup:** Each twist reverses the polarity of induced EMF from a uniform magnetic field. Over a full twist cycle, the net induced voltage is approximately zero. 2. **Differential reception:** The receiver reads only the *difference* between the two wires. Common-mode noise appears equally on both wires and is rejected. 3. **Capacitance balancing:** The symmetric geometry ensures uniform distributed capacitance, giving a constant **characteristic impedance** along the line — this prevents signal reflections at high frequencies. ### 2.2 Transmission-Line Electrical Model A twisted pair is modelled as a **lumped-element distributed parameter line**, characterised per unit length by: - **R** = series resistance (Ω/m) — increases with √f due to skin effect - **L** = series inductance (H/m) — from mutual coupling between the wires - **G** = shunt conductance (S/m) — represents dielectric loss in insulation - **C** = shunt capacitance (F/m) — between the two conductors > [!IMPORTANT] > At frequencies above about 100 kHz, the skin effect causes current to flow only in a thin outer shell of the conductor, raising R. This is why attenuation worsens with frequency and why higher categories use larger conductors (24 → 23 AWG) and better dielectrics. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit | |--------|----------|----------------------|------| | α | Attenuation coefficient | $\alpha = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | dB | | α(ℓ) | Total attenuation over length ℓ | $\alpha(\ell) = \alpha_{0} \cdot \ell$ | dB | | Z₀ | Characteristic impedance | $Z_{0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Ω | | v_p | Propagation velocity | $v_{p} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}}$ | m/s | | t_pd | Propagation delay per unit length | $t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}$ | s/m | | v | Velocity factor (fraction of c) | $v_{f} = \dfrac{v_{p}}{c}$ | dimensionless | | NEXT | Near-End Crosstalk | $\text{NEXT} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{crosstalk}}}{V_{\text{signal}}}\right)$ | dB | | ACR | Attenuation-to-Crosstalk Ratio | $\text{ACR} = \text{NEXT} - \alpha$ | dB | | C | Shannon channel capacity | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bps | | C | Nyquist noiseless capacity | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | bps | | RL | Return Loss | $\text{RL} = -20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB | > [!NOTE] > In the Return Loss formula, $\Gamma$ is the **voltage reflection coefficient** at an impedance mismatch. A perfect match has $\Gamma = 0$, so RL → ∞. Always use the vertical bar via `\vert` in LaTeX. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Twisted pairs dominate **enterprise LANs, building wiring, telephone local loops (DSL), and industrial Ethernet** because they offer the most favourable trade-off between: - **Cost per metre** (cheap copper, simple plastic insulation) - **Ease of installation** (flexible, terminates on standard RJ-45 connectors) - **Adequate performance** up to 10 Gbps at 100 m - **Compatibility with Power over Ethernet (PoE)** In modern data centres, twisted pair (Cat 6a / Cat 8) is used for short-reach 25G/40G links where fibre would be over-engineered. In telecommunications, the unshielded voice-grade pair remains the global access medium for the last kilometre. ### 2.5 Key Performance Parameters Explained 1. **Attenuation (α):** Signal power loss over distance. Specified in dB per 100 m. Increases with frequency. 2. **Crosstalk (NEXT/FEXT):** Signal coupling between adjacent pairs. NEXT is measured at the transmitter end; FEXT at the far end. 3. **Return Loss (RL):** Signal reflected back due to impedance mismatch (e.g., at connectors). 4. **Delay Skew:** Difference in propagation delay between the fastest and slowest pair in a cable. Critical for parallel transmission schemes like 1000BASE-T (uses all 4 pairs simultaneously). 5. **Characteristic Impedance (Z₀):** The natural impedance seen by a signal travelling down an infinite line. Must be matched by terminations to prevent reflections. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Attenuation from Power Ratio **Problem statement:** A 50 m Cat 5e cable is measured to have an input signal power of **+4 dBm** and an output power of **−13.5 dBm** at 100 MHz. Verify whether this meets the TIA-568 specification (which states attenuation must be ≤ 22 dB / 100 m at 100 MHz for Cat 5e). **Step 1 — Express powers in linear scale (watts or milliwatts).** The dBm reference is 1 mW. Therefore: $$P_{\text{in}}(\text{mW}) = 10^{\,4/10} = 10^{0.4} = 2.512\ \text{mW}$$ $$P_{\text{out}}(\text{mW}) = 10^{-13.5/10} = 10^{-1.35} = 0.0447\ \text{mW}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the power ratio.** $$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}} = \frac{2.512}{0.0447} = 56.2$$ **Step 3 — Convert to decibels using the attenuation formula.** $$\alpha = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(56.2) = 10 \times 1.750 = 17.5\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Scale to 100 m reference (since our cable is 50 m).** $$\alpha_{100\text{m}} = 17.5 \times \frac{100}{50} = 35.0\ \text{dB/100 m}$$ **Step 5 — Compare with specification.** $$\alpha_{100\text{m}} = 35.0\ \text{dB/100 m} \;>\; 22\ \text{dB/100 m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{FAIL}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The cable is performing 13 dB worse than spec — likely damaged, kinked, or counterfeit. This kind of attenuation budget calculation appears routinely in KTU problems. --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Characteristic Impedance of a Balanced Pair **Problem statement:** For a typical Cat 5e twisted pair, the per-unit-length inductance is **L = 525 nH/m** and the per-unit-length capacitance is **C = 49 pF/m**. Compute the characteristic impedance Z₀ and the propagation velocity v_p. **Step 1 — Apply the characteristic-impedance formula for a lossless line.** $$Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{525 \times 10^{-9}}{49 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the ratio L/C.** $$\frac{L}{C} = \frac{525 \times 10^{-9}}{49 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{525}{49} \times 10^{3} = 10.71 \times 10^{3} = 1.071 \times 10^{4}\ \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Take the square root.** $$Z_{0} = \sqrt{1.071 \times 10^{4}} = 1.035 \times 10^{2} = 103.5\ \Omega$$ This is in excellent agreement with the **100 Ω** standard for Ethernet twisted pair. **Step 4 — Compute propagation velocity.** $$v_{p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{525 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 49 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the product LC.** $$LC = 525 \times 49 \times 10^{-21} = 25725 \times 10^{-21} = 2.5725 \times 10^{-17}$$ **Step 6 — Take the square root and the reciprocal.** $$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{2.5725 \times 10^{-17}} = 5.072 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s/m}$$ $$v_{p} = \frac{1}{5.072 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.972 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 7 — Express as a fraction of c.** $$v_{f} = \frac{v_{p}}{c} = \frac{1.972 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.657 \;\; (\text{i.e. } 65.7\% \text{ of speed of light})$$ > [!NOTE] > This matches the typical "velocity factor" of 0.6 c to 0.77 c observed in real UTP cables. The signal literally travels at only 2/3 the speed of light in vacuum. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cat 5e vs Cat 6a vs Cat 7 (Engineering Trade-offs) | Parameter | Cat 5e | Cat 6a | Cat 7 | |-----------|--------|--------|-------| | Rated Bandwidth | 100 MHz | 500 MHz | 600 MHz | | Max Data Rate | 1 Gbps | 10 Gbps | 10 Gbps | | Max Distance (10G) | Not supported | 100 m | 100 m | | Conductor | 24 AWG | 23 AWG | 22–23 AWG | | Pair Shielding | None | None (UTP) | Individual + overall | | Attenuation @100 MHz | 22 dB/100 m | 19.1 dB/100 m | 18.5 dB/100 m | | NEXT @100 MHz | 35 dB | 44 dB | 75 dB | | PS-NEXT | 32 dB | 42 dB | 72 dB | | Delay Skew | 45 ns/100 m | 45 ns/100 m | 25 ns/100 m | | Connectors | RJ-45 | RJ-45 | GG45 / TERA | | Typical Cost (per m) | ₹ 18 | ₹ 45 | ₹ 110 | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that **Cat 7** is shielded; that is why NEXT is dramatically better (75 dB vs 35 dB for Cat 5e). Shielding directly suppresses inductive coupling between pairs. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Maximum Data Rate Using Nyquist **Problem statement:** A voice-grade twisted pair (Cat 3) has a usable bandwidth of 16 MHz. Using a 4-level pulse scheme (M = 4), what is the maximum theoretical bit rate? **Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist formula.** $$C = 2 B \log_{2}(M)$$ **Step 2 — Substitute B = 16 × 10⁶ and M = 4.** $$C = 2 \times 16 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(4)$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate log₂(4) = 2.** $$C = 2 \times 16 \times 10^{6} \times 2 = 64 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bps}$$ **Step 4 — State the answer.** $$C = 64\ \text{Mbps}$$ > [!NOTE] > Compare this to **Shannon capacity** for the same 16 MHz with SNR = 30 dB (i.e. S/N = 1000): > $$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 16 \times 10^{6} \times \log_{2}(1001) \approx 16 \times 10^{6} \times 9.97 \approx 159.4\ \text{Mbps}$$ > This is the **fundamental upper bound** — even the best coding cannot exceed Shannon's limit. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Architecture of a Twisted Pair Cable (Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Insulated Copper Wire 1] --> C[Twist Region
Lay Length 10-15mm] B[Insulated Copper Wire 2] --> C C --> D[Pair Unit] D --> E[4 Twisted Pairs Combined] E --> F[Inner Jacket
Polyethylene] F --> G[Optional Overall Shield
Foil or Braid] G --> H[Outer Jacket
PVC or LSZH] H --> I[RJ45 Terminated Cable] ``` ### 4.2 UTP vs STP — Functional Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB UTP[UTP Unshielded Twisted Pair] STP[STP Shielded Twisted Pair] UTP --> U1[No metallic shield] UTP --> U2[Twist cancellation only] UTP --> U3[Cheaper, lighter, easier to install] UTP --> U4[Limited to 100m 10Gbps] STP --> S1[Foil shield per pair or overall] STP --> S2[Twist plus Faraday cage effect] STP --> S3[Heavier, more expensive] STP --> S4[Supports 40Gbps at 30m Cat8] ``` ### 4.3 Signal Flow and Crosstalk Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR TX[Transmitter Pair 1
Differential signal] -->|intended signal| RX1[Receiver Pair 1] TX2[Transmitter Pair 2
Differential signal] -->|intended signal| RX2[Receiver Pair 2] TX -.->|leakage via mutual capacitance| RX1 TX2 -.->|NEXT coupling| RX1 RX1 --> OUT[Desired differential output] RX1 -.->|interference added| OUT ``` ### 4.4 Layered Functional Model of a Twisted-Pair Link ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1 Physical Conductor
22 to 26 AWG Copper] L2[Layer 2 Insulation
Polyethylene or FEP Dielectric] L3[Layer 3 Twisting Geometry
Variable Lay Length per Pair] L4[Layer 4 Pair Shielding
Optional Foil or Braid] L5[Layer 5 Overall Jacket
PVC or LSZH] L6[Layer 6 Connector
RJ45 or GG45] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 ``` ### 4.5 Frequency vs Attenuation Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[1 MHz region
Attenuation 2 dB per 100m] --> F2[10 MHz region
Attenuation 6 to 7 dB per 100m] F2 --> F3[100 MHz region
Attenuation 22 dB per 100m] F3 --> F4[500 MHz region
Attenuation 45 dB per 100m] F4 --> F5[2000 MHz region Cat 8
Attenuation 70 dB per 100m] ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of the twisted pair, its sub-components, and its signal-flow behaviour. Physical photorealistic schematics (such as a cut-away of a Cat 6a cable showing the spline separator) cannot be generated in Mermaid; the block-level flow provides equivalent structural clarity for KTU answer scripts. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Explain the significance of "twisting" in a twisted pair cable. How does it help reduce crosstalk?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** In a twisted pair, the two insulated copper conductors are **helically wound around each other** with a defined lay length. The mechanical twist performs two critical electrical functions: 1. **Electromagnetic interference cancellation:** When an external uniform magnetic field cuts across the pair, each twist reverses the polarity of the induced EMF. The induced voltages in successive half-twists therefore add to zero, so net external pickup is minimised. 2. **Crosstalk reduction between adjacent pairs:** Each pair is twisted at a **different (non-multiple) lay length** compared to its neighbours. This prevents the magnetic field from one pair from resonating with another pair's geometry, reducing inductive coupling. The result is a significant increase in **NEXT** (Near-End Crosstalk) margin. > [!NOTE] > Awarding tip: Students often mention "twisting cancels interference" but forget the second point about *different lay lengths between pairs* — KTU examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for the inter-pair twist differential. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Differentiate between UTP and STP. State one advantage and one limitation of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) | STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) | |--------|--------------------------------|------------------------------| | Construction | Only twist geometry; no metallic shield | Foil/braid shield around pairs or overall | | Cost | Lower; cheaper raw materials and connectors | Higher; specialised shielded connectors needed | | Flexibility | High; light and easy to route | Lower; rigid due to shielding | | EMI resistance | Moderate (depends on twist quality) | High (Faraday-cage effect) | | Grounding | Not required | Must be properly grounded to be effective | **Advantage of UTP:** Low cost and ease of installation in office/LAN environments. **Limitation of UTP:** Poor performance in high-EMI industrial environments. **Advantage of STP:** Excellent noise immunity, suitable for industrial/factory floors. **Limitation of STP:** Requires correct grounding — ungrounded shield acts as an antenna and can worsen crosstalk. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the constructional features of a **Category 6a UTP cable**. Identify the function of each layer in the cable assembly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Cat 5e cable has L = 525 nH/m and C = 49 pF/m. Compute its characteristic impedance, propagation velocity, and the total propagation delay for a 100 m run. Comment on the suitability for 1000BASE-T Ethernet. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks The constructional layers of a Cat 6a cable from innermost to outermost: 1. **Conductor (1 mark):** 23 AWG solid bare copper, ~0.57 mm diameter — lower resistance than Cat 5e to support 500 MHz bandwidth. 2. **Insulation (1 mark):** High-density polyethylene or FEP dielectric, which determines the capacitance per unit length. 3. **Twist geometry (1 mark):** Each of the 4 pairs twisted at a **unique lay length** (non-multiples of each other) to break inter-pair coupling. 4. **Spline / cross-filler (1 mark):** A central plastic cross-shaped separator that physically keeps the four pairs apart, reducing alien crosstalk (ANEXT). 5. **Jacket (1 mark):** Outer LSZH or PVC sheath. 6. **Block diagram (1 mark):** Show Conductor → Insulation → Pair (twisted) → 4 pairs around spline → Jacket. 7. **Function of each layer (1 mark):** Conductor carries signal; insulation confines field; twisting cancels noise; spline maintains geometry; jacket protects mechanically. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Characteristic impedance. $$Z_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{525 \times 10^{-9}}{49 \times 10^{-12}}} = \sqrt{1.071 \times 10^{4}} = 103.5\ \Omega$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Propagation velocity. $$v_{p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{525 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 49 \times 10^{-12}}} = 1.972 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Velocity factor. $$v_{f} = \frac{1.972 \times 10^{8}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.657 \;\; (65.7\%\ \text{of } c)$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Total propagation delay for 100 m. $$t_{\text{pd}} = \sqrt{LC} = 5.072 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s/m}$$ $$t_{\text{total}} = 5.072 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 = 5.072 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} = 507.2\ \text{ns}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Comment on 1000BASE-T suitability. The 1000BASE-T standard (IEEE 802.3ab) requires the channel to support 62.5 MHz of usable bandwidth on each of the 4 pairs and a round-trip delay of under 1000 ns. A 100 m Cat 5e segment has total one-way delay of 507.2 ns, which is well within budget, confirming **suitability**. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Additional remark — Cat 5e supports 1 Gbps because 1000BASE-T uses **PAM-5 encoding** across all 4 pairs, achieving 250 Mbps per pair, exploiting the 100 MHz bandwidth via Nyquist's 2B rule. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion — 1000BASE-T over 100 m of Cat 5e is supported with margin. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** Define **crosstalk**. Explain the difference between **NEXT** and **FEXT** with reference to a 4-pair UTP cable used in Gigabit Ethernet. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 250 m run of Cat 5e cable is used as a single link. The TIA-568 standard limits the attenuation to 22 dB at 100 MHz over 100 m. Calculate the **maximum allowable input power** in dBm such that the received power remains above the receiver sensitivity of **−30 dBm**. State whether the link is usable at 100 MHz. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define crosstalk. *Crosstalk is the unwanted coupling of signal energy from one twisted pair (the disturbing pair) to an adjacent pair (the disturbed pair), measured in dB.* **Step 2 (1 mark):** NEXT definition. *NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk) is the crosstalk measured at the same end as the transmitter — the disturbing signal has only travelled a few metres when it leaks into the adjacent pair, so the offending signal is still strong.* **Step 3 (1 mark):** FEXT definition. *FEXT (Far-End Crosstalk) is the crosstalk measured at the opposite end of the cable from the transmitter. The disturbing signal has travelled nearly the full cable length, so it is much weaker when it couples in; therefore FEXT is usually a smaller value (less harmful) than NEXT.* **Step 4 (1 mark):** Relevance to Gigabit Ethernet. 1000BASE-T uses all 4 pairs simultaneously in full duplex, each pair carrying 250 Mbps of PAM-5. Because every pair both transmits and receives simultaneously, **NEXT is the dominant impairment** and must be cancelled using DSP techniques like **NEXT cancellation** at the PHY. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Typical NEXT value for Cat 5e at 100 MHz is **≥ 35 dB**. Below this, the link fails. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Equation and difference. $$\text{NEXT}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{couple}}}{V_{\text{send}}}\right)_{\text{same end}}$$ $$\text{FEXT}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{couple}}}{V_{\text{send}}}\right)_{\text{far end}}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Why NEXT is worse — geometric proximity at the near end and the disturbing signal has not yet suffered attenuation when it leaks. #### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute attenuation for 250 m. $$\alpha_{100\text{m}} = 22\ \text{dB} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha_{250\text{m}} = 22 \times \frac{250}{100} = 55\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the power-budget equation. $$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} - \alpha_{\text{total}}$$ $$-30\ \text{dBm} = P_{\text{in}} - 55\ \text{dB}$$ $$P_{\text{in}} = -30 + 55 = +25\ \text{dBm}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Maximum allowable input power is **+25 dBm**. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Convert to milliwatts. $$P_{\text{in}} = 10^{25/10}\ \text{mW} = 10^{2.5} = 316.2\ \text{mW}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Usability check. Cat 5e is rated for a **maximum channel length of 100 m** for 1000BASE-T and **100 m** even for 100BASE-TX. A 250 m run **violates the standard** — it is unusable for any Ethernet standard irrespective of power budget. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Even with adequate power, **propagation delay and jitter budgets** would be exceeded. Round-trip propagation delay at 0.66 c for 250 m is about **2.53 µs**, exceeding the IEEE 802.3 collision-detection timing. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Verdict — **Link NOT usable**. Either shorten to 100 m, use a repeater, or upgrade to fibre. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common marking pitfalls KTU examiners penalise heavily:** > > 1. **Forgetting to scale attenuation correctly** — students frequently write 22 dB as the loss for *any* length, ignoring that it is per 100 m. Always state the reference length. > > 2. **Mixing up dB and dBm** — *dB* is a *ratio*; *dBm* is an *absolute* power referenced to 1 mW. The difference between them is worth 1 full mark in KTU scripts. > > 3. **Using dB where a ratio is needed in a formula** — when applying Shannon's formula or Nyquist's formula, the SNR must be a *linear* ratio (e.g. 1000), not 30 dB. Converting 30 dB → 1000 is the step students miss. > > 4. **Confusing NEXT and FEXT** — they are measured at *different ends*. Drawing a one-line diagram showing where the transmitter and receiver are is worth a free 1 mark. > > 5. **Ignoring the velocity factor** — propagation delay in a cable is **not** $\ell / c$. The actual value is $\ell / (0.66 c)$. This mistake causes off-by-50% answers in delay calculations. > > 6. **Not identifying that 250 m of Cat 5e is non-compliant** — even if the power budget works, the *length* itself violates the IEEE 802.3 standard. Examiners look for this exact point. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Twisted pair** = two insulated copper wires twisted helically to cancel EMI and crosstalk. - **Categories** range from Cat 1 (telephony) to Cat 8 (data centres, 2 GHz); the higher the category, the higher the bandwidth. - **Z₀ = 100 Ω** for Ethernet-grade UTP, **120 Ω** for older ISDN/telephony. - **Velocity factor** is 0.6 c – 0.77 c; never assume c. - **Attenuation scales linearly with length** (in dB), but with the square root of frequency (due to skin effect). - **NEXT > FEXT** typically; NEXT is the dominant impairment in full-duplex multi-pair systems. - **Differential signalling** with balanced pairs is the noise-cancellation mechanism. - **Maximum segment length = 100 m** for all IEEE 802.3 twisted-pair Ethernet. - **Capacity limits:** Nyquist (noiseless) C = 2B log₂(M); Shannon (noisy) C = B log₂(1 + S/N). - **STP needs proper grounding** — otherwise the shield becomes an antenna and worsens the problem. - **Characteristic impedance** of a lossless line: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. - **Propagation delay per metre:** $t_{pd} = \sqrt{LC}$. - **Twisting differential between pairs** (different lay lengths) is what reduces inter-pair crosstalk — not just twisting itself.

topics.title: Coaxial
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coaxial

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coaxial Cable (Coax):** A coaxial cable is a **guided transmission medium** consisting of a central inner conductor surrounded by a concentric (co-axial) outer conductor, separated by a dielectric insulator and enclosed within an outer protective jacket. It is designed to carry high-frequency electrical signals with minimal electromagnetic interference (EMI) and crosstalk.

The term **"co-axial"** literally means *"sharing a common axis"* — the inner conductor and the outer braided/foiled shield share the **same geometric axis**, hence the name.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍜

> **The "Two-Pipe Water Highway" Analogy**
> Imagine two concentric water pipes — a thin one inside a thick one — with insulating foam between them.
> *   The **inner pipe** carries the actual water (your data signal) forward.
> *   The **outer pipe** carries the return current back, completely shielded from the outside world.
> *   The **foam** between them ensures no water leakage (signal loss) and keeps the flow directional.
> *   An external magnetic storm (EMI/Noise) cannot enter the system because the outer pipe acts as a **Faraday Cage**.

This is *exactly* how a coax cable works: the central wire is the forward path, the mesh/foil shield is the return path, and the dielectric ensures clean, contained signal propagation.

## 1.3 Physical Construction (Layer-by-Layer)

| Layer | Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. Inner Conductor** | Solid or stranded copper wire (or copper-clad steel) | Carries the **forward signal current** |
| **2. Dielectric Insulator** | Polyethylene / Foam PE / Teflon (PTFE) | Maintains spacing; determines **capacitance & velocity** |
| **3. Outer Conductor (Shield)** | Braided copper, aluminum foil, or both | Carries **return current**; provides **EMI shielding** |
| **4. Outer Jacket** | PVC or PE polymer | Mechanical **protection** against moisture, abrasion, heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric Constants for Coaxial Cables:**
> *   Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> *   Intrinsic impedance of free space: $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0 / \varepsilon_0} \approx 377 \, \Omega$
> *   Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
> *   Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> *   **Standard coax impedances:** **$50\,\Omega$** (Ethernet, RF) and **$75\,\Omega$** (Cable TV, Video)

## 1.4 Two Variants of Coaxial Cable

*   **Baseband Coax (e.g., 50 Ω):** Used for **digital signaling** in Ethernet (10BASE-2, 10BASE-5 legacy). Single channel, baseband transmission.
*   **Broadband Coax (e.g., 75 Ω):** Used for **cable television, cable internet, and long-distance telecom**. Supports multiple channels using **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** and analog/RF signaling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Why Coax Works

The genius of the coaxial design lies in the **concentric symmetry**. Because the electromagnetic field is fully confined between the inner and outer conductor in a **TEM (Transverse ElectroMagnetic)** mode, four critical things happen:

1.  **No External Radiation:** The outer shield absorbs the field, so signals don't leak out (low eavesdropping risk).
2.  **No External Interference:** External fields are blocked by the shield (excellent noise immunity).
3.  **No Crosstalk:** Adjacent coax cables don't interfere with each other.
4.  **Controlled Impedance:** The geometry precisely determines the characteristic impedance.

## 2.2 Primary Transmission Parameters (Per Unit Length)

For a coax of length $\ell$, we define distributed parameters per meter:

*   **$R$** → Resistance per unit length (Ω/m) — ohmic loss in both conductors (skin effect dominates at high frequencies).
*   **$L$** → Inductance per unit length (H/m) — from magnetic field around the inner conductor.
*   **$G$** → Conductance per unit length (S/m) — leakage through the dielectric.
*   **$C$** → Capacitance per unit length (F/m) — from the electric field between conductors.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📜

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All exam-critical equations for a coaxial cable (inner radius $=a$, outer radius $=b$, relative permittivity $=\varepsilon_r$):

| # | Parameter | Formula | KTU Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Capacitance / unit length** | $C = \dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$ | Depends only on geometry & dielectric |
| 2 | **Inductance / unit length** | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)$ | Independent of dielectric |
| 3 | **Conductance / unit length** | $G = \dfrac{2\pi\sigma_d}{\ln(b/a)}$ | $\sigma_d$ = dielectric conductivity |
| 4 | **Resistance / unit length** | $R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\left(\dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{b}\right)\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi f \mu_0}{\sigma_c}}$ | Skin effect formula; $\sigma_c$ = conductor conductivity |
| 5 | **Characteristic Impedance** | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) = \dfrac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | For low-loss coax ($R \ll \omega L$, $G \ll \omega C$) |
| 6 | **Propagation Velocity** | $v_p = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Independent of geometry! |
| 7 | **Attenuation Constant** | $\alpha \approx \dfrac{R}{2Z_0} + \dfrac{GZ_0}{2}$ | Units: **Nepers/m** (N/m); convert to dB: $\alpha_{dB} = 8.686\alpha$ |
| 8 | **Phase Constant** | $\beta = \omega\sqrt{LC} = \dfrac{\omega\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | Determines wavelength on line |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick Question Alert:** Many students wrongly write $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$ *forgetting* that this is the *general* transmission-line formula. The **coax-specific** $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(b/a)$ is what the **step-by-step derivation** in Section 3 will mathematically prove. Both are equivalent — the coax form is just *simplified* for $\mu=\mu_0$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Cable TV Distribution (CATV):** 75 Ω broadband coax carries hundreds of MHz across cities.
*   **Legacy Ethernet:** 10BASE-2 (Thinnet) and 10BASE-5 (Thicknet) used 50 Ω baseband coax.
*   **Telecommunications:** Long-haul trunk lines between exchanges (historically dominant; now mostly fiber).
*   **RF & Microwave:** Antenna feed lines, test equipment (e.g., lab coax to oscilloscope).
*   **Cable Internet (DOCSIS):** Hybrid Fiber-Coax (HFC) networks for last-mile broadband.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ of a Coaxial Cable ⭐

> **Goal:** Starting from the fundamental distributed transmission-line equation $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$, derive the geometric formula specific to a coax.

**Step 1: State the General TL Formula**

For any lossless TEM transmission line:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 2: Apply Gauss's Law to Find $C$**

The electric field between concentric cylinders (inner at radius $a$, outer at radius $b$, length $\ell$) carrying charge $+Q$ and $-Q$ is:

$$E(r) = \frac{V}{r \ln(b/a)} \quad \text{for } a \le r \le b$$

The voltage between conductors:

$$V = \int_a^b E(r)\,dr = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \ell}\int_a^b \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{Q}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \ell}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

Therefore, capacitance per unit length:

$$C = \frac{Q}{V \ell} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 3: Apply Ampere's Law to Find $L$**

The magnetic field between the conductors carrying current $I$:

$$B(r) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$$

The magnetic flux linkage per unit length:

$$\Phi = \int_a^b B(r)\,dr = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

Therefore, inductance per unit length:

$$L = \frac{\Phi}{I} = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 4: Combine via $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)}{\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}}} \\[6pt]
&= \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi^2\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\right]^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{\eta_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad \left(\text{since } \eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} \approx 377\,\Omega\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify Using $\eta_0 = 120\pi\,\Omega$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{120\pi}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Convert Natural Log to Base-10 Log**

Since $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \frac{60 \times 2.303}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final Result:** $Z_0 = \dfrac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \Omega$ **(Most frequently asked KTU form)**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Attenuation Constant $\alpha$

For a low-loss line (valid for coax at moderate frequencies):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \frac{R}{2Z_0} + \frac{GZ_0}{2} \quad \text{(Nepers/m)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why?** From the propagation constant $\gamma = \sqrt{(R+j\omega L)(G+j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$. For low-loss, expand using binomial approximation. The **conductor loss term** is $R/(2Z_0)$ and the **dielectric loss term** is $GZ_0/2$.

To convert Nepers to decibels (per meter):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{dB/m} = 8.686 \times \alpha_{Np/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Insight:** $\alpha$ rises as $\sqrt{f}$ (because $R$ rises as $\sqrt{f}$ from skin effect), meaning **higher frequencies attenuate more**. This limits practical usable bandwidth.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Propagation Velocity $v_p$

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &= \frac{\omega}{\beta} = \frac{\omega}{\omega\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln(b/a)\cdot\dfrac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The geometry ($a$, $b$) cancels out completely!** The propagation speed depends *only* on the dielectric material. For polyethylene ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2.3$): $v_p \approx 0.66c \approx 1.98 \times 10^8$ m/s.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Worked-Out)

> **Q:** A coaxial cable has inner radius $a = 0.5$ mm, outer inner radius $b = 2.5$ mm, and polyethylene dielectric ($\varepsilon_r = 2.3$). Find (i) $Z_0$, (ii) $v_p$, (iii) time to travel 1 km.

**(i) Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \frac{138}{\sqrt{2.3}}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{0.5}\right) = \frac{138}{1.517}\log_{10}(5) = 90.97 \times 0.6990 \\[4pt]
&\approx 63.6 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Propagation Velocity:**

$$v_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.3}} \approx 1.98 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Time for 1 km:**

$$t = \frac{1000}{1.98 \times 10^8} \approx 5.05 \;\; \mu s$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since Mermaid cannot render true physical cross-sections of a cable, the following **block-level schematic** shows the concentric functional architecture and signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["LAYER 1: INNER CONDUCTOR Copper wire radius a Forward signal current I in"]
    nodeB["LAYER 2: DIELECTRIC INSULATOR Polyethylene Er 2.3 Stores energy as EM field"]
    nodeC["LAYER 3: OUTER CONDUCTOR SHIELD Braided copper inner radius b Return current I out Faraday cage"]
    nodeD["LAYER 4: OUTER JACKET PVC polymer Mechanical weather protection"]
    nodeE["TEM WAVE PROPAGATION Confined between inner and outer conductor Zero external radiation"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA -. "Field confined in" .-> nodeE
    nodeB -. "Field confined in" .-> nodeE
    nodeC -. "Field confined in" .-> nodeE
    nodeD -. "Protects entire" .-> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow & Shielding Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Signal Source Tx 50 ohm 75 ohm"] -->|Forward current| inner["Inner Conductor radius a"]
    inner --> field["EM Field confined in dielectric"]
    field -->|Return current| outer["Outer Shield radius b"]
    outer --> gnd["Ground"]
    ext1["External EMI Source"] -.->|"Blocked by Faraday cage"| outer
    ext2["Adjacent Coax Crosstalk"] -.->|"Blocked by shield"| outer
```

## 4.3 Transmission-Line Parameter Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    geom["Geometry a b Er"] --> L["Inductance L per unit length"]
    geom --> C["Capacitance C per unit length"]
    L --> z0["Characteristic Impedance Z0 = 138 by sqrt Er times log10 b over a"]
    C --> z0
    L --> vp["Propagation Velocity vp = c by sqrt Er"]
    C --> vp
    freq["Frequency f"] --> R["Resistance R from skin effect sqrt f"]
    R --> alpha["Attenuation alpha Np per m"]
    cond["Conductor conductivity sigma c"] --> R
    diel["Dielectric loss sigma d"] --> G["Conductance G"]
    G --> alpha
```

```mermaid
        b2 --> b3
    end

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define characteristic impedance of a transmission line. What is its value for a standard Ethernet coaxial cable?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Characteristic Impedance ($Z_0$):** It is the ratio of the voltage to current of a propagating wave at any point on an infinitely long, lossless transmission line. It is a **purely resistive** quantity determined by the geometry and material of the line.
>
> For a standard Ethernet coaxial cable (e.g., 10BASE-2 / 10BASE-5), the characteristic impedance is:
>
> $$Z_0 = 50 \;\; \Omega$$
>
> This $50\,\Omega$ standard is a compromise between maximum power transfer ($\approx 30\,\Omega$) and minimum attenuation ($\approx 77\,\Omega$).
> **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Compare baseband and broadband transmission in the context of coaxial cable.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Baseband Coax | Broadband Coax |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Impedance** | $50\,\Omega$ | $75\,\Omega$ |
| **Signaling** | Digital, single channel | Analog, multiple channels (FDM) |
| **Direction** | Bidirectional (half/full duplex) | Unidirectional (requires amplifiers for two-way) |
| **Application** | Legacy Ethernet | Cable TV, Cable Internet |
| **Topology** | Bus | Tree / Star |
| **Distance** | Limited (~500 m for 10BASE-5) | Long-haul with amplifiers |
> **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **characteristic impedance** of a coaxial cable starting from the fundamental definition $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction of a coaxial cable**. List any **four advantages** of coaxial cable over twisted-pair cable. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Step 1:** Assume a lossless, low-loss TEM mode propagation. Apply **Gauss's Law** for the electric field between inner conductor (radius $a$) and outer shield (inner radius $b$):

$$E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r}$$

**Step 2:** Voltage between conductors:

$$V = \int_a^b E\,dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Capacitance per unit length:

$$C = \frac{\lambda}{V} = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$$

**Step 4:** Apply **Ampere's Law**:

$$B(r) = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 5:** Flux linkage and inductance per unit length:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0 \ln(b/a)/2\pi}{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r/\ln(b/a)}} = \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Using $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) = \frac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:** TEM mode, low-loss ($R \ll \omega L$, $G \ll \omega C$), $\mu = \mu_0$, perfect conductors and homogeneous dielectric. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Construction of Coaxial Cable (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["CENTER Conductor Solid or stranded copper radius a"]
    layer2["DIELECTRIC INSULATOR Polyethylene Er 2.3 thickness b minus a"]
    layer3["OUTER CONDUCTOR Braided copper or Al foil radius b"]
    layer4["OUTER JACKET PVC mechanical protection"]
    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4
```

**Four Advantages of Coax over Twisted-Pair:**

1.  **Higher Bandwidth:** Supports hundreds of MHz vs. only a few MHz for UTP.
2.  **Better Noise Immunity:** Outer shield blocks EMI (Faraday cage effect).
3.  **Lower Crosstalk:** Field is fully contained; no coupling between adjacent cables.
4.  **Longer Repeater Spacing:** Lower attenuation per meter → fewer repeaters needed.
5.  *(Bonus)* Can be used for broadband (multi-channel FDM) transmission.

**[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Explain the **transmission characteristics** of a coaxial cable. Discuss how attenuation varies with frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius **0.4 mm** and an outer conductor of inner radius **1.6 mm**. The dielectric has a relative permittivity of **2.25**. Calculate:
  (i) The characteristic impedance
  (ii) The propagation velocity
  (iii) The time delay for a signal travelling **500 meters** through this cable. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Transmission Characteristics of Coaxial Cable:**

The performance of a coax is quantified by four primary parameters per unit length: $R$, $L$, $G$, $C$ (and derived: $Z_0$, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $v_p$).

**1. Attenuation (α):**
$$
\alpha = \frac{R}{2Z_0} + \frac{GZ_0}{2} \;\; \text{Np/m}
$$
*   The **conductor loss term** ($R/(2Z_0)$) is dominant for coax.
*   At high frequencies, due to the **skin effect**, the resistance per unit length:
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left(\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{b}\right)\sqrt{\frac{\pi f \mu_0}{\sigma_c}}$$
*   Since $R \propto \sqrt{f}$, the attenuation $\alpha \propto \sqrt{f}$ for the conductor-loss term. **Higher frequencies attenuate more**, which is why long-distance coax needs equalizers.

**2. Phase Distortion:**
Different frequency components travel at slightly different velocities due to dispersion in the dielectric, causing pulse spreading at long distances.

**3. Characteristic Impedance:**
Constant across frequencies (in the low-loss regime) and matched to $50\,\Omega$ or $75\,\Omega$ terminations to prevent reflections.

**4. Propagation Velocity:**
$$v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \approx 0.66c \text{ to } 0.9c$$
This means signals take ~$3.3$ ns to travel 1 m in polyethylene-dielectric coax.

**5. Bandwidth-Distance Product:** Typically 100 MHz·km to 1 GHz·km. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Given:** $a = 0.4$ mm, $b = 1.6$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 2.25$, distance $= 500$ m.

**(i) Characteristic Impedance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_0 &= \frac{138}{\sqrt{2.25}}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.6}{0.4}\right) = \frac{138}{1.5}\log_{10}(4) \\[4pt]
&= 92 \times 0.6021 \approx 55.39 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Propagation Velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{2.25}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Time Delay for 500 m:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t = \frac{d}{v_p} = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.5 \;\; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** $Z_0 \approx 55.4\,\Omega$, $v_p = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $t = 2.5\,\mu s$. **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Coax Questions:**
> 1. **Sign Error in $Z_0$ formula:** Some students write $Z_0 = 60\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}\ln(b/a)$ (multiplying by $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ instead of dividing). **Always divide** by $\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ — a higher dielectric permittivity *lowers* the impedance.
> 2. **Forgetting Units:** $\alpha$ in **Nepers/m** ≠ decibels. Conversion: $\alpha_{dB} = 8.686\,\alpha_{Np}$.
> 3. **Skin Effect Ignored:** At high frequencies, current flows only on the **outer skin** of the inner conductor and **inner skin** of the outer shield. $R$ is *not* the DC resistance — use the $\sqrt{f}$ formula.
> 4. **Mixing $a$ and $b$:** $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius. $b/a$ must always be $> 1$.
> 5. **Neglecting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ conversion:** The $60$ vs. $138$ factor — both are correct, just in different log bases. Use the form your textbook chapter uses.
> 6. **In Part B derivations, skipping the "Assumptions" section** costs 1–2 marks. KTU examiners *require* you to state: low-loss, TEM mode, homogeneous dielectric.

---

## 5.5 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Coaxial Cable" (Module 1):**

*   **Definition:** Two concentric conductors sharing a common axis, separated by a dielectric.
*   **Key Constant:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\,\Omega$ (intrinsic impedance of free space).
*   **Standard Impedances:** $50\,\Omega$ (Ethernet/RF) and $75\,\Omega$ (Cable TV).
*   **$Z_0$ Formula:** $Z_0 = \dfrac{138}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\log_{10}(b/a) = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\ln(b/a)$ $\Omega$.
*   **$v_p$ Formula:** $v_p = c/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ — **geometry-independent**!
*   **$C$ Formula:** $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r / \ln(b/a)$ F/m.
*   **$L$ Formula:** $L = (\mu_0/2\pi)\ln(b/a)$ H/m.
*   **Attenuation:** $\alpha = R/(2Z_0) + GZ_0/2$; rises as $\sqrt{f}$ (skin effect).
*   **Nepers vs dB:** $1$ Np $\approx 8.686$ dB.
*   **Modes:** TEM (transverse EM) is the dominant mode for coax.
*   **Advantages:** High BW, low EMI, low crosstalk, long distances, supports broadband (FDM).
*   **Disadvantages:** Stiffer, heavier, costlier than UTP; difficult to install in tight spaces.
*   **Applications:** CATV, HFC broadband, legacy Ethernet, RF test setups, antenna feeders.
*   **Baseband (50Ω)** ↔ Digital, single channel. **Broadband (75Ω)** ↔ Analog, FDM, multi-channel.
*   **Memory Hook:** *"A thick dielectric in a coax slows the signal, lowers the impedance."* ($\varepsilon_r \uparrow \Rightarrow v_p \downarrow, Z_0 \downarrow$)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
