topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fiber Optics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Optical Fiber (Fiber Optics)** is a dielectric waveguide, typically made of ultra-pure silica glass (SiO₂) or plastic, that guides information-bearing light waves from one end to the other by exploiting the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** between a high-refractive-index **core** and a lower-refractive-index **cladding**.

In the context of **Data and Computer Communication (PECCT751)**, fiber optics is a physical-layer guided transmission medium that encodes digital bits (0s and 1s) as pulses of light (typically in the near-infrared bands of **850 nm, 1300 nm, and 1550 nm**) generated by **Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)** or **Laser Diodes (LDs)**, and detected at the receiver by photodiodes (PIN or APD).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Light Pipe" 💡

Imagine you are standing inside a long, perfectly straight glass hallway with mirrored walls. If you shine a flashlight at one end at just the right angle, the light beam will *bounce* off the mirrored walls repeatedly without ever escaping, eventually reaching the far end. This is exactly how an optical fiber works:

- The **core** is the central corridor where light travels.
- The **cladding** is the "mirror" — but it does not use real silver. Instead, it has a slightly lower refractive index, so light bouncing inside the core hits a boundary it simply *cannot cross*, forcing it to reflect back.
- The **jacket** is the protective outer plastic, just like the concrete walls of the building protecting the mirrored hallway.

Because light travels at roughly **2 × 10⁸ m/s** in glass (about 2/3 the speed of light in vacuum), and because it experiences almost no electromagnetic interference, fiber optic links can carry data over tens of kilometers at multi-gigabit speeds — something no copper cable can match.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of light in vacuum (c)** = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **Speed of light in fiber (v)** ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (silica-based fiber)
> - **Refractive index of vacuum (n₀)** = **1.0**
> - **Typical core index (n₁)** = **1.48** (silica)
> - **Typical cladding index (n₂)** = **1.46** (silica)
> - **Operating wavelengths:** **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm** (near-infrared "windows")
> - **Lowest attenuation window:** **1550 nm** (~0.2 dB/km for modern silica)

## 1.4 Engineering Significance in Data Communication

| Parameter | Copper (Twisted Pair/Coax) | Optical Fiber |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | ~100 MHz–1 GHz | > 50 THz (theoretical) |
| Attenuation/km | 20–35 dB (twisted pair) | **0.2–3 dB** |
| EMI Susceptibility | High | **None** |
| Security | Eavesdroppable | Very hard to tap |
| Weight / Size | Heavy, bulky | **Light, thin** |
| Repeater spacing | ~100 m – 1 km | **50–100 km** |

This is why fiber forms the **backbone of the global Internet, undersea cables, FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home), and modern data centers**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Structure of an Optical Fiber

A standard optical fiber is a concentric cylinder built from four layers:

1. **Core** — Central light-carrying region, diameter **8–62.5 μm** (single-mode / multi-mode). Higher refractive index **n₁**.
2. **Cladding** — Surrounds the core, diameter typically **125 μm**. Lower refractive index **n₂**.
3. **Buffer (Coating)** — Soft plastic layer (~250 μm) for mechanical cushioning.
4. **Jacket** — Hard outer polymer for environmental protection (~900 μm final diameter).

> [!NOTE]
> Light is confined strictly to the **core**; the cladding's job is purely optical (to provide a low-index boundary for TIR), not to carry any light itself.

## 2.2 The Operating Principle — Total Internal Reflection (TIR)

Total Internal Reflection occurs when a light ray traveling in a **denser medium (higher n)** strikes the boundary with a **rarer medium (lower n)** at an angle **greater than the critical angle θc**. Under this condition, *no* light refracts out — **100% is reflected back**.

**Two physical conditions MUST both be satisfied for TIR:**
- ✅ The ray must travel from **higher-n to lower-n** medium (i.e., n₁ > n₂).
- ✅ The angle of incidence at the core–cladding boundary must **exceed the critical angle θc**.

## 2.3 Snell's Law and the Critical Angle

Snell's Law governs refraction at any dielectric boundary:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$

At the **critical angle** θc, the refracted ray grazes the boundary (refraction angle = 90°), so:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$

Solving for θc:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

## 2.4 Numerical Aperture (NA) and Acceptance Angle (θₐ)

The **Numerical Aperture** is the single most important parameter of an optical fiber. It quantifies the fiber's ability to *collect* light from an external source.

**Derivation logic (expanded in Section 3):**
- Apply Snell's law at the **air–core interface** (entrance face) to find the maximum external entry angle θa.
- This θa is the **acceptance angle**.
- NA is defined as sin(θa).

**Compact result:**

$$\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A larger NA → the fiber captures more light → easier to couple sources in.
> - However, larger NA also means **more modal dispersion** in multi-mode fibers (a trade-off).

## 2.5 Types of Optical Fibers

| Type | Core Diameter | Index Profile | Bandwidth | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Step-Index Multi-Mode (MMF)** | 50 / 62.5 μm | Abrupt step in n | ~50–200 MHz·km | LANs, short links |
| **Graded-Index Multi-Mode (GIF)** | 50 / 62.5 μm | Parabolic (n decreases radially) | ~500 MHz·km – 2 GHz·km | LANs, campus backbones |
| **Step-Index Single-Mode (SMF)** | 8–10 μm | Step index | **> 100 GHz·km** | Telecom, undersea, long-haul |

**Step-Index:** Different rays (modes) travel different path lengths → severe intermodal dispersion.

**Graded-Index:** Rays in outer (lower-n) regions travel *faster* (v = c/n) → all modes arrive nearly together → dispersion drastically reduced.

**Single-Mode:** Core diameter comparable to wavelength → only one propagation path exists → no intermodal dispersion.

## 2.6 Losses in Optical Fibers (Attenuation)

Attenuation α (in dB/km) is defined by:

$$\alpha = -\frac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$$

The three windows of operation correspond to local minima of attenuation vs. wavelength:
- **1st window (850 nm)** — early systems, LED sources.
- **2nd window (1300 nm)** — zero-dispersion point for silica.
- **3rd window (1550 nm)** — lowest attenuation; EDFA amplifiers work here.

## 2.7 Dispersion

Dispersion causes an optical pulse to *spread* in time as it propagates, limiting bit-rate × distance.

- **Intermodal (modal) dispersion** — different modes arrive at different times.
- **Intramodal (chromatic) dispersion** — material dispersion + waveguide dispersion.
- **Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD)** — relevant at very high bit rates (>10 Gbps).

**Pulse broadening for step-index multi-mode fiber:**

$$\Delta\tau_{\text{modal}} = \frac{L\,n_1\,\Delta}{c}, \quad \text{where } \Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \;\;(\text{relative index difference})$$

## 2.8 ⚡ KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorize the symbol meanings and the conditions under which each one applies.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{c}{v}$ | Refractive index (universal) |
| 2 | $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ | Snell's law at any interface |
| 3 | $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Critical angle for TIR |
| 4 | $\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$ | Numerical Aperture (core vs. cladding) |
| 5 | $\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a$ | Acceptance angle definition |
| 6 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Relative refractive index difference |
| 7 | $\text{NA} \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ | NA in terms of Δ (for small Δ) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$ | Attenuation in dB/km |
| 9 | $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$ | Power after length L (km) |
| 10 | $\Delta\tau_{\text{modal}} = \dfrac{L\,n_1\,\Delta}{c}$ | Pulse broadening in step-index MMF |
| 11 | $B \cdot L \leq \dfrac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{\text{modal}}}$ | Bandwidth–length product (approx.) |
| 12 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\,\text{NA}$ | Normalized frequency (V-number) |
| 13 | $N_{\text{modes}} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | Number of guided modes in MMF |
| 14 | Single-mode condition: $V < 2.405$ | Cutoff for single-mode operation |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Backbone of the Internet:** Submarine fiber cables (e.g., FLAG, AAE-1) carry > 95% of intercontinental data.
- **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home):** Modern broadband replaces last-mile copper.
- **Data Centers:** 400G/800G optical transceivers (QSFP-DD, OSFP) connect servers.
- **Medical endoscopy, military comms, industrial sensors** — wherever EMI-free, lightweight, secure links are needed.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains **exhaustive, line-by-line derivations** of all KTU-expected formulas. Each algebraic transition is explicitly written and explained.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Numerical Aperture (NA) and Acceptance Angle (θₐ)

**Setup:** A light ray in air (n₀ = 1) strikes the flat end-face of the fiber core (n₁) at an external entry angle **θa** with respect to the fiber axis. Inside the core, it refracts to angle **θ₁** (with respect to the axis). The ray then hits the core–cladding boundary at an angle of incidence **θ₂** (with respect to the normal to the boundary).

**Geometric relation between θ₁ and θ₂:**

From the right-angled triangle formed at the point where the ray hits the core–cladding boundary:

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 90° - \theta_1$$

Therefore:

$$\sin\theta_2 = \sin(90° - \theta_1) = \cos\theta_1$$

**Step 1 — Apply Snell's law at the core–cladding boundary (for TIR):**

For TIR to be just satisfied, θ₂ must equal the critical angle θc:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

So at the limiting case (acceptance angle), we have:

$$\sin\theta_2 = \sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's law at the air–core (entrance) face:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

With n₀ = 1 (air):

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 3 — Express sin θ₁ in terms of known quantities:**

Since $\sin\theta_2 = \cos\theta_1 = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$, we have:

$$\cos\theta_1 = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta_1 + \cos^2\theta_1 = 1$:

$$\sin\theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\theta_1} = \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2}$$

$$\sin\theta_1 = \sqrt{\frac{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}{n_1^{\,2}}} = \frac{\sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}}{n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the expression for sin θₐ:**

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$$

**Step 5 — Define Numerical Aperture (NA):**

$$\boxed{\;\text{NA} \;=\; \sin\theta_a \;=\; \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Approximate NA in Terms of Relative Index Difference (Δ)

**Step 1 — Start with the exact NA expression:**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{(n_1 - n_2)(n_1 + n_2)} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$$

**Step 2 — Factor out n₁:**

$$\text{NA} = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^{\!2}}$$

**Step 3 — Write the difference as n₁(1 − n₂/n₁):**

$$n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2} = (n_1 - n_2)(n_1 + n_2)$$

**Step 4 — Define the relative index difference Δ:**

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad n_2 = n_1(1 - \Delta)$$

Substituting:

$$n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2} = n_1^{\,2}\left[1 - (1 - \Delta)^2\right]$$

**Step 5 — Expand (1 − Δ)² for small Δ (typical Δ ≈ 0.01):**

$$(1 - \Delta)^2 \approx 1 - 2\Delta + \Delta^2 \approx 1 - 2\Delta \quad (\Delta^2 \text{ negligible})$$

So:

$$1 - (1 - \Delta)^2 \approx 2\Delta$$

**Step 6 — Final approximate form:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{NA} \;\approx\; n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}\;}$$

This is the form used in nearly all KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pulse Broadening in Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber

**Step 1 — The fastest mode** travels straight down the axis: path length = L, time = $\dfrac{L\,n_1}{c}$.

**Step 2 — The slowest mode** enters at the acceptance angle θa, zigzags with the longest geometric path:

$$\text{Path length} = \frac{L}{\cos\theta_1} = \frac{L}{\sin\theta_c} = \frac{L\,n_1}{n_2}$$

(using $\cos\theta_1 = \sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$)

**Step 3 — Time for slowest mode:**

$$t_{\text{slow}} = \frac{\text{path}}{v_{\text{core}}} = \frac{(L\,n_1/n_2)}{c/n_1} = \frac{L\,n_1^{\,2}}{c\,n_2}$$

**Step 4 — Time difference (pulse broadening):**

$$\Delta\tau = t_{\text{slow}} - t_{\text{fast}} = \frac{L\,n_1^{\,2}}{c\,n_2} - \frac{L\,n_1}{c}$$

Factor out $\dfrac{L\,n_1}{c}$:

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{L\,n_1}{c}\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \frac{L\,n_1}{c}\cdot\frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_2}$$

**Step 5 — For small Δ, n₂ ≈ n₁, so $\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_2} \approx \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \Delta$:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\tau_{\text{modal}} \;\approx\; \frac{L\,n_1\,\Delta}{c}\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Work-Out Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A step-index optical fiber has a core refractive index n₁ = 1.50, cladding index n₂ = 1.45, and core diameter 50 μm. A light source of wavelength 1300 nm is used. Compute:
(a) Numerical Aperture
(b) Acceptance angle
(c) Critical angle
(d) V-number
(e) Number of guided modes
(f) Pulse broadening per km

**Given:** n₁ = 1.50, n₂ = 1.45, a = 25 μm (radius), λ = 1300 nm, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.

**(a) NA:**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475}$$

$$\boxed{\text{NA} \approx 0.384}$$

**(b) Acceptance angle:**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA}) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)$$

$$\boxed{\theta_a \approx 22.6°}$$

**(c) Critical angle:**

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.45}{1.50} = 0.9667$$

$$\boxed{\theta_c \approx 75.2°}$$

**(d) V-number:**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\,\text{NA} = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{1300 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.384$$

$$V = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-4}}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.384 = 120.83 \times 0.384 \approx 46.4$$

**(e) Number of modes (since V >> 1, multi-mode):**

$$N_{\text{modes}} \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(46.4)^2}{2} = \frac{2152.96}{2} \approx 1076$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{modes}} \approx 1076}$$

**(f) Pulse broadening per km (Δ = (n₁ − n₂)/n₁ = 0.05/1.50 = 0.0333):**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{L\,n_1\,\Delta}{c} = \frac{(1000\,\text{m})(1.50)(0.0333)}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{50}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.667 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{s} = 166.7\,\text{ns/km}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\tau \approx 166.7 \text{ ns/km}}$$

---

## 3.5 Attenuation Calculation — Work-Out Example

**Problem:** A 25 km fiber link has input power 1 mW and output power 4 μW. Find (a) total loss in dB, (b) attenuation coefficient α in dB/km.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total loss:**

$$L_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$

$$= 10\log_{10}(250) = 10 \times 2.398 = 23.98 \text{ dB}$$

**(b) Attenuation coefficient:**

$$\alpha = \frac{L_{\text{dB}}}{L} = \frac{23.98}{25} \approx 0.96 \text{ dB/km}$$

This is consistent with the **2nd telecommunications window (1300 nm)**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Fiber Types

| Parameter | Step-Index MMF | Graded-Index MMF | Single-Mode SMF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | 50–62.5 μm | 50–62.5 μm | 8–10 μm |
| Index profile | Step | Parabolic (n(r) = n₁√(1 − 2Δ(r/a)²)) | Step |
| Number of modes | Hundreds–thousands | Hundreds | **1** |
| Intermodal dispersion | **High** | Reduced (~100×) | **None** |
| Source required | LED | LED / LD | **Laser (LD)** |
| Typical bandwidth × distance | 50 MHz·km | 1 GHz·km | > 100 GHz·km |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (precision alignment) |
| Application | LAN, very short | LAN, campus | **Telecom, long-haul** |

```mermaid
graph TD
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[INPUT ELECTRICAL SIGNAL DATA] --> ENC[ENCODER E O CONVERTER]
    ENC --> SRC1[LIGHT SOURCE LED OR LASER DIODE]
    SRC1 --> CPL[COUPLING LENS]
    CPL --> FIB[OPTICAL FIBER CORE CLADDING BUFFER JACKET]
```

## 4.3 Ray Propagation — Total Internal Reflection Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    J[EXTERNAL LIGHT RAY IN AIR] --> K[STRIKES CORE END FACE]
    K --> L{Snell Law Check}
    L -- Angle within acceptance cone --> M[REFRACTED INTO CORE]
    L -- Angle outside acceptance cone --> R[REFRACTED OUT ESCAPES]
    M --> N[HITS CORE CLADDING BOUNDARY]
    N --> O{Angle greater than critical angle}
    O -- YES TIR --> P[100 PERCENT REFLECTED BACK INTO CORE]
    O -- NO --> Q[PARTIAL REFRACTION LIGHT LOST]
    P --> N
```

```mermaid
    AC --> X2[MERIDIONAL RAY ZIGZAG VIA TIR]

    X1 --> PROP[ALL RAYS PROPAGATE TO RECEIVER]
    X2 --> PROP
    X3 --> PROP
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow of Light Inside Core

```mermaid
graph LR

    OUT1 --> PD[PULSE SPREAD DELTATAU OBSERVED]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Question structure mirrors the official KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern. Each Part A carries 3 marks, each Part B sub-question carries 7 marks (total 14 per main question).

---

## 5.1 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Numerical Aperture (NA) and acceptance angle for an optical fiber. Mention the formula relating them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Acceptance Angle (θₐ):** It is the **maximum angle** with respect to the fiber axis at which a light ray in air can enter the fiber and still be guided by total internal reflection. (1 mark)
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** It is a dimensionless quantity that measures the light-gathering ability of the fiber, defined as the sine of the acceptance angle. (1 mark)
- **Formula:** $\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$ where n₁ and n₂ are the refractive indices of the core and cladding respectively. (1 mark)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] List any three advantages of optical fiber communication over copper-based communication.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Much higher bandwidth** — Optical fibers can carry signals at frequencies of the order of 10¹⁴ Hz, supporting tens of THz of bandwidth, vastly exceeding copper.
2. **Low attenuation** — Typical attenuation is 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm, allowing repeater spacings of 50–100 km versus ~1 km for copper.
3. **Immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — Since fiber carries light, not electrical signals, it is unaffected by external EMI, crosstalk, or ground loops. *(Other valid answers: small size, light weight, security from tapping, no spark hazard.)*

---

## 5.2 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Pattern Note:** KTU ESE gives an internal choice — answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B from each module slot.

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat ray diagram, explain the principle of propagation of light through an optical fiber. Define the terms *critical angle*, *numerical aperture*, and *acceptance angle*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of propagation (3 marks):**
- Optical fiber consists of a cylindrical **core** of refractive index n₁ surrounded by a **cladding** of slightly lower refractive index n₂ (n₁ > n₂).
- When a light ray enters the core at an angle less than the acceptance angle, it undergoes **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** at the core–cladding boundary repeatedly and is guided along the fiber.
- For TIR, the angle of incidence at the boundary must exceed the **critical angle θc**, and the ray must travel from a denser (n₁) to rarer (n₂) medium.

**Ray diagram description (1 mark):**
- Diagram should show: external ray striking end face at θa, refracting into core at θ₁, hitting the core–cladding boundary at angle θ₂ = (90° − θ₁) > θc, undergoing TIR.

**Definitions (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
- **Critical Angle (θc):** The angle of incidence at the core–cladding boundary beyond which total internal reflection occurs. $\sin\theta_c = n_2 / n_1$.
- **Numerical Aperture (NA):** The light-collecting capability of the fiber. $\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$.
- **Acceptance Angle (θa):** The maximum external angle with respect to the fiber axis at which a ray can enter and be guided.

---

**Q. (b)** A step-index fiber has a core diameter of 50 μm, n₁ = 1.48, and n₂ = 1.46. Determine: (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance angle, (iii) Critical angle, (iv) V-number at λ = 1300 nm, (v) Number of modes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** n₁ = 1.48, n₂ = 1.46, a = 25 μm, λ = 1300 nm.

**(i) Numerical Aperture (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{NA} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.2425$$

**(ii) Acceptance angle (1 mark):**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) \approx 14.04°$$

**(iii) Critical angle (1 mark):**

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1) = \sin^{-1}(1.46/1.48) = \sin^{-1}(0.9865) \approx 80.55°$$

**(iv) V-number (1.5 marks):**

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\cdot\text{NA} = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2425$$

$$V = 120.83 \times 0.2425 \approx 29.3$$

**(v) Number of modes (2 marks):**
Since V ≫ 2.405, the fiber is **multi-mode**:

$$N_{\text{modes}} \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(29.3)^2}{2} = \frac{858.5}{2} \approx 429 \text{ modes}$$

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q. (a)** Explain the different types of optical fibers based on the index profile and mode of propagation. Compare their bandwidth and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (2 marks):**
- Core has uniform refractive index n₁; cladding has uniform n₂. Refractive index drops abruptly (step).
- Core diameter 50–62.5 μm. Many modes propagate via different zigzag paths.
- Different paths have different lengths → **severe intermodal dispersion** → bandwidth ~50 MHz·km.
- **Application:** Short-distance LANs, sensor systems.

**2. Graded-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (2.5 marks):**
- Core refractive index varies **parabolically**: maximum at center, decreases toward cladding.
- Rays in outer regions (lower n) travel **faster** (v = c/n), compensating for longer path → all rays arrive together.
- Bandwidth **~1–2 GHz·km** (100× better than step-index MMF).
- **Application:** Campus networks, moderate-distance LANs, data centers.

**3. Step-Index Single-Mode Fiber (2.5 marks):**
- Very small core (~8–10 μm), comparable to wavelength.
- Only **one mode** can propagate when V < 2.405.
- **No intermodal dispersion** (only chromatic and PMD).
- Bandwidth **> 100 GHz·km** → supports Tbps links.
- **Application:** Long-haul telecom, undersea cables, high-speed backbone.

**Summary table (extra, 1 mark):**

| Type | Bandwidth·Distance | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Step MMF | 50 MHz·km | LAN |
| Graded MMF | 1 GHz·km | Campus |
| SMF | 100+ GHz·km | Telecom |

---

**Q. (b)** A fiber link of length 40 km has a measured loss of 16 dB. If the input power is 200 μW, find (i) attenuation coefficient α in dB/km, (ii) output power P_out, (iii) the required input power if output must be 5 μW for the same link. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Attenuation coefficient (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Total loss}}{L} = \frac{16 \text{ dB}}{40 \text{ km}} = 0.4 \text{ dB/km}$$

**(ii) Output power (2.5 marks):**

$$\text{Loss (dB)} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

$$16 = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{P_{\text{out}}}\right) = 1.6 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{P_{\text{out}}} = 10^{1.6} = 39.81$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{39.81} \approx 5.024 \,\mu\text{W}$$

**(iii) Required input power for P_out = 5 μW (2.5 marks):**

$$16 = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; 10^{1.6} = 39.81$$

$$P_{\text{in}} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 39.81 \approx 1.99 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W} \approx 199 \,\mu\text{W}$$

(Slight discrepancy due to rounding in (ii); both consistent with α = 0.4 dB/km.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on Fiber Optics questions:**
> 1. **Wrong direction in Snell's law** — always confirm n₁ > n₂ and that the ray is going from denser → rarer for TIR.
> 2. **Confusion between acceptance angle and critical angle** — θa is in **air** (external), θc is at the **core–cladding boundary** (internal). They are not the same.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — wavelength in m, length in km, time in ns. Many errors arise from mixing μm with nm.
> 4. **NA ≈ 0 for matched indices** — if n₁ = n₂, NA = 0 (no light captured). Mentioning this conceptual extreme can earn a partial mark.
> 5. **Mixing Δ and NA** — Δ is the **relative index difference**; NA = n₁√(2Δ) is approximate and **only valid for small Δ**.
> 6. **Units of attenuation** — α in dB/km. Length L must be in **km**, not meters, when used in the dB/km formula.
> 7. **V-number mistake** — using diameter instead of radius, or omitting the factor 2π.
> 8. **Not labeling the ray diagram** — TIR ray diagram MUST show **angle of incidence > critical angle** with arrow directions; vague diagrams lose at least 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Fiber Optics (Module 1):**

- 🔹 **Optical fiber** = dielectric waveguide guiding light by **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.
- 🔹 **Structure:** Core (n₁) → Cladding (n₂ < n₁) → Buffer → Jacket.
- 🔹 **Snell's Law:** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ — fundamental equation.
- 🔹 **Critical Angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ — threshold for TIR.
- 🔹 **Acceptance Angle:** $\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}}$ — max external entry angle.
- 🔹 **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $\text{NA} = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^{\,2} - n_2^{\,2}} \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$.
- 🔹 **Relative index difference:** $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ (typically ~0.01 for telecom fibers).
- 🔹 **Three fiber types:** Step-Index MMF, Graded-Index MMF, Single-Mode Fiber (SMF).
- 🔹 **Attenuation formula:** $\alpha = (10/L)\log_{10}(P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{out}})$ dB/km.
- 🔹 **Power after length L:** $P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$.
- 🔹 **Modal dispersion (step-index MMF):** $\Delta\tau = (L n_1 \Delta)/c$ → limits bandwidth × distance.
- 🔹 **V-number:** $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot \text{NA}$ → single-mode cutoff at V < 2.405.
- 🔹 **Number of modes (MMF):** $N_{\text{modes}} \approx V^2/2$.
- 🔹 **Three wavelength windows:** 850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm (lowest attenuation).
- 🔹 **Sources:** LED (low cost, broad spectrum) and LASER (high power, narrow spectrum, long reach).
- 🔹 **Detectors:** PIN photodiode (simpler) and APD (Avalanche PD, higher sensitivity).
- 🔹 **Advantages over copper:** enormous bandwidth, low loss, EMI immunity, security, light weight.
- 🔹 **Disadvantages:** higher cost of source/detector, precision splicing/connectorization, fragility.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unguided Media: Radio Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unguided Media: Radio Waves # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unguided Media** refers to the wireless transmission medium in which data signals are propagated through the atmosphere (air, vacuum, or space) in the form of **electromagnetic (EM) waves**, without the use of a physical conductor. **Radio Waves** are a specific band of electromagnetic radiation used in communication systems, with frequencies ranging from **3 kHz to 300 GHz**, corresponding to wavelengths from **100 km down to 1 mm**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Radio Waves** fall under the *un-guided / wireless transmission* category of the OSI Physical Layer. Unlike guided media (twisted-pair, coaxial, optical fiber), radio waves **do not require a physical path**; instead, they radiate outward from a transmitting antenna and are picked up by a receiving antenna tuned to the same frequency band. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Lighthouse" Model** > Imagine you are standing on the top of a hill with a powerful flashlight (the **transmitting antenna**). When you switch on the flashlight, the light spreads outward in all directions in the form of invisible EM waves. A friend standing on another hill (the **receiving antenna**) holds a small mirror that can catch the light. If both friends agree on the "color" (i.e., the **frequency**) of light, the message is received. The atmosphere is the "air" through which the light travels — free, invisible, and unbounded. > - If the friend is **very far** → signal is **weak** (path loss). > - If a big **mountain comes in between** → signal is **blocked** (terrain obstruction). > - If the light is **focused into a beam** (using a parabolic dish) → it travels **further** (antenna gain). ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics The following physical constants and metrics are fundamental to radio wave analysis and are **frequently tested in KTU university examinations**: - Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s - Speed of EM wave in medium: $v = c / n$ (where $n$ is refractive index) - Wavelength-Frequency relationship: $\lambda = c / f$ - Typical Radio Band Span: **3 kHz – 300 GHz** - Antenna impedance standard: **50 Ω (commercial)** and **75 Ω (residential / TV)** ## 1.4 Why Radio Waves Matter in Data Communication > [!IMPORTANT] > Radio waves form the **backbone of all modern wireless data communication systems** — Wi-Fi (2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz), Bluetooth (2.4 GHz), 4G LTE (700 MHz – 2.6 GHz), 5G FR1 (sub-6 GHz), satellite communication (C, Ku, Ka bands), AM/FM radio broadcasting, and radar. Any data communication engineer must master radio wave propagation principles. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Electromagnetic Spectrum Position Radio waves occupy the **lowest frequency (longest wavelength)** region of the electromagnetic spectrum, sitting just above audio frequencies and below microwaves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. | Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength | Primary Use | |------|-----------------|------------|-------------| | VLF (Very Low Freq.) | 3 – 30 kHz | 100 – 10 km | Submarine communication | | LF (Low Freq.) | 30 – 300 kHz | 10 – 1 km | Navigation, RFID | | MF (Medium Freq.) | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 1 km – 100 m | AM radio broadcasting | | HF (High Freq.) | 3 – 30 MHz | 100 – 10 m | Shortwave radio, sky-wave | | VHF (Very High Freq.) | 30 – 300 MHz | 10 – 1 m | FM radio, TV, aviation | | UHF (Ultra High Freq.) | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 m – 10 cm | Mobile, Wi-Fi, GPS | | SHF (Super High Freq.) | 3 – 30 GHz | 10 – 1 cm | Satellite, radar, 5G | | EHF (Extremely High Freq.) | 30 – 300 GHz | 1 cm – 1 mm | mmWave 5G, radio astronomy | ## 2.2 The Three Propagation Modes of Radio Waves This is the **most heavily tested conceptual section** in KTU 2024 Scheme board exams. ### (A) Ground-Wave Propagation - Follows the **curvature of the Earth** by hugging the ground. - Effective for **low and medium frequencies** (LF, MF — up to ~2 MHz). - Works because the wave induces currents in the Earth's surface, which then re-radiate the wave. - **Why?** The Earth's surface behaves like a conductor at low frequencies; the wave "clings" to the surface. - Used in: **AM radio broadcasting**, maritime communication, navigation beacons. - Range: Up to a few hundred kilometers. ### (B) Sky-Wave Propagation (Ionospheric Propagation) - The radio wave is **bounced off the ionosphere** (a layer of charged particles, 60 – 400 km above Earth). - Effective for **HF band (3 – 30 MHz)**. - The ionosphere refracts the wave back to Earth, enabling **very long-distance (intercontinental) communication**. - **Why?** At HF, the wave is not absorbed by the ionosphere but is bent back due to refractive index gradients. - **Skip distance** is the minimum distance from the transmitter at which the sky wave returns to Earth. - **Maximum usable frequency (MUF)** and **critical frequency** are key parameters. - Used in: **Shortwave radio, international broadcasting, amateur radio**. ### (C) Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation - The wave travels in a **straight line** from transmitter to receiver. - Effective for **VHF and above (> 30 MHz)**. - Antennas must be within visual horizon of each other. - **Why?** At high frequencies, the ionosphere does not refract the wave; it passes through. Ground waves are also rapidly attenuated. - Limited by Earth's curvature: $d \approx 3.57 \sqrt{h}$ km, where $h$ is antenna height in meters. - Used in: **TV, FM, cellular, satellite, radar, Wi-Fi**. ## 2.3 Key Engineering Parameters - **Antenna Gain (G):** Ratio of power radiated in a specific direction to the power radiated by an isotropic (ideal) antenna. Measured in **dBi**. - **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP):** $EIRP = P_t \times G_t$ (in Watts) or $EIRP_{dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi}$. - **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL):** The attenuation of the EM signal as it travels through free space. - **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of received signal power to noise power. - **Bandwidth (B):** Range of frequencies occupied by the signal (Hz). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU university exam question on radio waves.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | meters | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | | 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (numeric) | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength | | 3 | FSPL in dB | $FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | | 4 | Friis Transmission Equation | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}$ | Watts | $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains (linear) | | 5 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t \times G_t$ | Watts | In dB: $EIRP_{dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi}$ | | 6 | LOS Range (Earth curvature) | $d \approx 3.57 \sqrt{h}$ | km | $h$ = antenna height in m | | 7 | SNR | $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$ | dB | $P_s$ = signal, $P_n$ = noise power | | 8 | Noise Power | $P_n = kTB$ | Watts | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann) | | 9 | Antenna Effective Area | $A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^{2} G}{4 \pi}$ | m² | For parabolic dish of gain $G$ | | 10 | Power received (Friis alt form) | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t A_e}{4 \pi d^{2}}$ | Watts | When one antenna is isotropic | > [!IMPORTANT] > In all the above, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value operations; in KTU answer scripts, simply write $\vert x \vert$ notation in math. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Radio wave principles are the foundation of: - **Cellular network planning** (link budget calculations, cell tower placement) - **Satellite link design** (EIRP budgeting, G/T figure of merit) - **Wi-Fi deployment** (FSPL drives access point placement) - **Radar cross-section estimation** (military and aviation) - **IoT and 5G mmWave cell planning** (line-of-sight dependency at 28 / 39 GHz) # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) From First Principles **Goal:** Show that the signal power density falls off with the square of the distance from an isotropic radiator. **Assumptions:** A point source radiates uniformly in all directions (isotropic antenna). The total radiated power $P_t$ spreads over the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $d$ centered at the source. **Step 1 — Surface area of the sphere:** The surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is $4 \pi d^{2}$. **Step 2 — Power per unit area (Power Flux Density):** By conservation of energy, the radiated power is distributed uniformly over the sphere. Hence the power density (power per square meter) at distance $d$ is: $$ S = \dfrac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}} $$ *Explanation:* The power spreads outward, and as the sphere's radius grows, its surface area grows as $d^{2}$, so the flux density falls as $1/d^{2}$. **Step 3 — Power captured by a receiving antenna:** The receiving antenna has an effective aperture area $A_e$. The power it captures is: $$ P_r = S \times A_e = \dfrac{P_t A_e}{4 \pi d^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute antenna effective area:** For an isotropic receiving antenna ($G_r = 1$): $$ A_e = \dfrac{\lambda^{2} G_r}{4 \pi} = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{4 \pi} $$ **Step 5 — Final form (Friis Equation, $G_r = 1$):** $$ P_r = \dfrac{P_t \lambda^{2}}{(4 \pi)^{2} d^{2}} $$ **Step 6 — Define Free-Space Path Loss:** The ratio of transmitted to received power for isotropic antennas: $$ FSPL = \dfrac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 7 — Convert to dB form:** $$ FSPL_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right) $$ Substituting $\lambda = c / f$ and using $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, the **KTU-board-friendly formula** becomes: $$ FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 $$ > [!NOTE] > The constant **32.44** arises as $20 \log_{10}(4 \pi \times 10^{3} / (3 \times 10^{8}) \times 10^{6}) = 20 \log_{10}(4 \pi / 300) \approx 20 \times (-1.378) \approx -27.56$ and the sign/scale conversion. *(Note: Different derivations yield 32.44 or 36.58 depending on units of d in km or m and f in MHz or kHz — always double-check unit conversion in the exam.)* ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Received Power via Friis Transmission Equation (with Antenna Gains) **Starting from:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2}$ **Step 1 — Convert each term to dB:** $$ P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB} $$ **Step 2 — Expand FSPL:** $$ P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - 20 \log_{10}(d) - 20 \log_{10}(f) - 32.44 $$ This is the **canonical link-budget equation** used in every cellular and satellite design. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Line-of-Sight (LOS) Maximum Range **Geometry:** Earth is a sphere of radius $R \approx 6371$ km. Two antennas of heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ are placed. The maximum line-of-sight distance $d$ is along the tangent to the Earth's surface. **Step 1 — Pythagoras theorem:** For antenna 1 of height $h_1$: $$ (R + h_1)^{2} = R^{2} + d_1^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Expand and simplify:** $$ R^{2} + 2 R h_1 + h_1^{2} = R^{2} + d_1^{2} \implies d_1^{2} \approx 2 R h_1 $$ (Since $h_1 \ll R$, the $h_1^{2}$ term is negligible.) **Step 3 — Solve for $d_1$:** $$ d_1 = \sqrt{2 R h_1} $$ **Step 4 — Total LOS distance** (sum from both antennas): $$ d = d_1 + d_2 = \sqrt{2 R h_1} + \sqrt{2 R h_2} $$ **Step 5 — Plug in $R = 6371$ km and $h$ in meters:** $$ d_{km} = \sqrt{2 \times 6371 \times h_{m} / 1000} = \sqrt{12.742 \, h_{m}} $$ **Step 6 — Numerical result:** $$ d \approx 3.57 \sqrt{h_{m}} \quad \text{km} $$ (For combined heights: $d \approx 3.57 (\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$ km.) ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Propagation Modes | Parameter | Ground Wave | Sky Wave | Line-of-Sight | |---|---|---|---| | Frequency band | LF, MF (< 2 MHz) | HF (3 – 30 MHz) | VHF & above (> 30 MHz) | | Mechanism | Earth conducts / refracts | Ionosphere reflects | Straight line | | Range | Hundreds of km | Thousands of km | ~ 50 – 100 km (terrestrial) | | Reliability | Stable, predictable | Varies with ionosphere (day/night) | Highly reliable, weather-dependent | | Typical use | AM radio, navigation | Shortwave, international broadcast | TV, mobile, satellite, Wi-Fi | | Limitation | Attenuates rapidly at higher freq. | Skip distance issue, ionospheric storms | Earth curvature, obstacles | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Radio Wave Propagation Modes (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD TX[Transmitting Antenna] RX[Receiving Antenna] TX --> MODE1 TX --> MODE2 TX --> MODE3 MODE1[Ground Wave
LF and MF up to 2 MHz] MODE2[Sky Wave
HF band 3 to 30 MHz] MODE3[Line of Sight
VHF and above 30 MHz] MODE1 --> EARTH[Earth Surface Conduction] EARTH --> RX MODE2 --> IONO[Ionosphere 60 to 400 km Altitude] IONO --> REFL[Refraction Back to Earth] REFL --> RX MODE3 --> STRAIGHT[Direct Straight Line Path] STRAIGHT --> RX classDef txrx fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mode fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef medium fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class TX,RX txrx class MODE1,MODE2,MODE3 mode class EARTH,IONO,REFL,STRAIGHT medium ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Electromagnetic Spectrum Position of Radio Waves ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Extremely Low Freq
below 3 kHz] B[Radio Waves
3 kHz to 300 GHz] C[Microwaves
300 GHz to 300 GHz overlap] D[Infrared
300 GHz to 400 THz] E[Visible Light
400 THz to 750 THz] F[Ultraviolet
750 THz to 30 PHz] G[X Rays
30 PHz to 30 EHz] H[Gamma Rays
above 30 EHz] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H classDef radio fill:#f5b041,stroke:#7e5109,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef other fill:#d6dbdf,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class B radio class A,C,D,E,F,G,H other ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Radio Link Block Diagram (Transmitter to Receiver) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Data Source] --> ENC[Encoder and Modulator] ENC --> PA[Power Amplifier] PA --> TXA[Transmitting Antenna] TXA --> CH[Free Space Channel
with Path Loss and Noise] CH --> RXA[Receiving Antenna] RXA --> LNA[Low Noise Amplifier] LNA --> DEC[Demodulator and Decoder] DEC --> DST[Data Destination] NOISE[Thermal Noise Source kTB] -.-> CH classDef active fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pass fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef noise fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class SRC,ENC,PA,TXA,RXA,LNA,DEC,DST active class CH pass class NOISE noise ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Radio Frequency Band Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD RW[Radio Waves 3 kHz to 300 GHz] RW --> VLF1[VLF 3 to 30 kHz] RW --> LF1[LF 30 to 300 kHz] RW --> MF1[MF 300 kHz to 3 MHz] RW --> HF1[HF 3 to 30 MHz] RW --> VHF1[VHF 30 to 300 MHz] RW --> UHF1[UHF 300 MHz to 3 GHz] RW --> SHF1[SHF 3 to 30 GHz] RW --> EHF1[EHF 30 to 300 GHz] VLF1 --> U1[Submarine Comm] LF1 --> U2[Navigation and RFID] MF1 --> U3[AM Radio] HF1 --> U4[Shortwave and Sky Wave] VHF1 --> U5[FM Radio and TV] UHF1 --> U6[Mobile WiFi GPS] SHF1 --> U7[Satellite Radar 5G] EHF1 --> U8[mmWave 5G Radio Astronomy] classDef root fill:#d4ac0d,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef band fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef use fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class RW root class VLF1,LF1,MF1,HF1,VHF1,UHF1,SHF1,EHF1 band class U1,U2,U3,U4,U5,U6,U7,U8 use ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define unguided media. List three types of unguided media used in data communication.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Unguided media is the transmission medium in which data signals are propagated through the atmosphere (air, vacuum, or free space) in the form of electromagnetic waves, without using any physical conductor or waveguide. **Three types (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):** 1. **Radio waves** (3 kHz – 300 GHz) — omnidirectional, used in broadcasting, mobile, Wi-Fi. 2. **Microwaves** (1 GHz – 300 GHz) — directional, used in point-to-point links, satellite, radar. 3. **Infrared waves** (300 GHz – 400 THz) — short-range, line-of-sight, used in remote controls and IrDA. **Concluding note (0.5 marks):** All three are sub-bands of the electromagnetic spectrum used for wireless data transfer. --- ### **Q2. What is ground wave propagation? Mention the frequency range in which it is effective.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Ground wave propagation is a mode of radio wave propagation in which the electromagnetic wave travels along the curvature of the Earth's surface. The wave induces alternating currents in the ground, which then re-radiate the signal, allowing it to follow the Earth's contour. This mode is dominant in the **LF (30 – 300 kHz)** and **MF (300 kHz – 2 MHz)** bands. **Why effective (1 mark):** At low frequencies, the Earth's surface acts as a good conductor; the wave is not significantly attenuated over a few hundred kilometers. Above 2 MHz, ground conductivity loss becomes excessive, and the wave radiates skyward. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer either Question A OR Question B.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Explain the three propagation modes of radio waves with suitable diagrams and examples. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Radio waves, being EM radiation, propagate from the transmitting antenna to the receiving antenna via three principal mechanisms, depending on the operating frequency, the antenna's elevation, and the Earth's ionospheric conditions. **(i) Ground-Wave Propagation (2 marks):** - The radio wave travels along the surface of the Earth. - Frequency band: **LF and MF (< 2 MHz)**. - Mechanism: Earth acts as a conductor; wave is guided by surface currents. - Examples: **AM radio broadcasting**, maritime communication, navigation beacons. - Range: Up to a few hundred kilometers. **(ii) Sky-Wave (Ionospheric) Propagation (2 marks):** - The wave is refracted (bent) back to Earth by the **ionosphere** (60 – 400 km altitude, contains ionized gas layers D, E, F1, F2). - Frequency band: **HF (3 – 30 MHz)**. - Mechanism: Refractive index gradient in the ionosphere bends the wave; below MUF it reflects, above MUF it passes through. - Examples: **International shortwave broadcasting, amateur (ham) radio, military HF communication**. - Range: Thousands of kilometers (intercontinental). **(iii) Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation (2 marks):** - The wave travels in a straight line from transmitter to receiver. - Frequency band: **VHF and above (> 30 MHz)**. - Mechanism: At VHF+, the wave passes through the ionosphere; Earth curvature limits range. - Examples: **TV transmission, FM radio, cellular, satellite links, Wi-Fi, radar**. - LOS range formula: $d \approx 3.57 (\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$ km, where $h_1, h_2$ are antenna heights in metres. --- #### **(b) Derive the Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) in dB. A transmitter sends 10 W at 900 MHz. Calculate the received power at 5 km assuming isotropic antennas. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]` **Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 marks):** Consider an isotropic radiator radiating $P_t$ watts uniformly. At distance $d$, the power is distributed over a spherical surface of area $4 \pi d^{2}$. The power flux density is: $$ S = \dfrac{P_t}{4 \pi d^{2}} $$ For an isotropic receiving antenna with effective aperture $A_e = \lambda^{2} / (4 \pi)$: $$ P_r = S \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t \lambda^{2}}{(4 \pi)^{2} d^{2}} $$ Hence, the **Free Space Path Loss** is: $$ FSPL = \dfrac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} $$ In decibels: $$ FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 $$ (where $d$ is in km, $f$ in MHz, with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s). **Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $P_t = 10$ W $= 10$ dBW, $f = 900$ MHz, $d = 5$ km, $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic). **Step 1 — Compute FSPL:** $$ FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(900) + 32.44 $$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(5) = 20 \times 0.699 = 13.98 \text{ dB} $$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(900) = 20 \times 2.954 = 59.08 \text{ dB} $$ $$ FSPL_{dB} = 13.98 + 59.08 + 32.44 = 105.50 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Received Power:** $$ P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} - FSPL_{dB} = 10 - 105.50 = -95.50 \text{ dBW} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to Watts:** $$ P_r = 10^{-95.50/10} = 10^{-9.55} \approx 2.82 \times 10^{-10} \text{ W} $$ **Final Answer:** $P_r \approx -95.5$ dBW or **0.282 nW**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a radio communication link. State the Friis transmission equation. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid Block 3 in Section 4.3. The major blocks are: 1. **Information source** → produces baseband data. 2. **Encoder + Modulator** → converts bits to passband signal. 3. **Power Amplifier (PA)** → boosts the signal for transmission. 4. **Transmitting Antenna** → radiates EM wave into free space. 5. **Free-space channel** → introduces path loss, fading, and noise. 6. **Receiving Antenna** → captures the EM wave. 7. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** → amplifies weak received signal with minimal added noise. 8. **Demodulator + Decoder** → recovers the original data. 9. **Destination** → receives the recovered data. **Friis Transmission Equation (4 marks):** $$ P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \pi d}\right)^{2} $$ where: - $P_r$ = received power (W) - $P_t$ = transmitted power (W) - $G_t, G_r$ = gains of transmitting and receiving antennas (linear, dimensionless) - $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) - $d$ = distance between antennas (m) **In dB form (1 mark included above):** $$ P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB} $$ **Assumption:** Antennas are in free space, polarization matched, far-field condition satisfied ($d \gg \lambda$ and $d \gg 2D^{2}/\lambda$). --- #### **(b) An antenna of gain 20 dBi is used for transmission at 2.4 GHz with a transmitter power of 1 W. If a receiver with 15 dBi gain is placed 2 km away (line-of-sight), find the received power. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $f = 2.4$ GHz $= 2400$ MHz - $P_t = 1$ W $= 0$ dBW - $G_t = 20$ dBi - $G_r = 15$ dBi - $d = 2$ km **Step 1 — Compute Free-Space Path Loss:** $$ FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 $$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB} $$ $$ 20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.380 = 67.61 \text{ dB} $$ $$ FSPL_{dB} = 6.02 + 67.61 + 32.44 = 106.07 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Friis equation in dB form:** $$ P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB} $$ $$ P_{r,dBW} = 0 + 20 + 15 - 106.07 = -71.07 \text{ dBW} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to Watts:** $$ P_r = 10^{-71.07/10} = 10^{-7.107} \approx 7.82 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} $$ **Final Answer:** $P_r \approx -71.07$ dBW or **78.2 nW**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Radio Wave Questions:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency in FSPL formula** — Mixing $d$ in km with $f$ in Hz produces a wrong constant. KTU expects the **32.44 constant with d in km and f in MHz**. Always re-check units. > 2. **Confusing dBi with dBd** — dBi is relative to isotropic; dBd is relative to a half-wave dipole. Relation: **dBi = dBd + 2.15**. Examiners deduct for this. > 3. **Forgetting the Earth-curvature LOS factor** — In a long link, students often use Euclidean distance but ignore the curvature formula. LOS range is $3.57 \sqrt{h}$ km. > 4. **Sign errors in Friis equation in dB form** — The path loss term is **subtracted** in dB; students sometimes add it. > 5. **Confusing sky-wave with line-of-sight** — Sky-wave is for HF (3–30 MHz); LOS is for VHF and above. Examiners check this distinction rigorously. > 6. **Skipping assumptions** in Friis derivation — Always state *far-field, aligned polarization, line-of-sight, no obstructions*. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Unguided media** = wireless EM wave transmission (no physical conductor). - **Radio waves** span **3 kHz to 300 GHz**, all in the EM spectrum. - **Speed** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; $\lambda = c / f$. - **Three propagation modes:** - **Ground wave** — LF/MF, hugs Earth, < 2 MHz. - **Sky wave** — HF, reflects off ionosphere, 3 – 30 MHz. - **Line-of-sight** — VHF+, straight line, > 30 MHz. - **Antenna gain** is in dBi (vs isotropic) or dBd (vs dipole): **dBi = dBd + 2.15**. - **EIRP** = $P_t \times G_t$ (W) or $P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi}$ (dBW). - **FSPL (numeric):** $(4 \pi d / \lambda)^{2}$. - **FSPL (dB):** $20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ with $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz. - **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4 \pi d)^{2}$. - **Friis in dB:** $P_{r,dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB}$. - **LOS range:** $d \approx 3.57 (\sqrt{h_1} + \sqrt{h_2})$ km. - **Bands:** VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF — memorize rough frequency boundaries. - **Noise power:** $P_n = kTB$, $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - **SNR (dB):** $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$. - **Free-space assumptions:** no obstacles, no multipath, matched polarization, far-field. - **Common applications:** AM (MF ground wave), shortwave (HF sky wave), TV/Wi-Fi/cellular (VHF/UHF LOS), satellite (SHF LOS), mmWave 5G (EHF LOS). - **Always write units in the exam** — KTU examiners award partial credit for correct units even if the numerical answer is off. - **Sketch a block diagram** whenever a long answer (≥ 7 marks) is asked — marks are reserved for diagrams in the KTU marking scheme. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as — (i) **Diagram (2–3 marks)**, (ii) **Definitions (2 marks)**, (iii) **Theory (4–5 marks)**, (iv) **Numerical / Formula derivation (4–5 marks)**. Examiners follow this mental rubric.

topics.title: Microwaves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Microwaves ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Data and Computer Communication** (KTU 2024 Scheme – PECCT751), **Microwaves** refer to a band of electromagnetic (EM) radiation whose frequencies lie in the range of approximately **1 GHz to 300 GHz**, corresponding to wavelengths from **30 cm down to 1 mm**. This places microwaves in the **UHF (Ultra High Frequency), SHF (Super High Frequency), and EHF (Extremely High Frequency)** bands of the radio spectrum. In telecommunication engineering, microwaves are predominantly used for **line-of-sight (LoS) point-to-point communication**, where highly directional parabolic (dish) antennas focus the EM energy into a narrow beam directed at a distant receiver. Because the wavelength is so short, the antenna can be made physically small yet still achieve very high gain and sharp directivity — a property that is impractical at lower (VHF/UHF-television) frequencies. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Microwaves are introduced as a guided/un-guided transmission medium, with emphasis on the physical band definition, propagation behaviour, antenna systems (parabolic dish, horn), repeater-based long-haul link design, and a comparison with twisted-pair, coaxial, and fibre-optic media. ### 1.2 Standard Frequency Bands Used in Microwave Communication The IEEE / ITU-R standard microwave bands, which are *highly expected* in KTU 2-mark/3-mark questions, are listed below. | Band | Frequency Range | Wavelength Range | Typical Application | |:-----|:---------------:|:----------------:|:--------------------| | **L band** | 1 – 2 GHz | 30 – 15 cm | Mobile, GPS, telemetry | | **S band** | 2 – 4 GHz | 15 – 7.5 cm | Radar, satellite, Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz) | | **C band** | 4 – 8 GHz | 7.5 – 3.75 cm | Satellite TV, long-haul terrestrial links | | **X band** | 8 – 12 GHz | 3.75 – 2.5 cm | Radar, satellite comms | | **Ku band** | 12 – 18 GHz | 2.5 – 1.67 cm | DBS TV, VSAT | | **K band** | 18 – 26.5 GHz | 1.67 – 1.13 cm | Radar, satellite uplinks | | **Ka band** | 26.5 – 40 GHz | 1.13 – 0.75 cm | High-throughput satellite (HTS) | ### 1.3 Physical Constants to Remember > [!NOTE] > - **Speed of light in free space, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** > - **Impedance of free space, η₀ = 377 Ω (≈ 120π Ω)** > - **Boltzmann constant, k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a large playground at night with a friend **100 metres** away, and you want to send a message: - If you use a **lantern** (omnidirectional light), the light spreads out in every direction, and only a tiny fraction reaches your friend — most of the energy is wasted illuminating the trees, the ground, and the sky. - If you use a **torchlight / flashlight** (directional light), almost all the energy is concentrated into a narrow cone aimed at your friend, and the message is delivered clearly even with a small battery. A **microwave link works exactly like the torchlight**. The parabolic dish acts as the "reflector" of the torch, focusing the EM energy into a narrow pencil-beam aimed at the distant receiving dish. This is why microwave links are also called **"line-of-sight"** links — the two dishes must be able to "see" each other, just like you and your friend must be visible to each other for the torchlight to work. > [!TIP] > The shorter the wavelength (higher the frequency), the **narrower** the beam you can form with a dish of a given physical size. This is why 6 GHz terrestrial links and 14/12 GHz satellite links can carry enormous amounts of data (hundreds of Mbps) using antennas only 1–3 metres across. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Microwaves? — The Physical Motivation Lower-frequency radio waves (VHF, MF) suffer from several limitations that make them unsuitable for high-capacity, long-distance, point-to-point data links: 1. **Antenna size:** At 100 MHz, a half-wave dipole is **1.5 m long**; at 10 GHz, a parabolic dish of similar gain is only **~0.3 m in diameter**. Higher frequency → smaller antenna. 2. **Bandwidth:** Information capacity is roughly proportional to bandwidth. Microwaves offer **GHz of bandwidth**, while MF radio offers only kHz. 3. **Directionality:** A dish of diameter D operating at wavelength λ has a beamwidth of order **λ/D**. At microwaves, λ/D is small → sharp beam → little interference, low power, secure. 4. **Penetration of ionosphere:** Below ~30 MHz, waves bounce off the ionosphere; microwaves pass straight through, enabling **space and satellite communication**. ### 2.2 Microwave Propagation Characteristics Microwave propagation is governed by **line-of-sight (LoS)** physics. The signal travels in a nearly straight line from the transmitting antenna to the receiving antenna. The dominant propagation effects are: - **Free-space loss** (spreading of the spherical wavefront) — the *primary* loss mechanism. - **Atmospheric attenuation** (rain fade, gaseous absorption) — significant above ~10 GHz. - **Reflection, refraction and diffraction** at obstacles and the ground. - **Fresnel zone clearance** — the elliptical region around the direct ray must be kept free of obstructions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 – Key Concept:** Microwave energy does *not* bend around the curvature of the Earth. Therefore, for long terrestrial hops, **repeater stations** are placed every 40–60 km, each with a tall tower and two dishes (one facing the previous tower, one facing the next). ### 2.3 Microwave Antenna Fundamentals The two antenna types most commonly asked in KTU exams are the **parabolic reflector (dish)** and the **horn antenna**. #### 2.3.1 Parabolic Reflector Antenna A paraboloid of revolution focuses a spherical wave from a feed-horn at its focus into a **plane wave** propagating along the axis. The mathematical surface is given by: $$y = \frac{x^{2}}{4f}$$ where **f** is the focal length and **x** is the radial distance from the axis. **Key parameters:** - **Diameter of the dish:** D - **Focal length:** f - **F/D ratio:** controls the shape (deep vs. shallow dish) - **Aperture area:** A = π(D/2)² #### 2.3.2 Horn Antenna A horn antenna is essentially a flared waveguide. It is used both as a **feed** for a parabolic dish and as a **stand-alone antenna** for shorter links. It has moderate gain and very wide bandwidth. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **must-know** formula set for any KTU numerical on microwaves. Memorise the units and the conditions of validity. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Validity / Notes | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:-----------------| | 1 | Wavelength–Frequency relation | $\lambda = c / f$ | m | c = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | 2 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ (linear)
or in dB: $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dimensionless / dB | d ≫ λ; isotropic antennas assumed | | 3 | Friis Transmission Equation | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless | Valid in far field (Fraunhofer region) | | 4 | Antenna Gain (parabolic dish, efficiency η) | $G = \eta \left(\dfrac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless (dBi) | 0.5 ≤ η ≤ 0.7 typical | | 5 | Half-Power Beam Width (HPBW) | $HPBW \approx 70 \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | degrees | Parabolic dish approximation | | 6 | Effective Isotropic Radiated Power | $EIRP = P_t G_t$ | W (or dBW) | Measured at the antenna feed input | | 7 | Received Power (link budget) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | W | Direct form of Friis | | 8 | Received Power in dB form | $P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} + G_{t,dBi} + G_{r,dBi} - FSPL_{dB} - L_{other,dB}$ | dBm | L_other = cables, connectors, rain | | 9 | Antenna Aperture / Effective Area | $A_e = \dfrac{G \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$ | m² | Reciprocity of gain and area | | 10 | Far-Field (Fraunhofer) distance | $d_{far} = \dfrac{2 D^{2}}{\lambda}$ | m | Minimum distance for Friis to be valid | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Microwave links form the **backbone of mobile telephone networks** (the connection between cell towers and the core network), **rural broadband** (where laying fibre is uneconomical), **private enterprise networks** (banks, oil pipelines, railways), and the **uplink/downlink of communication satellites**. Every time you make a mobile call, your voice is very likely to traverse at least one microwave hop before it reaches the wired (or fibre) backbone. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) **Statement:** When an ideal isotropic radiator radiates power **P_t** uniformly in all directions, the power density at distance **d** is reduced because the same power is spread over the surface of an ever-expanding sphere of radius **d**. **Step 1:** Surface area of a sphere of radius d is $$S = 4\pi d^{2}$$ **Step 2:** Power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) at distance d is $$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^{2}\right]$$ **Step 3:** If the receiving antenna has an effective aperture area A_e, the power captured is $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, A_e}{4\pi d^{2}}$$ **Step 4:** For an isotropic receiving antenna, A_e = λ² ⁄ (4π). Substituting, $$P_r = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} = P_t \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 5:** Therefore the **Free-Space Path Loss** (ratio P_t / P_r) is $$FSPL = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 6:** In decibels, taking 10·log₁₀ of both sides, $$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$ **Step 7:** Converting d to km and f to MHz (so that the constants simplify cleanly) and using c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, we obtain the engineer-friendly form $$FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$ > [!TIP] > The "32.44" constant comes from $20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \times 10^{6}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi/300) \approx 32.44$. This derivation is a **favourite KTU 7-mark derivation**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Friis Transmission Equation (Including Antenna Gains) **Statement:** Most practical links use directional antennas at both ends. The Friis equation extends FSPL by including transmitter and receiver gains. **Step 1:** Replace the isotropic transmit antenna (radiating P_t) with a directional antenna of gain G_t. The new power density in the boresight direction is $$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$ **Step 2:** The receiving antenna of effective aperture A_{e,r} captures power $$P_r = S \cdot A_{e,r} = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, A_{e,r}}{4\pi d^{2}}$$ **Step 3:** Use the reciprocity relation A_{e,r} = G_r λ² ⁄ (4π) (this is a direct consequence of the antenna being the same whether transmitting or receiving): $$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} = P_t \, G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 4:** Rearranged, this is the celebrated Friis transmission equation $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The Friis equation assumes **far-field (Fraunhofer) conditions**, i.e. d ≥ 2D²/λ. It also assumes **aligned, polarisation-matched, unobstructed** antennas. It is the foundation of every microwave link budget. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parabolic-Dish Gain **Step 1:** The physical aperture area of a parabolic dish of diameter D is $$A_{phys} = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$ **Step 2:** Because of spillover, blockage by the feed, surface errors, etc., only a fraction **η** (aperture efficiency) of the physical area is effectively used. Thus the effective aperture is $$A_e = \eta \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$ **Step 3:** Using the reciprocity relation A_e = G λ² ⁄ (4π), solve for G: $$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda^{2}} \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$ **Step 4:** In decibels (dBi — decibels relative to an isotropic radiator), $$G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(\eta) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > For a typical high-performance dish, **η ≈ 0.55–0.65**. If the problem states "ideal" or does not specify efficiency, take **η = 1** (i.e. 100 %). ### 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem (Model KTU 14-mark Part-a style):** A microwave link operates at **6 GHz** with a transmitter power of **5 W**, a transmit dish of diameter **1.2 m**, a receive dish of diameter **1.8 m**, and a hop length of **50 km**. Take aperture efficiency **η = 0.55**. Compute the received power in dBm. (Assume ideal alignment, no other losses.) **Solution:** **Step A — Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^{8})/(6 \times 10^{9}) = 0.05$ m = 5 cm **Step B — Transmit gain in dBi:** $G_t = 0.55 \times (\pi \times 1.2 / 0.05)^{2} = 0.55 \times (75.398)^{2} = 0.55 \times 5684.93 = 3126.71$ $G_{t,dBi} = 10\log_{10}(3126.71) \approx 34.95$ dBi **Step C — Receive gain in dBi:** $G_r = 0.55 \times (\pi \times 1.8 / 0.05)^{2} = 0.55 \times (113.10)^{2} = 0.55 \times 12791.4 = 7035.27$ $G_{r,dBi} = 10\log_{10}(7035.27) \approx 38.47$ dBi **Step D — Free-space path loss:** $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(6000)$ $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 33.98 + 75.56 = 141.98$ dB **Step E — Transmit power in dBm:** $P_{t,dBm} = 10\log_{10}(5000) \approx 36.99$ dBm **Step F — Received power (Friis in dB form):** $P_{r,dBm} = 36.99 + 34.95 + 38.47 - 141.98 = -31.57$ dBm **Answer:** P_r ≈ **−31.6 dBm** (which is well above the typical receiver threshold of −80 dBm, confirming a healthy link). ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Microwave vs Other Media | Parameter | Twisted Pair | Coaxial | Microwave (Free-Space) | Optical Fibre | |:----------|:------------:|:-------:|:----------------------:|:-------------:| | Typical Bandwidth | < 100 MHz | < 500 MHz | up to ~ 1 GHz (per channel) | THz regime | | Attenuation | High | Moderate | FSPL dominated | Very low (0.2 dB/km) | | Interference susceptibility | High | Moderate | Low (narrow beam) | Negligible | | Security | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate (narrow beam) | High | | Installation cost/km | Very low | Moderate | Moderate (towers) | High | | Repeater spacing | 100 m – 2 km | 1 – 10 km | 40 – 60 km | 50 – 100 km | | Mobility-friendly | No | No | **Yes** (point-to-point flexible) | No | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Terrestrial Microwave Communication Link The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end architecture of a single-hop terrestrial microwave link, including the transmitter chain, free-space channel, and receiver chain. This is the architecture you would draw in a KTU 14-mark "Explain the microwave communication system" question. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Data Source
Ethernet / E1] --> B[Baseband Processor
Modulation / Coding] B --> C[IF Up-Converter
70 MHz to RF] C --> D[High-Power Amplifier
HPA] D --> E[Tx Waveguide Feed] E --> F[Parabolic Dish
Transmit] G((Free Space
LoS Path)) --- F G --- H[Parabolic Dish
Receive] H --> I[Low-Noise Amplifier
LNA] I --> J[IF Down-Converter
RF to 70 MHz] J --> K[Baseband Processor
Demod / Decode] K --> L[Data Sink
LAN / PSTN] style A fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1 style L fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1 style F fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style H fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309 style G fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **left** chain is the **transmitter** (bottom-up): data → baseband → IF → RF → HPA → dish. - The **right** chain is the **receiver** (top-down): dish → LNA → down-converter → baseband → sink. - The **red ellipse** in the middle is the free-space LoS channel — the actual "microwave transmission medium" being studied. - **HPA** and **LNA** are the two most critical RF blocks; together they determine the noise figure and hence the receiver sensitivity. ### 4.2 Repeater (Relay) Topology for a Long-Haul Microwave Link ```mermaid flowchart LR N1[Node 1
City A Tower] -- Hop 1
50 km --> R1[Repeater 1
Tower 1] R1 -- Hop 2
50 km --> R2[Repeater 2
Tower 2] R2 -- Hop 3
50 km --> R3[Repeater 3
Tower 3] R3 -- Hop 4
50 km --> N2[Node 2
City B Tower] style R1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style R2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d style R3 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d ``` **Note:** A **repeater** in a microwave link is typically a **back-to-back** assembly of a receive dish + LNA + down-converter + up-converter + HPA + transmit dish, all mounted on the same tower. The signal is regenerated at IF (not at baseband) — this is called an **IF repeater** or **heterodyne repeater**. A **regenerative repeater** does demodulation → re-modulation at baseband and is used where bit-error-rate improvement is needed. ### 4.3 Microwave Antenna Radiation Pattern (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ANT[Parabolic Dish Antenna] direction LR FEED[Feed Horn
at Focus] -->|Spherical Wave| DISH[Paraboloid Reflector] DISH -->|Plane Wave| BORESIGHT[/Boresight Direction/] end MAIN[Main Lobe
Narrow Pencil Beam] -.->|Max Gain G| BORESIGHT SIDE1[Side Lobe 1] -.-> BORESIGHT SIDE2[Side Lobe 2] -.-> BORESIGHT BACK[Back Lobe] -.-> BORESIGHT style MAIN fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16653 style SIDE1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style SIDE2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style BACK fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` > [!TIP] > KTU exam tip: When asked to "sketch the radiation pattern of a parabolic dish antenna", always mark the **half-power beam width (HPBW) between the −3 dB points** of the main lobe, and label at least the **first side lobe**. The 3-D beamwidth is roughly circular, so the HPBW is the same in both E- and H-planes for a circular dish. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Define microwaves. Mention any four advantages of microwave communication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** *Definition (1 mark):* Microwaves are electromagnetic waves whose frequencies lie in the range **1 GHz to 300 GHz**, with corresponding wavelengths from **30 cm to 1 mm**. *Advantages (½ mark each, any four):* 1. **Large bandwidth** — GHz of spectrum available, enabling very high data rates. 2. **Small antenna size** — for a given gain, dish diameter scales linearly with wavelength; at microwaves, dishes of 1–3 m suffice. 3. **High directivity and gain** — narrow beam → less interference, better security, lower transmit power. 4. **Line-of-sight propagation** — predictable coverage; no ionospheric fading as in HF. 5. **Penetration of the ionosphere** — making satellite communication possible. 6. **Cost-effective for terrain where laying cable is impractical** (hills, rivers, deserts). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]** **Explain the working of a parabolic reflector antenna with a neat diagram.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A parabolic reflector (dish) antenna consists of a **paraboloidal reflecting surface** and a **small feed horn** placed at the **focus** of the paraboloid. *Working (2 marks):* When a spherical electromagnetic wave from the feed horn strikes the parabolic surface, the law of reflection (equal angles of incidence and reflection) causes all the reflected rays to emerge as a **parallel, collimated plane wave** along the dish's axis (boresight). Conversely, an incoming plane wave is focused back to the feed horn at the focus. This produces a highly directional, narrow pencil-beam pattern with very high gain. *Key formula (1 mark):* $G = \eta (\pi D / \lambda)^{2}$, with HPBW ≈ 70 λ / D degrees. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a) Derive the Free-Space Path Loss equation. State the engineering formula in dB and explain the role of each term. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Step 1 — Setup, 1 mark)* Consider an ideal isotropic antenna radiating power P_t uniformly in all directions. By symmetry, the power density at distance d is P_t divided by the surface area of the surrounding sphere of radius d. $$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}}$$ *(Step 2 — Receiver capture, 2 marks)* An isotropic receiving antenna has effective aperture A_e = λ² / (4π). The received power is therefore $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi} = P_t \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ *(Step 3 — Define FSPL, 1 mark)* The Free-Space Path Loss is the ratio P_t / P_r, which gives the loss that would be experienced by an ideal isotropic-to-isotropic link: $$FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$ *(Step 4 — Convert to dB, 2 marks)* Taking 10·log₁₀ of both sides, expanding, and substituting d in km and f in MHz, with c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, the constant 20·log₁₀(4π·10⁹/(3·10⁸)) collapses to **32.44**, giving the standard engineering form $$FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$ *(Step 5 — Role of each term, 1 mark)* The first term (32.44) is a constant arising from the unit conversion and 4π geometry. The second term (20·log₁₀ d) shows that path loss increases by **6 dB every time the distance doubles** (20·log₁₀ 2 = 6.02 dB). The third term (20·log₁� f) shows that loss also increases by **6 dB per octave** of frequency. This is why high-frequency links need high-gain antennas. --- **(b) State and derive the Friis transmission equation. A 6 GHz microwave link uses 1.5 m dishes at both ends, hop length 40 km, P_t = 10 W and η = 0.6. Compute the received power. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Statement (1 mark):* The Friis transmission equation gives the received power in a free-space, far-field, line-of-sight link in terms of transmit power, antenna gains, wavelength, and distance: $$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$ *Derivation (3 marks):* Start with the isotropic-radiated power density S = P_t / (4πd²). Replace the isotropic transmit antenna by a directional one of gain G_t, so S becomes P_t G_t / (4πd²). The receiving antenna of effective aperture A_{e,r} captures P_r = S·A_{e,r}. By the antenna reciprocity theorem, A_{e,r} = G_r λ² / (4π). Substituting and rearranging gives the Friis equation as above. The equation is valid only in the far field, d ≥ 2D²/λ. *Numerical computation (3 marks):* $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^{8})/(6 \times 10^{9}) = 0.05$ m $G_t = G_r = 0.6 \times (\pi \times 1.5/0.05)^{2} = 0.6 \times (94.248)^{2} = 0.6 \times 8882.6 = 5329.6$ $FSPL = (4\pi \times 40000 / 0.05)^{2} = (10.05 \times 10^{6})^{2} = 1.011 \times 10^{14}$ $P_r = 10 \times 5329.6 \times 5329.6 / (1.011 \times 10^{14}) = 2.81 \times 10^{-6}$ W $P_r \approx 2.81$ μW = **−55.5 dBm** --- #### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, Adapted]** **(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a terrestrial microwave communication system. Discuss the function of the HPA and LNA. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *Block diagram (3 marks):* The system consists of: **Data Source → Baseband Processor → Modulator → IF Up-Converter (70 MHz → RF) → HPA → Transmit Dish → Free-Space LoS Path → Receive Dish → LNA → IF Down-Converter (RF → 70 MHz) → Demodulator → Baseband Processor → Data Sink.** Repeaters are inserted every 40–60 km for long-haul links. *Working explanation (2 marks):* The baseband signal (digital/analog) modulates an IF carrier, which is then up-converted to the microwave RF, amplified, and radiated by the highly directional dish. After free-space propagation, the receive dish captures a small fraction of the transmitted power. The LNA boosts this weak signal *before* any major noise contribution. The down-converter returns it to IF for clean demodulation. *Function of HPA (1 mark):* The High-Power Amplifier (typically a TWT or solid-state GaAs PA) raises the RF power to the watt/kilowatt level needed to overcome the ~140 dB free-space path loss. It is the **final** amplifier in the transmit chain. *Function of LNA (1 mark):* The Low-Noise Amplifier (typically a GaAs HEMT or cooled parametric amp) is the **first** stage in the receiver. Its noise figure directly sets the overall receiver sensitivity — every 1 dB improvement in LNA noise figure reduces the minimum receivable signal by 1 dB. --- **(b) Compare microwave communication with optical-fibre communication on the basis of bandwidth, attenuation, security, repeater spacing, cost, and terrain flexibility. State two specific applications of microwave links.
