<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** State the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** and explain what happens if it is violated.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Nyquist Sampling Theorem states that a bandlimited analog signal with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

The minimum allowed rate, $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist rate**. If the theorem is violated (i.e., $f_s < 2 f_{max}$), the high-frequency spectral replicas overlap, producing an irreversible distortion called **aliasing**, in which high-frequency content appears as false low-frequency content. *(1 mark — theorem statement, 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — aliasing explanation.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Define **quantization error**. Derive the expression for the **maximum quantization error** in a uniform quantizer.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Quantization error is the difference between the actual sampled amplitude $v(t)$ and its quantized value $v_q(t)$. For a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ and $L = 2^n$ levels:

$$
e_q = v_q(t) - v(t)
$$

The maximum magnitude of the error occurs at the boundary between two adjacent levels and equals half the step size:

$$
\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta}{2} = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{2^{n+1}}
$$

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — setup, 1 mark — final expression.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the three fundamental steps involved in **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An analog signal bandlimited to **5 kHz** is sampled at **1.25 times the Nyquist rate** and quantized using a **10-bit uniform quantizer** with a peak-to-peak range of **8 V**. Calculate: (i) Sampling frequency, (ii) Quantization step size, (iii) Output bit rate, (iv) Quantization noise power, and (v) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio in dB. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Steps of PCM — Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Sampling (2 marks):** The continuous-time analog signal $v(t)$ is passed through a low-pass anti-aliasing filter to band-limit it to $f_{max}$. A Sample-and-Hold circuit then captures instantaneous amplitudes at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$, producing a PAM signal. The sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

2. **Quantization (2 marks):** Each sampled amplitude is rounded to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ is the number of bits. The step size is $\Delta = (V_{max} - V_{min})/2^n$. This process introduces quantization noise with mean-square value $\Delta^2/12$.

3. **Encoding (2 marks):** Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword (natural binary or Gray). The output is a serial bitstream with bit rate $R_b = f_s \times n$.

*(1 mark reserved for the block diagram: Anti-Alias Filter → Sample/Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → PCM Output, with a clock signal driving the timing.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $f_{max} = 5$ kHz, $f_s = 1.25 \times (2 f_{max}) = 1.25 \times 10$ kHz, $n = 10$, $V_{pp} = 8$ V.

**(i) Sampling frequency:**

$$
f_s = 1.25 \times 2 f_{max} = 1.25 \times 2 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 1.25 \times 10 \text{ kHz} = 12.5 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Quantization step size:**

$$
\Delta = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2^n} = \dfrac{8}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{8}{1024} = 7.8125 \text{ mV}
$$

**(iii) Output bit rate:**

$$
R_b = f_s \times n = 12{,}500 \times 10 = 125{,}000 \text{ bps} = 125 \text{ kbps}
$$

**(iv) Quantization noise power:**

$$
\overline{e_q^2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{12} = \dfrac{(7.8125 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \dfrac{6.104 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 5.087 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}^2
$$

**(v) SQNR in dB:**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 10 = 1.76 + 60.20 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **PCM** and **Delta Modulation (DM)**. Explain the concepts of **slope overload** and **granular noise** in Delta Modulation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of the **transmitter and receiver** of a Delta Modulation system. A DM system has step size $\Delta = 50$ mV and sampling period $T_s = 0.1$ ms. Determine the maximum amplitude and maximum frequency of a sinusoid that can be tracked without slope overload. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PCM vs DM (7 marks):**

- **PCM** encodes the *absolute* value of each sample using $n$ bits, giving a multi-level code per sample. **DM** encodes the *difference* between successive samples using **1 bit** ($+\Delta$ or $-\Delta$).
- PCM bit rate is $R_b = n f_s$; DM bit rate is $R_b = f_s$ (much higher oversampling required for DM to track signal changes).
- PCM complexity is higher (full A/D + multi-bit encoder); DM is simpler (a comparator and integrator).
- DM suffers from two distinctive errors:
  - **Slope Overload (2 marks):** Occurs when the analog signal changes faster than the DM step size can follow, i.e., $\vert dv/dt \vert_{max} > \Delta/T_s$. The staircase approximation lags behind the actual waveform, producing large distortion.
  - **Granular Noise (2 marks):** When the input is nearly constant (e.g., during silence), the DM output keeps toggling between $+\Delta$ and $-\Delta$, producing a noisy staircase of amplitude $\pm \Delta$ around the true value.
- **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)** varies $\Delta$ with signal slope to mitigate both issues.
- *(1 mark reserved for a comparison table or a clean block diagram.)*

**(b) DM Transmitter, Receiver, and Sinusoid Tracking (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):**
- **Transmitter:** Comparator → 1-bit quantizer ($\pm\Delta$) → Output bitstream. A local integrator generates the staircase estimate $\hat{v}(t)$ from past bits; the comparator computes $\text{sign}(v(t) - \hat{v}(t))$.
- **Receiver:** Integrator (accumulator) → Low-pass smoothing filter → Recovered analog.

**Sinusoid tracking (4 marks):**

Let the input be $v(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$. The maximum rate of change is:

$$
\left\vert \dfrac{dv}{dt} \right\vert_{max} = 2\pi f A
$$

For no slope overload, this must not exceed the maximum step slope $\Delta / T_s$:

$$
2\pi f A \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{T_s}
$$

**Rearrange for the boundary:**

$$
A \cdot f \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2\pi T_s}
$$

**Substitute values:** $\Delta = 50 \text{ mV} = 0.05 \text{ V}$, $T_s = 0.1 \text{ ms} = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$:

$$
A \cdot f \leq \dfrac{0.05}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{0.05}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 79.577 \text{ V·Hz}
$$

**Specific cases (showing the trade-off):**

- If $f = 1$ kHz, then $A \leq 0.0796$ V $\approx 79.6$ mV.
- If $A = 0.5$ V, then $f \leq 159.15$ Hz.
- If $f = 500$ Hz, then $A \leq 0.159$ V $\approx 159$ mV.

The **maximum-amplitude sinusoid** (at the lowest practical frequency) and **maximum-frequency sinusoid** (at the lowest practical amplitude) lie on the hyperbola $A \cdot f = 79.58$ V·Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1.76 dB" constant** in SQNR derivations → loses 1 mark. Always write the full form: $1.76 + 6.02n$.
> 2. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_{Nyquist}$**: $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ is the *minimum*, not the actual sampling rate. State explicitly which is being computed.
> 3. **Mixing up $\Delta$ and $V_{pp}$**: $\Delta$ is the *step*, $V_{pp}$ is the *full range*. Unit mismatch (mV vs V) is a frequent deduction.
> 4. **In DM problems, students often write $\Delta / T_s$ as the signal slope, then forget to include $2\pi$** in the derivative of a sinusoid. This is a 2-mark error.
> 5. **Aliasing is irreversible**: do *not* say it can be filtered out *after* sampling. Mitigation is the anti-alias filter *before* sampling.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert $T_s$ from ms to seconds** in numerical problems — common 0.5-mark slip.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Module 1)**

- ✅ ADC = **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**.
- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — never go below.
- ✅ **Aliasing** is irreversible; prevent it with an **anti-alias low-pass filter** *before* sampling.
- ✅ Quantization step: $\Delta = V_{pp} / 2^n$.
- ✅ Max quantization error magnitude: $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \Delta / 2$.
- ✅ Mean-square quantization noise: $\Delta^2 / 12$.
- ✅ **SQNR (dB)** for a full-range sine = $1.76 + 6.02n$ — the "*6 dB per bit*" rule.
- ✅ **PCM bit rate:** $R_b = f_s \times n$.
- ✅ **Standard telephony PCM:** 8 kHz × 8 bits = **64 kbps** (DS0 / G.711).
- ✅ **Standard CD audio:** 44.1 kHz × 16 bits × 2 channels = **1.411 Mbps**.
- ✅ **Companding (µ-law, A-law)** improves perceived SQNR for voice — uses non-uniform quantization.
- ✅ **Delta Modulation:** 1-bit encoder, samples *changes*; susceptible to **slope overload** and **granular noise**.
- ✅ **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM):** step size $\Delta$ varies with signal slope to overcome both DM noise types.
- ✅ **Nyquist bandwidth for PCM transmission:** $B \geq R_b / 2$ (NRZ signaling).
- ✅ Each extra bit of quantization ≈ +6 dB SQNR.
- ✅ For a sinusoid in DM: $A \cdot f \leq \Delta / (2\pi T_s)$ is the **no-overload boundary**.
- ✅ Aliasing, slope overload, and granular noise are the three classic **distortion phenomena** in ADC chains — know their cause, effect, and remedy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital-to-Analog Modulation (ASK)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital-to-Analog Modulation (ASK)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, also known as **Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK)** or **On-Off Keying (OOK)**, is a digital-to-analog modulation scheme in which the amplitude of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in accordance with the binary information signal, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "In ASK, the amplitude of the carrier signal is switched between two discrete levels (typically $A$ and $0$) in response to the binary data stream '1' and '0' respectively. The frequency and phase are held constant throughout the transmission."

Mathematically, the ASK modulated signal is represented as:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '1'} \\ 0 & \text{for binary '0'} \end{cases}$$

Or in compact form using the binary data signal $m(t)$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ represents the binary data, $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, and $f_c$ is the carrier frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Flashlight Telegraph:**
> Imagine you and a friend are communicating across a long distance at night using a flashlight. You agree on a simple rule: **flash ON = '1' and flashlight OFF = '0'**. The flashlight's position (phase) and color (frequency) are never changed — only its brightness (amplitude) toggles between two states.
>
> This is **exactly** how ASK works! The flashlight is the **carrier signal**, and the binary data controls whether the carrier is transmitted (binary 1) or suppressed (binary 0). The receiver simply checks "is the light on?" to recover the data.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the standard engineering metrics used to characterize any ASK system:

- **Bit Duration ($T_b$)** — Time taken to transmit one bit, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Bit Rate ($R_b$ or $S$)** — Number of bits transmitted per second, measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Baud Rate ($D$)** — Number of signal changes (symbols) per second, measured in **baud (Bd)**.
- **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$)** — Frequency of the unmodulated sinusoidal carrier, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Transmission Bandwidth ($B_T$)** — Spectral width required for transmission, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- **Energy per Bit ($E_b$)** — Energy contained in one bit duration, measured in **Joules (J)**.
- **Noise Spectral Density ($N_0/2$ or $N_0$)** — Two-sided or one-sided noise power per Hz, measured in **Watts/Hz**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** ASK is the simplest of all digital modulation schemes. It is **spectrally efficient in concept** but **highly susceptible to noise**, since noise directly affects amplitude. In real systems, ASK is rarely used for high-performance links — it is, however, the foundation for understanding more robust schemes like FSK and PSK.

## 1.4 Why Modulate? The Role of ASK in Communication

Low-frequency baseband signals cannot be transmitted efficiently over wireless channels because:
1. **Antenna Size:** Antennas must be at least $\lambda/10$ in size (where $\lambda = c/f$), making low-frequency antennas impractically large.
2. **Multiplexing:** Modulation allows multiple signals to share the medium using frequency division.
3. **Noise Immunity:** Higher frequencies suffer less from certain types of noise.

ASK provides a simple method to shift the baseband data spectrum to a higher frequency band centered at $f_c$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The generation of an ASK signal follows this structured flow:

**Step 1 — Generate the Binary Data Stream:**
A sequence of bits $b_n \in \{0, 1\}$ is produced by the source. This is the baseband NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) signal $m(t)$ with bit duration $T_b$.

**Step 2 — Generate the High-Frequency Carrier:**
A local oscillator produces a pure sinusoidal waveform $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with constant amplitude $A_c$ and constant frequency $f_c$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Product Modulation:**
The binary signal $m(t)$ multiplies the carrier. The result is:
- When $m(t) = 1$: the carrier is passed through with full amplitude $A_c$.
- When $m(t) = 0$: the multiplier outputs zero, suppressing the carrier completely.

**Step 4 — Transmission Over Channel:**
The modulated signal $s(t)$ propagates through the channel, where it is corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** $n(t)$, giving the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$.

**Step 5 — Demodulation (Coherent Detection):**
The receiver multiplies $r(t)$ by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ synchronized in phase and frequency, then low-pass filters the product to recover the baseband data.

## 2.2 BASK (OOK) — The "Why" Behind Its Name

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The name **On-Off Keying (OOK)** arises because the carrier is literally switched "ON" for a '1' and "OFF" (completely suppressed) for a '0'. This is the most common form of ASK used in practice (e.g., early optical fiber links, IR remote controls, RFID).

**Comparison of $m(t)$ and $s(t)$:**

| Binary Input $m(t)$ | ASK Output $s(t)$ | Description |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | $0$ | Carrier suppressed (OFF) |
| 1 | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Carrier transmitted (ON) |

## 2.3 Power Analysis of ASK Signal

The instantaneous power of the ASK signal (when '1' is being sent) is:

$$P_1 = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

When '0' is being sent, the power is:

$$P_0 = 0$$

Assuming equiprobable bits ($P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$), the **average transmitted power** is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_0 + P_1}{2} = \frac{A_c^2}{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A significant drawback of ASK is that the DC component (average power) carries no information. Half of the transmitted power is "wasted" because bit '0' transmits zero power. This makes ASK spectrally and power-inefficient compared to PSK or FSK.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|:--:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | ASK Modulated Signal (Time Domain) | $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ |
| 2 | Binary '1' Representation | $s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Carrier ON |
| 3 | Binary '0' Representation | $s_0(t) = 0$ | Carrier OFF |
| 4 | Bit Rate vs Bit Duration | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | bps |
| 5 | Baud Rate (For Binary ASK) | $D = R_b$ | Bd (1 bit = 1 symbol) |
| 6 | Transmission Bandwidth (Ideal) | $B_T = R_b$ | Hz (raised cosine, $r = 0$) |
| 7 | Transmission Bandwidth (Practical) | $B_T = (1 + r) \cdot R_b$ | Hz (raised cosine filter) |
| 8 | Power for Binary '1' | $P_1 = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Watts |
| 9 | Power for Binary '0' | $P_0 = 0$ | Watts |
| 10 | Average Power (Equiprobable Bits) | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}$ | Watts |
| 11 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = P_{avg} \cdot T_b = \dfrac{A_c^2 T_b}{4}$ | Joules |
| 12 | Bandwidth Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T}$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 13 | Bit Error Probability (Coherent ASK) | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{4 N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Bit Error Probability (Coherent, Equiv.) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)$ | Using Q-function |
| 15 | Noise Spectral Density Relation | $\dfrac{N_0}{2}$ (two-sided) $\leftrightarrow N_0$ (one-sided) | W/Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Bandwidth Formulas:** When the baseband data passes through a **raised-cosine filter with roll-off factor $r$**, the ASK transmission bandwidth becomes $B_T = (1+r)R_b$. For $r=0$ (ideal Nyquist), $B_T = R_b$. For $r=1$, $B_T = 2R_b$.

## 2.5 Power Spectral Density (PSD) Insight

The PSD of an ASK (OOK) signal consists of:
1. A **continuous spectrum** (from the random data) shaped by the baseband pulse spectrum.
2. A **discrete carrier component** at $f = \pm f_c$ when bit '1' is transmitted.

When the data has a rectangular NRZ pulse shape of width $T_b$, the baseband PSD is the familiar $\mathrm{sinc}^2(fT_b)$ shape. After modulation, this spectrum is shifted to be centered around $\pm f_c$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Optical Communication:** Early OOK was the first modulation used in fiber-optic links.
> - **RFID Systems:** Low-cost passive RFID tags use ASK for simplicity.
> - **IR Remote Controls:** Television remotes use OOK in the infrared band (~38 kHz).
> - **Aircraft Beacon Identification:** Older transponder systems use ASK.
> - **Morse Code Telegraphy:** Historical predecessor — continuous wave keying is essentially OOK.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ASK Modulated Signal Expression

### Starting Point
The binary data stream is represented as an NRZ waveform:

$$m(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n \cdot p(t - nT_b)$$

where $a_n \in \{0, 1\}$ are the binary symbols, and $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $T_b$ and unit amplitude.

### Step 1 — Apply Product Modulation
The modulator multiplies $m(t)$ with the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Data Signal
Replacing $m(t)$ with its pulse-train expansion:

$$s(t) = \left[ \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n \cdot p(t - nT_b) \right] \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange the Product
The summation can be brought outside the cosine product since the cosine is time-independent with respect to the summation index:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n \cdot p(t - nT_b)$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form
This is the standard KTU form for the ASK signal:

$$\boxed{s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the ASK Transmission Bandwidth

### Given
- Baseband data: NRZ waveform with bit duration $T_b$.
- Bit rate: $R_b = 1/T_b$.

### Step 1 — Baseband Spectrum of NRZ
The Fourier transform of a rectangular pulse of width $T_b$ is:

$$P(f) = T_b \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(f T_b) = T_b \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f T_b)}{\pi f T_b}$$

### Step 2 — Modulation Shifts the Spectrum
Multiplication by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in time domain corresponds to frequency shifting in the frequency domain:

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \left[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \right]$$

where $M(f)$ is the baseband spectrum of $m(t)$.

### Step 3 — Main Lobe Width of the Sinc Function
The $\mathrm{sinc}(fT_b)$ function has its first spectral null at $f = \pm 1/T_b$. Beyond this point, the spectrum still contains energy (side lobes), but the **main lobe** extends to $\pm R_b$ from the origin.

### Step 4 — Total Transmission Bandwidth
After modulation, the main lobe is centered at $f_c$ with width $2R_b$. Thus the **two-sided** transmission bandwidth is:

$$B_T = 2 R_b = \frac{2}{T_b}$$

### Step 5 — Practical Filter Case (Raised Cosine)
To eliminate intersymbol interference (ISI) and limit bandwidth, a raised-cosine filter with roll-off factor $r$ is used:

$$B_T = (1 + r) R_b$$

- If $r = 0$ (ideal): $B_T = R_b$ — minimum theoretical bandwidth.
- If $r = 1$: $B_T = 2R_b$ — twice the bit rate.
- If $r = 0.5$: $B_T = 1.5 R_b$.

$$\boxed{B_T = (1 + r) R_b}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Average Power and Energy per Bit

### Step 1 — Instantaneous Power Expression
The instantaneous power of a signal $s(t)$ of duration $T_b$ is $P(t) = s^2(t)$.

For binary '1' (carrier ON for duration $T_b$):

$$P_1 = \frac{1}{T_b} \int_0^{T_b} A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral
Using the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P_1 = \frac{A_c^2}{T_b} \int_0^{T_b} \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2}\, dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Term by Term
Assuming $f_c \gg R_b$ (i.e., many carrier cycles per bit), the second term averages to zero:

$$P_1 = \frac{A_c^2}{2 T_b} \left[ t \Big|_0^{T_b} + \frac{\sin(4\pi f_c t)}{4\pi f_c}\Big|_0^{T_b} \right]$$

$$P_1 = \frac{A_c^2}{2 T_b} \left[ T_b + 0 \right] = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Average Power with Equiprobable Bits

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} (P_0 + P_1) = \frac{1}{2} \left( 0 + \frac{A_c^2}{2} \right) = \frac{A_c^2}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Energy per Bit

$$E_b = P_{avg} \cdot T_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Bit Error Probability for Coherent ASK

### Setup
- Received signal: $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.
- Coherent detector multiplies $r(t)$ by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrates over $T_b$.
- Decision variable: $V = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$

### Step 1 — Decision Variable for Each Hypothesis
- **If '1' was sent:** $V = \dfrac{A_c T_b}{2} + N$, where $N$ is a Gaussian RV.
- **If '0' was sent:** $V = N$ (mean = 0).

### Step 2 — Statistics of the Noise
The variance of the noise sample $N$ is:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_0 T_b}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Optimal Threshold
The optimum decision threshold $\gamma$ minimizes total error. For equiprobable symbols and equal-noise variances:

$$\gamma = \frac{A_c T_b}{4} = \frac{E_b}{T_b} \cdot \frac{T_b}{2} = \frac{A_c T_b}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Probability of Error
The probability that the noise pushes $V$ across the threshold is:

$$P_e = P(0) \cdot P(V > \gamma \mid 0) + P(1) \cdot P(V < \gamma \mid 1)$$

For symmetric case with $P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$:

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b / 4}{\sigma}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b / 4}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b / 4}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Final Expression

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4 N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Comparison with BPSK:** BPSK has $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$, which is **3 dB better** than coherent ASK. This is why ASK is rarely used in noisy channels.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Value / Derivation Result | Symbol |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| Carrier Expression | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $c(t)$ |
| Modulating Signal | $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ | $m(t)$ |
| ASK Output | $A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $s(t)$ |
| Bandwidth (Ideal) | $B_T = R_b$ | $R_b$ in Hz |
| Bandwidth (Practical) | $B_T = (1 + r) R_b$ | $r \in [0, 1]$ |
| Power (Symbol 1) | $A_c^2 / 2$ | $P_1$ |
| Power (Symbol 0) | $0$ | $P_0$ |
| Average Power | $A_c^2 / 4$ | $P_{avg}$ |
| Energy per Bit | $A_c^2 T_b / 4$ | $E_b$ |
| Bit Error Rate (Coherent) | $\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / 2N_0}\right)$ | $P_e$ |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | $R_b / B_T$ | $\eta$ |
| Noise Susceptibility | **High** (amplitude-based) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ASK Transmitter (Modulator) — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional architecture of a Binary ASK (OOK) transmitter. The data source produces a binary stream, which is multiplied with a high-frequency carrier to produce the modulated output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Binary Data Source m of t] -->|NRZ Pulses| B[Multiplier X]
    O[Local Oscillator] -->|Ac cos 2 pi fc t| B
    B -->|s of t = Ac m of t cos 2 pi fc t| C[Bandpass Filter BPF]
    C -->|Filtered ASK Signal| D[Channel Output to Antenna]
    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2C3E50
    style O fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404
    style B fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style C fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style D fill:#E2E3E5,stroke:#383D41
```

### Explanation of Each Block
- **Binary Data Source:** Produces NRZ waveform $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **Local Oscillator:** Generates the unmodulated carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Multiplier:** The core of the modulator — outputs $s(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **BPF:** Limits out-of-band emissions and shapes the spectrum.
- **Channel Output:** The filtered ASK signal is sent to the antenna for transmission.

## 4.2 ASK Receiver (Coherent Demodulator) — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Received Signal r of t = s of t + n of t] -->|r of t| M[Multiplier X]
    LO[Local Oscillator Cos 2 pi fc t Phase Synced] -->|Recovered Carrier| M
    M -->|Product r of t times cos| LPF[Low Pass Filter LPF]
    LPF -->|Baseband Estimate| S[Sampler at t = nTb]
    S -->|Sampled Values| D[Threshold Detector Gamma]
    D -->|Decision 1 or 0| OUT[Recovered Binary Data]
    style P fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F
    style LO fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404
    style M fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style LPF fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style S fill:#CFFAFC,stroke:#0E7490
    style D fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style OUT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

### Explanation of Each Block
- **Received Signal $r(t)$:** Contains the transmitted ASK plus channel noise.
- **Local Oscillator:** Must be **phase-synchronized** with the transmitter's carrier.
- **Multiplier:** Demodulates by multiplying with the recovered carrier.
- **LPF:** Removes the high-frequency $2f_c$ component, leaving the baseband estimate.
- **Sampler:** Samples the baseband signal at the symbol period $T_b$.
- **Threshold Detector:** Compares each sample against the threshold $\gamma = A_c T_b / 4$ to make a binary decision.

## 4.3 ASK Signal — Time Domain Waveform Schematic

The following diagram represents the relationship between binary data, carrier, and the resulting ASK signal. It is rendered as a Mermaid state-flow diagram to avoid drawing complex waveforms (which Mermaid cannot directly render).

```mermaid
graph TD
    ST1[Time Slot 1: Bit 1] -->|m of t = 1| R1[Carrier ON: Ac cos 2 pi fc t]
    ST2[Time Slot 2: Bit 0] -->|m of t = 0| R2[Carrier OFF: 0]
    ST3[Time Slot 3: Bit 1] -->|m of t = 1| R3[Carrier ON: Ac cos 2 pi fc t]
    ST4[Time Slot 4: Bit 1] -->|m of t = 1| R4[Carrier ON: Ac cos 2 pi fc t]
    ST5[Time Slot 5: Bit 0] -->|m of t = 0| R5[Carrier OFF: 0]
    R1 --> F[Final ASK Signal s of t]
    R2 --> F
    R3 --> F
    R4 --> F
    R5 --> F
    style ST1 fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style ST2 fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style ST3 fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style ST4 fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style ST5 fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style F fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404
```

**Binary Sequence Example:** `1 0 1 1 0`

| Time Slot | Binary Bit | Carrier State | Output Amplitude |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $0 \to T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c$ |
| $T_b \to 2T_b$ | 0 | OFF | $0$ |
| $2T_b \to 3T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c$ |
| $3T_b \to 4T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c$ |
| $4T_b \to 5T_b$ | 0 | OFF | $0$ |

## 4.4 ASK Constellation Diagram Representation

For Binary ASK, the constellation has only **two points** along the in-phase ($I$) axis:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Origin 0,0] -->|Binary 0| B[Point 0 on I axis]
    A -->|Binary 1| C[Point d on I axis]
    style A fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style C fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
```

### Interpretation
- **Binary '0'** is mapped to the **origin** $(0, 0)$ — no carrier transmitted.
- **Binary '1'** is mapped to the point $(d, 0)$ on the in-phase axis — carrier with amplitude $A_c$ transmitted.
- The **distance $d$** between the two points is proportional to $\sqrt{E_b}$, which directly determines noise immunity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Because the two constellation points are at $(0,0)$ and $(d,0)$, the effective decision distance is **half** of what it would be if both symbols had equal energy. This geometric asymmetry is exactly why coherent ASK has a 3 dB SNR penalty compared to BPSK (whose points are at $(+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ and $(-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$).

## 4.5 Complete End-to-End ASK Communication System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source: Binary Data] --> ENC[NRZ Encoder]
    ENC --> MOD[ASK Modulator Multiplier]
    OSC1[Carrier Oscillator] --> MOD
    MOD --> CH[AWGN Channel]
    CH --> DEM[ASK Demodulator Multiplier]
    OSC2[Local Oscillator Synced] --> DEM
    DEM --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> TH[Threshold Detector]
    TH --> OUT[Output: Recovered Binary Data]
    style SRC fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#2C3E50
    style ENC fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#856404
    style MOD fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style OSC1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style CH fill:#E2E3E5,stroke:#383D41
    style DEM fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#721C24
    style OSC2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style LPF fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#155724
    style TH fill:#CFFAFC,stroke:#0E7490
    style OUT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define Amplitude Shift Keying. Draw the ASK waveform for the binary data `1 0 1 1 0` and mention its key disadvantage.**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition:**
Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of the high-frequency carrier signal is varied in accordance with the binary data signal, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is the binary data and $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the carrier.

**ASK Waveform for `1 0 1 1 0` (conceptual description):**

| Time Slot | Bit | Carrier State | Output |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $0$–$T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| $T_b$–$2T_b$ | 0 | OFF | $0$ |
| $2T_b$–$3T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| $3T_b$–$4T_b$ | 1 | ON | $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ |
| $4T_b$–$5T_b$ | 0 | OFF | $0$ |

**Key Disadvantage:** ASK is highly susceptible to amplitude noise, leading to high bit error rates in noisy channels. Additionally, it is **power-inefficient** since half the bits (the '0's) carry no signal energy.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**What is On-Off Keying (OOK)? Why is ASK also called OOK?**

#### Model Answer:
**Definition of OOK:**
On-Off Keying (OOK) is a special and most commonly used form of Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) in which the carrier is completely switched **ON** for binary '1' and completely switched **OFF** (i.e., amplitude = 0) for binary '0'.

**Why ASK = OOK:**
In the standard BASK scheme, the two amplitude levels used are $A_c$ and $0$. This means the carrier is either fully present (ON) or fully absent (OFF) — which is precisely the definition of On-Off Keying. Hence, the term **OOK** is a descriptive alias for the binary form of ASK.

**Representation:**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for bit '1'} \\ 0 & \text{for bit '0'} \end{cases}$$

**Application Example:** OOK is widely used in IR remote controls, RFID tags, and early optical fiber communication systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of a Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) signal. Derive the mathematical expression for the BASK signal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a BASK system, the carrier amplitude is $A_c = 10$ V and the bit duration is $T_b = 1$ μs. Calculate: **(i)** the average transmitted power, **(ii)** the energy per bit, and **(iii)** the transmission bandwidth assuming a raised-cosine filter with roll-off factor $r = 0.5$ and bit rate $R_b = 1$ Mbps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram of BASK Transmitter:**

```
Binary Data m(t) ──┐
                   ├──► [Multiplier] ──► [BPF] ──► s(t) → Channel
Carrier A_c cos(2πf_c t) ──┘
```

**Block Diagram of BASK Receiver (Coherent Detection):**

```
Received r(t) ──┐
                ├──► [Multiplier] ──► [LPF] ──► [Sampler] ──► [Threshold γ] ──► Output
Local cos(2πf_c t) ──┘
```

**Generation Process:**
1. The binary data $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is generated as an NRZ waveform.
2. A local oscillator produces the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
3. A multiplier (product modulator) produces $s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$.
4. The BPF limits the spectrum and removes out-of-band noise.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let $m(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n p(t - nT_b)$ where $a_n \in \{0, 1\}$.

Multiplying with carrier:

$$s(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Substituting the data signal:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n p(t - nT_b)$$

**Detection Process (Coherent):**
1. The received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is multiplied by a synchronized local carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. The product contains a baseband term and a $2f_c$ term.
3. The LPF removes the $2f_c$ term, leaving a baseband signal proportional to the original data.
4. A sampler at $t = nT_b$ extracts each bit's value.
5. A threshold detector $\gamma = A_c T_b / 4$ makes the binary decision.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $A_c = 10$ V
- $T_b = 1 \, \mu s = 10^{-6}$ s
- $R_b = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- $r = 0.5$

**(i) Average Transmitted Power:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A_c^2}{4} = \frac{(10)^2}{4} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Energy per Bit:**

$$E_b = P_{avg} \cdot T_b = 25 \times 10^{-6} = 25 \, \mu J$$

Alternative direct calculation:

$$E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 25 \, \mu J$$

**(iii) Transmission Bandwidth:**

$$B_T = (1 + r) R_b = (1 + 0.5) \times 10^6 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Calculated Value |
|:---|:---:|
| $P_{avg}$ | $25$ W |
| $E_b$ | $25 \, \mu J$ |
| $B_T$ | $1.5$ MHz |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the coherent detection method for ASK. Derive the bit error probability $P_e$ for coherent binary ASK in the presence of AWGN. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare ASK, FSK, and PSK in terms of bandwidth, noise immunity, and complexity. Justify why ASK is rarely used in modern high-speed communication systems. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Coherent Detection Principle:**
In coherent (synchronous) detection, the receiver generates a local carrier that is **exactly synchronized in both phase and frequency** with the transmitter's carrier. This local carrier is multiplied with the received signal, and a low-pass filter recovers the baseband data.

**Receiver Block Diagram:**

```
r(t) ──► [×] ──► [Integrator 0 to Tb] ──► [Sampler] ──► [Threshold γ] ──► Output
      ▲
      │
Local cos(2πf_c t)
```

**Mathematical Analysis:**

Received signal:

$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

The decision variable is:

$$V = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \, dt$$

**Case 1 — When '1' is sent ($s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$):**

$$V \vert_{H_1} = \int_0^{T_b} A_c \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \, dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \, dt$$

Using $\cos^2 \theta = \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$ and assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$:

$$V \vert_{H_1} = \frac{A_c T_b}{2} + N$$

**Case 2 — When '0' is sent ($s(t) = 0$):**

$$V \vert_{H_0} = N$$

**Noise Statistics:**
The noise term $N$ is Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance:

$$\sigma_N^2 = \frac{N_0 T_b}{4}$$

**Optimal Threshold:**
The minimum-error threshold is the midpoint of the two means:

$$\gamma_{opt} = \frac{A_c T_b}{4}$$

**Probability of Error:**
The probability of error is the probability that noise pushes the decision variable across the threshold. With equiprobable symbols:

$$P_e = P(0) P(V > \gamma \mid H_0) + P(1) P(V < \gamma \mid H_1)$$

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2} P\!\left(N > \frac{A_c T_b}{4}\right) + \frac{1}{2} P\!\left(N < -\frac{A_c T_b}{4}\right)$$

By symmetry:

$$P_e = \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b / 4}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b / 4}}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4 N_0}}\right)$$

Substituting $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4$:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{2 N_0}}\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Comparison Table: ASK vs FSK vs PSK**

| Parameter | ASK (OOK) | BFSK | BPSK |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Bandwidth** | $B_T = (1+r) R_b$ | $B_T = 2(1+r) R_b$ (coherent) | $B_T = (1+r) R_b$ |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | **Highest** (lowest BW) | **Lowest** (highest BW) | **Highest** (lowest BW) |
| **Noise Immunity** | **Poorest** (amplitude-based) | Moderate | **Best** |
| **BER @ same SNR** | Highest | Moderate | **Lowest** |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor (one symbol = 0 power) | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| **Complexity (TX)** | **Lowest** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Complexity (RX)** | **Lowest** (needs sync) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **SNR Penalty vs BPSK** | **3 dB** | ~3 dB | Reference (0 dB) |

**Why ASK is Rarely Used in Modern High-Speed Systems:**

1. **High Susceptibility to Amplitude Noise:** Any channel impairment that affects amplitude (fading, interference, multi-path) directly corrupts the data. Modern wireless channels are heavily affected by amplitude fading.

2. **3 dB Power Penalty:** Compared to BPSK, ASK requires **double the power** to achieve the same bit error rate. This is unacceptable in power-constrained systems (mobile, IoT, satellite).

3. **Power Inefficiency:** Half the bits ('0' symbols) transmit zero power, wasting DC and contributing to high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR).

4. **Threshold Sensitivity:** Coherent detection requires a precise threshold $\gamma = A_c T_b / 4$. In fading channels, the received amplitude varies, making the optimal threshold difficult to set.

5. **Historical Replacements:** Modern systems use QPSK, QAM, OFDM, etc., which offer superior spectral and power efficiency.

6. **Limited Use Cases:** ASK/OOK survives only in low-cost, short-range applications like IR remotes, RFID, and some optical links.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ASK with AM:** Students often treat ASK like standard AM. Remember: in ASK, only **one amplitude level is non-zero**, whereas in AM, both levels are non-zero.
> 2. **Wrong Threshold Value:** In $P_e$ derivation, the threshold is $\gamma = A_c T_b / 4$, not $A_c T_b / 2$. Marks are lost for missing the factor of 2.
> 3. **Mixing Bandwidth Formulas:** ASK bandwidth is $B_T = (1+r) R_b$, not $2R_b$. The factor of 2 appears only in the two-sided spectrum view, not the transmission bandwidth.
> 4. **Forgetting Energy Expression:** Many students write $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 2$ instead of the correct $A_c^2 T_b / 4$ for ASK. The factor of 4 (not 2) comes from the equiprobable average over both symbols.
> 5. **Phase Coherence:** Coherent ASK requires phase synchronization. Forgetting to mention the need for a **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** or **Costas loop** at the receiver costs marks.
> 6. **Roll-off Factor:** The KTU exam often tests $r = 0$ (ideal) vs $r = 1$ cases. State the bandwidth numerically in each case.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Know Points for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** ASK varies the **amplitude** of the carrier in step with binary data; frequency and phase stay constant.
- **Mathematical Form:** $s(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, where $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$.
- **BASK = OOK:** Binary '1' → carrier ON, Binary '0' → carrier OFF.
- **Power for '1':** $P_1 = A_c^2 / 2$.
- **Power for '0':** $P_0 = 0$.
- **Average Power:** $P_{avg} = A_c^2 / 4$ (assuming equiprobable bits).
- **Energy per Bit:** $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4$.
- **Bandwidth (Ideal Nyquist, $r=0$):** $B_T = R_b$.
- **Bandwidth (Raised Cosine, roll-off $r$):** $B_T = (1 + r) R_b$.
- **Bit Error Probability (Coherent Detection):** $P_e = \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / (2 N_0)}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / (2 N_0)}\right)$.
- **3 dB Penalty vs BPSK:** ASK requires double the power of BPSK for the same $P_e$.
- **Constellation:** Two points at $(0, 0)$ and $(d, 0)$ on the I-axis.
- **Detection:** Coherent (synchronous) detection is required for optimal performance.
- **Noise Susceptibility:** High — amplitude-based systems are vulnerable to fading.
- **Applications:** IR remotes, RFID, optical OOK, low-cost telemetry.
- **KTU High-Yield Keywords to Use in Answers:** *bandwidth efficiency, power efficiency, On-Off Keying, coherent detection, threshold, raised cosine filter, AWGN, erfc, Q-function, roll-off factor, NRZ encoding, constellation diagram*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mnemonic for ASK:** **"Amplitude Alone, Noise Pronounced, Power Half-Wasted, Yet Simple and Sound."** This captures the essence — ASK only uses amplitude, suffers from noise, wastes half its power in '0' symbols, but is the simplest digital modulation scheme to implement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog-to-Analog Modulation (AM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog-to-Analog Modulation (AM) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Analog-to-Analog Modulation** is the process of converting a low-frequency analog baseband (message) signal into a higher-frequency analog passband signal by varying one or more parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) of a high-frequency sinusoidal **carrier wave** in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal. In the specific case of **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, the **amplitude** of the carrier is varied proportionally to the message signal, while the **frequency** and **phase** of the carrier remain constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Amplitude Modulation is a modulation technique in which the amplitude of the carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the modulating (message) signal, while the carrier frequency and phase are held constant." The standard mathematical model for a double-sideband, full-carrier AM signal is given by: $$s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Where: - $A_c$ is the **peak amplitude of the carrier** (volts, **V**) - $m(t)$ is the **analog message signal** (volts, **V**) - $f_c$ is the **carrier frequency** (Hertz, **Hz**) - $t$ is the **time** (seconds, **s**) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Floating Boat on Waves" Analogy:** > Imagine you are standing on a beach watching ocean waves. The waves are regular and uniform — they have a fixed height pattern and arrive at fixed intervals (this is your **carrier** signal). Now, picture a small boat floating on top of these waves. The boat bobs up and down, but the *spacing between waves* (frequency) does not change. > > If you measure the *height of the wave-crest* at any instant, you will notice it changes based on where the boat is — if the boat is high, the wave appears taller; if the boat dips low, the wave appears shorter. **The information about the boat's position (the message signal) is now encoded in the height (amplitude) of the waves.** > > At the receiver, you simply track the wave heights to reconstruct the boat's position — this is **demodulation**. ## 1.3 Why Modulation is Necessary > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Antenna Size Reduction:** A 1 kHz audio signal needs an antenna of length $\approx 150,000$ m for efficient radiation. Modulating it onto a 1 MHz carrier reduces the antenna to $\approx 150$ m. > - **Multiplexing (FDM):** Multiple signals can share the same medium by translating each to a different carrier frequency. > - **Reduced Noise & Interference:** Higher carrier frequencies are less affected by low-frequency noise like 1/f noise and hum. ## 1.4 Classification of Analog-to-Analog Modulation | Modulation Type | Parameter Varied | KTU Sub-Topic Reference | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** | Amplitude of carrier | **Current Topic** | | Frequency Modulation (FM) | Frequency of carrier | Module 1 (next topic) | | Phase Modulation (PM) | Phase of carrier | Module 1 (advanced topic) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Components of an AM Signal Let the message be a **single-tone sinusoid**: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. Substituting into the AM equation: $$s_{AM}(t) = [A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Expanding using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity: $$\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$$ We obtain three distinct frequency components: | Component | Frequency | Amplitude | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Carrier (C)** | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | Unmodulated transmission reference | | **Lower Sideband (LSB)** | $f_c - f_m$ | $A_m / 2$ | Difference frequency component | | **Upper Sideband (USB)** | $f_c + f_m$ | $A_m / 2$ | Sum frequency component | > [!NOTE] > **Spectrum Insight:** The AM signal spectrum is **NOT** a single line at $f_c$. It consists of three spectral lines — one at the carrier and two sidebands symmetrically placed around it. This is why AM is formally called **Double-Sideband Full-Carrier (DSB-FC)**. ## 2.2 The Modulation Index ($a$) The **modulation index** (or modulation depth) is the ratio of the peak message amplitude to the peak carrier amplitude: $$a = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$ - **Condition for no over-modulation:** $0 \le a \le 1$ (i.e., $A_m \le A_c$) - If $a > 1$ → **Over-modulation** → envelope distortion → information loss - If $a = 1$ → **100% modulation** → maximum undistorted output - If $a = 0$ → **No modulation** → pure carrier transmitted > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Many students write $a = A_m/A_c$ without specifying that this applies to single-tone modulation. For a general message $m(t)$, the **peak modulation index** is $a = \max\vert m(t)\vert / A_c$, and the **RMS modulation index** is $\mu = \sqrt{\overline{m^2(t)}}/A_c$. ## 2.3 Power Distribution in an AM Signal The total transmitted power of an AM signal carrying a single-tone message is: $$P_t = P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = P_c\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{2}\right)$$ Where $P_c = A_c^2/2$ is the carrier power and $P_{sidebands} = a^2 A_c^2/4$. ### Power Efficiency ($\eta$) $$\eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_t} = \frac{a^2/2}{1 + a^2/2} = \frac{a^2}{2 + a^2}$$ - At $a = 1$ (100% modulation): $\eta_{max} = 1/3 \approx \mathbf{33.33\%}$ - This means **at best, only 33% of transmitted power carries information**. The remaining 67% is wasted in the carrier. > [!WARNING] > This low efficiency is the **primary reason** DSB-FC AM is replaced by DSB-SC, SSB, or VSB systems in modern engineering applications. ## 2.4 Bandwidth of AM Signal For a general message signal $m(t)$ with highest frequency $W$ (Hz): $$BW_{AM} = f_{USB} - f_{LSB} = (f_c + W) - (f_c - W) = 2W$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The bandwidth of a DSB-FC AM signal is **twice the bandwidth of the message signal**, irrespective of the modulation index. This is a guaranteed 3-mark question. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **AM Signal** | $s(t) = A_c[1 + a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \le a \le 1$ | V | | **Modulation Index** | $a = A_m / A_c$ | $a \le 1$ for undistorted | dimensionless | | **Total Power** | $P_t = P_c(1 + a^2/2)$ | $P_c = A_c^2/2$ | W (Watts) | | **Sideband Power** | $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot a^2/2$ | — | W (Watts) | | **Carrier Power** | $P_c = A_c^2/2$ | — | W (Watts) | | **Power Efficiency** | $\eta = a^2/(2 + a^2)$ | Max $= 33.33\%$ at $a=1$ | % | | **Bandwidth** | $BW = 2W = 2f_m$ (single tone) | $W = $ max message freq | Hz | | **Over-modulation** | $A_m > A_c \Rightarrow a > 1$ | Envelope distortion occurs | — | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Broadcast Radio (MW/SW):** Long-range commercial AM radio broadcasting (540–1700 kHz). - **Aviation (VHF):** Aircraft communication uses AM in the 118–137 MHz band. - **Citizens Band (CB) Radio:** AM at 27 MHz for short-range two-way communication. - **Modem Signaling:** Early modems used AM-based ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying) for low-speed data. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: AM Signal Expression for Single-Tone Message ### Given - Carrier: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ - Modulating signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ - Condition: $f_c \gg f_m$ (carrier frequency much greater than message frequency) ### Step 1 — Definition of Amplitude Modulation By definition, the instantaneous amplitude of the modulated wave is varied linearly with $m(t)$: $$A(t) = A_c + k_a \cdot m(t)$$ Where $k_a$ is the **amplitude sensitivity** of the modulator (units: V/V or dimensionless). ### Step 2 — Simplification (Normalized Modulator) For an ideal normalized modulator, $k_a = 1$ V/V: $$A(t) = A_c + m(t) = A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute Into Carrier Equation The modulated signal is the carrier with its amplitude replaced by $A(t)$: $$s_{AM}(t) = A(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = [A_c + A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ ### Step 4 — Factor Out the Carrier Amplitude Pull $A_c$ outside to introduce the modulation index $a$: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\left[1 + \frac{A_m}{A_c}\cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Using $a = A_m / A_c$: $$\boxed{s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$ ### Step 5 — Expand Using Trigonometric Identity Apply $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c \cdot a}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{A_c \cdot a}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$ ### Step 6 — Identify the Three Spectral Components $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{aA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{aA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$ | Term | Frequency | Amplitude | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Carrier | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | | LSB | $f_c - f_m$ | $aA_c/2$ | | USB | $f_c + f_m$ | $aA_c/2$ | --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Power in an AM Signal ### Step 1 — Power of a Sinusoid The average power dissipated across a $1\,\Omega$ load by a signal $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f t)$ is $P = A^2/2$. ### Step 2 — Apply to Each Component **Carrier Power:** $$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$ **Power in Each Sideband:** $$P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{aA_c}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$ ### Step 3 — Total Power (Sum of Components) Since the carrier, LSB, and USB are at different frequencies, their powers add directly: $$P_t = P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{a^2 A_c^2}{8}$$ $$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{a^2 A_c^2}{4}$$ ### Step 4 — Factor Out $P_c = A_c^2/2$ $$P_t = P_c + \frac{a^2}{2} P_c = P_c\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{2}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Power Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_t} = \frac{a^2 P_c/2}{P_c(1 + a^2/2)} = \frac{a^2}{2 + a^2}$$ --- ## 3.3 Solved Analytical Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Similar)]** > An AM broadcast station transmits a total power of 50 kW with a modulation index of 0.8. Calculate: > (a) Carrier power > (b) Power in each sideband > (c) Power efficiency > (d) Maximum modulation index if 80% of the total power must reside in the sidebands. ### Solution **Given:** $P_t = 50$ kW, $a = 0.8$. **(a) Carrier Power:** $$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{2}\right) \Rightarrow P_c = \frac{P_t}{1 + a^2/2}$$ $$P_c = \frac{50{,}000}{1 + 0.64/2} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.32} \approx 37{,}878.79 \text{ W}$$ **(b) Power in Each Sideband:** $$P_{SB,total} = P_t - P_c = 50{,}000 - 37{,}878.79 = 12{,}121.21 \text{ W}$$ $$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{P_{SB,total}}{2} = 6{,}060.60 \text{ W}$$ **(c) Power Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{a^2}{2 + a^2} = \frac{0.64}{2.64} \approx 0.2424 = \mathbf{24.24\%}$$ **(d) Condition:** $P_{SB}/P_t = 0.80$ $$\frac{a^2/2}{1 + a^2/2} = 0.80 \Rightarrow \frac{a^2}{2 + a^2} = 0.80$$ $$a^2 = 0.80(2 + a^2) = 1.6 + 0.8a^2 \Rightarrow 0.2a^2 = 1.6 \Rightarrow a^2 = 8$$ $$\boxed{a = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828}$$ > [!NOTE] > This value $a > 1$ is mathematically valid for *power distribution analysis* but indicates **over-modulation** in practice. It is a classic KTU trick question to test conceptual clarity. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | DSB-FC (Standard AM) | DSB-SC | SSB-SC | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Carrier Transmitted? | **Yes** | No | No | | Sidebands Transmitted? | Both | Both | Only one | | Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ | $W$ | | Power Efficiency | $33.33\%$ max | $100\%$ | $100\%$ | | Demodulation | Envelope detector | Coherent | Coherent | | Complexity | Low | High | Very High | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AM Transmitter — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR MSG[Message Signal m of t] --> MOD[Balanced Modulator with DC Bias] BIAS[DC Source equals Ac] --> MOD OSC[Carrier Oscillator fc] --> MOD MOD --> AMP[Linear Power Amplifier] AMP --> ANT[Antenna] ANT -.AM Wave.-> RX[Receiving Antenna] classDef src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 class MSG,BIAS,OSC src class MOD,AMP proc class ANT,RX out ``` ## 4.2 AM Demodulator — Envelope Detector ```mermaid flowchart LR RFIN[AM Signal s of t] --> DIODE[Diode Rectifier] DIODE --> CAP[RC Low Pass Filter] CAP --> LOAD[Load Speaker] CAP -.Recovered.-> OUT[Audio Output m hat of t] classDef inp fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 class RFIN inp class DIODE,CAP,LOAD proc class OUT out ``` ## 4.3 AM Frequency Spectrum — Single Tone ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SPECTRUM[Frequency Domain Representation of AM Signal] direction LR LSB["LSB fc minus fm
Amplitude equals aAc over 2"] --- CAR["CARRIER fc
Amplitude equals Ac"] --- USB["USB fc plus fm
Amplitude equals aAc over 2"] end classDef sb fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef cr fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000 class LSB,USB sb class CAR cr ``` ## 4.4 Time-Domain Envelope Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Message m of t = Am cos 2 pi fm t] --> B{Is a less than or equal to 1?} B -- Yes --> C[Clean Envelope follows m of t] B -- No --> D[Envelope Distortion at zero crossings] C --> E[Receiver recovers signal via Envelope Detector] D --> F[Information loss at detector] E --> G[Output m hat of t equals k times m of t] F --> H[Distorted audio output] classDef ok fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef node fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 class A,B node class C,E,G ok class D,F,H bad ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Define Amplitude Modulation. Why is the modulation index kept below unity in AM systems? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a modulation technique in which the **amplitude of the high-frequency carrier signal** is varied in proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message signal**, while the carrier frequency and phase remain unchanged. > > The **modulation index** $a = A_m/A_c$ is kept below unity ($a \le 1$) to **prevent over-modulation**. If $a > 1$, the message signal $m(t)$ exceeds the carrier amplitude $A_c$ in the negative half-cycle, causing a **phase reversal of 180°** at zero-crossings. This results in **envelope distortion**, and a simple envelope detector at the receiver will fail to recover the original message, leading to **permanent information loss**. Hence, to ensure **distortion-free demodulation**, $a \le 1$ is a strict design constraint. **(3 marks)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** List the three frequency components present in an AM signal for a single-tone modulating signal and state their relative amplitudes. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the AM spectrum contains the following three components: **(1 mark)** > > 1. **Carrier Component** at frequency $f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$. **(1 mark)** > 2. **Lower Sideband (LSB)** at frequency $f_c - f_m$ with amplitude $aA_c/2$. **(0.5 mark)** > 3. **Upper Sideband (USB)** at frequency $f_c + f_m$ with amplitude $aA_c/2$. **(0.5 mark)** > > The carrier amplitude $A_c$ is **larger** than each sideband amplitude (since $A_c \ge aA_c/2$ for $a \le 1$). This is the reason the carrier is transmitted with full strength in DSB-FC AM, even though it carries **no information**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for an AM signal in the time domain. Sketch its frequency spectrum for a single-tone modulating signal. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An AM transmitter radiates 12 kW of power when the carrier is unmodulated. When the carrier is modulated sinusoidally by a single tone, the total radiated power rises to 15 kW. Calculate the modulation index and the power in each sideband. **(7 marks)** ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Adapted)]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and demodulation of a DSB-FC AM signal. Compare envelope detection with synchronous detection. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1000 kHz carrier is amplitude modulated by a 5 kHz audio sine wave. The peak carrier amplitude at the antenna is 800 V, and the peak message amplitude is 600 V. Calculate: (i) Modulation index, (ii) Sideband frequencies, (iii) Total sideband power if the antenna resistance is 50 Ω, (iv) Maximum possible power efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Detailed Model Solutions** #### **Question A — Model Solution** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** The carrier signal is $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the modulating signal is $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. **(1 mark)** By definition of AM, the modulated wave's amplitude becomes: $$A(t) = A_c + A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t) = A_c[1 + a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$$ **(1 mark)** where $a = A_m/A_c$ is the modulation index. The AM signal is: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ Expanding: $$s_{AM}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{aA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t] + \frac{aA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$$ **(2 marks)** The frequency spectrum shows three lines: a tall line at $f_c$ of height $A_c$ and two shorter lines at $f_c \pm f_m$ of height $aA_c/2$. **(1 mark)** A clear spectrum sketch (described verbally) shows the carrier as the central tallest spectral line, with LSB and USB as equal-height shorter lines symmetrically placed. **(2 marks for figure)** **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** Unmodulated carrier power $P_c = 12$ kW, total modulated power $P_t = 15$ kW. **Step 1:** Using the AM power equation $P_t = P_c(1 + a^2/2)$: $$15 = 12\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{2}\right)$$ $$\frac{15}{12} - 1 = \frac{a^2}{2} \Rightarrow 0.25 = \frac{a^2}{2}$$ **(2 marks)** $$a^2 = 0.5 \Rightarrow a = \sqrt{0.5} \approx \mathbf{0.707}$$ **(1 mark)** **Step 2:** Total sideband power: $$P_{SB,total} = P_t - P_c = 15 - 12 = 3 \text{ kW}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3:** Power in each sideband: $$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{P_{SB,total}}{2} = \mathbf{1.5 \text{ kW}}$$ **(2 marks)** --- #### **Question B — Model Solution** **Part (a) — AM Generation and Demodulation (7 marks):** **Generation Block Diagram** (drawn and explained): The message signal $m(t)$ is added to a DC bias equal to $A_c$ in a summing amplifier. The sum $[A_c + m(t)]$ is fed to a balanced modulator (or a non-linear device) along with the carrier $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ from a local oscillator. The output is the AM signal. **(2 marks)** **Envelope Detection** (Demodulation): A diode rectifier followed by an RC low-pass filter. The diode rectifies the AM signal, and the capacitor charges to the peak envelope and discharges slowly through the resistor, reproducing $m(t)$. Simple, cheap, but works only for $a \le 1$ and $f_c \gg f_m$. **(2 marks)** **Synchronous Detection:** The received AM signal is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (synchronized in phase and frequency). After low-pass filtering, the message is recovered. Works for any $a$ (including $a > 1$) and can demodulate DSB-SC. **(2 marks)** **Comparison Table** (1 mark): | Feature | Envelope Detector | Synchronous Detector | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Complexity | Low | High (needs local oscillator) | | Distortion | Requires $a \le 1$ | No restriction on $a$ | | Output SNR | Lower | Higher | | Cost | Cheap | Expensive | **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $f_c = 1000$ kHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $A_c = 800$ V, $A_m = 600$ V, $R = 50$ Ω. **(i) Modulation Index:** $$a = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{600}{800} = \mathbf{0.75}$$ **(2 marks)** **(ii) Sideband Frequencies:** $$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 1000 - 5 = \mathbf{995 \text{ kHz}}$$ $$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 1000 + 5 = \mathbf{1005 \text{ kHz}}$$ **(1 mark)** **(iii) Total Sideband Power:** $$P_{SB,total} = P_c \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} \cdot \frac{a^2}{2} = \frac{800^2}{2 \times 50} \cdot \frac{0.75^2}{2}$$ $$P_{SB,total} = 6400 \times 0.28125 = \mathbf{1800 \text{ W}}$$ **(2 marks)** **(iv) Maximum Power Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{a^2}{2 + a^2} = \frac{0.5625}{2.5625} \approx \mathbf{21.95\%}$$ **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Students often write $P_{SB,total} = P_c \cdot a^2$ (single sideband value) instead of $P_c \cdot a^2/2$ (both sidebands). This loses **1 mark** consistently. > 2. **Mixing up frequencies:** $f_c - f_m$ is the **lower** sideband, not the upper. Forgetting this costs **1 mark**. > 3. **No diagram in derivation:** A 7-mark AM derivation question **always** requires a labelled spectrum sketch. Skipping it loses **2 marks**. > 4. **Assuming $a = 1$:** Never assume 100% modulation unless the problem states it. Compute $a$ from given $A_m$ and $A_c$ values. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **AM stands for Amplitude Modulation** — vary amplitude of carrier proportionally to the message. - **Time-domain AM signal:** $s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (single-tone case). - **Spectrum contains 3 components:** Carrier ($f_c$), LSB ($f_c - f_m$), USB ($f_c + f_m$). - **Modulation index $a = A_m/A_c$**, and must satisfy $0 \le a \le 1$ for distortion-free envelope detection. - **Total power:** $P_t = P_c(1 + a^2/2)$. Carrier dominates: $\sim 2/3$ of power is wasted at $a=1$. - **Maximum power efficiency = 33.33%** (at $a = 1$). This is a structural limitation of DSB-FC AM. - **Bandwidth = $2W$** (twice the message bandwidth) — independent of $a$. - **Envelope detector** (diode + RC filter) is the simplest demodulator, but requires $a \le 1$ and $f_c \gg W$. - **Over-modulation** ($a > 1$) causes phase reversal and envelope distortion → information loss. - **Real-world uses:** MW/SW radio broadcast, aviation VHF, CB radio. Replaced by FM/SSB in modern efficient systems. - **Generation:** Message + DC bias = envelope → fed to balanced modulator with carrier. - **Demodulation choice:** Envelope detector (simple, $a \le 1$) vs Synchronous detector (complex, any $a$). - **Key identity used:** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ — must be memorized. - **KTU favourite numerical pattern:** Given $P_t$ and $P_c$ → find $a$ → find sideband powers. This 7-mark template repeats every year.

topics.title: OSI Reference Model: Layers and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** OSI Reference Model: Layers and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Reference Model** is a **7-layer abstract architectural framework** standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under the document **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It defines a standardized methodology for heterogeneous computer systems to communicate over a network by partitioning the complex communication process into distinct, manageable, and interoperable functional layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** The OSI model is a *conceptual reference model* that prescribes a hierarchical decomposition of the functions required for network communication into **seven abstraction layers**, each communicating only with its immediate adjacent layers through well-defined **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Postal Service

Imagine you want to send a gift to a friend in another country:

| Real-World Analogy | OSI Layer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You write a letter in your language (English/Malayalam) | **Application Layer** (human-to-software interface) |
| You translate the letter to the recipient's language | **Presentation Layer** (translation, encryption) |
| The postal clerk hands it to the right department | **Session Layer** (manages the dialogue) |
| You write the friend's address on the envelope | **Transport Layer** (end-to-end addressing/port) |
| The postman decides the route (air/sea/road) | **Network Layer** (routing of packets) |
| The mail truck physically moves the letter | **Data Link Layer** (frame delivery on a single link) |
| The road itself | **Physical Layer** (raw bit transmission on media) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 7 layers?** Each layer addresses a *specific class of network problem* (physical, logical, end-to-end, application, etc.). This separation of concerns allows engineers to develop protocols for one layer independently of others — the core principle of **modular networking design**.

### 1.3 Core Design Principles of the OSI Model

- **Layering:** Every system is built as a stack of layers (n=1 to n=7).
- **Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Layer *n* on one host logically communicates with Layer *n* on another host using **protocols**.
- **Service Provision:** Layer *n* provides services to layer *(n+1)* via SAPs.
- **Encapsulation:** Each layer appends its own control information (header/trailer) to the data unit received from the layer above.

### 1.4 The 7 Layers — Quick Snapshot (Top-Down)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic (Top → Bottom):** "**A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing"
> **A**pplication, **P**resentation, **S**ession, **T**ransport, **N**etwork, **D**ata Link, **P**hysical

- **Layer 7 — Application**
- **Layer 6 — Presentation**
- **Layer 5 — Session**
- **Layer 4 — Transport**
- **Layer 3 — Network**
- **Layer 2 — Data Link**
- **Layer 1 — Physical**

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always list layers in a specific direction (either top-down or bottom-up) and stick to it. Examiners award marks for systematic presentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Function Decomposition of Each Layer

#### **Layer 1: Physical Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To transmit raw unstructured bit streams over a physical medium.
- **Core 'How':** Defines electrical, mechanical, procedural, and functional specifications.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Bit synchronization (clock recovery)
  * Bit rate control
  * Physical topologies (bus, star, mesh, ring)
  * Transmission mode (simplex, half-duplex, full-duplex)
  * Physical media specification (copper, fiber, RF)
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** **Bit**
- **Example Standards:** RS-232, V.35, 100BASE-TX, IEEE 802.3 (physical signaling)

#### **Layer 2: Data Link Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To provide **reliable node-to-node** frame transfer across a single link, hiding physical-layer errors.
- **Core 'How':** Frames are constructed with MAC addresses, CRC checksums, and flow control.
- **Sub-layers (IEEE 802 split):**
  * **LLC (Logical Link Control) — IEEE 802.2:** error notification, flow control
  * **MAC (Media Access Control):** addressing (48-bit MAC), channel access
- **Key Functions:**
  * Framing (start/stop flags, bit stuffing)
  * Physical addressing (MAC address)
  * Error detection (CRC) and correction
  * Flow control
  * Access control (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA, Token Passing)
- **PDU:** **Frame**
- **Example Protocols/Standards:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC

#### **Layer 3: Network Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To deliver packets **end-to-end across multiple networks** (internetworking).
- **Core 'How':** Uses logical addressing (IP) and routing tables; routers operate here.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Logical addressing (IPv4/IPv6)
  * Routing (path determination: RIP, OSPF, BGP)
  * Packet forwarding and switching
  * Fragmentation and reassembly
  * Congestion control (router-level)
- **PDU:** **Packet** (or **Datagram** for connectionless)
- **Example Protocols:** IP, ICMP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP, ARP

#### **Layer 4: Transport Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To provide **process-to-process** (end-to-end) data delivery with quality-of-service guarantees.
- **Core 'How':** Uses port numbers (16-bit) to multiplex multiple applications; ensures completeness.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Port-based process addressing
  * Segmentation and reassembly
  * Connection establishment (3-way handshake in TCP)
  * Flow control (end-to-end)
  * Error recovery (retransmission)
  * Connection-oriented (TCP) vs. Connectionless (UDP)
- **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP)
- **Example Protocols:** TCP (RFC 793), UDP (RFC 768), SCTP, DCCP

#### **Layer 5: Session Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To manage and control **dialogues (sessions)** between two communicating hosts.
- **Core 'How':** Establishes, maintains, and terminates sessions; provides synchronization points.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Session establishment, maintenance, termination
  * Dialog control (half-duplex/full-duplex)
  * Synchronization (checkpoints for long transfers)
  * Token management (which side can transmit)
- **PDU:** **Data** (or **SPDU — Session Protocol Data Unit**)
- **Example Protocols:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP, H.245

#### **Layer 6: Presentation Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To ensure data is in a **usable format** for the application layer — handles syntax and semantics.
- **Core 'How':** Translates between different data representations; applies encryption and compression.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Data translation (ASCII ↔ EBCDIC)
  * Data encoding/decoding (MIME, JPEG, MPEG, TIFF)
  * Data compression (lossy/lossless)
  * Data encryption/decryption (TLS handshake, though TLS is application-layer in practice)
- **PDU:** **Data** (or **PPDU — Presentation Protocol Data Unit**)
- **Example Standards:** SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII, MPEG, XDR

#### **Layer 7: Application Layer**
- **Core 'Why':** To provide **network services directly to end-user applications**.
- **Core 'How':** Defines high-level APIs and protocols that software uses to communicate.
- **Key Functions:**
  * Network virtual terminal (remote login)
  * File transfer, access, and management
  * Mail services
  * Directory services
  * Resource sharing
- **PDU:** **Data** (or **APDU — Application Protocol Data Unit**)
- **Example Protocols:** HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, POP3, IMAP, SNMP, Telnet, SSH

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — The Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. It is the single most-asked structure in KTU 2024 board exams for this module.**

| Layer # | Layer Name | PDU Name | Addressing Type | Key Device / Unit | Example Protocol |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **7** | Application | Data / APDU | Message | End-user application | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP |
| **6** | Presentation | Data / PPDU | — | — | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII |
| **5** | Session | Data / SPDU | — | — | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| **4** | Transport | Segment / Datagram | **Port Number (16-bit)** | Gateway (sometimes) | TCP, UDP, SCTP |
| **3** | Network | **Packet / Datagram** | **Logical Address (IP)** | **Router** | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP |
| **2** | Data Link | **Frame** | **Physical Address (MAC 48-bit)** | **Bridge, Switch** | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, HDLC |
| **1** | Physical | **Bit** | — (electrical/optical) | **Hub, Repeater, Cable** | RS-232, 100BASE-T, DSL |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineers** use the OSI model to **troubleshoot** methodically (bottom-up for physical faults, top-down for application issues).
- **Equipment Vendors** design hardware for specific layers (a *Layer 2 switch* vs. a *Layer 3 router*).
- **Protocol Designers** follow layer boundaries to ensure **interoperability** between vendors.
- **Cybersecurity Analysts** use the model to understand **attack surfaces** at every layer (ARP spoofing at L2, IP spoofing at L3, etc.).

### 2.4 Key Mathematical / Quantitative Relationships

For bandwidth-delay product and throughput questions (often combined in KTU questions):

$$
\text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Delay} = \text{Volume of data in transit (bits)}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \min(\text{Link Rate},\ \text{Window Size} / \text{RTT})
$$

$$
\text{Transmission Time } T_t = \frac{\text{File Size } (L)}{\text{Bandwidth } (B)}
$$

$$
\text{Propagation Time } T_p = \frac{\text{Distance } (d)}{\text{Propagation Speed } (v)}
$$
