## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Encapsulation / Decapsulation Process — Step-by-Step Derivation

When a sender (Host A) transmits data to a receiver (Host B), each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer). This is called **encapsulation**. The reverse at the receiver is **decapsulation**.

**Scenario:** Host A sends a 1000-byte HTTP message to Host B over Ethernet using TCP/IP.

#### Step 1: Application Layer (Layer 7) — At Host A
The application (e.g., web browser) creates a message:
$$
M_{\text{app}} = \text{HTTP GET request} \quad (\text{size} = 1000\ \text{bytes})
$$
The application hands this to the Presentation layer (Layer 6). **No header added at this conceptual stage in most KTU explanations; data is treated as the message itself.**

#### Step 2: Presentation Layer (Layer 6) — Translation/Encryption
The presentation layer may compress or encrypt. Let $C$ denote compression ratio. For uncompressed data:
$$
M_{\text{pres}} = M_{\text{app}}
$$
For encryption, the encrypted payload length grows by a small fixed header $H_{\text{enc}}$:
$$
M_{\text{pres}} = M_{\text{app}} + H_{\text{enc}}
$$

#### Step 3: Session Layer (Layer 5) — Session Header
The session layer adds a session header $H_{\text{sess}}$ to maintain dialogue state:
$$
M_{\text{sess}} = M_{\text{pres}} + H_{\text{sess}}
$$

#### Step 4: Transport Layer (Layer 4) — Segmentation
The transport layer (TCP) breaks the message into **segments**. Let $L$ = total bytes, $S$ = segment size, $k$ = number of segments.

$$
k = \left\lceil \frac{L}{S} \right\rceil
$$

Each segment becomes:
$$
\text{Segment}_i = \text{Data}_i + H_{\text{TCP}}
$$
where $H_{\text{TCP}}$ is the **20-byte** TCP header (minimum) containing source port, destination port, sequence number, ACK number, flags, window size, checksum, urgent pointer.

#### Step 5: Network Layer (Layer 3) — Packetization
Each segment gets an IP header $H_{\text{IP}}$ (**20 bytes** minimum for IPv4):
$$
\text{Packet}_i = \text{Segment}_i + H_{\text{IP}}
$$
If the packet exceeds the **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)**, fragmentation occurs. For example, if MTU = 1500 bytes and $H_{\text{IP}} = 20$ bytes, the maximum payload per fragment is:
$$
\text{Data per fragment} = \text{MTU} - H_{\text{IP}} = 1500 - 20 = 1480\ \text{bytes}
$$

#### Step 6: Data Link Layer (Layer 2) — Framing
Each packet is wrapped in a frame. Ethernet II frame structure:
$$
\text{Frame} = \text{Preamble} + \text{Dest MAC} + \text{Src MAC} + \text{Type} + \text{Packet} + \text{CRC}
$$
Total Ethernet frame size:
$$
F_{\text{total}} = 8 + 6 + 6 + 2 + (\text{Packet}) + 4
$$
$$
F_{\text{total}} = 26 + L_{\text{packet}} \quad \text{(bytes)}
$$

#### Step 7: Physical Layer (Layer 1) — Bit Transmission
The frame is converted to bits and sent over the medium:
$$
N_{\text{bits}} = F_{\text{total}} \times 8
$$
Transmission time:
$$
T_t = \frac{N_{\text{bits}}}{\text{Bandwidth}}
$$

#### Step 8: Reverse Process at Host B (Decapsulation)
Host B receives bits → assembles frame (L2) → verifies CRC → strips MAC headers → passes packet to L3 → strips IP header → passes segment to L4 → reassembles segments → strips TCP header → passes data upward to L7 application.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Layer-wise Encapsulation Summary

| Layer | Input | Process | Output (PDU) | Header/Trailer Added |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 7 (App) | User data | Service request | Data | None (semantic) |
| 6 (Pres) | Data | Translate/encrypt | Data | Encryption header (variable) |
| 5 (Sess) | Data | Session management | Data | Session header |
| 4 (Trans) | Data | Segment, port addressing | **Segment** | TCP/UDP header (20/8 bytes) |
| 3 (Net) | Segment | Logical addressing, routing | **Packet** | IP header (20 bytes min) |
| 2 (DLL) | Packet | Framing, MAC, CRC | **Frame** | MAC header + CRC trailer |
| 1 (Phys) | Frame | Bit encoding | **Bits** | Preamble, electrical signal |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A 4 MB file is to be transmitted over a network. TCP segments data into 1 KB units. Compute: (a) number of segments, (b) total TCP header overhead, (c) total bytes passed to IP layer (assume TCP header = 20 bytes), (d) number of IP packets if each IP packet carries one segment.

**Given:**
$$
L = 4\ \text{MB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024\ \text{bytes} = 4{,}194{,}304\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
S = 1\ \text{KB} = 1024\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
H_{\text{TCP}} = 20\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Solution (a): Number of Segments**

$$
k = \left\lceil \frac{L}{S} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{1024} \right\rceil
$$

$$
k = 4096\ \text{segments}
$$

**Solution (b): Total TCP Header Overhead**

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\text{TCP}} = k \times H_{\text{TCP}} = 4096 \times 20 = 81{,}920\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Solution (c): Total Bytes Passed to IP Layer**

$$
\text{Total}_{\text{IP input}} = L + \text{Overhead}_{\text{TCP}} = 4{,}194{,}304 + 81{,}920 = 4{,}276{,}224\ \text{bytes}
$$

**Solution (d): Number of IP Packets**

Since 1 IP packet carries 1 TCP segment:
$$
N_{\text{IP}} = k = 4096\ \text{packets}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Such numerical problems test whether students understand that **headers consume bandwidth**. KTU often asks students to compute the **efficiency** of the protocol overhead.
>
> $$
> \eta = \frac{L}{L + \text{Total Overhead}} = \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{4{,}276{,}224} \approx 98.08\%
> $$

The diagram below illustrates how a **single message** is progressively encapsulated as it travels **down** the layers at the sender, and is transmitted as **bits** on the physical medium.

### 5.1 KTU University Examination — Practice Question Bank

---

#### **PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

*List the seven layers of the OSI reference model. Briefly explain the function of the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The seven layers of the OSI model, from top to bottom, are:
1. Application Layer (Layer 7)
2. Presentation Layer (Layer 6)
3. Session Layer (Layer 5)
4. **Transport Layer (Layer 4)**
5. Network Layer (Layer 3)
6. Data Link Layer (Layer 2)
7. Physical Layer (Layer 1)

*(+1 mark for correct listing)*

**Functions of the Transport Layer:**
- Provides **process-to-process** (end-to-end) data delivery using **port numbers**.
- Performs **segmentation and reassembly** of application messages.
- Offers **connection-oriented** (TCP) or **connectionless** (UDP) services.
- Ensures **error recovery, flow control**, and ordered delivery.
- The PDU is called a **segment** (TCP) or **datagram** (UDP).

*(+2 marks for functional explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

*Distinguish between the functions of the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Network Layer (L3) | Data Link Layer (L2) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Primary Function** | End-to-end packet delivery across networks (internetworking) | Node-to-node frame delivery on a single link |
| **Addressing** | Logical addressing (IP address) | Physical addressing (MAC address) |
| **PDU** | Packet / Datagram | Frame |
| **Key Device** | Router | Bridge, Switch |
| **Routing** | Performs path determination (routing) | Does not perform routing |
| **Scope** | Across multiple networks (inter-network) | Within a single network segment (intra-network) |

*(+3 marks for clear distinction with examples)*

---

#### **PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice as per KTU 2024 Pattern)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. List and briefly describe the functions of all seven layers. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the concept of **encapsulation and decapsulation** during data transmission. Show how data is processed at each layer. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The OSI Reference Model (7 Marks)**

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** is a seven-layer conceptual framework developed by **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** to standardize network communication between heterogeneous systems. It enables interoperability by breaking communication into modular layers, each providing services to the layer above.

**Layer-wise Functions:**

**1. Physical Layer (Layer 1)**
- Transmits raw bits over the physical medium.
- Defines mechanical, electrical, and functional specifications.
- Handles bit synchronization, transmission mode (simplex/half/full duplex), and physical topology.
- **PDU:** Bit. **Devices:** Hub, Repeater. **Examples:** RS-232, 100BASE-T.

**2. Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**
- Provides reliable **node-to-node** frame delivery on a single link.
- Performs framing, **MAC addressing (48-bit)**, error detection (CRC), and flow control.
- Sub-layers: **LLC (Logical Link Control)** and **MAC (Media Access Control)**.
- **PDU:** Frame. **Devices:** Bridge, Switch. **Examples:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11).

**3. Network Layer (Layer 3)**
- Handles **end-to-end** packet delivery across multiple networks (internetworking).
- Performs **logical addressing (IP)** and **routing**.
- Manages fragmentation and congestion at the router level.
- **PDU:** Packet. **Devices:** Router. **Examples:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF.

**4. Transport Layer (Layer 4)**
- Provides **process-to-process** delivery using **port numbers (16-bit)**.
- Performs segmentation, flow control, error recovery, and connection management.
- Connection-oriented (**TCP**) and connectionless (**UDP**) services.
- **PDU:** Segment / Datagram. **Examples:** TCP, UDP.

**5. Session Layer (Layer 5)**
- Establishes, manages, and terminates **sessions (dialogues)** between applications.
- Provides dialog control (half/full-duplex), synchronization, and token management.
- **PDU:** Data. **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, SIP.

**6. Presentation Layer (Layer 6)**
- Handles **data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression**.
- Ensures syntax and semantic compatibility between systems.
- **Examples:** SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII, MPEG.

**7. Application Layer (Layer 7)**
- Provides **network services directly to end-user applications**.
- Includes file transfer, email, web browsing, and directory services.
- **Examples:** HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet.

*(+1 mark for OSI definition; +6 marks for layer-wise functions)*

---

**(b) Encapsulation and Decapsulation (7 Marks)**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which a layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the data unit received from the upper layer. **Decapsulation** is the reverse process at the receiver.

**Diagram (Sender-Side Encapsulation):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef hdr fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000

    L7[LAYER 7: HTTP DATA]:::data
    L6[LAYER 6: ENCRYPTED DATA plus PH]:::hdr
    L5[LAYER 5: SESSION DATA plus SH]:::hdr
    L4[LAYER 4: SEGMENT plus TCP Header]:::hdr
    L3[LAYER 3: PACKET plus IP Header]:::hdr
    L2[LAYER 2: FRAME Packet plus MAC Header plus CRC]:::hdr
    L1[LAYER 1: BITS transmitted]:::data

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

**Process Explanation:**
- **Application Layer:** Generates the user message (e.g., HTTP request).
- **Presentation Layer:** Encrypts/compresses data and adds a Presentation Header (PH).
- **Session Layer:** Adds Session Header (SH) for dialogue control.
- **Transport Layer:** Segments data; each segment gets a **TCP Header** (20 bytes min).
- **Network Layer:** Wraps each segment with an **IP Header** (20 bytes min) — PDU becomes a packet.
- **Data Link Layer:** Adds **MAC Header** (source + destination MAC) and **CRC Trailer** — PDU becomes a frame.
- **Physical Layer:** Converts frame into **electrical/optical signals (bits)** for transmission.

**At the Receiver (Decapsulation):** Each layer removes its corresponding header and passes the payload to the upper layer, ultimately delivering the original HTTP message to the receiving application.

*(+2 marks for diagram; +3 marks for explanation of encapsulation; +2 marks for decapsulation explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Compare the **OSI Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Model** with a neat diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 2 MB file is transmitted using TCP with a segment size of 512 bytes. The TCP header is 20 bytes and the IP header is 20 bytes. The MTU of the link is 576 bytes. Compute: (i) number of TCP segments, (ii) number of IP packets, (iii) total bytes transmitted including all headers. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Comparison of OSI and TCP/IP Models (7 Marks)**

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef osi fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef tcp fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000

    O1[OSI: APPLICATION]:::osi
    O2[OSI: PRESENTATION]:::osi
    O3[OSI: SESSION]:::osi
    O4[OSI: TRANSPORT]:::osi
    O5[OSI: NETWORK]:::osi
    O6[OSI: DATA LINK]:::osi
    O7[OSI: PHYSICAL]:::osi

    T1[TCP/IP: APPLICATION]:::tcp
    T2[TCP/IP: TRANSPORT]:::tcp
    T3[TCP/IP: INTERNET]:::tcp
    T4[TCP/IP: NETWORK ACCESS]:::tcp

    O1 --- T1
    O2 --- T1
    O3 --- T1
    O4 --- T2
    O5 --- T3
    O6 --- T4
    O7 --- T4
```

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Number of Layers** | 7 layers | 4 layers |
| **Development** | ISO standard (ISO/IEC 7498) | DARPA (US Department of Defense) |
| **Approach** | Protocol-independent (theoretical) | Protocol-centric (practical) |
| **Adoption** | Reference model; not directly implemented | Foundation of the Internet |
| **Top Layers** | Application, Presentation, Session | Combined into single "Application" layer |
| **Transport Layer** | Present (TCP/UDP-like) | Present (TCP, UDP) |
| **Network Layer** | Network Layer | Internet Layer |
| **Bottom Layers** | Data Link + Physical | Combined as "Network Access" (Link) Layer |
| **Rigor** | Strict layer boundaries | Looser; allows cross-layer interactions |
| **Use Case** | Teaching, design, troubleshooting | Real-world Internet implementation |

*(+2 marks for diagram; +5 marks for tabular comparison with key contrasts)*

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
$$
L = 2\ \text{MB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 2{,}097{,}152\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
S = 512\ \text{bytes (TCP segment payload)}
$$
$$
H_{\text{TCP}} = 20\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
H_{\text{IP}} = 20\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
\text{MTU} = 576\ \text{bytes}
$$

**(i) Number of TCP Segments**

$$
k_{\text{TCP}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{S} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{512} \right\rceil = 4096\ \text{segments}
$$

*(+2 marks)*

**(ii) Number of IP Packets**

Each TCP segment becomes one IP packet (since $S + H_{\text{TCP}} + H_{\text{IP}} = 552 < 576$ MTU, **no fragmentation**):
$$
k_{\text{IP}} = 4096\ \text{packets}
$$

*(+2 marks)*

**(iii) Total Bytes Transmitted**

Per packet overhead = $H_{\text{TCP}} + H_{\text{IP}} = 20 + 20 = 40$ bytes
Payload per packet = 512 bytes
Per packet total = $512 + 40 = 552$ bytes

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = 4096 \times 552
$$
$$
= 4096 \times 552 = 2{,}261{,}504\ \text{bytes}
$$

Equivalently:
$$
\text{Total} = L + (k \times H_{\text{TCP}}) + (k \times H_{\text{IP}})
$$
$$
= 2{,}097{,}152 + (4096 \times 20) + (4096 \times 20)
$$
$$
= 2{,}097{,}152 + 81{,}920 + 81{,}920 = 2{,}261{,}504\ \text{bytes}
$$

*(+3 marks with full working shown)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MB to bytes correctly** (1 MB = 1024 × 1024 = 1,048,576 bytes, not 1,000,000). Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Forgetting the ceiling function** when the file size is not an exact multiple of the segment size.
> 3. **Ignoring the MTU check**: Students must verify whether fragmentation is needed. Here, $552 < 576$ so no fragmentation. Had $S$ been larger, fragmentation would have occurred and the IP packet count would be higher.
> 4. **Missing both headers**: Some students add only TCP header, forgetting the IP header, and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not showing formula substitution** in the final step — examiners in KTU mark the methodology, not just the final answer.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — OSI Reference Model**

- ✅ The OSI model has **7 layers**; memorize the mnemonic **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** (top → bottom).
- ✅ The **PDU names** are: **Application/Presentation/Session = Data**, **Transport = Segment**, **Network = Packet**, **Data Link = Frame**, **Physical = Bit**.
- ✅ The **three upper layers (5, 6, 7)** deal with **application-level concerns**; the **four lower layers (1, 2, 3, 4)** deal with **data transport concerns**.
- ✅ **Addressing** by layer: **MAC (L2)**, **IP (L3)**, **Port (L4)**.
- ✅ **Key devices** by layer: Hub/Repeater (L1) → Switch (L2) → Router (L3) → Gateway (L4–L7).
- ✅ **Encapsulation** = adding headers; **Decapsulation** = removing headers.
- ✅ **TCP = connection-oriented, reliable**; **UDP = connectionless, fast**.
- ✅ **ISO standard reference:** ISO/IEC 7498-1.
- ✅ **TCP/IP model has 4 layers**: Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access.
- ✅ **MTU** limits IP packet size → fragmentation may be needed.
- ✅ **TCP header** = 20 bytes (min); **IP header** = 20 bytes (min); **Ethernet frame overhead** = 26 bytes (header + trailer).
- ✅ **Three-way handshake** is a Transport Layer (TCP) function.
- ✅ **Routing decisions** happen at **Layer 3**; **switching** happens at **Layer 2**.
- ✅ Always check **MTU vs. (Payload + Header)** to determine **fragmentation** in numerical problems.
- ✅ For encapsulation problems, **total transmitted bytes = payload + sum of all headers** added.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP/IP Model: Layers and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TCP/IP Model: Layers and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **TCP/IP Model** (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol Model) is a concise, four-layered **conceptual framework** and **protocol suite** that governs modern data communication over the Internet. Originally developed by the U.S. **Department of Defense (DoD)** through the **DARPA** project in the **1970s**, the TCP/IP model describes the set of rules, conventions, and procedures required for end-to-end communication between heterogeneous networked devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT751 – Module 2):**
> *TCP/IP is a layered protocol architecture designed to enable reliable, connection-oriented and connectionless communication across interconnected networks. It consists of four abstraction layers — Application, Transport, Internet, and Network Access — each performing a well-defined set of functions to ensure end-to-end delivery of data.*

The model is often called the **Internet Protocol Suite** because it bundles a family of protocols (TCP, UDP, IP, ARP, ICMP, HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, etc.) that work cooperatively across the four layers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Postal Service

Imagine the TCP/IP model as a **national postal system**:

| Postal Component | TCP/IP Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Letter (Content) | Application Data | The actual message you want to send |
| Envelope + Address | Transport Header (TCP/UDP) | Adds source/destination port + sequencing |
| Postal Code / Region | IP Header (Internet Layer) | Logical addressing & routing |
| Postal Van / Truck | Network Access Layer | Physical delivery over cables/wireless |

Just as a letter is *sealed* (encapsulated) at each stage with additional addressing information as it travels from sender to receiver, **each layer of TCP/IP encapsulates** the data unit received from the layer above it, appending its own header (and sometimes trailer) to form a new **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**.

## 1.3 Why TCP/IP Matters – Engineering Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TCP/IP is the de-facto global standard:**
> 1. **Protocol Independence** – Works with any underlying physical medium (copper, fiber, RF, satellite).
> 2. **Vendor Neutrality** – Open standard governed by **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)** via **RFCs (Request for Comments)**.
> 3. **Scalability** – Supports networks from small LANs to the global Internet.
> 4. **Robustness** – Designed to survive partial network failures (e.g., packet loss, node crashes).
> 5. **Interoperability** – Enables communication between diverse hardware/OS platforms.

## 1.4 Historical & Standards Background

- **1969** – ARPANET pioneered packet switching
- **1974** – Cerf & Kahn publish the foundational TCP paper
- **1983** – ARPANET officially switches from **NCP** to **TCP/IP** on "Flag Day" (January 1)
- **1989** – **Tim Berners-Lee** at CERN invents **HTTP/HTML** on top of TCP/IP
- **Modern era** – Governed by **IETF** through **RFC 793 (TCP)**, **RFC 791 (IPv4)**, **RFC 8200 (IPv6)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Layers – Architecture Overview

The TCP/IP model is a **strictly layered architecture** where each layer provides services to the layer above it and consumes services from the layer below it. Communication is **virtual** between peer layers on different hosts, but **physical** between adjacent layers on the same host.

### **Layer 4 – Application Layer (Top)**

- **Position:** Highest abstraction layer; closest to the **end-user**.
- **Function:** Provides **network services** directly to user applications (browsers, email clients, file transfer tools).
- **PDU Name:** **Message** or **Data Stream**.
- **Key Protocols:** HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SFTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, DHCP, Telnet, SSH, SNMP, NTP.
- **Service Type:** Process-to-process user services (port-addressed).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**H**yper **T**ext **T**ransfer, **F**ile **T**ransfer, **S**imple **M**ail – all run on **Application Layer**."

### **Layer 3 – Transport Layer (Host-to-Host)**

- **Position:** Core of end-to-end communication.
- **Function:** Provides **process-to-process delivery** of messages using **port numbers**; offers reliability, flow control, multiplexing, and congestion control.
- **PDU Name:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP).
- **Key Protocols:**
  * **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** – Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream.
  * **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** – Connectionless, best-effort, low overhead.
- **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing:** Uses **16-bit source/destination port numbers** (range: **0 to 65535**).

### **Layer 2 – Internet Layer (Network Layer equivalent)**

- **Position:** Handles **logical addressing** and **routing** of packets across interconnected networks.
- **Function:** Best-effort **packet forwarding**; defines IP addressing, fragmentation/reassembly, and routing protocols.
- **PDU Name:** **Packet** or **Datagram**.
- **Key Protocols:**
  * **IP (Internet Protocol) v4 / v6** – Logical addressing & routing
  * **ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)** – Error reporting & diagnostics (e.g., `ping`, `traceroute`)
  * **ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** – Maps IP ↔ MAC
  * **RARP, IGMP, OSPF, BGP** – Routing & group management

### **Layer 1 – Network Access Layer (Link/Physical equivalent)**

- **Position:** Lowest layer; interfaces directly with the **physical transmission medium**.
- **Function:** Defines **hardware addressing (MAC)**, **framing**, **bit synchronization**, **topology**, and **physical signaling**.
- **PDU Name:** **Frame** (Data Link) / **Bits / Signals** (Physical).
- **Key Protocols/Standards:**
  * **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **PPP**, **HDLC**, **Frame Relay**, **SONET/SDH**
  * **ARP** technically straddles Layers 2 and 3

## 2.2 TCP/IP vs OSI – A Comparative Matrix

| Feature | **OSI Model (7 layers)** | **TCP/IP Model (4 layers)** |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Datalink, Physical) | 4 (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access) |
| Origin | **ISO** standard (1984) | **DARPA / DoD** (1970s) |
| Adoption | Theoretical reference model | Practical implementation model |
| Session & Presentation | Explicit separate layers | Merged into Application layer |
| Datalink & Physical | Separate layers | Combined into Network Access |
| Protocol Dependence | Protocol-independent | Protocol-centric (TCP/IP centric) |
| Usage | Teaching & design reference | Real-world Internet backbone |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The TCP/IP model collapses OSI's **Session** and **Presentation** layers into the **Application** layer, and merges **Datalink** and **Physical** into the **Network Access** layer. This is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

## 2.3 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Process

Data flow in TCP/IP is governed by **encapsulation** (sender side) and **decapsulation** (receiver side):

| Step | Layer | Action | PDU Produced |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application | User data generated | **Data** |
| 2 | Transport | Add TCP/UDP header (ports, seq#) | **Segment / Datagram** |
| 3 | Internet | Add IP header (src/dst IP, TTL) | **Packet** |
| 4 | Network Access | Add MAC header + trailer (CRC) | **Frame** |
| 5 | Physical | Convert to signals/bits | **Bits** |

On the **receiver**, the process is **reversed** — each layer **strips** its corresponding header and **passes** the payload to the layer above.

## 2.4 TCP vs UDP – Transport Layer Comparison

| Parameter | **TCP** | **UDP** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery (ACK, retransmit) | Best-effort, no ACK |
| Ordering | Maintains sequence | No sequencing |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes (slow start, cwnd) | No |
| Header Size | **20 bytes** (min) | **8 bytes** (fixed) |
| Speed | Slower (overhead) | Faster (low latency) |
| Use Cases | HTTP, FTP, Email, SSH | DNS, VoIP, Video streaming, Gaming |
| Multiplexing | Port numbers | Port numbers |

## 2.5 Port Number Ranges (KTU Important)

| Range | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **0 – 1023** | **Well-Known Ports** (system) | HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, SMTP=25, DNS=53 |
| **1024 – 49151** | **Registered Ports** (user processes) | MySQL=3306, RDP=3389 |
| **49152 – 65535** | **Dynamic/Private/Ephemeral Ports** | Assigned temporarily to client apps |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **TCP Header Min Size** | $H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ (no options) | Bytes |
| **UDP Header Size** | $H_{UDP} = 8 \text{ bytes}$ (fixed) | Bytes |
| **IP Header Size** | $H_{IPv4} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ (no options) | Bytes |
| **TCP Header Max Size** | $H_{TCP,max} = 60 \text{ bytes}$ (with options) | Bytes |
| **Port Number Range** | $0 \le P \le 65535$ | 16-bit unsigned integer |
| **Total Ports** | $N_{ports} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | Scalar |
| **IPv4 Address Size** | 32 bits = 4 octets | Dotted decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.1) |
| **IPv6 Address Size** | 128 bits = 8 hextets | Hexadecimal colon-separated |
| **MAC Address Size** | 48 bits = 6 octets | Hexadecimal (e.g., AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF) |
| **Throughput (Goodput) Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload} + H_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |
| **Total Frame Size** | $F_{total} = H_{MAC} + H_{IP} + H_{TCP/UDP} + L_{payload} + T_{FCS}$ | Bytes |
| **Header Overhead Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{H_{total}}{F_{total}}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Engineering:** Designing corporate WANs, SD-WAN, and data-center fabrics.
- **Cybersecurity:** Analysing packet captures in **Wireshark**, building firewalls, IDS/IPS systems.
- **Cloud & DevOps:** Configuring load balancers (L4 vs L7), reverse proxies, and service meshes.
- **IoT Systems:** Lightweight UDP-based protocols (MQTT, CoAP) for constrained devices.
- **5G & Telecom:** TCP/IP underpins LTE/5G core networks for IP packet switching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Header Overhead Calculation (Classic KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-Mark Question):**
A user sends an HTTP GET request over TCP/IP via Ethernet. The HTTP request payload is **800 bytes**. Compute:
- (a) Total bytes transmitted including all headers.
- (b) Header overhead as a percentage.
- (c) Protocol efficiency (goodput ratio).

### Given Data

- Payload (Application data): $L_{payload} = 800 \text{ bytes}$
- HTTP adds a small header (assume): $H_{HTTP} = 0 \text{ bytes}$ (request line + headers neglected for simplicity)
- TCP header (no options): $H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ bytes}$
- IPv4 header (no options): $H_{IPv4} = 20 \text{ bytes}$
- Ethernet header: $H_{ETH} = 14 \text{ bytes}$
- Ethernet trailer (FCS): $T_{FCS} = 4 \text{ bytes}$

### Step 1 – Total Header + Trailer Sum

$$
H_{total} = H_{ETH} + H_{IPv4} + H_{TCP} + H_{HTTP} + T_{FCS}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 14 + 20 + 20 + 0 + 4
$$

$$
H_{total} = 58 \text{ bytes}
$$

*Explanation:* At every layer of encapsulation, the corresponding protocol adds its own control information. The Ethernet layer adds a 14-byte header (MAC addresses + EtherType) and a 4-byte trailer (Frame Check Sequence for CRC error detection). The Internet layer (IPv4) adds a 20-byte header with logical addresses, TTL, etc. The Transport layer (TCP) adds a 20-byte header with ports, sequence numbers, and flags.

### Step 2 – Total Frame Size

$$
F_{total} = L_{payload} + H_{total}
$$

$$
F_{total} = 800 + 58 = 858 \text{ bytes}
$$

*Explanation:* The final frame on the wire is the sum of the original user data and all headers/trailers accumulated through the four-layer encapsulation process.

### Step 3 – Header Overhead Percentage

$$
\rho_{OH} = \frac{H_{total}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\rho_{OH} = \frac{58}{858} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\rho_{OH} \approx 6.76\%
$$

*Explanation:* The header overhead tells us what fraction of the transmitted data is "control" rather than "content". A low percentage is desirable for large payloads; for tiny packets (e.g., Telnet keystrokes), the overhead can exceed 90%.

### Step 4 – Protocol Efficiency (Goodput Ratio)

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{800}{858} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta \approx 93.24\%
$$

*Explanation:* The efficiency is the percentage of useful user data carried per frame. As the payload size grows, the efficiency approaches 100% because the fixed 58-byte header becomes proportionally smaller.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** For a **1-byte Telnet character** (e.g., keyboard key press), the efficiency drops to:
> $$\eta = \frac{1}{1+58} \times 100\% = 1.69\%$$
> This explains why interactive protocols feel "chatty" and why techniques like **Nagle's Algorithm** are used to batch small packets.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Port Number Identification & Service Mapping

**Problem Statement:** A packet arrives at a server with the following transport-layer information:

- Source IP: 192.168.1.50
- Destination IP: 10.0.0.5
- Source Port: 54321
- Destination Port: 443
- Protocol: TCP

Identify the application-layer service and classify the port type.

### Step 1 – Identify the Well-Known Port

The destination port is **443**. From the **IANA Service Name and Transport Protocol Port Number Registry**, port 443 is assigned to **HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL)**.

$$
P_{dst} = 443 \implies \text{Service} = \text{HTTPS}
$$

### Step 2 – Classify the Port Range

$$
0 \le 443 \le 1023 \implies \text{Well-Known Port (System)}
$$

The source port **54321** lies in:

$$
49152 \le 54321 \le 65535 \implies \text{Ephemeral / Dynamic Port}
$$

The client OS has assigned this temporary port to its browser for the duration of the connection.

### Step 3 – Determine the Protocol State

Since **TCP** is used, the connection would have completed a **3-way handshake**:

- SYN (client → server)
- SYN-ACK (server → client)
- ACK (client → server)

This is a **connection-oriented, reliable** HTTPS session, likely serving an encrypted web page.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — TCP/IP Layer Mapping of Real-World Protocols

**Problem Statement:** Map the following protocols to their respective TCP/IP layers:
HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet, UDP, ARP, ICMP, DNS, FTP, IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi.

### Layer-by-Layer Mapping Table

| TCP/IP Layer | Protocols Mapped |
|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | HTTP, DNS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, SNMP, Telnet, SSH, HTTPS |
| **Transport Layer** | **TCP, UDP** |
| **Internet Layer** | **IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, ARP**, IGMP, OSPF, BGP |
| **Network Access Layer** | **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, PPP, HDLC, Frame Relay |

*Explanation:* Each protocol has a specific scope of responsibility. Application-layer protocols interact with end-user software. Transport protocols manage end-to-end sessions. Internet protocols handle global addressing/routing. Network Access protocols deal with the local physical and link-layer transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** **ARP is often mistakenly placed in the Network Access layer only.** Technically, ARP **bridges** Layer 2 and Layer 3 — it maps a known Layer-3 IP address to an unknown Layer-2 MAC address. In KTU answers, it is safest to place ARP in the **Internet Layer** with a note about its dual-layer nature.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Encapsulation Walkthrough (Full Stack)

**Problem:** A user types `https://www.ktu.ac.in` in a browser. Describe the encapsulation that occurs at each TCP/IP layer.

### Detailed Walkthrough

**Step 1 – Application Layer:**
Browser generates an **HTTP request message** (e.g., `GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: www.ktu.ac.in\r\n\r\n`). This is plaintext application data of, say, **400 bytes**.

$$
PDU_{App} = \text{HTTP Request (400 bytes)}
$$

**Step 2 – Transport Layer (TCP):**
TCP adds a **20-byte header** containing source port (ephemeral, e.g., 49152), destination port (443), sequence number, ACK number, flags (SYN initially, then PSH/ACK), window size, and checksum.

$$
PDU_{Transport} = \text{TCP Header (20 B)} + \text{HTTP Request (400 B)} = 420 \text{ bytes (segment)}
$$

**Step 3 – Internet Layer (IP):**
IPv4 adds a **20-byte header** containing source IP (e.g., 192.168.1.10), destination IP (e.g., 103.0.0.1 resolved from DNS), TTL (e.g., 64), protocol (6 for TCP), and header checksum.

$$
PDU_{Network} = \text{IP Header (20 B)} + \text{TCP Segment (420 B)} = 440 \text{ bytes (packet)}
$$

**Step 4 – Network Access Layer (Ethernet):**
Ethernet adds a **14-byte header** (destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType 0x0800 for IPv4) and a **4-byte FCS trailer** for CRC error detection.

$$
PDU_{Link} = \text{Eth Header (14 B)} + \text{IP Packet (440 B)} + \text{FCS (4 B)} = 458 \text{ bytes (frame)}
$$

**Step 5 – Physical Layer:**
The 458-byte frame is converted into **electrical/optical/RF signals** and transmitted bit by bit over the medium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bidirectional Note:** On the server side, decapsulation occurs in **reverse order** — the Ethernet frame is parsed first, the FCS is checked, then the IP header is examined for routing decisions, then the TCP header is processed for reliability, and finally the HTTP payload is handed to the web server software.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TCP/IP Four-Layer Model — Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["LAYER 4 APPLICATION LAYER"]
        A1[HTTP HTTPS]
        A2[FTP SFTP]
        A3[SMTP POP3 IMAP]
        A4[DNS DHCP SSH]
    end

    subgraph TR["LAYER 3 TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        T1[TCP Transmission Control Protocol]
        T2[UDP User Datagram Protocol]
    end

    subgraph NET["LAYER 2 INTERNET LAYER"]
        N1[IPv4 IPv6 Logical Addressing]
        N2[ICMP ARP IGMP]
        N3[OSPF BGP Routing]
    end

    subgraph NA["LAYER 1 NETWORK ACCESS LAYER"]
        L1[Ethernet IEEE 802.3]
        L2[Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11]
        L3[PPP HDLC Frame Relay]
        L4[MAC Addressing Framing]
    end

    APP --> TR
    TR --> NET
    NET --> NA
    NA --> PHY[Physical Medium Copper Fiber RF]

    classDef appLayer fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef trLayer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef netLayer fill:#90EE90,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
    classDef naLayer fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 appLayer
    class T1,T2 trLayer
    class N1,N2,N3 netLayer
    class L1,L2,L3,L4 naLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Data flows **downward** during encapsulation (sender side) and **upward** during decapsulation (receiver side). The arrow `APP → TR` indicates the Application layer passes its PDU to the Transport layer for additional header encapsulation.

---

## 4.2 Encapsulation & Decapsulation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER["SENDER HOST"]
        direction TB
        S1[User Data 800 bytes] --> S2[TCP Header 20 bytes]
        S2 --> S3[IP Header 20 bytes]
        S3 --> S4[Ethernet Header 14 bytes]
        S4 --> S5[FCS Trailer 4 bytes]
        S5 --> S6[Physical Signal Bits]
    end

    S6 -->|Network Medium| R1

    subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVER HOST"]
        direction TB
        R1[Receive Signal Bits] --> R2[Strip FCS Check CRC]
        R2 --> R3[Strip Ethernet Header]
        R3 --> R4[Strip IP Header]
        R4 --> R5[Strip TCP Header]
        R5 --> R6[Deliver to Application]
    end

    classDef encap fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef decap fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef medium fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6 encap
    class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5,R6 decap
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always label the **PDU at each stage** — Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bits. Examiners award marks for correct PDU naming.

---

## 4.3 TCP 3-Way Handshake Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLIENT
    participant SERVER

    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Connection Establishment Phase

    CLIENT->>SERVER: SYN seq=100 ACK=0
    Note over CLIENT: Client enters SYN_SENT state

    SERVER->>CLIENT: SYN seq=300 ACK=101
    Note over SERVER: Server enters SYN_RECEIVED state

    CLIENT->>SERVER: ACK=301 seq=101
    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Connection ESTABLISHED

    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Data Transfer Phase

    CLIENT->>SERVER: Data Packet seq=101 ACK=301
    SERVER->>CLIENT: ACK seq=301 ACK=102

    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Connection Termination Phase

    CLIENT->>SERVER: FIN seq=102
    SERVER->>CLIENT: ACK seq=103
    SERVER->>CLIENT: FIN seq=302
    CLIENT->>SERVER: ACK seq=303
    Note over CLIENT,SERVER: Connection CLOSED
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3-way?** The 3-way handshake ensures that **both sides** have sent and received a sequence-number synchronization packet, guaranteeing that the connection is fully bidirectional and reliable before any data is exchanged.

---

## 4.4 TCP/IP vs OSI — Layer Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OSI["OSI MODEL 7 LAYERS"]
        O1[7 Application]
        O2[6 Presentation]
        O3[5 Session]
        O4[4 Transport]
        O5[3 Network]
        O6[2 Data Link]
        O7[1 Physical]
    end

    subgraph TCP["TCP/IP MODEL 4 LAYERS"]
        T1[4 Application Layer]
        T2[3 Transport Layer]
        T3[2 Internet Layer]
        T4[1 Network Access Layer]
    end

    O1 --- T1
    O2 --- T1
    O3 --- T1
    O4 --- T2
    O5 --- T3
    O6 --- T4
    O7 --- T4

    classDef osiStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    classDef tcpStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5,O6,O7 osiStyle
    class T1,T2,T3,T4 tcpStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping Note:** The dashed lines indicate functional equivalence. OSI Layers 5, 6, and 7 are all absorbed into TCP/IP's single **Application Layer**. OSI Layers 1 and 2 are merged into TCP/IP's **Network Access Layer**.

---

## 4.5 Port Number Categorization — Visual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PORTS["TCP UDP PORT RANGE 0 to 65535"]
        P1["WELL-KNOWN PORTS 0 to 1023"]
        P2["REGISTERED PORTS 1024 to 49151"]
        P3["DYNAMIC EPHEMERAL PORTS 49152 to 65535"]
    end

    P1 --> P1A["HTTP 80"]
    P1 --> P1B["HTTPS 443"]
    P1 --> P1C["FTP 21 SSH 22"]
    P1 --> P1D["SMTP 25 DNS 53"]

    P2 --> P2A["MySQL 3306"]
    P2 --> P2B["RDP 3389"]

    P3 --> P3A["Assigned by OS to clients"]
    P3 --> P3B["Released after connection close"]

    classDef wkPort fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff
    classDef regPort fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef dynPort fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class P1,P1A,P1B,P1C,P1D wkPort
    class P2,P2A,P2B regPort
    class P3,P3A,P3B dynPort
```

---

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Data Flow Across Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    USER[End User Action Browse Web] --> APP1[Application Layer HTTP Request Generated]
    APP1 --> APP2[Data Unit 800 bytes]

    APP2 --> TR1[Transport Layer TCP Header Added]
    TR1 --> TR2[Segment 820 bytes Source Port Dst Port 443]

    TR2 --> NET1[Internet Layer IP Header Added]
    NET1 --> NET2[Packet 840 bytes Src IP Dst IP TTL 64]

    NET2 --> NA1[Network Access Layer Ethernet Header FCS Added]
    NA1 --> NA2[Frame 858 bytes MAC src MAC dst]

    NA2 --> PHY1[Physical Layer Electrical Optical RF Signals]
    PHY1 --> WIRE[Transmission Medium Cable Fiber Air]

    WIRE --> PHY2[Physical Layer Receiver Bits Recovered]
    PHY2 --> NA3[Network Access Layer FCS Checked Ethernet Header Stripped]
    NA3 --> NET3[Internet Layer IP Header Stripped Routing Decided]
    NET3 --> TR3[Transport Layer TCP Header Stripped Port Demultiplexed]
    TR3 --> APP3[Application Layer HTTP Payload Delivered to Web Server]

    classDef sendSide fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000
    classDef recvSide fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B
    class USER,APP1,APP2,TR1,TR2,NET1,NET2,NA1,NA2,PHY1 sendSide
    class PHY2,NA3,NET3,TR3,APP3 recvSide
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** Always draw **PDU names** on the right side of each layer block — *Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Signals*. This shows the examiner that you understand the encapsulation mechanism, not just layer names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List the four layers of the TCP/IP model and state two protocols that operate at each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The TCP/IP model consists of four layers, each responsible for a specific set of communication functions:

1. **Application Layer (Layer 4)** – Provides network services to end-user applications.
   * Protocols: **HTTP**, **HTTPS**, **FTP**, **SMTP**, **DNS**, **SSH**, **Telnet**, **SNMP**.
2. **Transport Layer (Layer 3)** – Provides process-to-process delivery using port numbers.
   * Protocols: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**, **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**.
3. **Internet Layer (Layer 2)** – Handles logical addressing, routing, and packet forwarding.
   * Protocols: **IP (IPv4/IPv6)**, **ICMP**, **ARP**, **IGMP**, **OSPF**, **BGP**.
4. **Network Access Layer (Layer 1)** – Interfaces with the physical medium; handles framing and MAC addressing.
   * Protocols/Standards: **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **PPP**, **HDLC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for listing layers + 1 mark for 2 protocols per layer + 1 mark for layer numbering.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between TCP and UDP in the Transport Layer of the TCP/IP model.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **TCP** | **UDP** |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented (3-way handshake) | Connectionless (no handshake) |
| Reliability | Guaranteed delivery using ACK, sequence numbers, retransmission | Best-effort, no ACK, no retransmission |
| Header Size | 20 bytes (min), 60 bytes (max with options) | 8 bytes (fixed) |
| Flow & Congestion Control | Yes (sliding window, slow start) | No |
| Speed & Overhead | Slower, more overhead | Faster, minimal overhead |
| Use Cases | HTTP, FTP, Email, SSH, Banking apps | DNS queries, VoIP, video streaming, gaming |

**Conclusion:** TCP is used when **reliability and ordered delivery** are critical (e.g., file transfer, web pages), whereas UDP is used when **low latency and speed** matter more than perfect reliability (e.g., live video, online gaming).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for connection type & reliability, 1 mark for header + use case comparison.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the TCP/IP model in detail. Compare the TCP/IP model with the OSI model. List the functions of each layer in TCP/IP.**

#### **(a) TCP/IP Model Architecture and Layer Functions (7 Marks)**

The TCP/IP model is a four-layered protocol architecture designed to enable end-to-end communication across heterogeneous networks.

**Layer 1 – Network Access Layer (Link/Physical):**
- Combines OSI's Data Link and Physical layers.
- Defines **MAC addressing (48-bit)**, **framing**, **bit synchronization**, **topology**, and **physical signaling** (electrical, optical, RF).
- Protocols: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, HDLC.
- PDU: **Frame**.

**Layer 2 – Internet Layer:**
- Provides **logical addressing (IP)** and **routing** of packets across networks.
- Handles **fragmentation** and **reassembly** of packets.
- Protocols: IPv4, IPv6, ICMP (for `ping`, errors), ARP (IP↔MAC), IGMP, OSPF, BGP.
- PDU: **Packet / Datagram**.

**Layer 3 – Transport Layer:**
- Provides **host-to-host**, **process-to-process** delivery using **port numbers** (0–65535).
- **TCP**: Connection-oriented, reliable, byte-stream oriented.
- **UDP**: Connectionless, best-effort, low-overhead.
- Multiplexing/Demultiplexing of applications.
- PDU: **Segment (TCP)** / **Datagram (UDP)**.

**Layer 4 – Application Layer:**
- Provides **user-level services** and interfaces with software applications.
- Combines OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers.
- Protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, DNS, DHCP, Telnet, SSH.
- PDU: **Message / Data**.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LAYER 4 APPLICATION HTTP FTP DNS SMTP"]
    L3["LAYER 3 TRANSPORT TCP UDP Port Based"]
    L2["LAYER 2 INTERNET IP ICMP ARP Routing"]
    L1["LAYER 1 NETWORK ACCESS Ethernet WiFi MAC Framing"]

    L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1

    classDef l4Style fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    classDef l3Style fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4
    classDef l2Style fill:#90EE90,stroke:#2E8B57
    classDef l1Style fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C
    class L4 l4Style
    class L3 l3Style
    class L2 l2Style
    class L1 l1Style
```

#### **(b) TCP/IP vs OSI Model Comparison (7 Marks)**

| Feature | **OSI Model** | **TCP/IP Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Layers | 7 (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) | 4 (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access) |
| Developed By | ISO (International Organization for Standardization) | DARPA / DoD (USA) |
| Year of Origin | 1984 | 1970s (standardized 1983) |
| Approach | Protocol-independent generic model | Protocol-centric (built around TCP/IP) |
| Layer Separation | Strict separation of Session & Presentation | Session & Presentation merged into Application |
| Physical & Data Link | Two separate layers | Combined into Network Access Layer |
| Adoption | Reference model for teaching and design | Practical implementation model for the Internet |
| Top-Down vs Bottom-Up | Top-down design philosophy | Bottom-up (protocol-first) philosophy |
| Example Protocols | Generic (any protocol fits) | TCP, IP, UDP, HTTP, FTP (specific) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** OSI is a **theoretical framework** developed by standards bodies, while TCP/IP is a **practical implementation** that became the de-facto global standard for the Internet. TCP/IP essentially merged and simplified three of the OSI layers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Don't confuse Application Layer positions** — In TCP/IP, the Application Layer is the **topmost** (Layer 4), and Network Access is the **bottommost** (Layer 1). In some textbooks, layers are numbered top-down, in others bottom-up. **State your numbering convention clearly** at the start of your answer.
> 2. **Don't claim OSI is obsolete** — The OSI model is still widely used for **teaching, design, and troubleshooting**. It is the **TCP/IP protocols** that are dominant in implementation, not necessarily the OSI model.
> 3. **Don't omit the diagram** — A neat diagram (even hand-drawn) of the four layers with their functions is mandatory for full marks (at least 2–3 marks reserved for it).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: Explain the concept of encapsulation and decapsulation in the TCP/IP model. With a suitable example, compute the total frame size, header overhead percentage, and protocol efficiency for an HTTP request of 600 bytes sent over TCP, IPv4, and Ethernet.**

#### **(a) Concept of Encapsulation and Decapsulation (7 Marks)**

**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer in the TCP/IP model **adds its own header (and sometimes trailer)** to the data unit received from the layer above, before passing it to the lower layer. This creates a nested structure of Protocol Data Units (PDUs).

**Decapsulation** is the **reverse process** performed at the receiver, where each layer **removes (strips)** its corresponding header and passes the payload to the layer above.

**Encapsulation Process (Sender Side):**

| Step | Layer | Action | PDU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application | User data generated | **Data** |
| 2 | Transport | TCP/UDP header added | **Segment / Datagram** |
| 3 | Internet | IP header added | **Packet** |
| 4 | Network Access | MAC header + FCS trailer added | **Frame** |
| 5 | Physical | Converted to signals | **Bits** |

**Decapsulation Process (Receiver Side):**

| Step | Layer | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical | Bits recovered from signal |
| 2 | Network Access | FCS verified, MAC header removed, frame handed up |
| 3 | Internet | IP header removed, packet handed up |
| 4 | Transport | TCP/UDP header removed, segment handed up |
| 5 | Application | Data delivered to application software |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Data] --> B[+TCP Header 20B]
    B --> C[Segment 620B]
    C --> D[+IP Header 20B]
    D --> E[Packet 640B]
    E --> F[+Eth Header 14B + FCS 4B]
    F --> G[Frame 658B]
    G --> H[Physical Bits]
```

#### **(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- HTTP payload: $L_{payload} = 600 \text{ bytes}$
- TCP header (no options): $H_{TCP} = 20 \text{ bytes}$
- IPv4 header (no options): $H_{IPv4} = 20 \text{ bytes}$
- Ethernet header: $H_{ETH} = 14 \text{ bytes}$
- Ethernet trailer (FCS): $T_{FCS} = 4 \text{ bytes}$

**Step 1 – Total Header + Trailer**

$$
H_{total} = H_{ETH} + H_{IPv4} + H_{TCP} + T_{FCS}
$$

$$
H_{total} = 14 + 20 + 20 + 4 = 58 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 – Total Frame Size**

$$
F_{total} = L_{payload} + H_{total} = 600 + 58 = 658 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 – Header Overhead Percentage**

$$
\rho_{OH} = \frac{H_{total}}{F_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{58}{658} \times 100\% \approx 8.81\%
$$

**Step 4 – Protocol Efficiency (Goodput Ratio)**

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{F_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{600}{658} \times 100\% \approx 91.19\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For this 600-byte HTTP request over TCP/IP/Ethernet, the protocol stack adds **58 bytes of overhead**, resulting in a final frame of **658 bytes** on the wire. The header overhead is approximately **8.81%**, and the protocol efficiency is **91.19%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting the FCS trailer** — Many students add only the headers and forget the 4-byte Ethernet FCS trailer. This results in a wrong frame size. Always include the trailer for Ethernet frames.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion** — If the question gives payload in **bits**, convert to **bytes** first (divide by 8) before applying the formulas.
> 3. **Confusing "efficiency" and "overhead"** — They are complementary but not the same. Overhead = $\frac{H}{F}\times 100\%$, Efficiency = $\frac{L}{F}\times 100\%$. Their sum equals 100%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – TCP/IP Model**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize**

- TCP/IP has **4 layers**: Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access (top → bottom).
- TCP/IP was developed by **DARPA (DoD)** in the **1970s**; standardized in **1983**.
- Governed by **IETF** through **RFCs** (e.g., RFC 791 for IPv4, RFC 793 for TCP, RFC 768 for UDP, RFC 8200 for IPv6).
- The TCP/IP model is **practical** and **protocol-centric**, whereas the OSI model is **theoretical** and **generic**.

### **Layer-wise Functions (Quick Recall)**

- **Application Layer** → User services, process-to-process (port-addressed); PDU = **Data/Message**.
- **Transport Layer** → Host-to-host delivery; **TCP (reliable)** vs **UDP (unreliable)**; PDU = **Segment / Datagram**.
- **Internet Layer** → Logical addressing & routing; IP, ICMP, ARP; PDU = **Packet**.
- **Network Access Layer** → Physical + Data Link; MAC addressing, framing; PDU = **Frame / Bits**.

### **Critical Port Numbers (Must-Know)**

- **HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, FTP = 21, SSH = 22, Telnet = 23, SMTP = 25, DNS = 53, DHCP = 67/68, POP3 = 110, IMAP = 143**.
- Well-known ports: **0–1023**; Registered: **1024–49151**; Ephemeral: **49152–65535**.

### **Header Size Reference**

- **TCP Header** = 20 bytes (min), 60 bytes (max).
- **UDP Header** = 8 bytes (fixed).
- **IPv4 Header** = 20 bytes (min), 60 bytes (max).
- **Ethernet Header** = 14 bytes; **FCS Trailer** = 4 bytes.

### **PDU Naming Convention (PDU Per Layer)**

| Layer | PDU Name |
|---|---|
| Application | Data / Message |
| Transport | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) |
| Internet | Packet / Datagram |
| Network Access | Frame |
| Physical | Bits / Signals |

### **TCP vs UDP – One-Liners for Exam**

- TCP = **3-way handshake + ACK + retransmission + ordered**.
- UDP = **No handshake + no ACK + no order + low overhead + low latency**.

### **Encapsulation/Decapsulation Order**

- Sender: Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bits (top-down).
- Receiver: Bits → Frame → Packet → Segment → Data (bottom-up).

### **Frequently Asked Numerical Formula**

$$
F_{total} = H_{MAC} + H_{IP} + H_{TCP/UDP} + L_{payload} + T_{FCS}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\rho_{OH} = \frac{H_{total}}{F_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

### **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid**

- **Placing ARP in Network Access layer only** — It's a Layer 2/3 hybrid.
- **Calling TCP/IP a 5-layer or 7-layer model** — It has exactly 4 layers.
- **Confusing "Transport" with "Transmission"** — The Transport layer is host-to-host, NOT signal transmission (that's Physical).
- **Mixing up port and IP addresses** — Ports are **16-bit Transport-layer** identifiers; IPs are **32/128-bit Network-layer** identifiers.
- **Forgetting the FCS trailer in frame-size calculations** — Always add 4 bytes for Ethernet CRC.
- **Saying "TCP/IP is the same as OSI"** — They are different in design, layer count, and origin.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Schemes: MAC Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Addressing Schemes: MAC Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Media Access Control (MAC) Address** is a **48-bit** (or 64-bit in the IPv6/IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 era) hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface controller (NIC) at the time of manufacture. It operates at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model and is standardized under the **IEEE 802** family of protocols (specifically IEEE 802.3 for Ethernet and IEEE 802.11 for Wi-Fi).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT751 Module 2):**
> The MAC sublayer of the Data Link Layer is responsible for physical addressing, frame delimitation, channel access control, and error checking. The MAC address is the cornerstone identifier used by the MAC sublayer to deliver frames to the correct node on a Local Area Network (LAN).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your college's internal mailroom. Every student has a **Roll Number** that never changes regardless of which classroom they sit in (this is your **IP address** — it is *logical* and *changeable*). However, to deliver a physical letter inside a single classroom, the peon needs to know the **exact seat number** of the student (this is your **MAC address** — it is *physical*, *burned-in*, and *unique* to the hardware interface).

- **IP Address** = Your current *mailing address* (logical, hierarchical, can change).
- **MAC Address** = Your *fingerprint* (physical, flat, permanent to the NIC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MAC address is sometimes referred to as the **BIA (Burned-In Address)** because it is physically hard-coded into the NIC's ROM by the manufacturer. However, modern operating systems allow software-level override known as **MAC Spoofing**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Length:** **48 bits** = 6 Bytes = 12 Hexadecimal digits.
- **Total unique addresses:** **2^48 ≈ 281.47 trillion** (formally 281,474,976,710,656).
- **Notation:** Colon-Hexadecimal (`00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`) or Hyphen-Hexadecimal (`00-1A-2B-3C-4D-5E`).
- **Governing Body:** **IEEE Registration Authority** (allocates the OUI prefix to vendors).
- **Example:** The MAC address `AC:DE:48:00:11:22` is assigned by the vendor holding OUI `AC:DE:48`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 48-Bit MAC Address Structure

A 48-bit MAC address is conceptually divided into two primary fields, each 24 bits long:

| Field | Size | Allocation Authority | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)** | **24 bits** | IEEE Registration Authority | Identifies the **vendor / manufacturer** (e.g., Cisco, Intel, Apple). |
| **NIC Specific (Device ID)** | **24 bits** | The Vendor (manufacturer) | Identifies the **individual hardware interface** (the unique serial of that NIC). |

#### 2.1.1 The Two Special Control Bits (Hidden in the OUI)

Although a MAC address looks like 12 raw hex digits, two bits inside the **first byte** of the OUI carry special meaning:

- **I/G Bit (Individual/Group Bit) — Least Significant Bit (LSB) of the first byte.**
  - `0` → **Unicast** (frame destined for a single specific NIC).
  - `1` → **Multicast** (frame destined for a group of NICs).

- **U/L Bit (Universal/Local Bit) — Second LSB of the first byte.**
  - `0` → **UAA (Universally Administered Address)** — burned-in by the manufacturer.
  - `1` → **LAA (Locally Administered Address)** — manually overridden by a system administrator.

### 2.2 Address Types in Ethernet

| Type | MAC Pattern | Scope | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unicast** | I/G bit = 0 | One specific device | `AC:DE:48:00:11:22` |
| **Multicast** | I/G bit = 1, LSB = 1 | A logical group of devices | `01:00:5E:00:00:FB` (OSPF) |
| **Broadcast** | All 48 bits = 1 | Every device on the LAN | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total unique 48-bit MACs | $N = 2^{48}$ | Addresses | ≈ $2.81 \times 10^{14}$ |
| Vendor-assignable space | $S = 2^{24}$ | Per OUI | Each vendor gets this many device IDs per OUI block. |
| First-byte I/G bit | $\text{bit}_0 = \text{LSB}(\text{Byte}_1)$ | Bit (0 or 1) | Determines Unicast vs. Multicast. |
| First-byte U/L bit | $\text{bit}_1 = \text{LSB}_{2}(\text{Byte}_1)$ | Bit (0 or 1) | Determines Universal vs. Local. |
| Hex-to-Binary weight | $1 \text{ hex digit} = 4 \text{ bits}$ | Bits | Useful for bit extraction problems. |
| Broadcast address | $\underbrace{\text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}}_{48 \text{ 1-bits}}$ | Hex | Always forwarded by all switches (flooded). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** MAC addresses are the foundation of **switched Ethernet**. When a switch receives a frame, it reads the **Source MAC** to build its **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) Table**, and uses the **Destination MAC** to forward the frame out of the correct port. Without MAC addresses, Layer 2 switching is impossible.

### 2.4 MAC Address vs. IP Address — Layered Interaction

The MAC address is used strictly within a **single broadcast domain (LAN)**. When a packet must traverse routers, the Layer 2 frame is stripped and re-encapsulated at every hop. Therefore:
- **Source MAC & Destination MAC change** at every router hop.
- **Source IP & Destination IP remain constant** end-to-end (assuming no NAT).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Extracting the I/G and U/L Bits

**Given:** MAC Address = `A2:1B:3C:4D:5E:6F`

**Step 1 — Identify the first byte.**
The first byte of the MAC address is `A2` (in hexadecimal).

**Step 2 — Convert the first byte to binary.**

The hex digit `A` represents decimal 10, and `2` represents decimal 2. We convert each hex digit into its 4-bit binary equivalent.

$$
A = 10_{10} = 1010_2
$$

$$
2 = 2_{10} = 0010_2
$$

Concatenating these two nibbles, we get the full 8-bit binary representation:

$$
\text{Byte}_1 = A2_{16} = \underbrace{1010}_{A} \;\underbrace{0010}_{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the I/G bit (LSB of the first byte).**

The I/G bit is the *least significant bit* of the first byte. In the binary string `1010 0010`, the LSB is the rightmost bit.

$$
\text{I/G bit} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{This is a \textbf{Unicast} address.}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the U/L bit (second LSB of the first byte).**

The U/L bit is the *second least significant bit* of the first byte. In the binary string `1010 0010`, counting from the right, bit 1 is the LSB and bit 2 is the U/L bit.

$$
\text{U/L bit} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{This is a \textbf{Locally Administered Address (LAA)}.}
$$

**Conclusion:** The MAC address `A2:1B:3C:4D:5E:6F` is a **Unicast, Locally Administered Address** (modified from its original burned-in value).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Number of Unique MAC Addresses

**Derivation:**

A MAC address consists of 48 independent bits. Each bit can be either 0 or 1. The total number of unique combinations is therefore:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^{48}
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{10} \times 2^{8}
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 256
$$

$$
N_{\text{total}} \approx 281{,}474{,}976{,}710{,}656 \text{ addresses}
$$

**Step 2 — Per Vendor (OUI) space.**

The IEEE assigns a 24-bit OUI to each vendor. The remaining 24 bits are used for device-specific addressing, giving each vendor:

$$
N_{\text{OUI}} = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ devices per OUI}
$$

**Step 3 — Reserved Multicast Space.**

IEEE has reserved the top 24 bits as `01:00:5E` (for IPv4 multicast) and `33:33:` (for IPv6 multicast). The total reserved multicast space reserved from the OUI space is:

$$
N_{\text{multicast}} = 2^{24} \text{ (multicast OUI prefix pool)}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Vendor Identification from a MAC

**Given:** MAC Address = `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`

**Step 1 — Extract the OUI (first 3 bytes).**

$$
\text{OUI} = 00:1A:2B
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-reference the IEEE OUI database.**

The OUI `00:1A:2B` is registered to **Apple, Inc.** This means any device whose MAC address begins with `00:1A:2B` was manufactured by Apple (or an Apple subsidiary).

**Step 3 — Extract the NIC-specific identifier (last 3 bytes).**

$$
\text{NIC ID} = 3C:4D:5E
$$

This identifies *this specific NIC* among the $2^{24}$ devices Apple can produce under this OUI block.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Problem 4 — Forming a Multicast MAC from an IP

IPv4 multicast MACs are derived from IPv4 multicast group addresses (Class D, range `224.0.0.0` to `239.255.255.255`).

**Given:** Multicast IP = `224.0.0.10` (OSPF routers)

**Step 1 — Convert IP to binary.**

$$
224.0.0.10 = \underbrace{11100000}_{224} \; \underbrace{00000000}_{0} \; \underbrace{00000000}_{0} \; \underbrace{00001010}_{10}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mapping rule.**

Drop the upper 9 bits of the IP and prepend the fixed OUI `01:00:5E`. The lowest bit of the first octet is forced to 0.

$$
\text{Multicast MAC} = 01:00:5E:00:00:0A
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Notice that the **last 23 bits** of the IP are mapped, but only **23 bits** of the IP fit into the **lower 23 bits** of the MAC. This means **32 different multicast IP addresses can map to the same multicast MAC**, causing the NIC to filter and pass them to the upper layers for disambiguation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MAC Address Field Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["48-BIT MAC ADDRESS"] --> B["FIRST 24 BITS : OUI : VENDOR ID"]
    A --> C["LAST 24 BITS : NIC SERIAL : DEVICE ID"]
    B --> B1["BIT 7 : U/L FLAG"]
    B --> B2["BIT 6 : I/G FLAG"]
    B --> B3["BITS 5-0 : VENDOR SPECIFIC"]
    C --> D["UNIQUE PER HARDWARE INTERFACE"]
    B1 --> E["0 EQUALS UNIVERSAL"]
    B1 --> F["1 EQUALS LOCAL OVERRIDE"]
    B2 --> G["0 EQUALS UNICAST"]
    B2 --> H["1 EQUALS MULTICAST"]
```

### 4.2 Ethernet Frame Encapsulation Showing MAC Fields

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["PREAMBLE : 7 BYTES"] --> SFD["SFD : 1 BYTE"]
    SFD --> DA["DESTINATION MAC : 6 BYTES"]
    DA --> SA["SOURCE MAC : 6 BYTES"]
    SA --> LT["LENGTH OR TYPE : 2 BYTES"]
    LT --> PAY["PAYLOAD : 46 TO 1500 BYTES"]
    PAY --> FCS["FCS CRC32 : 4 BYTES"]
    DA --> DAN["EQUALS FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF IF BROADCAST"]
    SA --> SAN["ORIGINATING NIC HARDWARE ADDRESS"]
```

### 4.3 MAC Address Resolution Flow (ARP Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HOST A WANTS TO SEND IP PACKET TO HOST B"] --> B["IS HOST B MAC IN ARP CACHE"]
    B -->|YES| C["ENCAP IN FRAME WITH DEST MAC OF HOST B"]
    B -->|NO| D["BROADCAST ARP REQUEST : WHO HAS IP B TELL IP A"]
    D --> E["ALL HOSTS ON LAN RECEIVE ARP REQUEST"]
    E --> F["HOST B RECOGNIZES ITS OWN IP"]
    F --> G["HOST B SENDS UNICAST ARP REPLY WITH ITS MAC"]
    G --> H["HOST A STORES HOST B MAC IN ARP CACHE"]
    H --> C
    C --> I["SWITCH FORWARDS FRAME OUT OF HOST B PORT"]
```

### 4.4 Address Hierarchy Across OSI Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 7 TO 3 : LOGICAL ADDRESSING : IP ADDRESS : 32 BIT IPV4 OR 128 BIT IPV6"] --> L2["LAYER 2 : PHYSICAL ADDRESSING : MAC ADDRESS : 48 BIT BURNED IN"]
    L2 --> L1["LAYER 1 : ELECTRICAL OR OPTICAL SIGNALS : BITS ON WIRE"]
    L5 --> NOTE["IP CHANGES AT EVERY ROUTER HOP"]
    L2 --> NOTE2["MAC CHANGES AT EVERY ROUTER HOP"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a MAC address? Explain the significance of the I/G and U/L bits in a MAC address.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **MAC (Media Access Control) address** is a 48-bit physical address uniquely assigned to a network interface card (NIC) at the time of manufacture, operating at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. (1 mark)

The **I/G bit (Individual/Group bit)** is the least significant bit of the first byte of the MAC address.
- If `0` → the address is a **unicast** address (single recipient).
- If `1` → the address is a **multicast/broadcast** address (group or all recipients). (1 mark)

The **U/L bit (Universal/Local bit)** is the second least significant bit of the first byte.
- If `0` → the address is a **Universally Administered Address (UAA)**, assigned by the manufacturer.
- If `1` → the address is a **Locally Administered Address (LAA)**, manually overridden by software. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Unicast, Multicast, and Broadcast MAC addresses with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | I/G Bit | Scope | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Unicast** | 0 | Single specific NIC | `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E` |
| **Multicast** | 1 | A subscribed group of NICs | `01:00:5E:00:00:0A` |
| **Broadcast** | 1 (all bits 1) | All NICs in the LAN | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` |

(3 marks — 1 for each correct type with example)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Explain the structure of a 48-bit MAC address. Differentiate between OUI and NIC-specific portions. How many unique MAC addresses can be assigned per OUI block? (7 Marks)

**(b)** Given the MAC address `4C:CC:6A:AB:CD:EF`, determine whether it is Unicast/Multicast and Universal/Local. Explain the role of the ARP protocol in mapping IP addresses to MAC addresses. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — Structure (3 marks):**
A 48-bit MAC address is divided into two 24-bit halves. The **first 24 bits** form the **OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier)**, assigned by the IEEE to identify the manufacturer (e.g., Cisco, Intel). The **last 24 bits** form the **NIC-specific (Device ID)** portion, assigned by the manufacturer to uniquely identify each NIC.

**Step 2 — Unique addresses per OUI (2 marks):**
Since the device ID is 24 bits wide, each vendor OUI supports:

$$
N = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216 \text{ unique devices}
$$

**Step 3 — Total address space (2 marks):**
Globally, the total number of unique MAC addresses is:

$$
N_{\text{global}} = 2^{48} = 281{,}474{,}976{,}710{,}656
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert the first byte `4C` to binary (3 marks):**

$$
4 = 0100_2 \quad ; \quad C = 1100_2
$$

$$
\text{Byte}_1 = 4C_{16} = \underbrace{0100}_{4} \;\underbrace{1100}_{C} = 01001100_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify I/G and U/L bits (2 marks):**
- **I/G bit** = LSB of `4C` = `0` → **Unicast**.
- **U/L bit** = Second LSB of `4C` = `0` → **Universally Administered Address (UAA)**.

**Step 3 — ARP role (2 marks):**
**ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)** maps a known Layer 3 IPv4 address to an unknown Layer 2 MAC address within a local broadcast domain. The requesting host broadcasts an ARP Request ("Who has IP X? Tell me your MAC"), and the owner of IP X responds with a unicast ARP Reply containing its MAC address, which is then cached in the ARP table.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]*

**(a)** Describe the IEEE 802 MAC sublayer addressing scheme. Explain how a multicast MAC address is derived from an IPv4 multicast group address with a suitable example. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A host has MAC address `12:34:56:78:9A:BC`. Determine the I/G and U/L bits, the OUI, and the NIC-specific identifier. Also explain how switches use MAC addresses for frame forwarding. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Step 1 — IEEE 802 MAC sublayer (3 marks):**
The IEEE 802 standard defines the MAC sublayer of the Data Link Layer. It uses 48-bit addresses (sometimes extended to 64-bit for IEEE 802.15.4 / EUI-64). The MAC sublayer handles **physical addressing**, **frame delimitation**, **CSMA/CD or CSMA/CA channel access**, and **error detection** via CRC.

**Step 2 — Multicast mapping rule (2 marks):**
For IPv4 multicast (Class D, range `224.0.0.0`–`239.255.255.255`):
- The lower **23 bits** of the IP are mapped into the lower 23 bits of the MAC.
- The upper 25 bits are fixed as `00000001:00000000:01011110:0` → `01:00:5E:0`.

**Step 3 — Worked example (2 marks):**
For IP `239.192.15.34` (binary ends with `...11000000:00001111:00100010`):
- Drop the upper 9 bits of the IP.
- Append the lower 23 bits to the fixed OUI `01:00:5E:0`.
- Result: `01:00:5E:40:0F:22`.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Step 1 — First byte conversion (2 marks):**

$$
1 = 0001_2 \quad ; \quad 2 = 0010_2
$$

$$
\text{Byte}_1 = 12_{16} = 00010010_2
$$

**Step 2 — Bit identification (1 mark):**
- **I/G bit** = LSB = `0` → **Unicast**.
- **U/L bit** = Second LSB = `1` → **Locally Administered Address (LAA)**.

**Step 3 — OUI and NIC ID (2 marks):**
- **OUI:** `12:34:56`
- **NIC ID:** `78:9A:BC`

**Step 4 — Switch forwarding (2 marks):**
A switch maintains a **CAM (Content Addressable Memory) Table** mapping **MAC addresses → switch ports**. On receiving a frame:
- The switch reads the **Source MAC** and records the port it arrived on (learning).
- The switch looks up the **Destination MAC** in its CAM table (forwarding).
- If unknown, the frame is **flooded** out of all ports except the incoming one.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Bit Numbering Confusion:** Many students confuse the I/G bit (bit 0, the rightmost) with the U/L bit (bit 1). KTU examiners specifically test this — always label which bit you are extracting.
> 2. **Hex-Binary Conversion Errors:** A single wrong nibble (e.g., writing `1101` for hex `D` correctly but then misaligning the bytes) leads to cascading errors. Always re-verify: `D = 13_{10} = 8+4+0+1 = 1101_2`.
> 3. **OUI Confusion:** The OUI is the **first 3 bytes**, not the first 2 or 4. Memorize: 24 bits = 3 bytes.
> 4. **ARP Scope:** Students often forget that ARP only works within a single broadcast domain (LAN). It does not cross routers. For inter-router resolution, the host ARPs for the **default gateway's MAC**, not the final destination's MAC.
> 5. **Multicast MAC Mapping:** Forgetting to **force the last bit of the OUI to 0** when mapping IPv4 multicast is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MAC Address** = **48-bit** physical address, **6 bytes**, 12 hex digits, burned into NIC ROM.
- **Structure** = **OUI (24 bits)** + **NIC ID (24 bits)**.
- **Total addresses** = **2^48 ≈ 281 trillion**.
- **Per OUI** = **2^24 = 16,777,216** devices.
- **I/G bit (LSB of byte 1)**: `0` = Unicast, `1` = Multicast.
- **U/L bit (2nd LSB of byte 1)**: `0` = Universal (manufacturer), `1` = Local (admin override).
- **Broadcast address** = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (all 48 bits are 1).
- **Notation** = Colon-hex (`:`) or hyphen-hex (`-`).
- **Layer** = **OSI Layer 2 (Data Link → MAC sublayer)**.
- **Standard** = **IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet)** and **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)**.
- **ARP** maps **IP → MAC** within a local broadcast domain.
- **Switches** use MAC addresses to build the **CAM table** for Layer 2 forwarding.
- **Multicast MAC prefix for IPv4** = `01:00:5E:0x` (lowest bit forced to 0).
- **Multicast MAC prefix for IPv6** = `33:33:xx:xx:xx:xx`.
- **MAC changes at every router hop; IP remains end-to-end (without NAT).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IP Addresses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IP Addresses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Internet Protocol (IP) Address** is a unique, numerically labeled 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) logical identifier assigned to every device participating in a computer network that uses the Internet Protocol for communication. In the OSI model, the IP address operates at the **Network Layer (Layer 3)** and serves two primary functions: **host identification** (who is sending/receiving) and **location addressing** (where the host is located within the network topology).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"An IP address is a 32-bit logical address in IPv4 (or 128-bit in IPv6) that uniquely identifies a network interface of a host/device connected to a TCP/IP network, enabling end-to-end packet routing across interconnected subnetworks."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Address System"

Imagine you are sending a letter to a friend in another city. The envelope needs two pieces of information:

1. **The House Number + Street (Host Part)** → Identifies *exactly which person* should receive the letter.
2. **The City, State, and PIN Code (Network Part)** → Tells the postal system *where the locality* is, so it can route the letter through the correct regional hubs.

In the same way:
- The **Network ID** (analogous to PIN code/city) tells routers *which network* the packet must travel to.
- The **Host ID** (analogous to house number) tells the final router *which specific device* on that network should receive the packet.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as no two houses in India can share the same PIN + House Number combination, no two devices on a global TCP/IP network can share the same (Network ID + Host ID) pair. That is the meaning of "uniqueness."

## 1.3 Why IP Addressing Matters (Engineering Relevance)

- **Routing**: Routers forward packets based on the Network ID portion.
- **Logical Independence**: Unlike MAC addresses (which are physically burned into the NIC), IP addresses are *logical* and can be reassigned, renumbered, or hierarchical.
- **Scalability**: Hierarchical addressing (Network + Host) prevents the global routing table from collapsing under billions of devices.
- **Security and Policy**: IP addresses are used in firewalls, ACLs, NAT, and QoS.

## 1.4 Standards, Constants, and Reserved Metrics

| Standard Parameter | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **IPv4 Address Length** | **32 bits** (4 bytes) |
| **IPv6 Address Length** | **128 bits** (16 bytes) |
| **Total IPv4 Addresses** | **2³² = 4,294,967,296** (≈ 4.3 billion) |
| **Dotted-Decimal Notation** | Four decimal octets (0–255) separated by dots |
| **Default Ports (out of scope here)** | TCP: 6, UDP: 17, ICMP: 1 |
| **Loopback Address** | **127.0.0.1** (always refers to the local host) |
| **RFC Defining IPv4** | RFC 791 (1981) |
| **RFC Defining IPv6** | RFC 2460 / RFC 8200 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loopback Address 127.0.0.1** is conventionally used to test the local TCP/IP stack. Any packet sent to 127.x.x.x is *never* placed on the physical network — it loops back internally inside the host's networking subsystem.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IPv4 Address Structure (Dotted-Decimal)
