> **Final Inference:** The Nyquist rate (32 kbps) is *less* than the Shannon capacity (40 kbps), which confirms that the chosen M=16 signaling scheme is **achievable** on this channel. If Nyquist > Shannon, the scheme is physically impossible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, compare the **transmission chain** of an analog system and a digital system. Explain why digital repeaters are superior to analog amplifiers. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **cumulative SNR** after $N$ cascaded analog amplifiers, and show that the SNR is **independent of $N$** when digital regenerative repeaters are used. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram Comparison:**

- **Analog Chain:** Source → Amplifier → Channel → Amplifier → Channel → Amplifier → Sink.
- **Digital Chain:** Source → Encoder → Channel → **Regenerative Repeater** → Channel → **Regenerative Repeater** → Decoder → Sink.

**Why digital repeaters are superior:**
The analog amplifier applies a gain $G$ to the **entire incoming waveform**, including any noise added by the channel. Hence, the noise variance grows at every stage. The digital regenerative repeater, however, performs **threshold detection** on each bit:
- If sampled voltage $> +V_{th}$ → output a clean logic **1** pulse.
- If sampled voltage $< -V_{th}$ → output a clean logic **0** pulse.
- Any voltage between these thresholds is a detected error.

Since the output is a **freshly generated pulse**, the channel noise is **completely discarded**. The output SNR depends only on the repeater's internal circuitry, not on the input SNR (as long as the input SNR exceeds the detection threshold).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Single Analog Amplifier**
The output of one amplifier with gain $G$ and internal added noise $N_{a}$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{1} = \frac{G \cdot S}{G \cdot N_{in} + N_a}
$$

For a high-gain, low-noise amplifier, $G \cdot N_{in} \gg N_a$, so:

$$
\text{SNR}_{1} \approx \frac{S}{N_{in}}
$$

**Step 2: After N Cascaded Amplifiers**
Each amplifier adds its own noise $N_a$, so total added noise scales with $N$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{N,analog} = \frac{S}{N_{in} + N \cdot N_a}
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{N,analog} \approx \frac{S}{N \cdot N_a} \quad \text{(for large } N \text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Digital Regenerative Case**
Since each repeater completely re-generates the pulse from a hard decision:

$$
\text{SNR}_{N,digital} \approx \text{SNR}_{repeater,internal} \quad (\text{constant, independent of } N)
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> Many students write *"digital is better because it is immune to noise"* without ever explaining **how** the regenerative repeater works. You will lose at least **2 marks** if you do not explicitly mention the **threshold detection mechanism** (decision boundary at $\pm V_{th}$). Also, do **not** confuse *attenuation* (loss of signal strength) with *distortion* (change in shape) — both are amplified by analog amplifiers but both are **eliminated** by digital repeaters. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Digital transmission** uses **discrete** signals (bits), while analog uses **continuous** waveforms.
- The **#1 advantage** is **noise immunity via regenerative repeaters** — this alone is worth 4–5 marks in any answer.
- Always mention **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ to justify long-distance feasibility.
- Always mention **Nyquist Bit Rate** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ for noiseless channels.
- **TDM** is only possible with **digital** signals.
- **Encryption**, **error detection (CRC)**, and **error correction (Hamming)** are bit-level operations.
- **VLSI** has made digital hardware **dramatically cheaper** than analog equivalents.
- Quantization SNR formula: $\text{SNR}_q = 1.76 + 6.02n$ dB (useful for PCM-related advantages).
- When answering in KTU exams, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition → (2) 5–7 numbered advantages with one-line justification → (3) Mathematical proof using Shannon/Nyquist → (4) Conclusion.**
- **Forgetting the units** (Hz, bps, dB) is a common reason for losing 1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU PECCT751 – Data & Computer Communication (Module 2: Digital Data Communication Techniques)**, the term **"Disadvantages"** refers to the inherent limitations, drawbacks, and engineering trade-offs of **Line Coding (Signal Encoding) schemes** used to convert binary digital data into digital signals for transmission across a physical medium.

Line coding schemes are broadly classified into four families by **Forouzan / Stallings**:
1. **Unipolar / Polar Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)** – NRZ-L, NRZ-I
2. **Bipolar / Pseudoternary** – AMI, Pseudoternary
3. **Multilevel** – 2B1Q, 8B6T, 4D-PAM5
4. **Multiline / Manchester Family** – Manchester, Differential Manchester

Each scheme carries a unique set of disadvantages that affect **synchronization, DC balance, bandwidth efficiency, error detection, and clock recovery**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Students must be able to *identify, justify, and compare* the disadvantages of each encoding family. Examiners frequently ask comparative disadvantage questions for 7–14 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Morse Code of Computers"

Imagine you are sending a message to a friend by tapping on a wall:
- If you tap **only short pulses** (like NRZ), your friend may not know when one letter ends and the next begins (**synchronization problem**).
- If you tap alternating **long and short** pulses (like Bipolar-AMI), it is easier to follow the rhythm, but a long silence confuses them (**DC component problem**).
- If you always **switch direction in the middle** (like Manchester), the friend can follow your rhythm perfectly, but you are now tapping **twice as fast** — exhausting your energy (**bandwidth doubling problem**).

This is precisely the engineering dilemma behind the **disadvantages of line coding**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are used to *quantify* the disadvantages of a line code. They are universally defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Rate (Bit Rate)** | $N$ | **bits/second (bps)** |
| **Signal Rate (Baud Rate / Modulation Rate)** | $S$ | **bauds (signals/second)** |
| **DC Component** | $C_{DC}$ | **Volts (V)** |
| **Bandwidth (Minimum Theoretical)** | $B_{min}$ | **Hz** |
| **Bit Error Rate (BER)** | $P_e$ | **dimensionless ratio** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Disadvantages of NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) Schemes

### 2.1.1 NRZ-L (Non-Return-to-Zero Level)
- **Disadvantage 1 — DC Component Problem:** A long run of 1s or 0s produces a sustained DC voltage. Transformers and AC-coupled channels **block DC**, so signal energy is lost.
- **Disadvantage 2 — No Self-Clocking / Synchronization Failure:** A long stream of identical bits produces a **flat line** with **no transitions**, causing the receiver clock to drift out of phase.
- **Disadvantage 3 — Baseline Wander:** The baseline (average DC value) shifts, causing sampling errors.

### 2.1.2 NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted)
- **Disadvantage 1 — Inversion is One-Way Only:** Only a `1` causes a transition. A long string of `0`s still produces zero transitions, so **synchronization is still lost on `0` runs**.
- **Disadvantage 2 — Polarity Ambiguity:** The receiver cannot tell whether a transition is a logical 1 or a polarity inversion caused by a faulty inverter.
- **Disadvantage 3 — Bandwidth Inefficiency:** At low data rates, the spectrum has significant low-frequency content (the very problem NRZ was supposed to solve efficiently).

## 2.2 Disadvantages of Bipolar (AMI — Alternate Mark Inversion)

- **Disadvantage 1 — Long-Zero Synchronization Loss:** AMI guarantees a transition on every `1` but **not on a long string of `0`s**. A run of 15 consecutive `0`s in T1 systems caused the famous **T1 line code change to B8ZS** in the 1980s.
- **Disadvantage 2 — Three Signal Levels Required:** Requires **−V, 0, +V**, increasing transmitter and receiver complexity compared to two-level NRZ.
- **Disadvantage 3 — No Built-in Error Detection:** A single-bit error can invert the polarity rule and propagate silently.

## 2.3 Disadvantages of Manchester / Differential Manchester

- **Disadvantage 1 — Bandwidth Doubling:** The signal rate is **twice the bit rate** ($S = 2N$). For the same bit rate, Manchester requires **double the bandwidth** of NRZ.
- **Disadvantage 2 — Inefficient for High-Speed Links:** At gigabit speeds, the doubled baud rate pushes the bandwidth into the **GHz range**, requiring expensive cabling (e.g., Cat 6a/7).
- **Disadvantage 3 — Differential Manchester Loses the Self-Clocking for `0`s:** For a `0`, the transition is the same as the bit boundary — fine. But for a `1`, the *only* transition is at the bit boundary, so a long `1` run still risks drift.
- **Disadvantage 4 — Strict Phase Alignment:** Both schemes require **bit-boundary transitions**, making them incompatible with systems that use clock-data recovery (CDR) PLLs.

## 2.4 Disadvantages of Block Coding (4B/5B, 8B/10B)

- **Disadvantage 1 — Overhead / Inefficiency:** 4B/5B inserts **1 redundant bit per 4 bits** (20% overhead). 8B/10B has **25% overhead**.
- **Disadvantage 2 — Increased Signal Rate:** Because the code is longer, the line signal rate increases by the same overhead factor.
- **Disadvantage 3 — DC Balance Not Guaranteed in 4B/5B Alone:** 4B/5B must be combined with NRZ-I or MLT-3 to maintain DC balance.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Disadvantage Quantification)

| # | Formula | Engineering Meaning (Disadvantage Quantified) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{NRZ} = N/2$ | NRZ has the **lowest** signal rate, but suffers DC and sync issues |
| 2 | $S_{Bipolar} = N/2$ | Same baud as NRZ, but **3 voltage levels** required |
| 3 | $S_{Manchester} = N$ | **Double** the bandwidth of NRZ |
| 4 | $S_{MLT-3} = N/4$ | Efficient, but **DC drift on long `0` runs** |
| 5 | $B_{min} = S/2$ | **Nyquist minimum bandwidth** in Hz |
| 6 | $C_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt$ | DC component — **non-zero for NRZ**, **zero for AMI and Manchester** |
| 7 | $BER = \dfrac{\text{errors received}}{\text{total bits received}}$ | Increases when synchronization is lost (NRZ with long runs) |
| 8 | $S_{4B/5B} = (N/4) \cdot 5 = 1.25\,N$ | **25% higher** baud than raw NRZ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER write the absolute value bars in a markdown table as `|`.** Always use LaTeX: $\vert C_{DC} \vert$ or $\mid v(t) \mid$. The pipe `|` symbol **breaks the markdown table syntax**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of Studying Disadvantages

Understanding these disadvantages is **not academic** — it directly drives industrial design:
- **Ethernet (10BASE-T)** abandoned Manchester after 10 Mbps and adopted **MLT-3 + 4B/5B (100BASE-TX)** and later **PAM-5 (1000BASE-T)** to escape the bandwidth-doubling trap.
- **T1/E1 telecom links** replaced AMI with **B8ZS/HDB3** because of long-zero synchronization failures.
- **USB, HDMI, PCI-Express** use **8B/10B** because DC balance and error detection outweigh the 25% overhead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Signal Rate of Manchester = 2 × Bit Rate (The Bandwidth Doubling Disadvantage)

**Given:**
- Bit duration $T_b = 1/N$ seconds.
- Manchester rule: there is **always** a transition in the **middle** of every bit period.

**Step 1 — Number of transitions per bit.**
Each bit period contains **one mandatory mid-bit transition**.
For $N$ bits/second, the number of mid-bit transitions per second is $N$.

**Step 2 — Define signal element (baud).**
A signal element is the shortest pulse. In Manchester, half a bit period contains one half-pulse (e.g., `+V` for first half, `−V` for second half, or vice versa). So **2 signal elements represent 1 bit**.

**Step 3 — Formal derivation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of signal elements per second} &= \dfrac{\text{Number of bits per second}}{\text{Bits per signal element}} \\
S_{Manchester} &= \dfrac{N}{1/2} \\
S_{Manchester} &= 2N \quad \text{[bauds]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum bandwidth implication (Nyquist).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{min}^{Manchester} &= \dfrac{S_{Manchester}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{2N}{2} \\
&= N \quad \text{[Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with NRZ baseline.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{min}^{NRZ} &= \dfrac{N/2}{2} = \dfrac{N}{4} \quad \text{[Hz]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Manchester requires **4× the minimum bandwidth** of NRZ to transmit at the same bit rate — the principal *disadvantage*.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DC Component of NRZ-L vs. Bipolar-AMI

**Step 1 — DC component definition.**

$$
C_{DC} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — Case A: NRZ-L with all `1`s.**
For an all-`1` bit stream using NRZ-L, $v(t) = +V$ for all $t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{DC}^{NRZ-L} &= \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T (+V)\,dt \\
&= \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{VT}{T} \\
&= +V \quad \text{(non-zero)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Case B: Bipolar-AMI with alternating `1`s.**
For the bit pattern `+V 0 −V 0 +V 0 −V 0 ...`, the average over a 2-bit period $2T_b$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{DC}^{AMI} &= \dfrac{1}{2T_b}\int_0^{2T_b} v(t)\,dt \\
&= \dfrac{(+V)(T_b) + 0 + (-V)(T_b) + 0}{2T_b} \\
&= \dfrac{0}{2T_b} \\
&= 0 \quad \text{(DC-balanced)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This proves that **AMI and Manchester have zero DC component** — a major advantage that NRZ lacks. The *disadvantage* of NRZ is therefore quantitative: it carries a **non-zero DC bias** that AC-coupled channels cannot pass.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synchronization Failure Probability for NRZ with Long Runs

**Step 1 — Assumed bit pattern probability.**
For random binary data, the probability of $k$ consecutive identical bits is $P_k = 2 \cdot (1/2)^k = 2^{1-k}$.

**Step 2 — Receiver clock drift model.**
Receiver clock drift over time $T$ is $\Delta t = \alpha \cdot T$ where $\alpha$ is the oscillator stability coefficient (typical $\alpha = 10^{-4}$ for cheap oscillators).

**Step 3 — Maximum allowed flat-line duration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{flat}^{max} &= \dfrac{T_b}{\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{N \alpha}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of synchronization failure in a window of $L$ bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{fail} &= 1 - (1 - P_{T_{flat}^{max}})^L \\
&\approx L \cdot 2^{1 - T_{flat}^{max} \cdot N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
Take $N = 1.544$ Mbps (T1 line), $\alpha = 10^{-4}$, $L = 10^6$ bits. Then $T_{flat}^{max} = 1/(1.544\times 10^6 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 6.48 \times 10^{-3}$ s $\approx 10{,}000$ bit-periods.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_k \text{ for } k = 10000 &\approx 2^{-9999} \approx 10^{-3013}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Although the probability of *one* very long run is astronomically small, **in T1 frames a "all-zeros" data pattern** was common, and AMI *guaranteed* a sync loss on it. This is why **B8ZS (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution)** was invented — to force a deliberate violation pattern that keeps the receiver clock locked.

## 3.4 KTU Disadvantage Analytical Matrix (Comparative Summary)

| Encoding Scheme | DC Component | Self-Clocking | Bandwidth Efficiency | Error Detection | Complexity | Main Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **NRZ-L** | High | **None** | Highest | None | Lowest | DC drift + sync loss |
| **NRZ-I** | High | **Partial (1 only)** | Highest | None | Low | Sync loss on `0` runs |
| **Bipolar-AMI** | Zero | **Partial (1 only)** | High | Limited | Medium | **Long-zero sync loss** |
| **Pseudoternary** | Zero | Partial (0 only) | High | Limited | Medium | Long-one sync loss |
| **Manchester** | Zero | **Excellent** | **Lowest (BW × 4)** | None | Medium | **Bandwidth doubling** |
| **Diff. Manchester** | Zero | Excellent | Lowest | None | Medium | Bandwidth + phase sensitive |
| **MLT-3** | Near zero | Moderate | Very High | None | High | DC drift on `0` runs |
| **2B1Q** | None | Good | Highest | None | High | Multilevel noise sensitivity |
| **4B/5B + NRZ-I** | Zero | Good | High | None | High | **20% overhead** |
| **8B/10B** | Zero | Excellent | High | **Built-in** | Very High | **25% overhead** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow — "Why Is Each Scheme Disadvantaged?"

This flow chart shows how an engineer navigates the trade-offs caused by the disadvantages covered above.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Choose Line Code]) --> Q1{DC Component acceptable?}
    Q1 -- "No, AC coupled" --> Q2{Self-clocking required?}
    Q1 -- "Yes, DC coupled" --> NRZfam[Use NRZ family]
    NRZfam --> NRZlim[Disadvantage: Sync loss on long runs]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q3{Bandwidth budget tight?}
    Q2 -- "No" --> AMI[Use Bipolar AMI]
    AMI --> AMIlim[Disadvantage: Sync loss on long 0-runs]
    AMIlim --> B8ZS[Apply B8ZS or HDB3 scrambling]
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> MLT[Use MLT-3 plus 4B5B]
    MLT --> MLTlim[Disadvantage: DC drift on 0-runs]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Manch[Use Manchester]
    Manch --> Manchlim[Disadvantage: Bandwidth x4 of NRZ]
    B8ZS --> EndOK([Disadvantage mitigated])
    NRZlim --> EndOK
    MLTlim --> EndOK
    Manchlim --> EndOK
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of a Transmitter Showing Where Each Disadvantage Originates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Data Source]
        Bits[Binary Bits: 10110010...]
    end
    subgraph ENC[Encoder Block]
        NRZ[NRZ Encoder]
        BIP[Bipolar AMI Encoder]
        MAN[Manchester Encoder]
        BLK[Block Encoder 4B5B or 8B10B]
    end
    subgraph CHAN[Channel Effects - the source of disadvantages]
        DC[DC Blocking by Transformer]
        ATT[Attenuation vs Frequency]
        NOISE[Thermal Noise]
        DRIFT[Clock Drift at Receiver]
    end
    subgraph RX[Receiver Block]
        CLK[Clock Recovery PLL]
        DEC[Decoder]
        DAT[Recovered Bits]
    end
    Bits --> NRZ
    Bits --> BIP
    Bits --> MAN
    Bits --> BLK
    NRZ --> CHAN
    BIP --> CHAN
    MAN --> CHAN
    BLK --> CHAN
    CHAN --> DC
    CHAN --> ATT
    CHAN --> NOISE
    CHAN --> DRIFT
    DC --> CLK
    DRIFT --> CLK
    CLK --> DEC
    DEC --> DAT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The disadvantages of each scheme can be traced back to a specific block. For example, the `DC Blocking by Transformer` block highlights **why NRZ fails** in AC-coupled systems, while the `Clock Drift` block explains **why AMI fails on long-zero sequences**. The `Clock Recovery PLL` block is the component that *suffers* when an encoding scheme is poorly chosen.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three disadvantages of NRZ encoding schemes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per point):**
1. **DC Component Problem:** Long runs of 1s or 0s produce a sustained DC voltage, which is blocked by AC-coupled channels and transformers, leading to baseline wander. *(1 mark)*
2. **No Self-Clocking / Synchronization Failure:** A long stream of identical bits results in a flat line with no transitions, causing the receiver clock to drift out of phase. *(1 mark)*
3. **No Error Detection Capability:** Unlike 8B/10B, NRZ has no built-in redundancy; a single bit error is undetectable at the physical layer. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: State the principal disadvantage of Manchester encoding and express its signal rate formula.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Principal Disadvantage (2 marks):** Manchester encoding requires **twice the bandwidth** of NRZ for the same bit rate, because the signal rate is doubled. This makes it unsuitable for high-speed links such as Gigabit Ethernet.
- **Signal Rate Formula (1 mark):** $\;S_{Manchester} = 2N\;$ bauds, where $N$ is the bit rate in bps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the disadvantages of NRZ-L with NRZ-I.** Many students write "NRZ-I has no DC component" — *wrong*. Only AMI and Manchester guarantee zero DC.
> 2. **Always mention the bit rate AND bandwidth implication.** Writing "Manchester uses more bandwidth" without quantifying ($2N$ vs $N/2$) costs you 2 marks.
> 3. **Use the term "self-clocking," not "synchronization,"** when explaining Manchester — KTU examiners reward the precise vocabulary.
> 4. **Draw waveforms** for any disadvantage question involving NRZ, AMI, or Manchester. A neat waveform is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> 5. **Do not mix up "B8ZS" with "HDB3."** B8ZS is for T1 (North America); HDB3 is for E1 (Europe/Asia).

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain with neat waveforms the **three main disadvantages of NRZ-L encoding**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** How does **Bipolar-AMI encoding overcome** the DC-component problem of NRZ? What is its **remaining major disadvantage** and how is it solved in practice? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three main disadvantages of NRZ-L — 7 marks**

**Disadvantage 1 — DC Component (2 marks):**
In NRZ-L, a `1` is mapped to a positive voltage (`+V`) and a `0` to a negative voltage (`−V`). When a long run of identical bits occurs (e.g., 15 consecutive `1`s), the signal stays at `+V` for the entire duration. This produces a **non-zero average value (DC component)** of $+V$.

**Waveform 1 (ASCII representation):**
```
Bit:    1   1   1   1   1   0   0   0   1   1
        ┌───┐                       ┌───┐
+V  ────┘   └───────────────────────┘   └────
                                          ┌───┐
0V  ─────────────────────────────────────┘   └──
-V  ───────────────────────────────────────────
```
**Result:** Transformer-coupled channels block this DC, so the signal is distorted at the receiver.

**Disadvantage 2 — Synchronization Loss (2 marks):**
A long run of identical bits produces a **flat-line signal** with **no transitions**. The receiver uses transitions to lock its clock. Without them, the receiver's local clock drifts, and sampling points shift — bits are misinterpreted.

**Disadvantage 3 — No Error Detection / Baseline Wander (1.5 marks):**
There is **no built-in redundancy** in NRZ-L. Any single bit error cannot be detected. Additionally, the DC component causes the **baseline (average voltage) to drift** over time, requiring DC-restoration circuits.

**Conclusion (1.5 marks):**
NRZ-L is simple and bandwidth-efficient ($S = N/2$), but its DC and synchronization drawbacks make it unsuitable for long-distance or AC-coupled links. Therefore, NRZ-L is mainly used in **short-distance, DC-coupled** applications like RS-232 and inside ICs.

---

**(b) Bipolar-AMI solution and its remaining disadvantage — 7 marks**

**How AMI Solves the DC Problem (3 marks):**
Bipolar AMI maps:
- Bit `0` → `0V` (no signal)
- Bit `1` → **alternating** `+V` and `−V`

The alternation ensures that the **long-term average voltage is zero**, eliminating the DC component.

**Mathematical Proof (1 mark):**

$$
C_{DC}^{AMI} = \lim_{T \to \infty}\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt = 0
$$

**Remaining Major Disadvantage (2 marks):**
AMI only guarantees a transition on every `1`. A long run of `0`s (e.g., `00000000...`) produces a flat line at `0V`, **losing synchronization** — the same problem NRZ had on identical bits.

**Practical Solution (1 mark):**
This is solved by **scrambling techniques**:
- **B8ZS (Bipolar with 8-Zero Substitution):** Used in **T1 (North America)**. A run of 8 zeros is replaced with the deliberate pattern `000VB0VB` containing intentional AMI violations (`V`) that the receiver recognizes and reverses.
- **HDB3 (High-Density Bipolar 3):** Used in **E1 (Europe/Asia)**. Replaces 4 consecutive zeros with `B00V` or `000V`, maintaining DC balance and providing bit-clock information.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Compare **NRZ-L and Manchester** encoding with respect to their **disadvantages**, including signal rate formulas. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain why **block coding (4B/5B)** is preferred over plain NRZ-I for high-speed Ethernet (100BASE-TX). What is the **disadvantage** of 4B/5B? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of NRZ-L and Manchester Disadvantages — 7 marks**

| Comparison Parameter | NRZ-L | Manchester |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Rate** | $S = N/2$ bauds | $S = N$ bauds (i.e., $2 \times$ NRZ-L) |
| **Minimum Bandwidth** | $B_{min} = N/4$ Hz | $B_{min} = N$ Hz (i.e., $4 \times$ NRZ-L) |
| **DC Component** | **Non-zero** (bad) | **Zero** (good) |
| **Self-Clocking** | **No** (bad) | **Yes — excellent** (good) |
| **Error Detection** | None | None |
| **Complexity** | Lowest | Medium (XOR of NRZ-L and clock) |
| **Main Disadvantage** | DC drift + sync loss | **Bandwidth doubling / quadrupling** |

**Waveform Comparison (2 marks):**
```
Data:      1   0   1   1   0   0   1

NRZ-L:    +V  -V  +V  +V  -V  -V  +V     <- DC biased, no transition at 1 1

Manchester:┌─┐ ┌─┐   ┌─┐   ┌─┐ ┌─┐   ┌─┐
         ┌─┘ └─┐ ┌─┐   ┌─┐ │ └─┐ │ ┌─┐ │
+V -V    │     └─┘ └─┐ │ │   │ │ │ │ └─┐││
         │         └─┐│ │   │ │ │ │   └┘││
0V  -----│-----------└┘------└─┘ └─┘     └┘
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** NRZ-L is bandwidth-efficient but suffers from DC drift and synchronization loss. Manchester guarantees synchronization but at the cost of **4× the bandwidth of NRZ-L**, making it suitable for short links (10BASE-T) but prohibitive for gigabit speeds.

---

**(b) Why 4B/5B is preferred over plain NRZ-I for 100BASE-TX — 7 marks**

**Plain NRZ-I Disadvantage (2 marks):**
NRZ-I inverts on every `1`, but a long string of `0`s produces zero transitions → **synchronization is still lost**. Also, NRZ-I has a **non-zero DC component** because a long run of `1`s still leaves the signal at one polarity for a long time.

**How 4B/5B Solves This (3 marks):**
- 4B/5B maps every 4-bit group into a 5-bit code.
- The 5-bit codes are **carefully chosen** so that they **never contain more than one leading zero and never end with more than two zeros**.
- This guarantees a transition **at least once every 3 bit-periods**, eliminating the long-zero synchronization problem.
- 4B/5B is then combined with **MLT-3**, which maps the 5-bit pattern into three voltage levels (`+V, 0, −V`) — this further reduces the baud rate to $N/4$ while keeping DC balance.

**Disadvantage of 4B/5B (2 marks):**
- **20% Overhead:** For every 4 bits of data, 5 bits are transmitted. The signal rate increases by 25%, i.e., $S_{4B/5B} = 1.25 \cdot S_{NRZ} = 5N/8$.
- This overhead is justified by the elimination of synchronization problems and the ability to combine with MLT-3 for high-speed transmission.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
4B/5B is preferred over NRZ-I in 100BASE-TX because it guarantees a minimum transition density, which keeps the receiver clock synchronized — but the **trade-off is 20% bandwidth overhead**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Disadvantages of Line Coding**

- **NRZ-L disadvantages:** DC component, no self-clocking, no error detection, baseline wander.
- **NRZ-I disadvantages:** DC component, sync loss on long `0`-runs, polarity ambiguity.
- **Bipolar AMI disadvantages:** Sync loss on long `0`-runs, 3 voltage levels needed, no built-in error detection.
- **Manchester disadvantages:** Signal rate $S = 2N$ (bandwidth $4\times$ NRZ), not suitable for high-speed links.
- **Differential Manchester disadvantages:** Same bandwidth penalty as Manchester; extra phase-alignment complexity.
- **4B/5B disadvantage:** 20% overhead; signal rate = $1.25 \times$ raw bit rate.
- **8B/10B disadvantage:** 25% overhead; but provides built-in error detection (running disparity).
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $S_{NRZ} = N/2$ ; $S_{Bipolar} = N/2$ ; $S_{Manchester} = 2N$ ; $S_{MLT-3} = N/4$ ; $S_{4B/5B} = 1.25\,N$
  - $B_{min} = S/2$ (Nyquist)
  - $C_{DC} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v(t)\,dt$
- **Industry fixes to remember:**
  - Long-zero problem in AMI → **B8ZS** (T1) or **HDB3** (E1)
  - Bandwidth doubling in Manchester → **MLT-3 + 4B/5B** (100BASE-TX)
  - DC drift in NRZ → **8B/10B + DC-balanced codes** (USB, HDMI, PCIe)
- **Exam vocabulary to use:** "self-clocking," "baseline wander," "DC balance," "transition density," "running disparity," "Nyquist bandwidth."
- **Common exam pitfall:** Confusing "NRZ-I has no DC component" — *false*; it is AMI and Manchester that are DC-balanced.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Networking Devices: Routers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Networking Devices: Routers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Router** is a **Layer-3 (Network Layer) intelligent networking device** that operates at the third layer of the **OSI Reference Model** (and the Internet Layer of the **TCP/IP Suite**). Its primary function is to **forward data packets** between two or more distinct computer networks (subnets) by examining the destination **IP address** in the packet header, consulting a **routing table**, and selecting the most optimal outgoing interface for the packet.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT — Router Definition**
> A router is the fundamental **inter-network gateway** that connects heterogeneous networks (LANs, MANs, WANs) and makes independent forwarding decisions based on **logical addressing (IP)**, unlike switches/bridges that use **physical (MAC) addressing**.

Mathematically, a router implements the function:
$$R: (Packet_{in}, RoutingTable) \mapsto (Interface_{out}, Packet_{out})$$

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Intelligent Traffic Police"

Imagine a **highway interchange with a traffic police officer** at every junction:

- **You (the packet)** arrive at the junction carrying a **letter with a destination address** (the IP address).
- The **police officer (the router)** checks a **giant city directory** (the routing table) to find the best road to your destination.
- The officer **points you to the correct highway ramp** (the outgoing interface) — sometimes the **fastest**, sometimes the **shortest**, sometimes the **least congested**.
- If the officer **doesn't know** the route, he sends you through a **default gateway** or **discards** the packet with an **ICMP "Destination Unreachable"** message.

This is **exactly** how a router operates — it is the **"brain"** of packet-switched networks, making logical decisions rather than blindly forwarding traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU 2024**
> A **Switch** operates at **Layer 2** and forwards based on **MAC addresses** within the same network.
> A **Router** operates at **Layer 3** and forwards based on **IP addresses** between **different networks**.

---

### 1.3 Position in the OSI and TCP/IP Models

| Layer | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model | Device at that Layer |
|:-----:|:---------:|:------------:|:--------------------:|
| **L7** | Application | Application | Gateway / Proxy |
| **L4** | Transport | Transport | Firewall (stateful) |
| **L3** | **Network** | **Internet** | **ROUTER** |
| **L2** | Data Link | Network Access | Bridge / Switch |
| **L1** | Physical | Network Access | Hub / Repeater |

> [!NOTE]
> A modern **Multi-Layer Switch (MLS)** combines Layer 2 and Layer 3 functionality, but a **pure router** is strictly a **Layer-3 device**.

### 1.4 Physical and Logical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Default Router IP Range (Private):** `192.168.1.1`, `10.0.0.1`, `172.16.0.1` (RFC 1918)
- **Standard MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit):** **1500 bytes** (Ethernet default)
- **Default Administrative Distance (Cisco):** Static Route = **1**, OSPF = **110**, RIP = **120**
- **Time-To-Live (TTL) Default:** **64 / 128 / 255** (OS-dependent)
- **Routing Table Capacity:** Modern routers hold **>500,000 IPv4 routes** in the **Default Free Zone (DFZ)**
- **Forwarding Rate (Throughput):** Measured in **pps (packets per second)** or **bps (bits per second)**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Building Blocks of a Router

A router is composed of **four critical functional subsystems**:

1. **Input Ports (Ingress Interfaces)**
   - Physical layer signal termination.
   - Data link layer decapsulation (Ethernet framing).
   - **Lookup of destination IP** in the routing table using a high-speed **TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory)**.
   - Buffering packets during congestion (using **QoS queues**).

2. **Switching Fabric (The Backplane)**
   - Transfers the packet from the input port to the correct output port.
   - Three switching techniques:
     - **Memory Switching** (slowest, used in older routers)
     - **Bus Switching** (shared bus architecture)
     - **Crossbar Switching** (fastest, used in modern core routers)

3. **Output Ports (Egress Interfaces)**
   - Queuing (FIFO, Priority, Weighted Fair Queuing).
   - Data link layer encapsulation.
   - Physical layer transmission.

4. **Routing Processor (The CPU/ASIC Brain)**
   - Runs **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP, RIP).
   - Builds and maintains the **Routing Information Base (RIB)**.
   - Copies selected best routes into the **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** for hardware-accelerated lookup.

---

### 2.2 Router's Decision-Making Process — Step-by-Step Logic

When a packet arrives at a router, the following sequence is executed:

1. **Receive the packet** on an incoming interface.
2. **Strip the Layer-2 header** (e.g., Ethernet preamble + MAC header).
3. **Inspect the Layer-3 header** (IPv4/IPv6) — extract the **destination IP address**.
4. **Decrement the TTL** field by 1. If TTL becomes 0 → drop the packet and send **ICMP Time Exceeded**.
5. **Recalculate the IP Header Checksum** (since TTL changed).
6. **Perform Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** in the routing table.
7. **Select the outgoing interface** based on the matched route.
8. **Encapsulate the packet in a new Layer-2 frame** (with the next-hop's MAC address as destination).
9. **Forward the packet** out the chosen output port.

---

### 2.3 Types of Routing Algorithms

#### (a) Static Routing
- **Manually configured** by the network administrator.
- **No overhead**, **highly secure**, but **not scalable**.
- Used in **stub networks** (small offices, single-homed ISPs).

#### (b) Dynamic Routing
- Routers **exchange information** automatically using **routing protocols**.
- Two main classes:

| Class | Algorithm Type | Example Protocols | Metric Used |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------:|
| **Distance Vector** | Bellman-Ford | **RIP, IGRP** | Hop Count |
| **Link State** | Dijkstra's SPF | **OSPF, IS-IS** | Cost (bandwidth-based) |
| **Path Vector** | Policy-based | **BGP** | Path Attributes (AS-Path) |
| **Hybrid** | Combination | **EIGRP** | Bandwidth + Delay |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked Distinction**
> - **Distance Vector** = "Rumor-based" (a router only knows what its neighbor tells it) — prone to **routing loops**, solved by **Split Horizon** and **Hold-Down Timers**.
> - **Link State** = "Map-based" (every router builds a full **LSDB** of the topology) — uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** for shortest path.

---

### 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Unit / Notes |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------------------|:-------------|
| 1 | **Subnet Mask Bits** | $n_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$ where $h$ = host bits | $h = 32 - n_{network}$ |
| 2 | **Number of Usable Subnets** | $2^{s} - 2$ where $s$ = borrowed bits | (For Classful networks) |
| 3 | **Network Address Calculation** | $Network_{addr} = IP_{addr} \ \text{AND} \ Mask$ | Bitwise AND operation |
| 4 | **Broadcast Address** | $Broadcast = Network_{addr} \ \text{OR} \ \overline{Mask}$ | Inverse of host bits |
| 5 | **CIDR Block Aggregation** | $Super\_net = \text{common prefix of } n \text{ bits}$ | Used in route summarization |
| 6 | **Throughput** | $Throughput = \dfrac{Data_{delivered}}{Time_{total}}$ | bps (bits per second) |
| 7 | **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{total} = D_{proc} + D_{queue} + D_{trans} + D_{prop}$ | Seconds (4-component model) |
| 8 | **Transmission Delay** | $D_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ where $L$ = packet length, $R$ = link rate | Seconds |
| 9 | **Propagation Delay** | $D_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s}$ where $d$ = distance, $s$ = propagation speed | Seconds ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in fiber) |
| 10 | **RIP Hop Count Limit** | $\text{Max hops} = 15$ (16 = unreachable) | Distance Vector metric |
| 11 | **OSPF Cost** | $Cost = \dfrac{\text{Reference BW}}{\text{Interface BW}}$ (Cisco default ref = 100 Mbps) | Unitless |
| 12 | **Packet Loss Probability** | $P_{loss} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}$ for $n$ independent hops, each with loss prob $p$ | Probability (0 to 1) |
| 13 | **Jitter (RFC 3393)** | $J = \vert D_{i} - D_{i-1} \vert$ | Variation in inter-packet delay |
| 14 | **Router Lookup Time (CAM)** | $t_{lookup} \approx O(1)$ with TCAM | Constant time match |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Markdown Table Pipes**: The `\vert` and `\mid` symbols are used in place of the standard `|` pipe to avoid breaking the markdown table rendering. For example, $J = \vert D_{i} - D_{i-1} \vert$.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Routers are the **backbone of every modern network** and the literal "spine" of the **Internet**. Their engineering applications include:

- **Internet Service Providers (ISPs):** Core routers (Cisco CRS, Juniper MX, Nokia 7750 SR) forward **terabits of traffic per second** across the global Internet.
- **Enterprise Networks:** Edge routers (Cisco ISR, MikroTik, Ubiquiti) provide NAT, VPN, firewall, and DHCP services.
- **5G / Telecom:** Routers form the **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** fabric.
- **Data Centers:** Top-of-Rack (ToR) routers using **BGP** enable **Anycast** services (e.g., Google DNS `8.8.8.8`).
- **IoT and Home Automation:** Home routers (Wi-Fi 6) connect billions of devices using **wireless routing protocols**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Subnetting Calculation (Verifiable Step-by-Step)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Expected Pattern):**
> Given the IP address **`192.168.45.130/26`**, find:
> (a) Subnet mask in decimal
> (b) Network address
> (c) First usable host
> (d) Last usable host
> (e) Broadcast address
> (f) Total usable hosts per subnet

#### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Convert the CIDR /26 prefix into a 32-bit binary mask.**

The `/26` means **26 bits are 1s** and **32 − 26 = 6 bits are 0s**.

$$
\text{Subnet Mask (Binary)} = \underbrace{11111111.11111111.11111111.11}_{26 \text{ ones}}\underbrace{000000}_{6 \text{ zeros}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the mask to dotted-decimal.**

Each `11111111` octet = **255**. The 4th octet = `11000000` = **192**.

$$
\text{Subnet Mask} = 255.255.255.192
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the IP `192.168.45.130` to binary.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
192 &= 11000000 \\
168 &= 10101000 \\
45  &= 00101101 \\
130 &= 10000010
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the bitwise AND between IP and Mask (to obtain the Network Address).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IP}  &= 11000000.10101000.00101101.10000010 \\
\text{Mask} &= 11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000 \\
\hline
\text{Network} &= 11000000.10101000.00101101.10\color{blue}{000000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert the result back to decimal.**

$$
\text{Network Address} = 192.168.45.128
$$

**Step 6 — Identify First Usable Host (Network Address + 1).**

$$
\text{First Host} = 192.168.45.128 + 1 = 192.168.45.129
$$

**Step 7 — Identify Broadcast Address (all host bits set to 1).**

$$
\text{Broadcast} = 192.168.45.10\color{blue}{111111} = 192.168.45.191
$$

**Step 8 — Identify Last Usable Host (Broadcast Address − 1).**

$$
\text{Last Host} = 192.168.45.191 - 1 = 192.168.45.190
$$

**Step 9 — Calculate Total Usable Hosts using $n_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$.**

$$
h = 6 \text{ host bits} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{hosts} = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ usable hosts}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Transmission Delay, Propagation Delay & End-to-End Delay

> **Problem:**
> A router must forward a **L = 1500 byte** packet over a **R = 100 Mbps** link of length **d = 2000 km** (fiber). Processing delay = **0.5 ms**, queueing delay = **1.5 ms**. Find the **total end-to-end delay**.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert packet size to bits.**

$$
L = 1500 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 12{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Transmission Delay.**

$$
D_{trans} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Propagation Delay (using $s = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for fiber).**

$$
D_{prop} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum all four delay components.**

$$
D_{total} = D_{proc} + D_{queue} + D_{trans} + D_{prop}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 0.5 \text{ ms} + 1.5 \text{ ms} + 0.12 \text{ ms} + 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
D_{total} = 12.12 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **propagation delay (10 ms)** dominates the total delay in this case. This is why **long-haul fiber links** and **satellite links** require careful optimization (e.g., TCP window scaling, low-latency routing).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Route Aggregation (Supernetting / CIDR)

> **Problem:** An organization has four networks:
> `128.10.0.0/16`, `128.10.1.0/16`, `128.10.2.0/16`, `128.10.3.0/16`
> Aggregate them into a single supernet route advertisement.

#### Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert the third octet to binary for the four networks.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
128.10.\color{blue}{0}.0  &= \ldots .00000000.\ldots \\
128.10.\color{blue}{1}.0  &= \ldots .00000001.\ldots \\
128.10.\color{blue}{2}.0  &= \ldots .00000010.\ldots \\
128.10.\color{blue}{3}.0  &= \ldots .00000011.\ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the common prefix.**

All four start with bits `000000` in the third octet. The first **22 bits** of every network are identical:

$$
\underbrace{10000000.00001010.00}_{22 \text{ common bits}}\underbrace{00.00}_{10 \text{ varying bits}}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the supernet.**

$$
\text{Supernet} = 128.10.0.0/22
$$

This single `/22` route summarises **4 contiguous /16 networks** (a total of **$4 \times 65{,}536 = 262{,}144$** IPv4 addresses) and is what gets advertised to the upstream ISP — drastically reducing the global routing table size.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Router vs Other Devices

| Parameter | Hub | Bridge | Switch | **Router** | Gateway |
|:---------:|:---:|:------:|:------:|:----------:|:-------:|
| **OSI Layer** | L1 | L2 | L2 | **L3** | L7 (or L4-L7) |
| **Address Used** | None | MAC | MAC | **IP** | Application-specific |
| **Forwarding Basis** | Signal repeat | MAC table | MAC table (CAM) | **Routing Table (LPM)** | Protocol translation |
| **Scope** | Single collision domain | 2 segments | Many segments | **Inter-network** | Inter-network (with protocol conversion) |
| **Broadcast Handling** | Forwards all | Forwards | Forwards | **Drops/Segments** | Variable |
| **Intelligence** | None | Low | Medium | **High** | Highest |
| **Example Device** | Old 4-port hub | Netgear bridge | Cisco Catalyst 2960 | **Cisco ISR 4000** | Email gateway |

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Decision Tree for Router Packet Processing (FIB Lookup)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PACKET ARRIVES] --> B{TTL GREATER THAN 0}
    B -- NO --> X1[DROP PACKET AND SEND ICMP TIME EXCEEDED]
    B -- YES --> C[EXTRACT DESTINATION IP]
    C --> D{LONGEST PREFIX MATCH IN FIB}
    D -- MATCH FOUND --> E[SELECT OUTGOING INTERFACE]
    D -- NO MATCH --> F{DEFAULT ROUTE EXISTS}
    F -- YES --> G[FORWARD TO DEFAULT GATEWAY]
    F -- NO --> H[DROP AND SEND ICMP DESTINATION UNREACHABLE]
    E --> I[DECREMENT TTL]
    I --> J[RECALCULATE CHECKSUM]
    J --> K[ENCAPSULATE IN NEW L2 FRAME]
    K --> L[TRANSMIT OUT EGRESS PORT]
```

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> *As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 questions carry 14 marks with an internal choice. The question must have sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the internal architecture of a router with a neat block diagram.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast Distance Vector and Link State routing algorithms. Give one example protocol for each.**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution — Question A

#### **Part (a) — Router Architecture (7 marks)**

A modern router consists of **four major functional components** working in unison:

**1. Input Ports (2 marks):**
- Terminate the physical link and perform Layer-1 (signal) and Layer-2 (framing) functions.
- Strip the L2 header and pass the packet's IP payload to the switching fabric.
- Perform **destination IP lookup** against the **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** stored in high-speed **TCAM** memory.
- Buffer the packet using **input queues** during congestion.

**2. Switching Fabric (2 marks):**
- The internal "highway" that transfers the packet from input to output port.
- **Three implementation styles:**
  - **Memory-based** (shared bus to CPU memory — slow)
  - **Bus-based** (shared datapath through a backplane)
  - **Crossbar-based** (parallel paths — used in high-speed core routers like Cisco CRS-3)

**3. Output Ports (1.5 marks):**
- Receive packets from the switching fabric.
- Buffer them in **output queues** (FIFO, PQ, WFQ, CBQ).
- Encapsulate in the appropriate **Layer-2 frame** for the outgoing link.
- Transmit onto the physical medium.

**4. Routing Processor (1.5 marks):**
- A powerful **CPU/ASIC** running the router's operating system (Cisco IOS, Juniper Junos, MikroTik RouterOS).
- Runs **routing protocols** (OSPF, BGP, RIP, IS-IS).
- Builds and maintains the **Routing Information Base (RIB)**.
- Computes best paths and programs the **FIB** into hardware TCAMs for line-rate forwarding.

**Supporting Block Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):**
The crossbar switching fabric connects **N input ports** to **N output ports**, while the **routing processor** (with the RIB/FIB) provides control-plane intelligence and supplies lookup tables to the data plane.

---

#### **Part (b) — Distance Vector vs Link State (7 marks)**

| Parameter | **Distance Vector** | **Link State** |
|:----------|:-------------------:|:--------------:|
| **Algorithm** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra's SPF |
| **Information Shared** | Entire routing table (to neighbors only) | Link-state advertisements (LSAs) to ALL routers |
| **Topology View** | "Rumor" — only neighbor's perspective | "Map" — full LSDB of entire area |
| **Metric** | Hop count (RIP) | Cost based on bandwidth (OSPF) |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (minutes) | Fast (seconds) |
| **CPU/Memory Usage** | Low | High |
| **Routing Loops?** | Yes (unless split horizon / poison reverse used) | No |
| **Scalability** | Limited (max 15 hops in RIP) | High (hierarchical areas) |
| **Example Protocol** | **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** | **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)** |
| **Network Size** | Small (< 15 hops) | Large enterprise / ISP |

**Explanation (3.5 marks for in-depth content):**

- **Distance Vector (RIP):** Each router periodically (every 30s in RIP) sends its **entire routing table** to its directly connected neighbors. The neighbor adds **1 to the hop count** and updates its own table. The **maximum hop count = 15** in RIP, and **16 = unreachable**. Prone to **count-to-infinity** problems, mitigated by **split horizon**, **route poisoning**, and **hold-down timers**.

- **Link State (OSPF):** Each router floods **LSAs (Link State Advertisements)** describing its directly connected links to **all routers in the area**. Every router builds an identical **Link State Database (LSDB)** and runs **Dijkstra's SPF (Shortest Path First)** algorithm to compute the shortest-path tree. OSPF uses **cost** = Reference BW (100 Mbps) / Interface BW as its metric, converges in seconds, and supports **hierarchical areas (Area 0 backbone)** for scalability.

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2 / Alternative) — [KTU University Exam - May 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of a router with the help of a flowchart. Discuss its key functions.**

**(b) [7 Marks] What is a routing table? Explain its structure with an example. List the fields present in a typical routing table entry.**

---

### 🔑 Model Solution — Question B

#### **Part (a) — Working of a Router (7 marks)**

A router operates through a **sequential packet-processing pipeline**. The flowchart (already provided in Section 4.5) depicts the following steps:

1. **Packet Reception** – Bits arrive at the input port; L1/L2 decapsulation occurs.
2. **TTL Check** – If TTL = 0, the packet is dropped and an **ICMP Time Exceeded** message is sent back to the source.
3. **Header Inspection** – The destination IP is extracted from the IPv4 header.
4. **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** – The FIB is searched; the **most specific matching prefix** wins (e.g., `/24` beats `/16` beats `/0`).
5. **Route Decision**:
   - **Direct delivery** (same subnet) → ARP for the destination's MAC.
   - **Indirect delivery** → forward to the next-hop router.
   - **No match, default route exists** → forward to default gateway.
   - **No match, no default** → drop and send **ICMP Destination Unreachable**.
6. **TTL Decrement + Checksum Recalculation** – Because TTL changed.
7. **L2 Re-encapsulation** – New Ethernet frame with the **next-hop MAC** as destination.
8. **Egress Transmission** – Packet leaves via the chosen output port.

**Key Functions of a Router (3 marks):**
- **Path Determination & Forwarding** (the primary role)
- **Filtering (ACLs)** – Access Control Lists block/permit traffic.
- **NAT (Network Address Translation)** – Maps private → public IPs.
- **DHCP Server/Relay** – Dynamic IP address allocation.
- **VPN Termination** – IPsec/GRE tunnels.
- **QoS / Traffic Shaping** – Prioritising voice/video traffic.

---

#### **Part (b) — Routing Table Structure (7 marks)**

A **routing table** is a data file stored in **RAM** (volatile) that contains a list of known **destination networks**, the **next-hop address** to reach them, the **outgoing interface**, and the **administrative distance / metric** of the route.

**Example Routing Table (Cisco IOS):**

```
Codes: C - connected, S - static, O - OSPF, R - RIP, B - BGP
       D - EIGRP, * - candidate default

Gateway of last resort is 10.0.0.1 to network 0.0.0.0

S*   0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 10.0.0.1
C    10.0.0.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0
O    172.16.5.0/28 [110/2] via 192.168.1.2, 00:12:45, GigabitEthernet0/1
R    192.168.10.0/24 [120/3] via 192.168.1.5, 00:00:18, GigabitEthernet0/2
```

**Fields Explained (7 marks allocation):**

| Field | Meaning |
|:------|:--------|
| **Code (S, C, O, R, B)** | Source of the route — Static, Connected, OSPF, RIP, BGP |
| **Destination Network** | The IP prefix the route applies to (e.g., `172.16.5.0/28`) |
| **Administrative Distance** | Trustworthiness of the route source (lower = better) |
| **Metric** | Cost to reach the destination (OSPF cost, RIP hops) |
| **Next-Hop IP** | IP address of the adjacent router to forward the packet to |
| **Outgoing Interface** | Local router port used (GigabitEthernet0/0) |
| **Uptime / Age** | How long the route has been in the table |

**Key Principle — Administrative Distance (2 marks for emphasis):**
When multiple routing protocols provide a route to the same destination, the router selects the route with the **lowest Administrative Distance (AD)**. Standard values:
- **Connected = 0**, **Static = 1**, **eBGP = 20**, **OSPF = 110**, **RIP = 120**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Switch" with "Router" in definitions** — Always explicitly state **Layer 3** and **IP-based forwarding** when defining a router.
> 2. **Missing the Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** — In routing table questions, students often stop at the **first match** instead of the **most specific prefix**.
> 3. **Forgetting TTL Decrement + Checksum Recalculation** — In packet processing questions, omitting these two steps is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing up RIB vs FIB** — **RIB** is the control-plane (full routing table from all protocols); **FIB** is the data-plane (best routes, TCAM-loaded for fast lookup).
> 5. **Distance Vector vs Link State confusion** — RIP is **NOT** link state. BGP is **NOT** distance vector (it is **path vector**).
> 6. **Subnetting errors** — Forgetting to subtract **2** for the **network and broadcast addresses** when computing usable hosts.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark questions** — A neatly labelled block diagram of router architecture is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **router** is a **Layer-3** device that forwards packets between **different IP networks** using a **routing table**.
- ✅ Router architecture: **Input Ports → Switching Fabric → Output Ports**, all controlled by a **Routing Processor**.
- ✅ Three switching fabric types: **Memory, Bus, Crossbar** (crossbar is the fastest).
- ✅ **Static routing** = manually configured, used in stub networks; **Dynamic routing** = automatic, uses protocols.
- ✅ **Distance Vector** (RIP, Bellman-Ford) uses **hop count**; **Link State** (OSPF, Dijkstra SPF) uses **cost**.
- ✅ **RIP max hop = 15**; **16 = unreachable**.
- ✅ **OSPF Cost** = Reference BW / Interface BW (Cisco default = 100 Mbps).
- ✅ **Administrative Distance** decides which route wins when multiple protocols advertise the same prefix.
- ✅ **Longest Prefix Match (LPM)** is the core lookup algorithm in the FIB.
- ✅ **TTL** must be decremented and **checksum recalculated** on every router hop.
- ✅ **Subnet formula**: $n_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$ (exclude network and broadcast).
- ✅ **Network Address** = IP **AND** Mask (bitwise).
- ✅ **Broadcast Address** = Network **OR** (Inverse Mask).
- ✅ **Route aggregation (CIDR/supernetting)** reduces the global routing table size — `/22` aggregates 4 contiguous `/24` networks.
- ✅ **End-to-end delay** = $D_{proc} + D_{queue} + D_{trans} + D_{prop}$.
- ✅ **Transmission delay** = $L/R$; **Propagation delay** = $d/s$.
- ✅ Routers use **TCAM (Ternary CAM)** for $O(1)$ longest-prefix lookups.
- ✅ **Default MTU = 1500 bytes** for Ethernet.
- ✅ A router connects heterogeneous networks (LAN, MAN, WAN) and forms the **backbone of the Internet**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data and Computer Communication**, a **Switch** is a multiport networking device that operates primarily at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI Reference Model, designed to receive, process, and forward data frames between interconnected network segments using **Media Access Control (MAC) addresses**. Switches build and dynamically maintain a **MAC Address Table** (also called a Content Addressable Memory or CAM table) to intelligently direct incoming frames only toward the specific destination port, thereby reducing collision domains and improving overall network efficiency.

Switches form the backbone of modern **Local Area Networks (LANs)** and serve as the fundamental building blocks for **packet-switched networks** as defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus under Module 2 (Switching Techniques and Networks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):**
> A switch is a device that connects multiple devices within a Local Area Network (LAN) and uses packet switching to receive, process, and forward data to the destination device. It is fundamentally classified into **Circuit Switches**, **Message Switches**, and **Packet Switches** (which further include **Datagram** and **Virtual Circuit** approaches).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Railway Junction Analogy**
>
> Imagine a **railway junction** in a busy city. Trains (data packets) arrive at the junction (switch) from multiple directions. The junction master (the switch's logic) checks the train's destination tag (MAC address) and switches the tracks (port selection) to send the train to the correct platform (output port).
>
> - A **Hub** is like a railway announcer shouting the train's destination in every platform — everyone hears, including those not going anywhere — causing "collisions."
> - A **Switch** is like a smart junction master who silently and directly routes the train to the exact platform — only the correct destination receives the data.

This is precisely how a switch works: it listens to incoming frames, learns which device lives at which port (by reading source MAC addresses), and creates a private "conversation path" between sender and receiver.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized for switches in LAN environments:

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Forwarding Rate** | Measured in **Mpps** (Million Packets Per Second) | Indicates switching speed |
| **Backplane Bandwidth** | Measured in **Gbps** (Gigabits Per Second) | Internal switching fabric capacity |
| **MAC Table Size** | Typically **4,000 to 32,000+ entries** | Maximum learned addresses |
| **Switching Latency** | Measured in **microseconds (µs)** | Time taken to forward a frame |
| **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** | Standard **1500 bytes** (Ethernet) | Maximum frame size handled |

### 1.4 Three Major Categories of Switches (KTU Syllabus Focus)

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 specifically focuses on the following three classifications based on the **switching technique** used:

1. **Circuit Switching** — A dedicated physical path is established before communication (like traditional telephone networks).
2. **Message Switching** — The entire message is stored at each intermediate node (store-and-forward) and then forwarded.
3. **Packet Switching** — The message is broken into smaller packets, each routed independently.
   - **Datagram Approach** — Each packet is independent (connectionless).
   - **Virtual Circuit Approach** — A logical path is established before data transfer (connection-oriented).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most frequently asked distinction in university exams is between **Datagram Packet Switching** and **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** — students must know the comparison table by heart, including delay calculations and path determination logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Switching — Operational Breakdown

In circuit switching, three sequential phases occur:

1. **Connection Establishment Phase**: A dedicated end-to-end physical path (circuit) is reserved between source and destination before any data transfer. All switches along the path reserve bandwidth for this exclusive call.
2. **Data Transfer Phase**: Data flows continuously through the established circuit. The path is dedicated — no other user can use it, even during silent periods.
3. **Connection Termination Phase**: The dedicated circuit is torn down and resources are released back to the network pool.

**Why use Circuit Switching?**
- Guaranteed bandwidth and constant transmission delay.
- Suitable for real-time voice traffic (traditional PSTN telephony).
- Simple, predictable performance once the circuit is established.

**Disadvantages:**
- Inefficient bandwidth utilization during silence/idle periods.
- Long setup delay (dialing, ringing, connecting).
- If a link in the circuit fails, the entire connection breaks.

### 2.2 Message Switching — Operational Breakdown

Message switching uses the **store-and-forward** principle:

1. The **entire message** is received completely at each intermediate switch.
2. The switch stores the message in its buffer (memory/disk).
3. When the outgoing link is free, the entire message is forwarded to the next switch.
4. This process repeats until the message reaches the destination.

**Why use Message Switching?**
- No dedicated path is reserved — better bandwidth utilization.
- Each switch can perform error checking; corrupted messages are discarded.
- Suitable for non-real-time applications (early email systems, telegraph networks).

**Disadvantages:**
- Very high delay because the entire message must be received before forwarding can begin.
- Each switch must have huge buffer storage (entire message must fit).
- Not suitable for interactive or real-time applications.

### 2.3 Packet Switching — The Modern Standard

Packet switching divides the message into smaller **packets** (typically 1000–1500 bytes), each with a **header** containing source/destination addresses and sequence information. There are two variants:

#### 2.3.1 Datagram Packet Switching (Connectionless)

- **Each packet is independent** — no prior connection setup.
- Every packet contains **full source and destination addresses**.
- Different packets of the same message may take **different paths** through the network.
- Packets may arrive **out of order** at the destination; the receiver must reassemble them.
- The network has **no state information** about the flow; each switch decides independently (hop-by-hop routing).
- Analogy: Sending a letter via postal service — each letter is routed independently.

#### 2.3.2 Virtual Circuit Packet Switching (Connection-Oriented)

- A **logical connection (virtual circuit)** is established before data transfer using a setup phase.
- All packets follow the **same path** through the network.
- Each packet contains a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** instead of full addresses — much shorter.
- The destination receives packets in order (no reordering needed).
- Switches maintain **state information** (VCI translation tables) for active virtual circuits.
- Analogy: A phone call that uses a logical channel rather than a physical one.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet is the most essential part for KTU 2024 ESE preparation.** Practice these formulas with numerical substitution.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Total Transmission Delay (Packet)** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) | seconds |
| 2 | **Propagation Delay (per link)** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $s$ = signal speed (m/s) | seconds |
| 3 | **End-to-End Delay (N hops, store-and-forward)** | $T_{total} = N \cdot \left( T_t + T_p \right)$ | $N$ = number of links; each switch adds one $T_t$ | seconds |
| 4 | **Number of Packets** | $n = \dfrac{L_{message}}{L_{packet}}$ | Total message bits divided by packet bits | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Total Time (Message Switching, N switches)** | $T_{msg} = (N) \cdot \left( T_t + T_p \right)$ | Each switch stores and forwards the whole message | seconds |
| 6 | **Total Time (Datagram, P packets, N links)** | $T_{dg} = P \cdot N \cdot \left( T_t + T_p \right)$ | Each packet processed at every node | seconds |
| 7 | **Header Overhead Ratio** | $H\% = \dfrac{h}{L} \times 100$ | $h$ = header size in bits, $L$ = total packet size | percent |
| 8 | **Throughput (Maximum)** | $Throughput \le R$ | Limited by bottleneck link bandwidth | bps |
| 9 | **Circuit Switching Setup Time** | $T_{setup} = N \cdot T_p$ | Path establishment time across N links | seconds |
| 10 | **Virtual Circuit Setup Time** | $T_{vc} = N \cdot \left( T_t + T_p \right)$ | Each node processes setup packet | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction in Delay Formulas:** In **datagram packet switching**, even though the message is split into P packets, EACH packet still incurs store-and-forward delay at EACH of the N intermediate switches. This is a common exam trap — students often forget to multiply by P (number of packets).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Switches are deployed extensively in:

- **Enterprise LANs** — Connecting desktops, servers, printers in offices.
- **Data Centers** — High-speed Top-of-Rack (ToR) and Spine-Leaf switches (e.g., Cisco Nexus, Arista).
- **Telecommunications Backbone** — Virtual Circuit Switching forms the basis of **ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode)** and **Frame Relay** networks.
- **Internet Routing** — Datagram packet switching is the fundamental principle of the modern **Internet Protocol (IP)**.
- **5G Core Networks** — Use a hybrid approach combining circuit-switched (voice) and packet-switched (data) domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Note:** Modern programmable switches (whitebox switches) running **SONiC** or **DENT** operating systems are now replacing proprietary hardware in hyperscale data centers — students should be aware of this industry shift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Datagram vs Virtual Circuit vs Circuit

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Datagram Packet Switching | Virtual Circuit Packet Switching |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connection Setup** | Required (physical) | Not required | Required (logical) |
| **Path Determination** | At setup time (fixed) | Per packet (dynamic) | At setup time (fixed logical path) |
| **Addressing Used** | End-to-end address | Full source + destination per packet | Short VCI per packet |
| **Packet Order at Destination** | In order | May arrive out of order | In order |
| **Switch State** | Connection state maintained | Stateless | VCI translation table maintained |
| **Delay During Transfer** | Constant, low | Variable (jitter possible) | Variable but predictable |
| **Bandwidth Utilization** | Poor (reserved but unused) | Excellent | Good |
| **Failure Recovery** | Entire call fails | Each packet rerouted independently | New VC established |
| **Overhead** | Lowest | Highest (long headers per packet) | Medium |
| **Best Suited For** | Voice (PSTN) | Internet data, bursty traffic | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 |
| **Example Real System** | Traditional telephone | **Internet (IP)** | ATM networks |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Total Delay in Datagram Packet Switching

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a message of total size **L bits** that must travel across a network path with **N intermediate links** (i.e., N-1 intermediate switches). The link bandwidth is **R bits per second** and the propagation delay per link is **T_p** seconds. The message is split into **P packets** of equal size.

**Step 1: Determine the packet size**

Since the message L is split into P equal packets, the size of each packet (including header) is:

$$L_{packet} = \dfrac{L}{P} \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the transmission time for one packet**

The time taken to push all bits of one packet onto the link is the transmission delay:

$$T_t = \dfrac{L_{packet}}{R} = \dfrac{L}{P \cdot R} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Determine store-and-forward delay at one switch**

In store-and-forward switching, a switch must receive the **entire packet** before it can begin forwarding. Thus, at every intermediate switch:

$$T_{per\_switch} = T_t + T_p = \dfrac{L}{P \cdot R} + T_p$$

**Step 4: Count the number of switches**

If there are **N links**, then there are **N-1 intermediate switches** plus the destination (which receives but does not forward). The source is the origin. Total forwarding operations = **N** (one transmission delay per link).

**Step 5: Handle pipeline effect between packets**

This is the trickiest part. Once the first packet reaches the second switch and begins being forwarded, the first switch can begin receiving the second packet. This **pipelining** drastically reduces the total delay compared to a naive summation.

**The total delay for the first packet to reach the destination** is:

$$T_{first} = N \cdot T_t + N \cdot T_p = N \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{P \cdot R} + T_p\right)$$

**The remaining (P-1) packets arrive at intervals of T_t** (because of pipelining), so the total time for the entire message to arrive is:

$$T_{first} + (P - 1) \cdot T_t$$

**Step 6: Final General Formula**

$$T_{datagram} = N \cdot T_p + (N + P - 1) \cdot T_t$$

Substituting $T_t = \dfrac{L}{P \cdot R}$:

$$T_{datagram} = N \cdot T_p + (N + P - 1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{P \cdot R}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Style):**
> Suppose a message of **8000 bits** is split into **4 packets** (each 2000 bits) and must traverse **3 links** at **R = 1000 bps**, with **T_p = 0.01 s** per link.
>
> - $T_t = 2000 / 1000 = 2$ seconds
> - $T_{datagram} = 3 \cdot 0.01 + (3 + 4 - 1) \cdot 2 = 0.03 + 12 = \mathbf{12.03 \text{ seconds}}$
>
> Compare this with message switching where the ENTIRE 8000-bit message is sent as one block:
> - $T_{message} = 3 \cdot 0.01 + 3 \cdot (8000/1000) = 0.03 + 24 = \mathbf{24.03 \text{ seconds}}$
>
> **Conclusion:** Packet switching is **2x faster** than message switching for this configuration.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Virtual Circuit vs Datagram — Path Determination Cost

In **Datagram** switching, each switch must perform a **full routing table lookup** based on the destination address contained in every packet header.

$$C_{datagram} = P \cdot N \cdot C_{lookup}$$

where $C_{lookup}$ is the cost of one table lookup.

In **Virtual Circuit** switching, the lookup is much faster because the switch only matches the **short VCI** (typically 12–24 bits) in its translation table, not the full IP address (32 or 128 bits).

$$C_{vc} = P \cdot N \cdot C_{VCI} + 1 \cdot C_{setup}$$

Since $C_{VCI} \ll C_{lookup}$ but the setup cost $C_{setup}$ is incurred only **once**, for large P (many packets), Virtual Circuit becomes more efficient **per packet**.

**For the breakeven point** where both methods have equal cost:

$$P \cdot C_{VCI} + C_{setup} = P \cdot C_{lookup}$$

Solving for P:

$$P_{breakeven} = \dfrac{C_{setup}}{C_{lookup} - C_{VCI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is precisely why **X.25** and **ATM** (which are virtual-circuit-based) dominated the 1980s–1990s for high-traffic enterprise networks. The Internet (datagram-based) won eventually because of its **simplicity and resilience**, not raw speed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of a Network Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Frame Arrives on Port 1] --> B[Physical Layer PHY Receiver]
    B --> C[MAC Layer Frame Parser]
    C --> D[Source MAC Address Extracted]
    D --> E[CAM Table Lookup and Learning]
    E --> F{Destination MAC Known in CAM Table?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Retrieve Destination Port Number]
    F -- No --> H[Flood Frame to All Ports Except Source]
    G --> I[Frame Queue and Buffer Management]
    H --> I
    I --> J[QoS and Priority Scheduling]
    J --> K[Switch Fabric Crossbar or Shared Bus]
    K --> L[PHY Transmitter on Destination Port]
    L --> M[Outgoing Frame Leaves on Correct Port]
    E --> N[Update CAM Table Entry Timer]
    N --> O[CAM Table Stored in TCAM Memory]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This flow shows the complete decision-making process inside a Layer-2 switch. The CAM (Content Addressable Memory) lookup at node F is the heart of the switch — if the MAC address is known, the frame is forwarded unicast; if unknown, it is flooded. The CAM table timer at node N ensures stale entries are removed automatically (typically every **300 seconds** by default in Cisco switches).

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Three Switching Techniques Compared

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host] --> B1[Switch 1]
    A --> B2[Switch 1]
    A --> B3[Switch 1]
    B1 --> C1[Switch 2 Circuit]
    B1 --> C2[Switch 2 Message]
    B1 --> C3[Switch 2 Datagram]
    C1 --> D1[Switch 3 Dedicated Physical Path]
    C2 --> D2[Switch 3 Store Entire Message]
    C3 --> D3[Switch 3 Forward Independent Packets]
    D1 --> E1[Destination Receives Continuous Stream]
    D2 --> E2[Destination Receives Whole Message After Delay]
    D3 --> E3[Destination Reassembles Packets in Order]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Virtual Circuit Setup, Data Transfer, Teardown Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source wants to send data] --> B[Send CALL REQUEST Packet to Destination]
    B --> C{Intermediate Switch Has Free Resources?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Reserve Bandwidth and Create VCI Mapping]
    C -- No --> E[Send CALL REJECTED Back to Source]
    D --> F[Switch Creates VCI Translation Table Entry]
    F --> G[Propagate CALL REQUEST to Next Switch]
    G --> C
    C -- All Switches OK --> H[Destination Sends CALL ACCEPTED Back]
    H --> I[Virtual Circuit Established and Ready]
    I --> J[Data Packets Flow Using Short VCI Only]
    J --> K[All Packets Follow Same Logical Path]
    K --> L[Destination Sends CLEAR REQUEST to Release VC]
    L --> M[All Switches Free VCI Table Entries]
    M --> N[VC Terminated and Resources Released]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Switch Internal Block Architecture (Hardware View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_PORTS
        P1[Port 1 PHY MAC Buffer]
        P2[Port 2 PHY MAC Buffer]
        P3[Port 3 PHY MAC Buffer]
        P4[Port 4 PHY MAC Buffer]
    end
    subgraph SWITCHING_ENGINE
        CAM[CAM Table MAC to Port Mapping]
        QUEUE[Packet Queue Manager]
        FABRIC[High Speed Crossbar Switch Fabric]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_PORTS
        O1[Port 1 PHY Output]
        O2[Port 2 PHY Output]
        O3[Port 3 PHY Output]
        O4[Port 4 PHY Output]
    end
    P1 --> FABRIC
    P2 --> FABRIC
    P3 --> FABRIC
    P4 --> FABRIC
    CAM --> FABRIC
    QUEUE --> FABRIC
    FABRIC --> O1
    FABRIC --> O2
    FABRIC --> O3
    FABRIC --> O4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Learning Tip:** The crossbar switch fabric (central block) is the most critical component. In modern high-end switches, the crossbar operates at multiple Tbps. Examples include the **Broadcom Tomahawk** series used in data center switches.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Circuit Switching and Packet Switching. List any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Path Setup** | Dedicated physical path established before data transfer | No dedicated path; packets routed independently |
| **Resource Utilization** | Inefficient (reserved even during silence) | Efficient (resources shared dynamically) |
| **Delay** | Constant after setup; no queuing | Variable due to queuing and congestion |

> **(1 mark each for any three correct points. Award full 3 marks if all three are clearly distinct.)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. What is a Virtual Circuit? Explain how it differs from a Datagram approach.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Circuit (VC)** is a logical connection-oriented path established between source and destination before data transfer begins. All packets follow the same logical route and are identified by a short **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** at each switch.

**Differences:**
1. **VC is connection-oriented** (setup phase required); **Datagram is connectionless** (no setup).
2. **VC uses short VCI** in packet headers; **Datagram uses full source and destination addresses**.
3. **VC delivers packets in order**; **Datagram may deliver packets out of order** requiring reassembly.

> **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three differences — award 1 mark for every two valid differences.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain the three phases of **Circuit Switching** with a neat diagram. Mention any **two advantages and two disadvantages** of circuit switching. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A message of **10,000 bits** is to be sent across **4 packet-switched datagram networks** with **3 intermediate links** of **1000 bps** each. The message is split into **5 packets** of equal size. Propagation delay is **0.005 seconds per link**. Calculate the total time required for the entire message to reach the destination using **datagram packet switching** with pipelining. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Three Phases of Circuit Switching (7 marks):**

The three phases of circuit switching are:

1. **Connection Establishment Phase:** A dedicated physical path is established from source to destination through a sequence of switches. The source sends a setup signal that propagates through the network, reserving bandwidth and switch resources at every node. Once the destination acknowledges, the circuit is ready.
2. **Data Transfer Phase:** Data flows continuously over the established dedicated path. The transmission delay is constant, and the path is exclusively reserved — no other user can use it even during silent periods.
3. **Connection Termination Phase:** After data transfer completes, a teardown signal is sent to release the reserved resources at all switches along the path.

**Diagram (1 mark — must show source, destination, and intermediate switches with reserved path):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Host] -- Dedicated Circuit --> B[Switch 1]
    B -- Dedicated Circuit --> C[Switch 2]
    C -- Dedicated Circuit --> D[Switch 3]
    D -- Dedicated Circuit --> E[Destination Host]
```

**Two Advantages (1 mark each):**
- Guaranteed bandwidth and constant transmission delay.
- Simple, predictable performance suitable for real-time voice.

**Two Disadvantages (1 mark each):**
- Inefficient bandwidth utilization during silent periods.
- Long setup delay before data transfer begins.

> **Total: 3 marks for phases + 1 mark for diagram + 2 marks for advantages + 2 marks for disadvantages = 8 marks** *(adjust to 7 by combining phase explanations concisely as per KTU valuation norms).*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
