- Total message size: L = 10,000 bits
- Number of packets: P = 5
- Link bandwidth: R = 1000 bps
- Number of links: N = 3
- Propagation delay per link: $T_p$ = 0.005 s

**Step 1: Calculate packet size and transmission delay**

$$L_{packet} = \dfrac{L}{P} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{5} = 2000 \text{ bits}$$

$$T_t = \dfrac{L_{packet}}{R} = \dfrac{2000}{1000} = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Apply the datagram formula with pipelining**

$$T_{datagram} = N \cdot T_p + (N + P - 1) \cdot T_t$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$T_{datagram} = 3 \cdot 0.005 + (3 + 5 - 1) \cdot 2$$

$$T_{datagram} = 0.015 + 7 \cdot 2 = 0.015 + 14$$

$$T_{datagram} = 14.015 \text{ seconds}$$

> **Answer: The total time required is 14.015 seconds.**
>
> **(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for each intermediate step, 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for units = 7 marks total)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** Compare **Datagram Packet Switching** and **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** in terms of **connection setup, addressing, packet ordering, routing decision, overhead, and reliability**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1500-byte message** is sent from host A to host B across a **4-link path** with link bandwidth **R = 56 kbps** and propagation delay **10 ms per link**. If the message is sent as a single message-switched unit, calculate the total delivery time. If instead it is sent as **3 datagram packets of 500 bytes each**, calculate the time with pipelining. Compare both results. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Connection Setup** | None — connectionless | Required — connection-oriented |
| **Addressing** | Full source + destination address in every packet | Short VCI in every packet |
| **Packet Ordering** | Out-of-order delivery possible | In-order delivery guaranteed |
| **Routing Decision** | Per packet, independent at each switch | At setup time, fixed logical path |
| **Header Overhead** | High (full address ~ 40 bytes for IP) | Low (VCI ~ 2–4 bytes) |
| **Switch State** | Stateless | Stateful (VCI table maintained) |
| **Reliability** | Better (packets rerouted on link failure) | Worse (entire VC fails on link failure) |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** L = 1500 bytes = 12,000 bits, R = 56 kbps = 56,000 bps, T_p = 10 ms = 0.01 s, N = 4 links

**Case 1: Message Switching (single unit)**

$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{56{,}000} = 0.2143 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{msg} = N \cdot (T_t + T_p) = 4 \cdot (0.2143 + 0.01) = 4 \cdot 0.2243 = 0.8971 \text{ s}$$

**Case 2: Datagram Packet Switching (3 packets of 500 bytes each)**

Packet size: $L_p = 500 \text{ bytes} = 4000 \text{ bits}$

$$T_t = \dfrac{4000}{56{,}000} = 0.0714 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{dg} = N \cdot T_p + (N + P - 1) \cdot T_t = 4 \cdot 0.01 + (4 + 3 - 1) \cdot 0.0714$$

$$T_{dg} = 0.04 + 6 \cdot 0.0714 = 0.04 + 0.4284 = 0.4684 \text{ s}$$

**Comparison:**

$$Speedup = \dfrac{0.8971}{0.4684} \approx 1.92$$

> **Answer:** Message switching takes **0.8971 s**; Datagram packet switching takes **0.4684 s**. Datagram switching is approximately **1.92 times faster** due to pipelining.
>
> **(1 mark for each correct intermediate result, 1 mark for comparison comment, 1 mark for final answer = 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the pipelining factor** — Students compute $(N \cdot P \cdot T_t)$ instead of $(N + P - 1) \cdot T_t$. This loses **3 marks** in numerical questions.
> 2. **Mixing up header overhead** — In datagram switching, each packet has its own header. The total bits transmitted are slightly MORE than the original message, not less.
> 3. **Confusing N (links) with N-1 (switches)** — A path with 4 links has exactly **3 intermediate switches**.
> 4. **Not mentioning connection-oriented vs connectionless** explicitly in theory answers — KTU examiners allocate **1 dedicated mark** for this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Writing "14.015" without "seconds" loses 0.5 mark in the final step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Switch**: A multiport Layer-2 device that forwards frames based on MAC addresses using a CAM table.
- **Three switching techniques**: Circuit, Message, and Packet Switching.
- **Circuit Switching**: Dedicated physical path; three phases — setup, transfer, teardown.
- **Message Switching**: Store-and-forward the ENTIRE message at each node; high delay, high storage.
- **Packet Switching**: Message broken into packets; pipelining reduces delay significantly.
- **Datagram**: Connectionless, stateless, full addressing, packets may arrive out of order.
- **Virtual Circuit**: Connection-oriented, stateful, uses short VCI, in-order delivery.
- **Master Formula (Datagram)**: $T_{dg} = N \cdot T_p + (N + P - 1) \cdot T_t$
- **Master Formula (Message)**: $T_{msg} = N \cdot (T_t + T_p)$
- **CAM Table**: Stores MAC-to-port mappings; auto-learned by reading source addresses.
- **Flooding**: When destination MAC is unknown, frame is sent to all ports except source.
- **Store-and-Forward**: Switch must receive full packet before forwarding begins.
- **Pipelining Effect**: Reduces total time by overlapping transmission across switches.
- **VCI**: Short identifier (typically 12–24 bits) used in virtual circuit switching.
- **Comparison Chart**: Must include — connection setup, addressing, ordering, routing, overhead, reliability.
- **Real-World Examples**: Internet (IP) uses Datagram; ATM/Frame Relay/X.25 use Virtual Circuit; PSTN uses Circuit.
- **MTU**: Standard Ethernet MTU is **1500 bytes**.
- **CAM Table Aging**: Default **300 seconds** in Cisco switches.
- **Switching Fabric**: The internal high-speed bus/crossbar connecting input and output ports.
- **Forouzan Reference**: Chapter on Switching (Data Communications and Networking) is the KTU prescribed reference for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hubs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hubs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Hub** is a **Physical Layer (Layer 1 of the OSI Reference Model)** networking device that acts as a common connection point for multiple devices in a network, typically arranged in a **star topology**. It is fundamentally a **signal repeater** that receives an incoming electrical signal on any of its ports and retransmits (broadcasts) that signal out through **all other active ports**, without performing any frame inspection, addressing lookup, or intelligent filtering.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, hubs are classified under **Signal Regeneration and Broadcasting Devices** and operate strictly on the **bit-level electrical/optical representation**, ignoring frame boundaries, MAC addresses, or logical addressing (IP).

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU Board-Definition (Reproduce verbatim in 2-mark answers):**
> *"A hub is a Layer-1 physical device that connects multiple network segments, repeating every incoming bit stream to all other ports, thereby forming a single shared collision domain."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bulletin Board Analogy"

Imagine a **public bulletin board** in a college corridor:
- **Hubs** function exactly like this bulletin board. When one student (device) pins a notice (data packet) to the board, **every other student in the corridor sees the notice simultaneously**, regardless of whether it is intended for them.
- There is **no logic** to decide *who* should see what; the notice is simply **broadcast to everyone**.
- The "intended recipient" has to read the notice and decide if it is for them.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Real-world mapping:** This is exactly how an Ethernet hub operates — it sends incoming data to **every connected device**, and the device's **Network Interface Card (NIC)** filters out packets not destined for it (based on MAC address). However, if **two devices transmit simultaneously**, a **collision** occurs, because they share the same physical medium.

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics & Physical Constants

| Property | Typical Value/Standard | Significance |
|----------|------------------------|--------------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical Layer) | Pure electrical/optical signal handling |
| **Device Class** | Repeater / Multi-port Repeater | Regenerates and forwards bits |
| **Broadcast Behavior** | Flooding (to all ports) | No intelligence at frame level |
| **Collision Domain** | **1 (single shared domain)** | All ports share bandwidth |
| **Typical Data Rate** | **10 Mbps / 100 Mbps** (Fast Ethernet legacy) | Standard hub speeds |
| **Port Count** | **4, 8, 12, 16, 24 ports** | Common in legacy LANs |
| **Signal Type** | Electrical (twisted-pair copper) | Sometimes fiber variants |
| **Logical Segmentation** | **None** | Cannot create VLANs or subnets |
| **Address Awareness** | **Zero** | Ignores MAC/IP addresses |

## 1.4 Classification of Hubs (KTU 2024 Module 2 Categorization)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> The KTU syllabus explicitly classifies hubs into three categories. Memorizing these is **mandatory** for short-answer questions.

1. **Passive Hub** — Acts as a **physical connector** only. Does **not** regenerate or amplify the signal. It simply routes signals from one device to another. Cable distance is severely limited (~30% of active hub).
2. **Active Hub (Repeater Hub)** — **Regenerates and amplifies** the incoming signal before broadcasting. Functions as a **multi-port repeater**. Most common hub type.
3. **Intelligent Hub (Smart Hub)** — An **active hub with management features** (SNMP monitoring, port configuration, fault isolation). Acts as a bridge between passive networking and managed switches.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Bit-by-Bit Operational Flow

A hub operates on an extremely simple principle: **"Hear one, transmit to all."** The internal architecture is essentially a **shared bus with multiple ports**, and the broadcast logic is implemented in hardware using a **backplane bus**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of a Hub's Operation:

1. **Signal Inception (Step 1 — Reception):**
   A device (e.g., PC-A) connected to port 3 transmits electrical pulses over the twisted pair cable. These pulses represent encoded **bits** (using **Manchester Encoding** in 10Base-T Ethernet or **MLT-3** in 100Base-TX).

2. **Signal Conditioning (Step 2 — Amplification):**
   If the hub is **active**, the repeater circuitry on the receiving port **re-shapes and re-amplifies** the weakened signal to restore the original voltage levels (typically **±2.5 V** for 10Base-T). This is critical because signal attenuation over Category 5 UTP cable degrades the SNR.

3. **Collision Detection (Step 3 — Sensing):**
   The hub continuously monitors for **signal collisions** (i.e., two devices transmitting at the same time). Modern hubs implement the **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** protocol inherited from Ethernet.

4. **Broadcast Distribution (Step 4 — Flooding):**
   The reconditioned bit stream is **simultaneously sent out** through every **other port** of the hub (except the originating port). This is the **flooding** behavior.

5. **Destination Filtering (Step 5 — Frame Acceptance at NIC):**
   Each receiving device's **Network Interface Card (NIC)** examines the **destination MAC address** in the Ethernet frame. If it matches, the NIC accepts the frame; otherwise, it discards it. (This filtering happens **at the NIC**, not at the hub.)

> [!NOTE] 
> **Why "Why" and "How" matter for KTU valuation:**
> Examiners award 2 marks specifically for explaining that hubs operate at **Layer 1**, work on **bits (not frames)**, and create a **single collision domain**. A common student mistake is confusing hubs with switches.

## 2.2 Throughput, Bandwidth, and Collision Domain Formulas

Because hubs do not segment collision domains, the **effective bandwidth** is shared among all connected devices. This is a **highly-favored numerical question topic** in KTU examinations.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|
| $B_{eff} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{N}$ | Effective per-station bandwidth | Mbps |
| $T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{B_{total}}$ | Maximum bit transmission time | seconds/bit |
| $N_{CSMA/CD} \le \dfrac{2 \cdot T_{prop}}{T_{frame}}$ | Maximum stations in a CSMA/CD domain | dimensionless |
| $D_{max} = \dfrac{c \cdot T_{prop}}{2}$ | Maximum cable distance for collision detection | meters |
| $T_{slot} = 512 \;\text{bits at}\; 10\;\text{Mbps}$ | Slot time (Ethernet 802.3) | **51.2 μs** |
| $L_{min} = \dfrac{2 \cdot d \cdot v}{B} \cdot 8$ | Minimum frame size for collision detection | bits |
| $\text{Collision Domain Size} = 1$ | All hub ports share a single domain | N/A |

> **Where:**
> - $B_{total}$ = total hub bandwidth (e.g., **10 Mbps**)
> - $N$ = number of active devices
> - $T_{prop}$ = one-way propagation delay
> - $T_{frame}$ = frame transmission time
> - $c$ = speed of light in copper ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s)
> - $v$ = propagation velocity in UTP ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s)
> - $d$ = cable length

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Legacy Ethernet Networks (1990s):** Hubs were the **backbone of all 10Base-T Ethernet LANs** before switches became affordable. IEEE **802.3 10Base-T** explicitly specified hub-based star topology.
- **Network Tapping / Monitoring:** Because hubs broadcast all traffic, security professionals use them as **passive network taps** to monitor traffic with packet sniffers (Wireshark).
- **Industrial Fieldbuses:** Some legacy **SCADA / Modbus** networks still use hubs for low-cost interconnection of sensors.

## 2.3 IEEE 802.3 10Base-T Hub Architecture (Reference Model)

The IEEE 802.3 standard (specifically **Section 802.3i** for 10Base-T) defines the hub's functional behavior using a state-machine called the **PLL (Physical Layer Link)** and **MAU (Medium Attachment Unit)**. The standard mandates that:

- The hub must support **Manchester Encoding/Decoding** for 10 Mbps.
- The hub must implement **SQE (Signal Quality Error)** heartbeats.
- The hub must support **Auto-Negotiation** (in later 100Base-TX variants).

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often write that hubs use "CSMA/CA" — this is **wrong**. Hubs use **CSMA/CD** (Collision Detection), as defined in IEEE 802.3. CSMA/CA is for Wi-Fi (802.11).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Effective Bandwidth Per Station (Highly Probable in KTU 14-Mark Questions)

**Problem Statement:** A **12-port 10Base-T Ethernet hub** has all ports active. If each station transmits data with equal probability, calculate:
(a) The effective bandwidth available to each station.
(b) The probability that **two specific stations** transmit simultaneously, causing a collision.
(c) The total network throughput degradation factor compared to a switched Ethernet.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- Number of active ports, $N = 12$
- Total hub bandwidth, $B_{total} = 10$ Mbps (since it is 10Base-T)
- Each station attempts transmission with probability, $p = 0.5$ per time slot

---

**Part (a): Effective Bandwidth per Station**

Since the hub is a **shared medium** with no segmentation, the bandwidth is equally divided.

$$
B_{eff} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{N}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
B_{eff} = \dfrac{10 \text{ Mbps}}{12} = 0.8333 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Result: $B_{eff} \approx \mathbf{833.33 \text{ kbps}}$ per station.**

> **Why this matters:** A 10 Mbps hub with 12 active devices gives only ~833 kbps per device — making it **12× slower** in practice than a switch.

---

**Part (b): Collision Probability for Two Specific Stations**

The probability of any single station transmitting in a given time slot is $p = 0.5$. The probability that **Station-A** transmits AND **Station-B** transmits simultaneously is:

$$
P_{collision} = p_A \times p_B = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25
$$

**Result: 25% chance of collision per time slot for any two specific stations.** This high collision rate is why hubs are unsuitable for networks with more than a few active users.

---

**Part (c): Throughput Degradation vs. Switched Ethernet**

For a hub, the maximum throughput is bounded by:

$$
S_{hub} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{1 + 2N \cdot t_{prop} \cdot B_{total}/L}
$$

Where $L$ = frame size in bits, $t_{prop}$ = propagation delay. For a fully-loaded hub with high collision rates, throughput can degrade to as low as **30-40% of nominal** bandwidth.

In contrast, a **switch** provides **dedicated 10 Mbps per port**, giving a 12-port switch an **aggregate throughput of 120 Mbps**. The improvement factor is:

$$
\text{Speedup Factor} = \dfrac{S_{switch}}{S_{hub}} = \dfrac{12 \times 10 \text{ Mbps}}{10 \text{ Mbps} \times \eta_{collision}}
$$

For typical collision efficiency $\eta_{collision} \approx 0.4$:

$$
\text{Speedup Factor} = \dfrac{120}{4} = \mathbf{30\times \text{ improvement}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Maximum Network Diameter (Cable Length) for 10Base-T

**Problem Statement:** For a 10Base-T Ethernet hub network, the standard mandates a **minimum frame size of 512 bits (64 bytes)** and a maximum **round-trip propagation delay of 51.2 μs** (the slot time). Calculate the **maximum end-to-end cable distance** allowed between the two farthest stations through the hub.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The IEEE 802.3 slot time is defined as:

$$
T_{slot} = 512 \text{ bits} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ s}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}} = 51.2 \;\mu s
$$

The **round-trip propagation delay** is the time taken for a signal to travel from the sender → hub → receiver → hub → sender. The relationship is:

$$
T_{slot} \geq 2 \cdot t_{prop}
$$

The propagation delay for a cable of length $d$ is:

$$
t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}
$$

Where $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s is the velocity in UTP cable. Therefore:

$$
T_{slot} \geq 2 \cdot \dfrac{d_{max}}{v}
$$

Rearranging for $d_{max}$:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{T_{slot} \cdot v}{2}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{51.2 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 2 \times 10^8}{2}
$$

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{51.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^8}{2}
$$

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{10240}{2} = 5120 \text{ meters}
$$

**Result: Theoretical $d_{max} = \mathbf{5120 \text{ meters}}$**, but practical IEEE 802.3 standards limit the maximum segment length to **100 meters** between hub and station, allowing up to **5 segments × 4 hubs = 2500 m** total using repeaters (5-4-3 rule).

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **The 5-4-3 Rule:** A 10Base-T network can have at most **5 segments**, connected by **4 repeaters/hubs**, with only **3 segments** having active user devices. The other 2 are inter-hub links. This is a **frequently asked 7-mark KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hub-Based Star Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC1[PC1 Station] --> Hub1[10Base-T Hub Layer1]
    PC2[PC2 Station] --> Hub1
    PC3[PC3 Station] --> Hub1
    PC4[PC4 Server] --> Hub1
    PC5[PC5 Workstation] --> Hub1
    Hub1 --> CD[Single Shared Collision Domain 10 Mbps]
    Hub1 --> BD[Single Broadcast Domain 255.255.255.255]

    classDef device fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hub fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef domain fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px

    class PC1,PC2,PC3,PC4,PC5 device
    class Hub1 hub
    class CD,BD domain
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture: Internal Hub Signal Path

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Incoming Port MAU] --> B[Manchester Decoder]
    B --> C[Signal Regenerator Amplifier]
    C --> D[Collision Detector CSMA-CD]
    D --> E[Backplane Bus Internal]
    E --> F[Port 2 MAU Driver]
    E --> G[Port 3 MAU Driver]
    E --> H[Port 4 MAU Driver]
    E --> I[Port 5 MAU Driver]
    E --> J[Port N MAU Driver]
    F --> K[Outgoing Port 2]
    G --> L[Outgoing Port 3]
    H --> M[Outgoing Port 4]
    I --> N[Outgoing Port 5]
    J --> O[Outgoing Port N]

    classDef input fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    classDef process fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000

    class A,B,C input
    class D,E process
    class F,G,H,I,J,K,L,M,N,O output
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hub vs. Switch Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HUB_LAYER1[Hub Based Network - Layer 1]
        H1[PC A] -->|10 Mbps shared| HUB_CENTRAL[Hub All Ports]
        H2[PC B] -->|10 Mbps shared| HUB_CENTRAL
        H3[PC C] -->|10 Mbps shared| HUB_CENTRAL
        HUB_CENTRAL -->|Floods all ports| H1
        HUB_CENTRAL -->|Floods all ports| H2
        HUB_CENTRAL -->|Floods all ports| H3
    end

    subgraph SWITCH_LAYER2[Switch Based Network - Layer 2]
        S1[PC A] -->|Dedicated 10 Mbps| SW_CENTRAL[Switch MAC Table]
        S2[PC B] -->|Dedicated 10 Mbps| SW_CENTRAL
        S3[PC C] -->|Dedicated 10 Mbps| SW_CENTRAL
        SW_CENTRAL -->|Forwards only to dest port| S1
        SW_CENTRAL -->|Forwards only to dest port| S2
        SW_CENTRAL -->|Forwards only to dest port| S3
    end

    classDef hubBox fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef switchBox fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef deviceNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000

    class HUB_CENTRAL hubBox
    class SW_CENTRAL switchBox
    class H1,H2,H3,S1,S2,S3 deviceNode
```

## 4.4 Hub Signal Regeneration — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Original Sender] -->|Attenuated Signal| B[Hub Input Port]
    B --> C[Receiver Threshold Detector]
    C --> D[Signal Re-shaper Squaring Circuit]
    D --> E[Amplifier Gain Block]
    E --> F[Fan-out Driver Stage]
    F -->|Restored Signal| G[Output Port 2]
    F -->|Restored Signal| H[Output Port 3]
    F -->|Restored Signal| I[Output Port 4]
    F -->|Restored Signal| J[Output Port N]

    classDef signal fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082
    classDef hw fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B4513
    classDef output fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400

    class A,B signal
    class C,D,E,F hw
    class G,H,I,J output
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between an active hub and a passive hub with suitable examples. Why are passive hubs rarely used in modern Ethernet deployments? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Active Hub | Passive Hub |
|---------|------------|-------------|
| **Signal Regeneration** | Yes — amplifies and re-times bits | No — pure physical connection |
| **Function** | Multi-port repeater | Cable splitter only |
| **Power Supply** | Required | Not required |
| **Maximum Cable Length** | Up to **100 m per segment** | Limited to ~**30%** of active hub range |
| **Example** | 8-port 10Base-T Ethernet hub | BNC T-connector / punch-down block |

**Why passive hubs are obsolete:**
1. Cannot overcome signal attenuation over long cable runs.
2. Cannot extend the 5-4-3 rule limitations of 10Base-T.
3. No collision detection capability — relies entirely on attached NICs.

> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1 mark** for definition, **1 mark** for tabular comparison, and **1 mark** for the "why obsolete" reasoning.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain why a hub cannot be used to create separate collision domains. How does this impact the throughput of a 16-port 10Base-T hub when all ports are active? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
A hub is a **Layer-1 device** that broadcasts incoming signals to all ports, meaning all attached devices share a **single physical medium** and therefore a **single collision domain**. It cannot isolate or segment traffic because it does not interpret MAC addresses or frames.

**Throughput Impact:**
Total bandwidth $B_{total}$ = 10 Mbps, Number of active stations $N$ = 16.

$$
B_{eff} = \dfrac{10 \text{ Mbps}}{16} = 0.625 \text{ Mbps per station}
$$

So each station effectively receives only **625 kbps** due to the shared medium and collision overhead. In contrast, a 16-port switch would provide **dedicated 10 Mbps per port**, giving an aggregate **160 Mbps throughput**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall / Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Writing that a hub is a "Layer 2 device" — **WRONG** (it's Layer 1).
> 2. Confusing the **5-4-3 rule** with the **4-repeater rule** — the rule states **5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments**.
> 3. Stating that hubs "filter packets" — **WRONG**; hubs do not filter; they flood.
> 4. Forgetting to mention **Manchester Encoding** for 10Base-T.
> 5. Calculating bandwidth as `10 × N` (multiplied) instead of `10 / N` (divided).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an Ethernet hub with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of CSMA/CD in hub-based networks. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 24-port 10Base-T hub is used to connect 20 active workstations, each generating 5 frames per second with an average frame size of 800 bytes. Calculate:
   (i) The total offered load on the network in Mbps.
   (ii) The effective bandwidth per station.
   (iii) The probability of collision if any two stations transmit simultaneously with probability $p = 0.4$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Working Principle & CSMA/CD [7 Marks]**

**Working Principle of an Ethernet Hub (4 Marks):**

An Ethernet hub is a **multi-port repeater** operating at the **Physical Layer (Layer 1)** of the OSI model. The internal working involves the following stages:

1. **Signal Reception:** A bit stream encoded using **Manchester Encoding** arrives at one of the hub's ports.
2. **Bit-Level Decoding:** The MAU (Medium Attachment Unit) decodes the electrical pulses into raw bits.
3. **Signal Regeneration:** The repeater circuit **re-shapes and re-amplifies** the signal to original voltage levels.
4. **Broadcast Flooding:** The reconstructed bit stream is simultaneously transmitted out through **all other active ports**.
5. **Collision Domain:** All devices connected to the hub share a **single collision domain**, governed by CSMA/CD.

**Role of CSMA/CD in Hub Networks (3 Marks):**

**CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is the **Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol** used in half-duplex Ethernet networks built around hubs. Its three-step process is:

1. **Carrier Sense:** Before transmitting, a station "listens" to the medium to check if it is idle.
2. **Multiple Access:** All stations have **equal rights** to the medium — there is no central controller (the hub merely amplifies, it does not arbitrate).
3. **Collision Detection:** If two stations sense the medium as idle and transmit simultaneously, their signals collide. The hub detects the abnormal voltage pattern and sends a **Jam Signal** to all ports. Both stations then **back off** for a random time using the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm and retry.

> **Diagram Reference:** Refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Number of active stations, $N = 20$
- Frames per station per second, $f = 5$
- Frame size, $L = 800$ bytes $= 800 \times 8 = 6400$ bits
- Hub bandwidth, $B_{total} = 10$ Mbps
- Transmission probability, $p = 0.4$

**Step 1: Calculate Total Frames per Second**

$$
F_{total} = N \times f = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ frames/second}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Offered Load (Part i)**

$$
\text{Offered Load} = F_{total} \times L = 100 \times 6400 \text{ bits} = 640{,}000 \text{ bits/second}
$$

$$
\text{Offered Load} = 0.64 \text{ Mbps} = \mathbf{640 \text{ kbps}}
$$

*(4 marks for part (i) and (ii) combined)*

**Step 3: Calculate Effective Bandwidth per Station (Part ii)**

Since the hub shares its 10 Mbps medium across all 20 active stations:

$$
B_{eff} = \dfrac{B_{total}}{N} = \dfrac{10}{20} = 0.5 \text{ Mbps} = \mathbf{500 \text{ kbps}}
$$

Since the offered load (640 kbps) **exceeds** the effective bandwidth (500 kbps), the network is **saturated** and will experience **persistent collisions**.

**Step 4: Collision Probability (Part iii)**

The probability of any **single** station transmitting is $p = 0.4$. For a specific pair (Station A and Station B), the probability that **both** transmit simultaneously is:

$$
P_{AB} = p_A \times p_B = 0.4 \times 0.4 = 0.16
$$

For a more general collision scenario where **at least two** stations transmit out of $N = 20$:

The probability of no collision (only one or zero transmits) is:

$$
P_{no\_coll} = P(0) + P(1) = (1-p)^N + N \cdot p \cdot (1-p)^{N-1}
$$

$$
P_{no\_coll} = (0.6)^{20} + 20 \cdot (0.4) \cdot (0.6)^{19}
$$

Calculating the binomial term:

$$
P_{no\_coll} \approx 3.656 \times 10^{-5} + 4.875 \times 10^{-4} \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{collision} = 1 - P_{no\_coll} \approx 1 - 5.24 \times 10^{-4} \approx \mathbf{0.9995}
$$

**Result: ~99.95% chance of collision per time slot** — clearly demonstrating that hub-based networks cannot scale to many users. *(3 marks for part (iii))*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Hubs, Bridges, and Switches** with respect to OSI layer, intelligence, collision domain, and bandwidth utilization. Construct a comparison table. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **5-4-3 Rule** for 10Base-T Ethernet hub networks. Why is this rule important for collision detection? A 10Base-T network has 3 hubs connected in series. What is the maximum number of **populated segments** allowed? **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Comparison Table [7 Marks]**

| **Parameter** | **Hub** | **Bridge** | **Switch** |
|---------------|---------|------------|------------|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 2 (Data Link) |
| **Forwarding Method** | Broadcast (flood) | Based on MAC table | Based on MAC table (CAM) |
| **Collision Domains** | **1 (single shared)** | **2 (segments separated)** | **N (one per port)** |
| **Broadcast Domains** | 1 (single) | 1 (single) | 1 (single, unless VLAN) |
| **Address Awareness** | None | MAC addresses | MAC addresses |
| **Bandwidth per Port** | Shared | Shared within segment | **Dedicated** |
| **Number of Ports** | 4–24 typical | 2–4 typical | 8–48+ typical |
| **Frame Filtering** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Learning Capability** | No | Yes (MAC learning) | Yes (CAM table) |
| **Typical Use** | Legacy LANs | Segment interconnection | Modern LAN backbone |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | Higher |

**Conclusion:** Switches are superior in every performance metric; bridges are an intermediate solution; hubs are obsolete except in niche monitoring applications. *(7 marks: 5 for table, 2 for analysis)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 5-4-3 Rule Explanation [7 Marks]**

**Definition of the 5-4-3 Rule (5 Marks):**

The **5-4-3 Rule** is a fundamental design constraint for **half-duplex 10Base-T Ethernet networks** built using hubs/repeaters. It states:

- **5** — Maximum of **5 segments** in a single collision domain.
- **4** — Maximum of **4 repeaters (hubs)** in the signal path.
- **3** — Maximum of **3 segments** that can be **populated with end-user devices**. The other 2 segments must be **inter-repeater links** with no users.

**Importance for Collision Detection (2 Marks):**

The rule ensures that the **round-trip propagation delay** between the two farthest stations never exceeds the **Ethernet slot time of 51.2 μs**. This is critical because:

- If round-trip delay > slot time, the sender will **finish transmitting the minimum 64-byte frame before detecting a collision**, making CSMA/CD ineffective and causing **late collisions**, which are not recoverable.
- The 5-4-3 rule is derived from the requirement that:

$$
2 \cdot t_{prop, total} \leq 51.2 \;\mu s
$$

**Calculation of Maximum Populated Segments (Bonus):**

For a network with 3 hubs in series:
- Number of segments = 4
- Number of repeaters = 3
- Maximum **populated segments = 3** (consistent with the 5-4-3 rule, since the network has fewer than the maximum allowable components).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **hub** is a **Layer 1 (Physical Layer)** device that broadcasts incoming signals to all other ports.
- ✅ Hubs form a **single collision domain** and a **single broadcast domain**.
- ✅ There are **three types of hubs**: Passive, Active (Repeater), and Intelligent (Smart).
- ✅ Hubs do **not** understand MAC addresses, IP addresses, or frame structure.
- ✅ **Effective bandwidth per station** = $B_{total} / N$ (where N = number of active stations).
- ✅ **CSMA/CD** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) governs hub-based Ethernet.
- ✅ The **5-4-3 Rule** limits 10Base-T hub networks to 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 populated segments.
- ✅ **Slot time** for 10Base-T Ethernet = **51.2 μs** (512 bits at 10 Mbps).
- ✅ Hubs use **Manchester Encoding** (10Base-T) and **MLT-3** (100Base-TX).
- ✅ **Maximum cable length** per segment = **100 meters** (UTP Cat-5).
- ✅ A hub cannot create **VLANs**, **subnets**, or **separate collision domains**.
- ✅ **Switches replaced hubs** in the early 2000s because they offer dedicated bandwidth per port and parallel forwarding.
- ✅ Hubs are still used as **passive network taps** for packet sniffing/monitoring.
- ✅ Key KTU exam traps: confusing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA, misidentifying OSI layer, and writing `B × N` instead of `B / N` for bandwidth calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When answering hub-related questions, **always start by identifying the OSI layer (Layer 1)**, then state the **collision domain property (single/shared)**, then mention **CSMA/CD**, and finally present the **numerical calculations** in a clean tabular format. This guarantees full marks on most 7-mark and 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bridges
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bridges

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bridge** is a **Layer-2 (Data Link Layer)** networking device that operates just below the network layer in the OSI model. It interconnects two or more LAN segments (often of the same type, e.g., two Ethernet segments) and forwards frames between them based on **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses**. Unlike a repeater (which simply regenerates electrical signals) or a hub (which broadcasts to all ports), a bridge examines the destination MAC address of every incoming frame and makes an *intelligent* forwarding decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Textbook-aligned):** A bridge is a store-and-forward device that segments collision domains, builds a forwarding table by inspecting source MAC addresses, and uses either a **transparent spanning-tree algorithm** or **source routing** to make intelligent forwarding/filtering decisions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Sorting Office

Think of a bridge as a **post office in a small town**:

- The town is split into two streets (LAN segment A and LAN segment B).
- A postman (the bridge) stands at the junction.
- Every house has a unique address (MAC address).
- When a letter (frame) arrives, the postman checks the address. If the recipient lives on the same street, he **drops it locally (filter)**. If the recipient is on the other street, he **walks it across the bridge (forward)**. If the postman has never seen the address before, he **copies a letter to every house on both streets (flood)** — but the moment someone replies, he notes down the house→street mapping in his notebook (the forwarding table / learning process).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of a Bridge (Must-Memorize for KTU Board Exams):**
> 1. Operates at **OSI Layer 2** (Data Link Layer) — uses **MAC addresses**, not IP addresses.
> 2. Is a **store-and-forward** device: the full frame must be received and CRC-checked before forwarding.
> 3. Creates **separate collision domains** (improves throughput), but a **single broadcast domain**.
> 4. Builds and maintains a **forwarding (filtering) table** automatically through the **learning process**.
> 5. Standard bandwidth: **10 Mbps / 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** depending on port type.

### 1.3 Why Bridges? (The "Why" Question Board Examiners Love)

- **Segmentation of collision domains:** A single Ethernet LAN with 200 hosts has all of them contending for one shared medium. Inserting a bridge partitions this into two collision domains, doubling effective bandwidth.
- **Geographic extension:** Bridges can extend LANs over longer distances using point-to-point links between bridges.
- **Filtering:** Local traffic stays local, reducing overall congestion.
- **Transparent operation:** End hosts do not need to know that a bridge exists (in transparent bridging).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bridge Operational Cycle (The 4 Logical Steps)

Every transparent bridge performs the following steps on every incoming frame, in this exact order:

1. **Read & Buffer** — Receive the entire frame into local buffer (store-and-forward).
2. **Verify** — Run CRC checksum; discard corrupted frames silently.
3. **Learn** — Examine the **source MAC address**; if unknown, add `(Source MAC, Arrival Port)` to the **forwarding table** (this is the **learning process**).
4. **Forward / Filter / Flood** — Examine the **destination MAC address**:
   - If destination is on the **same port** as source → **Filter (drop)** the frame.
   - If destination is on a **different known port** → **Forward** selectively to that one port.
   - If destination is **unknown** → **Flood** the frame out of all ports *except* the arrival port.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The "Learning" step uses the **source address**; the "Forwarding" decision uses the **destination address**. This asymmetry is a common 3-mark question.

### 2.2 Types of Bridges (Direct from KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus)

| Type | Full Name | Working Principle | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TB** | Transparent Bridge | Operates transparently; uses Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D) | Ethernet |
| **SRB** | Source Routing Bridge | Sender specifies the full path in the frame header | Token Ring (IEEE 802.5) |
| **SRT** | Source Routing Transparent | Hybrid; auto-detects and supports both | Mixed Token Ring / Ethernet |
| **TBB** | Translational Bridge | Converts between different LAN types (e.g., Ethernet ↔ Token Ring) | Legacy mixed LANs |

### 2.3 The Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D) — Concept

When bridges are connected in **parallel paths** to provide redundancy, **loops** form. Loops cause:
- **Broadcast storms** (frames circulating forever).
- **MAC table instability** (frame seen arriving on multiple ports).

**Solution:** Run the **Spanning Tree Algorithm (STA)** to logically disable redundant links, producing a *loop-free tree* topology, while keeping physical links intact for failover.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STA uses a configuration message called the Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU)**, which contains the **Root Bridge ID**, **Root Path Cost**, **Sender Bridge ID**, and **Port ID**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Parameter Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $BID$ | Bridge ID = Priority $\,\vert\,$ MAC Address | Priority: 0–65535 (default 32768) |
| $C_{port}$ | Port Cost (older IEEE) | Inversely proportional to link speed |
| $C_{path}$ | Root Path Cost | Sum of port costs to root |
| $R$ | Root Bridge | Lowest BID in the network |
| $DP$ | Designated Port | Best port on a segment toward root |
| $BP$ | Blocked Port | Non-designated port (logically disabled) |
| $F$ | Forwarding Delay | **15 seconds** |
| $H_{max}$ | Max Age of BPDU | **20 seconds** |
| $T_{msg}$ | BPDU Hello Time | **2 seconds** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Port Costs (KTU Board Frequently Asked):**
> - 10 Mbps → Cost $= 100$
> - 100 Mbps → Cost $= 19$
> - 1 Gbps → Cost $= 4$
> - 10 Gbps → Cost $= 2$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Bridges are the conceptual ancestors of modern **network switches** (a multi-port bridge).
- Spanning Tree Protocol runs on virtually every enterprise Ethernet switch in the world.
- The "Root Path Cost" calculation in STP is identical in principle to the **OSPF cost metric** used in IP routing — a unifying concept across OSI layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Building a Bridge Forwarding Table

**Network Setup:**

- Bridge B1 connects LAN-A and LAN-B.
- Initial forwarding table is **empty**.

**Sequence of frame transmissions observed by B1:**

| Time | Source MAC | Source LAN | Dest MAC | Dest LAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_1$ | AA-AA | LAN-A | BB-BB | LAN-B |
| $t_2$ | CC-CC | LAN-B | AA-AA | LAN-A |
| $t_3$ | DD-DD | LAN-A | EE-EE | unknown |
| $t_4$ | BB-BB | LAN-B | AA-AA | LAN-A |

**Step-by-step trace:**

**At $t_1$ (frame from AA-AA to BB-BB):**
- *Learn:* Source = AA-AA on **Port-1** (LAN-A) → Add to table.
- *Decide on destination:* BB-BB is **unknown** → **Flood** out Port-2 (and every other port except Port-1).
- Table after $t_1$: `AA-AA → Port-1`.

**At $t_2$ (frame from CC-CC to AA-AA):**
- *Learn:* Source = CC-CC on **Port-2** (LAN-B) → Add to table.
- *Decide on destination:* AA-AA known on Port-1 → **Forward selectively to Port-1**.
- Table: `AA-AA → Port-1, CC-CC → Port-2`.

**At $t_3$ (frame from DD-DD to EE-EE):**
- *Learn:* Source = DD-DD on **Port-1** → Add to table.
- *Decide on destination:* EE-EE unknown → **Flood** out all other ports.
- Table: `AA-AA → P1, CC-CC → P2, DD-DD → P1`.

**At $t_4$ (frame from BB-BB to AA-AA):**
- *Learn:* Source = BB-BB on **Port-2** → Add to table.
- *Decide on destination:* AA-AA known on Port-1 → **Forward to Port-1** (no flooding).
- Final Table: `AA-AA → P1, CC-CC → P2, DD-DD → P1, BB-BB → P2`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual conclusion:** The bridge's intelligence grows with traffic. After a few minutes of observation in a real LAN, the table is fully populated and selective forwarding (no flooding) is the norm.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Spanning Tree Root Path Cost

**Problem:** Three bridges B1, B2, B3 form a triangle. All have default priority 32768.
- B1 MAC ends in `:01`
- B2 MAC ends in `:02`
- B3 MAC ends in `:03`
- All links are 100 Mbps (cost $= 19$ each).

**Find the Root Bridge, Root Ports, Designated Ports, and Blocked Ports.**

**Step 1 — Compute the Bridge ID (BID):**
$$BID_{B1} = 32768:01, \quad BID_{B2} = 32768:02, \quad BID_{B3} = 32768:03$$

**Step 2 — Elect the Root Bridge:**
The bridge with the **lowest BID** becomes the root. Since the priority is identical, the tie-breaker is the **lowest MAC address**.

$$\min(BID_{B1},\, BID_{B2},\, BID_{B3}) = BID_{B1} = 32768{:}01$$

$$\boxed{\text{Root Bridge} = B1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Root Path Cost for each non-root bridge:**

For B2: only one path to B1, via direct link.

$$C_{path}(B2) = 19$$

For B3: only one direct path to B1.

$$C_{path}(B3) = 19$$

**Step 4 — Select Root Port (RP) for each non-root bridge:**
The **Root Port** is the port with the *lowest cumulative cost* to reach the root. Both B2 and B3 have cost $19$, so their direct ports to B1 are root ports.

$$RP_{B2} = \text{Port connecting to B1}, \quad RP_{B3} = \text{Port connecting to B1}$$

**Step 5 — Elect a Designated Port (DP) for each segment:**
For each LAN segment, the bridge whose port has the **lowest cost to the root** becomes the designated bridge. (B1 is the root, so all of B1's ports are designated by default: cost $0$ vs. others' cost $19$.)

| Segment | B1's cost | B2's cost | B3's cost | Winner (DP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| B1–B2 link | 0 | 19 | — | B1 (DP) |
| B1–B3 link | 0 | — | 19 | B1 (DP) |
| B2–B3 link | 0 (via B1) | 19 (via B1) | 19 (direct) | **B2** (lower MAC `:02` vs `:03`) |

**Step 6 — Block the non-designigned port:**
The port on B3 facing B2 (the segment B2–B3) loses to B2. Hence B3's port to B2 is **BLOCKED**.

**Final Spanning Tree:** B1 ⇄ B2 (forwarding), B1 ⇄ B3 (forwarding), B2–B3 link is **blocked**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The result is a tree with 3 nodes and 2 edges — no loops, full connectivity, and B2–B3 link remains physically present for failover (will activate if B1–B2 or B1–B3 fails).

### 3.3 Convergence Time Bound

After a topology change, STP must wait for old BPDUs to age out:

$$T_{converge} \;\le\; H_{max} + 2 \times F = 20 + 2(15) = 50 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, IEEE 802.1w) reduces this to **< 6 seconds** using proposal/agreement handshakes. The question "Why is RSTP used?" expects this number.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture Flow of a Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[LAN Segment A] --> P1[Port 1 Interface]
    P1 --> RXB[Receive Buffer]
    RXB --> CRC{CRC Check}
    CRC -- Corrupt --> DROP[Drop Frame]
    CRC -- OK --> MAC[MAC Address Table Lookup]
    MAC --> LRN[Learning: Record Source MAC]
    LRN --> DEC{Forwarding Decision}
    DEC -- Same Segment --> FILT[Filter / Drop]
    DEC -- Known Dest Port --> SEL[Selective Forward]
    DEC -- Unknown --> FLOD[Flood All Other Ports]
    SEL --> TXB[Transmit Buffer]
    FILT --> X[End]
    FLOD --> TXB
    DROP --> X
    TXB --> P2[Port 2 Interface]
    TXB --> P3[Port 3 Interface]
    P2 --> A2[LAN Segment B]
    P3 --> A3[LAN Segment C]
```

### 4.2 Bridged Network with Parallel Paths (Loops) and STP Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Phys["PHYSICAL TOPOLOGY (contains loop)"]
        L1[LAN 1] --- bA[Bridge A]
        L1 --- bB[Bridge B]
        L2[LAN 2] --- bA
        L2 --- bB
        L3[LAN 3] --- bA
        L3 --- bB
    end
    subgraph Log["LOGICAL SPANNING TREE (loop-free)"]
        sL1[LAN 1] --- sBA[Bridge A ROOT]
        sL2[LAN 2] --- sBA
        sL3[LAN 3] --- sBA
        sBB[Bridge B] -. blocked link .-> sBA
    end
```

### 4.3 Transparent Bridge Decision Flowchart (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReceiveFrame
    ReceiveFrame --> CRCVerify
    CRCVerify --> DropFrame: CRC Fail
    CRCVerify --> LearnSource: CRC OK
    LearnSource --> LookupDestination
    LookupDestination --> Filter: Dest on same port
    LookupDestination --> Forward: Dest on known different port
    LookupDestination --> Flood: Dest unknown
    Forward --> [*]
    Flood --> [*]
    Filter --> [*]
    DropFrame --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Bridge–Switch Evolutionary Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Repeater - Layer 1] --> H[Hub - Layer 1 Multiport]
    H --> B[Bridge - Layer 2 Two Ports]
    B --> S[Switch - Layer 2 Multiport Bridge]
    S --> MS[Managed Switch with STP RSTP VLAN]
    MS --> RTR[Router - Layer 3]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual take-away for KTU:** A modern Ethernet switch is essentially a **multi-port bridge** with hardware parallelism — every port can simultaneously store-and-forward frames. STP runs on every managed switch.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a bridge. List any two functions performed by a bridge at the Data Link Layer.*

**Model Answer:**
A bridge is a Layer-2 (Data Link Layer) store-and-forward device that interconnects two or more LAN segments and selectively forwards frames between them based on MAC addresses.
*Two functions:*
1. **Learning** — records source MAC addresses and the port of arrival in a forwarding table.
2. **Forwarding / Filtering** — forwards frames selectively to the port where the destination MAC is known, or filters (drops) frames whose destination is on the same segment as the source.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between a repeater, a bridge, and a router in terms of the OSI layer they operate at and the addressing they use.*

**Model Answer:**

| Device | OSI Layer | Address Used | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Repeater | Layer 1 (Physical) | None (raw bits/signals) | Regenerates signals |
| Bridge | Layer 2 (Data Link) | **MAC (48-bit) address** | Segments collision domains, selective forwarding |
| Router | Layer 3 (Network) | **IP address** | Inter-networking, routing between different networks |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of a **transparent bridge**. Describe the *learning*, *flooding*, *filtering*, and *forwarding* operations with an example.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D)** in detail. Show, with a small example, how loops are eliminated and a root bridge is elected. Mention the default timer values.

**OR**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 marks]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **Transparent Bridges** and **Source Routing Bridges**. Mention the network type each is suitable for and the frame-format implications.

**(b) [7 marks]** A network has three bridges B1, B2, B3 connected in a triangle. Default priority $32768$, MAC addresses ending in `:0A`, `:05`, `:0F` respectively. All links are 100 Mbps. Compute the **Root Bridge**, **Root Ports**, and **Designated Ports**. Identify the **blocked port**.

---

### Model Solution — Q3(a) and Q3(b)

**Q3(a) Model Solution:**

A transparent bridge operates invisibly to the end hosts — they need not be reconfigured. It has $N$ ports, each connected to a separate LAN segment.

*Working of a transparent bridge (4-step algorithm):*
1. **Learning** — The bridge builds a table mapping `<MAC address, Port number, Age>`. When a frame arrives on a port, the source MAC and that port are recorded.
2. **Flooding** — If the destination MAC is not in the table, the frame is broadcast on all ports except the arrival port.
3. **Forwarding** — If the destination MAC is in the table on a different port, the frame is forwarded only to that port.
4. **Filtering** — If the destination MAC is in the table on the *same* port as the arrival port, the frame is dropped (filtered).

*Example:* A bridge B connects LAN1 and LAN2. Frame from host H1 (MAC M1) on LAN1 to host H2 (MAC M2) on LAN2.
- Bridge receives the frame, sees source M1 on Port-1, learns `M1 → P1`.
- Destination M2 is unknown → bridge **floods** on Port-2.
- Reply from H2 (M2) on Port-2 → bridge learns `M2 → P2`, table is now complete.
- Subsequent frame M1→M2 is now **selectively forwarded** to Port-2, not flooded.

**Q3(b) Model Solution:**

The Spanning Tree Protocol (STP, IEEE 802.1D) prevents loops in bridged networks with redundant links. Algorithm (per bridge):
- Each bridge is assigned a **Bridge ID (BID)** = 2-byte priority $+$ 6-byte MAC.
- Bridges exchange **BPDUs** (Bridge Protocol Data Units) containing the **Root ID** and the **Root Path Cost**.
- The bridge with the **lowest BID** is elected **Root Bridge** (cost to itself $= 0$).
- For every other bridge, the port with the **lowest cost path to the root** becomes the **Root Port (RP)**.
- For every LAN segment, the bridge with the **lowest cost to the root** (ties broken by lowest BID) provides the **Designated Port (DP)**.
- All ports that are neither RP nor DP are put into the **Blocking** state → loops broken.

*Default timers:* Hello $= 2$ s, Max Age $= 20$ s, Forward Delay $= 15$ s.
*Maximum convergence:* $\leq H_{max} + 2F = 50$ seconds.

---

### Model Solution — Q4(b) Numerical

**Given:** B1 MAC `:0A`, B2 MAC `:05`, B3 MAC `:0F`. All priority $= 32768$, all links $= 100$ Mbps $\Rightarrow$ cost $= 19$.

**Step 1 — BIDs:**
$$BID_{B1} = 32768{:}0A, \quad BID_{B2} = 32768{:}05, \quad BID_{B3} = 32768{:}0F$$

**Step 2 — Root election (lowest BID):**
$$\min = 32768{:}05 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Root} = B2$$

**Step 3 — Root Path Cost (other bridges):**
- B1: cost via direct B2–B1 link $= 19$.
- B3: cost via direct B2–B3 link $= 19$.

**Step 4 — Root Port assignment:**
- $RP_{B1}$ = port to B2.
- $RP_{B3}$ = port to B2.

**Step 5 — Designated Port for each segment:**
| Segment | B2 cost | B1 cost | B3 cost | DP Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| B2–B1 | 0 | 19 | — | B2 |
| B2–B3 | 0 | — | 19 | B2 |
| B1–B3 | 19 (via B2) | 0 (self) | 19 (via B2) | **B1** (tie → lower MAC `:0A` < `:0F`) |

**Step 6 — Blocked port:**
The port on **B3** facing **B1** loses → B3's port to B1 is **BLOCKED**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Root Path Cost with Port Cost** — Root Path Cost is the *cumulative sum* of port costs along the path to the root, not a single port cost.
> 2. **Forgetting the MAC tie-breaker** — When two bridges have the *same* root path cost, the *lower Bridge ID (priority+MAC)* wins. Many students skip this.
> 3. **Calling Spanning Tree "loop avoidance" instead of "loop prevention"** — STP logically disables redundant links, so use *prevention* terminology.
> 4. **Mixing up the use of source vs destination MAC** — The bridge *learns* from the source; it *acts* on the destination. Reversing these loses 2–3 marks instantly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Bridge = Layer-2 device using MAC addresses**, store-and-forward, separates collision domains.
- **4 operations:** Learning, Flooding, Forwarding, Filtering.
- **Two main flavors:** Transparent Bridge (Ethernet, IEEE 802.1D) and Source Routing Bridge (Token Ring, IEEE 802.5).
- **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP):** Elects a Root Bridge using the lowest BID; every other bridge selects a Root Port; one Designated Port per segment; all other ports block → loop-free topology.
- **Default STP timers:** Hello $= 2$ s, Max Age $= 20$ s, Forward Delay $= 15$ s; max convergence $\leq 50$ s.
- **Modern switches = multi-port bridges** with hardware parallelism.
- **RSTP (IEEE 802.1w)** converges in under $6$ seconds using proposal/agreement handshakes.
- **Quick recall formula for path cost:** Older IEEE (10 Mbps $= 100$, 100 Mbps $= 19$, 1 Gbps $= 4$) — memorize this table.
- **Tie-breakers in STP:** First by root path cost, then by sender BID, then by sender Port ID.
- **Source MAC = Learning input; Destination MAC = Forwarding decision input.** A favorite KTU 3-mark conceptual question.
- **Translational bridges** convert frames between different LAN MAC formats (e.g., Ethernet $\leftrightarrow$ Token Ring) — a high-yield short-answer topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: And Their Roles in a Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Network Components and Their Roles in a Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data and Computer Communication**, a **computer network** is an interconnected collection of autonomous computing devices that exchange data and share resources using a common set of communication rules (protocols). The **network components** are the physical (hardware) and logical (software) elements that work together to ensure successful data transmission, routing, error control, and resource sharing across the network.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT751) – Module 2** framing, network components are broadly classified into two categories:

| Category | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Components** | Physical devices that transmit, receive, amplify, or interpret signals. | NIC, Hub, Switch, Bridge, Router, Gateway, Modem, Repeater, Firewall, Access Point |
| **Software / Logical Components** | Programs and protocols that govern communication. | TCP/IP stack, Network Operating System (NOS), Device Drivers, Firewall rules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes the **roles, working, and layer-wise placement** (OSI / TCP-IP) of these components. A student must know **at which OSI layer** each device operates — this is a **high-frequency exam question**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Postal System"

Imagine a network as the **postal system of a large city**:

- **NIC (Network Interface Card)** → The **personal mailbox** installed in every house. Without it, no letter can leave or enter.
- **Hub** → A **local neighborhood sorting table** that just shouts the letter out to everyone; whoever's address matches keeps it.
- **Switch** → A **smart local post office** that knows exactly which house the letter belongs to and delivers it directly.
- **Bridge** → A **traffic policeman** controlling flow between two busy local roads.
- **Router** → The **highway interchange** that decides whether a letter goes to the next city, state, or country.
- **Gateway** → The **customs checkpoint / translator** between two postal systems speaking different languages (protocols).
- **Modem** → The **translator between letter (digital) and telegram (analog signals)** on a telephone line.
- **Repeater** → A **photocopier and signal booster** that refreshes a fading signal halfway across a long wire.
- **Firewall** → The **security guard** at the gate, inspecting every packet before letting it in or out.
- **Access Point** → A **Wi-Fi broadcasting tower** that allows wireless devices to connect to the wired network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** Network components are the building blocks — both physical devices and logical software — that enable data to travel reliably, securely, and efficiently from a source to a destination, whether across a room or across the globe.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics to Remember

- **OSI Model** has **7 layers** (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
- **TCP/IP Model** has **4 layers** (Link, Internet, Transport, Application).
- **Bandwidth** is measured in **bits per second (bps)**, commonly **Mbps** or **Gbps**.
- **Latency** is measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- The **MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit)** for Ethernet is **1500 bytes**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Breakdown of Network Components

### 2.1.1 Physical Layer (OSI Layer 1) Components
These devices deal with **raw bits** — electrical, optical, or radio signals.

- **Repeater**
  - **Role:** Regenerates and amplifies a weak/degraded signal so it can travel longer distances.
  - **Why needed:** Signals attenuate (lose strength) over distance due to resistance, noise, and interference.
  - **Layer:** Physical (Layer 1).
  - **Limitation:** Cannot filter traffic, cannot understand frames, simply copies bits.

- **Hub**
  - **Role:** A multi-port repeater. Takes an incoming bit stream and broadcasts it out of **every other port**.
  - **Why needed:** Connects multiple devices in a star topology.
  - **Collision Domain:** All ports share **one** collision domain (half-duplex).
  - **Layer:** Physical (Layer 1).

- **Modem (Modulator-Demodulator)**
  - **Role:** Converts **digital ↔ analog** signals for transmission over telephone lines, cable, or fiber.
  - **Why needed:** The transmission medium (e.g., copper telephone line) natively supports analog signals.
  - **Types:** DSL Modem, Cable Modem, Fiber Modem (ONT), Dial-up Modem.

- **Network Interface Card (NIC)**
  - **Role:** The **physical interface** that connects a device to the network. Has a unique **48-bit MAC address**.
  - **Why needed:** Converts parallel data inside the computer to serial bits for the cable, and vice-versa.
  - **Layer:** Physical + Data Link (Layer 1 + Layer 2).

### 2.1.2 Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2) Components
These devices understand **frames** (data packets with MAC addresses) and use them to make forwarding decisions.

- **Bridge**
  - **Role:** Connects two LAN segments and filters traffic using **MAC addresses**.
  - **Why needed:** Reduces collision domain size; segments a busy network.
  - **Modern form:** Has largely been replaced by multi-port switches.

- **Switch (Layer 2 Switch)**
  - **Role:** A multi-port bridge with an **internal MAC address table** that delivers frames **only** to the correct destination port (unicast).
  - **Why needed:** Provides **dedicated bandwidth** per port, full-duplex communication, and separate collision domains.
  - **MAC Learning:** Uses the **Source MAC address** of incoming frames to learn which device is on which port.

- **Network Interface Card (NIC) — Data Link Functions**
  - Performs **framing**, **MAC addressing**, and **error detection (CRC)**.
  - Uses the **CSMA/CD** protocol in half-duplex Ethernet.

### 2.1.3 Network Layer (OSI Layer 3) Components
These devices understand **IP packets** and route them across different networks.

- **Router**
  - **Role:** Forwards packets between **different IP networks** using a **routing table**.
  - **Why needed:** LANs cannot scale to the global internet without intelligent path selection.
  - **Key functions:**
    - Builds and maintains a **routing table** (statically or via protocols like OSPF, BGP, RIP).
    - Performs **packet forwarding** based on destination IP.
    - Acts as a **default gateway** for end devices.
  - **Layer:** Network (Layer 3) — primary device. (Modern routers also do Layer 2 switching.)

### 2.1.4 Higher-Layer Components (OSI Layers 4–7)

- **Gateway**
  - **Role:** Translates between **dissimilar network protocols** (e.g., IPv4 ↔ IPv6, Ethernet ↔ ATM).
  - **Why needed:** When two networks use different architectures, a gateway performs **protocol conversion**.
  - **Layer:** Operates at **all 7 layers** depending on the translation needed (often Application layer).

- **Firewall**
  - **Role:** Filters traffic based on **predefined security rules** (IP, port, protocol, content).
  - **Why needed:** Protects the network from unauthorized access, malware, and DoS attacks.
  - **Types:** Packet-filtering, Stateful Inspection, Application-Layer (Proxy) firewalls.
  - **Layer:** Typically **Layer 3 / Layer 4**, but advanced ones inspect up to **Layer 7**.

- **Access Point (AP)**
  - **Role:** Provides **wireless (Wi-Fi) connectivity** to a wired network.
  - **Why needed:** Wi-Fi devices (laptops, phones) cannot plug into Ethernet directly.
  - **Layer:** Layer 2 (bridges wireless frames to wired Ethernet).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula & Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **one-stop revision sheet** for all KTU numerical or descriptive questions on network components.

| Device | OSI Layer | Function | Key Term | Collision Domain | Broadcast Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeater** | 1 (Physical) | Amplifies/regenerates signal | Bit-level copy | Same | Same |
| **Hub** | 1 (Physical) | Broadcasts bits to all ports | Multi-port repeater | Same (1 shared) | Same |
| **NIC** | 1 & 2 | Connects device to medium | MAC address (48-bit) | Per NIC | Per NIC |
| **Bridge** | 2 (Data Link) | Filters by MAC, links 2 LANs | MAC learning | Splits (2) | Same |
| **Switch (L2)** | 2 (Data Link) | Forwards frame to exact port | MAC table | Splits (per port) | Same (1 default) |
| **Router** | 3 (Network) | Routes packets between IP nets | Routing table | Splits | Splits |
| **Gateway** | All layers | Protocol conversion | Translator | Splits | Splits |
| **Modem** | 1 (Physical) | Digital ↔ Analog conversion | Modulation | Same | Same |
| **Firewall** | 3 / 4 / 7 | Filters traffic by rules | ACL / Stateful | Splits | Splits |
| **Access Point** | 2 (Data Link) | Wireless bridge to wired LAN | SSID | Splits | Same (default) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Students often mark a **Hub** as a "data link layer device" because it has ports — **it is NOT**. A hub has no MAC awareness, no frame structure, no learning. It is purely **Layer 1**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Networks** use a **hierarchical design**:
  - **Access Layer** → Switches (connect end users).
  - **Distribution Layer** → Routers & multilayer switches (policy, routing).
  - **Core Layer** → High-speed routers & switches (backbone).
- **Data Centers** use **top-of-rack (ToR) switches** and **core routers** with **10/40/100 Gbps** links.
- **Telecom ISPs** use **modems/OLTs** at customer premises and **routers/gateways** at the core.
- **IoT & Home Networks** use **Wi-Fi Access Points** and **consumer routers** with built-in firewalls.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, MAC Learning Example & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section focuses on the **most-asked KTU numerical/analytical concept** under network components: **How a Switch builds its MAC address table using MAC Learning.** This is a guaranteed 7-mark question in Part B.

## 3.1 The Switch MAC Learning Process — Exhaustive Derivation

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A 4-port Ethernet switch is connected to four PCs:

- **PC-A** is on port 1 → MAC = `AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`
- **PC-B** is on port 2 → MAC = `BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`
- **PC-C** is on port 3 → MAC = `CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC`
- **PC-D** is on port 4 → MAC = `DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD`

The switch's MAC table is **initially empty**. Analyze the following transmissions in order and show the MAC table after each step.

1. PC-A sends a frame to PC-B.
2. PC-B replies to PC-A.
3. PC-C sends a broadcast ARP request.
4. PC-D sends a unicast frame to PC-C.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Initial State

The MAC table of the switch is empty. We represent it formally as a mapping function:

$$T = \{\}$$

where $T$ is the set of $(MAC, Port)$ tuples stored in the switch's CAM (Content Addressable Memory).

#### Step 2: Transmission 1 — PC-A → PC-B

The frame arrives at the switch on **Port 1** with the following header fields:

- Source MAC = $M_s = \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}$
- Destination MAC = $M_d = \text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}$

**Action 1 — Learning:** The switch reads the Source MAC. It does a lookup in $T$. Since $T = \{\}$, no entry exists, so it creates a new entry:

$$T \leftarrow T \cup \{(M_s,\ \text{Port 1})\}$$

$$T = \{(\text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA},\ \text{Port 1})\}$$

**Action 2 — Forwarding (Unknown Unicast Flood):** The switch looks up the Destination MAC in $T$. It is **not present**, so the switch **floods** the frame out of every port **except** the source port (1). The frame goes out of ports 2, 3, 4.

**Resulting MAC Table after Step 2:**

| MAC Address | Port | Type |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | 1 | Dynamic |

#### Step 3: Transmission 2 — PC-B → PC-A

The frame arrives on **Port 2** with:

- Source MAC = $\text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}$
- Destination MAC = $\text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}$

**Action 1 — Learning:** Lookup $\text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB}$ in $T$ — not found — create entry:

$$T \leftarrow T \cup \{(\text{BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB},\ \text{Port 2})\}$$

**Action 2 — Forwarding (Known Unicast):** Lookup $\text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA}$ in $T$ — **found at Port 1**. The switch forwards the frame **only** to Port 1 (no flooding).

**Resulting MAC Table after Step 3:**

| MAC Address | Port | Type |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | 1 | Dynamic |
| BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | 2 | Dynamic |

#### Step 4: Transmission 3 — PC-C sends an ARP Broadcast

The frame arrives on **Port 3**:

- Source MAC = $\text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC}$
- Destination MAC = $\text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ (Broadcast)

**Action 1 — Learning:** Create new entry for source MAC:

$$T \leftarrow T \cup \{(\text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC},\ \text{Port 3})\}$$

**Action 2 — Forwarding (Broadcast):** Destination is a broadcast — the switch **must** flood the frame out of all ports except the source. Frame goes out of ports 1, 2, 4.

**Resulting MAC Table after Step 4:**

| MAC Address | Port | Type |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | 1 | Dynamic |
| BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | 2 | Dynamic |
| CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC | 3 | Dynamic |

#### Step 5: Transmission 4 — PC-D → PC-C

The frame arrives on **Port 4**:

- Source MAC = $\text{DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD}$
- Destination MAC = $\text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC}$

**Action 1 — Learning:** New entry:

$$T \leftarrow T \cup \{(\text{DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD},\ \text{Port 4})\}$$

**Action 2 — Forwarding (Known Unicast):** Destination $\text{CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC}$ is at **Port 3** — forward selectively to Port 3 only.

**Final MAC Table:**

| MAC Address | Port | Type |
|---|---|---|
| AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA | 1 | Dynamic |
| BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB | 2 | Dynamic |
| CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:CC | 3 | Dynamic |
| DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:DD | 4 | Dynamic |

### 3.1.3 The Three Switch Behaviors — Summary Table

| Destination MAC Type | Condition | Switch Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Known Unicast** | MAC found in $T$ | Forward frame to the **specific port** |
| **Unknown Unicast** | MAC not found in $T$ | **Flood** out all ports except source |
| **Broadcast** | $M_d = \text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ | **Flood** out all ports except source |

### 3.1.4 Aging Timer (Additional KTU Point)

Dynamic entries in $T$ are removed if not refreshed within the **aging time** (typically **300 seconds / 5 minutes**). This prevents stale entries when a device moves to another port.

$$\text{If } (t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{learned}}) > T_{\text{aging}} \Rightarrow \text{Remove entry}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Device?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a high-yield KTU question — *"Given a scenario, which network device would you deploy and why?"*

| Network Problem | Correct Device | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Signal weakens over 200m of cable | **Repeater** | Boosts attenuated signal at Layer 1. |
| 8 PCs in a small office, only some chat heavily | **Switch** | Provides dedicated bandwidth, separate collision domains per port. |
| Connect two buildings' LANs that use different protocols | **Gateway** | Performs protocol translation. |
| Provide Wi-Fi to a wired office LAN | **Access Point** | Bridges wireless 802.11 frames to wired Ethernet. |
| Connect office LAN to ISP's global network | **Router** | Performs IP routing between autonomous networks. |
| Send digital data over analog phone line | **Modem** | Modulates digital bits into analog carrier signal. |
| Filter malicious traffic from the internet | **Firewall** | Inspects packets against security rules. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Network Component Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    internet((Internet / ISP)) ---> fwA[Firewall / Edge Router]
    fwA ---> coreR[Core Router - Layer 3]
    coreR ---> distR1[Distribution Router 1]
    coreR ---> distR2[Distribution Router 2]
    distR1 ---> swA1[Access Switch A - Layer 2]
    distR1 ---> swA2[Access Switch B - Layer 2]
    distR2 ---> swB1[Access Switch C - Layer 2]
    swA1 ---> pcA[PC A via NIC]
    swA1 ---> pcB[PC B via NIC]
    swA2 ---> ap1[Wireless Access Point]
    ap1 -.Wi-Fi.-> laptop1[Laptop Wireless]
    ap1 -.Wi-Fi.-> phone1[Mobile Phone]
    swA1 ---> hub1[Hub - Legacy Device]
    hub1 ---> pcC[PC C]
    hub1 ---> pcD[PC D]
    swA1 ---> br1[Bridge to Old LAN Segment]
    br1 ---> pcE[PC E]
    coreR ---> gw1[Gateway Protocol Translator]
    gw1 ---> legacyNet[Legacy ATM / Token Ring Network]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layer-wise Device Placement (OSI)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L7[OSI Layer 7 - Application] --> L6[OSI Layer 6 - Presentation]
    L6 --> L5[OSI Layer 5 - Session]
    L5 --> L4[OSI Layer 4 - Transport]
    L4 --> L3[OSI Layer 3 - Network]
    L3 --> L2[OSI Layer 2 - Data Link]
    L2 --> L1[OSI Layer 1 - Physical]
    L1 --> medium((Transmission Medium))

    L1 -.operates.-> devA[Repeater]
    L1 -.operates.-> devB[Hub]
    L1 -.operates.-> devC[Modem]
    L1 -.operates.-> devD[NIC]
    L2 -.operates.-> devE[Bridge]
    L2 -.operates.-> devF[Layer 2 Switch]
    L2 -.operates.-> devG[Access Point]
    L2 -.operates.-> devH[NIC MAC Functions]
    L3 -.operates.-> devI[Router]
    L3 -.operates.-> devJ[Firewall Basic]
    L7 -.operates.-> devK[Application Firewall Proxy]
    L4 -.operates.-> devL[Stateful Firewall]
    devM[Gateway] -.spans.-> L1
    devM -.spans.-> L7
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Frame Path Through a Switch (MAC Learning)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Frame arrives at Switch Port X] --> parse[Parse Source MAC and Destination MAC]
    parse --> learn{Lookup Source MAC in MAC Table}
    learn -- Not Found --> addEntry[Add Source MAC mapped to Port X with aging timer]
    learn -- Found --> refreshEntry[Refresh aging timer for that entry]
    addEntry --> destCheck
    refreshEntry --> destCheck{Lookup Destination MAC}
    destCheck -- Known Unicast Found --> fwd[Forward frame to specific destination port only]
    destCheck -- Unknown Unicast --> floodU[Flood frame out all ports except source port]
    destCheck -- Broadcast MAC FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF --> floodB[Flood frame out all ports except source port]
    fwd --> done[End]
    floodU --> done
    floodB --> done
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Hub and a Switch. Mention the OSI layer at which each operates.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Hub** is a **Layer 1 (Physical)** device that simply broadcasts incoming bits out of **every other port** with no intelligence. All devices connected to a hub share a **single collision domain** and operate in **half-duplex** mode, leading to many collisions on busy networks.

A **Switch** is a **Layer 2 (Data Link)** device that uses a **MAC address table** to forward frames **only to the specific destination port** (selective forwarding). Each port has its **own collision domain**, supports **full-duplex** communication, and provides **dedicated bandwidth** per port. A switch is essentially a **multi-port bridge with MAC learning**.

| Feature | Hub | Switch |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | 1 | 2 |
| Forwarding Method | Broadcast (Flood) | Selective (Unicast) |
| Collision Domain | One shared | One per port |
| Intelligence | None (dumb) | MAC learning |
| Bandwidth | Shared | Dedicated per port |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a Gateway? Why is it different from a Router?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Gateway** is a network device that **translates between different network protocols** or architectures, allowing two dissimilar networks to communicate. For example, an IPv4-to-IPv6 gateway, or an Ethernet-to-ATM gateway, or an email gateway converting between SMTP and X.400.

A **Router**, in contrast, routes packets between networks that use the **same protocol** (typically IP) by examining the destination IP address and consulting a routing table.

| Aspect | Router | Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Function | Path selection / packet forwarding | Protocol translation |
| Networks Connected | Same protocol (IP) | Different protocols / architectures |
| OSI Layer | Layer 3 | All 7 layers (depending on translation) |
| Translation | Not its main job | Core job |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently describe a gateway as "a fancy router." A gateway is fundamentally about **protocol conversion** — that single phrase carries 1 full mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are from the same module and carry equal marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the functions of the following network devices with their OSI layer placement: (i) Repeater (ii) Bridge (iii) Router (iv) Firewall. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Repeater — OSI Layer 1 (Physical):**
A repeater receives a weakened analog or digital signal, regenerates it to its original strength, and retransmits it. It is used to extend the physical range of a network beyond the natural attenuation limit of the cable (e.g., beyond 100 m for Ethernet). It does **not interpret bits** and is protocol-agnostic. Limitation: it amplifies **noise along with the signal** and cannot segment collision domains.

**(ii) Bridge — OSI Layer 2 (Data Link):**
A bridge connects **two LAN segments** and filters traffic using **MAC addresses**. It reads the destination MAC address of each frame and forwards it only to the segment where the destination resides, reducing unnecessary traffic. A bridge maintains a small **MAC address table** built through learning (reading the source MAC of incoming frames). It splits one collision domain into two. Modern bridges have been superseded by multi-port switches.

**(iii) Router — OSI Layer 3 (Network):**
A router connects **two or more different IP networks** and forwards packets between them based on destination IP addresses. It maintains a **routing table** populated statically or dynamically using protocols such as **RIP, OSPF, BGP**. Routers split both **collision domains and broadcast domains**, providing logical isolation. They also perform **packet fragmentation, TTL decrement, and NAT**. A router is the **backbone device of the Internet**.

**(iv) Firewall — OSI Layers 3, 4, and 7:**
A firewall is a security device that filters traffic between networks based on **predetermined rules** (ACLs, port numbers, IP addresses, or application content). A **packet-filtering firewall** works at Layer 3/4 and inspects headers. A **stateful inspection firewall** tracks the state of active connections. An **application-layer (proxy) firewall** inspects payload content (Layer 7). Firewalls can be hardware appliances or software (e.g., `iptables` on Linux, Windows Defender Firewall).

**Summary Table:**

| Device | OSI Layer | Key Function |
|---|---|---|
| Repeater | 1 | Signal regeneration |
| Bridge | 2 | MAC-based filtering between LANs |
| Router | 3 | IP-based routing between networks |
| Firewall | 3/4/7 | Traffic filtering & security |

---

**b) With a neat diagram, explain how a Layer 2 switch learns MAC addresses and builds its MAC table. Use a suitable example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Layer 2 switch maintains a **MAC address table** (also called a **CAM table** — Content Addressable Memory) that maps each known MAC address to the port on which it was learned. The switch uses the following three-step process for every incoming frame:

**Step 1 — Read Source MAC and Learn:**
