<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stop-and-Wait Sequence Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant M as Physical Medium
    participant R as Receiver

    Note over S,R: Initial State: Sender has Data[0]
    S->>M: Transmit Frame 0 (Seq=0)
    Note right of S: Timer T started
    M->>R: Frame 0 propagates
    R->>R: Check CRC / Receive
    R->>M: Transmit ACK 0
    M->>S: ACK 0 propagates
    Note left of S: Timer T cancelled
    S->>M: Transmit Frame 1 (Seq=1)
    M->>R: Frame 1 propagates
    R->>R: Check CRC / Receive
    R->>M: Transmit ACK 1
    M->>S: ACK 1 propagates
    Note over S,R: Cycle complete

    Note over S,R: --- TIMEOUT / LOSS SCENARIO ---
    S->>M: Transmit Frame 0
    Note right of S: ACK lost or Frame lost
    M-->>R: Frame lost in transit
    Note left of S: Timer T expires
    S->>M: RETRANSMIT Frame 0
    M->>R: Frame 0 arrives
    R->>R: Discard if duplicate Seq seen
    R->>M: Send ACK 0
    M->>S: ACK 0 arrives
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph senderNode["SENDER STATION"]
        A[Application Layer Payload]
        B[Frame Encapsulation Unit]
        C[Sequence Number Generator SN0 or SN1]
        D[Transmit Buffer with Single Slot]
        E[Retransmission Timer Watchdog]
        F[ACK Receiver and Validator]
    end

    subgraph mediumNode["PHYSICAL TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
        G[Forward Channel for Data Frames]
        H[Reverse Channel for ACK Frames]
    end

    subgraph receiverNode["RECEIVER STATION"]
        I[Frame Reception Unit]
        J[CRC Checksum Verifier]
        K[Sequence Number Comparator]
        L[ACK Generation Unit]
        M[Deliver Payload Upward]
    end

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> G
    G --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
    K --> M
    K --> L
    L --> H
    H --> F
    F --> D
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram — Channel Activity

```mermaid
gantt
    title Stop-and-Wait Channel Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Sender
    Send Frame 0      :s1, 0, 12
    Wait for ACK      :s2, 12, 252
    Send Frame 1      :s3, 252, 264
    Wait for ACK      :s4, 264, 504

    section Receiver
    Receive Frame 0   :r1, 120, 132
    Send ACK 0        :r2, 132, 134
    Receive Frame 1   :r3, 372, 384
    Send ACK 1        :r4, 384, 386

    section Idle
    Channel Idle      :i1, 134, 252
    Channel Idle      :i2, 386, 504
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Stop-and-Wait flow control. Mention two of its major limitations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Stop-and-Wait is a Data Link Layer flow control protocol in which the sender transmits a single data frame and then suspends further transmission until it receives a positive acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiver. Only after the ACK is received does the sender transmit the next frame.

**Limitations (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Poor channel utilization** for long propagation delays (e.g., satellite links) because the channel sits idle for $2 T_p$ seconds per cycle.
2. **Throughput bottleneck** — the sender can transmit at most one frame per round-trip time, capping throughput to $L / (T_t + 2 T_p)$ frames per second.
3. **No pipelining** — it cannot overlap multiple in-flight frames.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**Why are sequence numbers necessary in Stop-and-Wait ARQ? What is the minimum sequence number space?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Necessity (2 marks):**
Sequence numbers are required to **disambiguate a fresh frame from a delayed duplicate**. When a frame (or its ACK) is lost, the sender retransmits after timeout. If the original frame was merely *delayed* (not lost), two identical copies eventually arrive at the receiver. Without a sequence bit, the receiver would deliver both to the upper layer, causing **data duplication and corruption**.

**Minimum sequence number space (1 mark):**
**1 bit** is sufficient. Alternating between 0 and 1 is enough to identify the current frame uniquely versus the previous one.

---

### **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of the Stop-and-Wait protocol for a noiseless channel. Discuss how sequence numbers resolve the ambiguity of duplicate frames in a noisy channel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A channel of bandwidth **1 Mbps** is used to transmit frames of size **1000 bits**. The propagation delay is **20 ms**. Compute:
  (i) Transmission time $T_t$
  (ii) Total cycle time
  (iii) Channel utilization $U$
  (iv) The throughput in frames per second and bits per second. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation in Noiseless Channel (7 marks):**

In a **noiseless channel**, no frame is ever lost or corrupted. The operational cycle is:
1. Sender transmits Frame 0 with sequence number 0.
2. Receiver receives the frame, delivers it to the upper layer, and immediately sends ACK.
3. Sender receives ACK 0, flips its sequence bit, and transmits Frame 1.
4. Cycle repeats alternating between Seq 0 and Seq 1.

> **Noiseless Timing (Key Insight):**
> No timer is needed because no retransmission will ever occur. The protocol reduces to a pure half-duplex handshake.

**Noisy Channel Ambiguity Resolution:**
In a **noisy channel**, three failure modes can occur:
- **Frame lost:** Sender's timer expires → retransmit.
- **ACK lost:** Sender's timer expires → retransmit; receiver discards the duplicate using sequence number.
- **Frame delayed (not lost):** Receiver discards the late arrival if its sequence number equals the *last accepted* one.

The **alternating 0-1 sequence bit** in the frame header lets the receiver detect that an arriving frame is identical to the *previous* one and silently drop it. This is the **minimum sufficient** state for reliability.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6$ bits/s
- $L = 1000$ bits
- $T_p = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.02$ s

**(i) Transmission time $T_t$:**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Total cycle time:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2 T_p = 1 + 2(20) = 41 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Channel utilization $U$:**

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p} = \frac{1}{41} \approx 0.0244 \approx 2.44\%
$$

**(iv) Throughput:**

$$
\text{Frames per second} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{1}{0.041} \approx 24.39 \text{ frames/s}
$$

$$
\text{Bits per second} = \frac{L}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{1000}{0.041} \approx 24{,}390 \text{ bps} \approx 24.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even though the channel capacity is **1 Mbps**, Stop-and-Wait delivers only **~24.4 kbps** — confirming the protocol's inefficiency for high-bandwidth long-delay links.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **channel utilization** of the Stop-and-Wait protocol. State clearly all assumptions. What is the maximum achievable utilization, and under what condition is it attained? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Stop-and-Wait ARQ system operates over a link of length **2000 km** with a signal propagation speed of $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The data rate is **64 kbps**, and frames are **1000 bits** long. ACK frames are **100 bits** long.
  (i) Find the propagation time $T_p$ and transmission time $T_t$ for data and ACK.
  (ii) Compute the channel utilization $U$.
  (iii) If the link is upgraded to **1 Mbps**, what is the new utilization? What does this reveal? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Channel Utilization (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
- Error-free channel (no retransmissions).
- Single one-way propagation delay $T_p$ for both data and ACK.
- Processing delays at sender and receiver are negligible.
- ACK transmission time is initially neglected (then re-introduced for refinement).

**Derivation:** *(As shown in Section 3.1 above)*

Result:

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}, \quad a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}
$$

**Maximum Utilization:** $U_{\max} = 1$ (or 100%), attained **only when $a \to 0$**, i.e., when $T_p \ll T_t$. This happens when:
- The link is very short (e.g., a LAN cable within a room), **or**
- The frame is very long (large $L$).

In all real long-distance scenarios, $U < 1$, often dramatically so.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $d = 2000 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^6$ m
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $R = 64 \text{ kbps} = 64{,}000$ bps (Part i, ii) and $1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6$ bps (Part iii)
- $L = 1000$ bits
- $L_{\text{ack}} = 100$ bits

**(i) Times:**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{64{,}000} = 0.015625 \text{ s} \approx 15.625 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{ack}} = \frac{L_{\text{ack}}}{R} = \frac{100}{64{,}000} \approx 1.5625 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Utilization at 64 kbps (with ACK time):**

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p + T_{\text{ack}}} = \frac{15.625}{15.625 + 20 + 1.5625} = \frac{15.625}{37.1875} \approx 0.4202 \approx 42.02\%
$$

**(iii) Utilization at 1 Mbps:**

$$
T_t' = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{ack}}' = \frac{100}{10^6} = 0.1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
U' = \frac{1}{1 + 20 + 0.1} = \frac{1}{21.1} \approx 0.0474 \approx 4.74\%
$$

**Engineering Insight:** Increasing the bandwidth **16× (from 64 kbps to 1 Mbps) actually *decreased* utilization from 42% to 4.7%**! This is the **bandwidth-delay product trap** of Stop-and-Wait. The protocol is **anti-scalable** — it performs *worse* on faster, longer links. This paradox was the historical motivation for Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat sliding-window protocols.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write $T_t$ in seconds **and** convert to ms when appropriate. Marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> 2. **Mixing up $T_p$ and $T_t$:** $T_p$ is *propagation* (depends on distance and speed of signal); $T_t$ is *transmission* (depends on frame size and data rate). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring ACK transmission time:** On long links with significant bandwidth, the ACK time is non-negligible. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong utilization formula:** Writing $U = T_t / (T_t + T_p)$ instead of $T_t / (T_t + 2 T_p)$ is a **fatal** error — the factor of 2 represents the *round-trip* delay.
> 5. **Forgetting to define $a$:** Always state the relationship $a = T_p / T_t$ explicitly before substituting.
> 6. **Not drawing the timing diagram** in Part (a) of 14-mark questions — this is a **mandatory 2-mark component**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** 🚀

- ✅ **Stop-and-Wait = send 1 frame, wait for ACK, repeat.** Simplest flow control mechanism.
- ✅ **Sequence numbers need only 1 bit** (alternating 0/1) to handle duplicates in noisy channels.
- ✅ **Timer mechanism** is essential for retransmission on loss.
- ✅ **Two critical time variables:** $T_t = L/R$ (transmission) and $T_p = d/v$ (propagation).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $\boxed{U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}}$ where $a = T_p / T_t$.
- ✅ **Channel is idle for $2 T_p$ seconds** per successful cycle — the source of inefficiency.
- ✅ **Anti-scalable:** Higher bandwidth or longer distance *reduces* $U$.
- ✅ **Foundation for:** Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ, TCP reliability, satellite link protocols.
- ✅ **KTU mantra:** Always state assumptions, label your timing diagram axes in seconds/ms, and double-check the factor of 2 in the round-trip delay.
- ✅ **Real-world application:** IoT, RFID, deep-space communication, half-duplex radio — anywhere simplicity outweighs throughput.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sliding Window
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sliding Window

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sliding Window Protocol** is a **Data Link Layer** flow control and error control mechanism in the OSI/TCP-IP layered architecture that allows a sender to transmit **multiple frames** before requiring an acknowledgment (ACK) for the earliest unacknowledged frame. The set of sequence numbers that the sender is permitted to transmit without waiting for an ACK is referred to as the **Send Window**, while the set of sequence numbers that the receiver is willing to accept is the **Receive Window**.

The "sliding" nomenclature originates from the way both windows **slide forward** along the sequence number space as acknowledgments are received and new frames are transmitted. This protocol fundamentally supersedes the inefficient **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** by exploiting the **bandwidth-delay product** of the underlying physical channel.

The two principal variants mandated by the KTU 2024 PECCT751 syllabus are:
- **Go-Back-N ARQ** (GBN)
- **Selective Repeat ARQ** (SR)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sequence Numbering** uses a modulo arithmetic system, typically modulo $2^k$ where **$k$** is the number of bits in the sequence number field of the data link layer frame header. The maximum send window size is therefore constrained to $W_s = 2^k - 1$ for Go-Back-N and $W_s = 2^{k-1}$ for Selective Repeat.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **postmaster at a sorting facility** who can dispatch **10 trucks** of parcels per shift. You do NOT wait for a "delivery confirmation" of truck #1 before sending truck #2 — instead, you maintain a **rolling queue of 10 active trucks**. The moment you receive a confirmation that **truck #1** has been delivered, your window **slides forward by one position**, permitting you to dispatch **truck #11**.

- **Sender's window** = your maximum outstanding truck dispatch limit.
- **Receiver's window** = the receiver's mailbox capacity before it gets overwhelmed.
- **Acknowledgment** = the "delivery confirmation" that causes the slide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Sliding Window protocols form the bedrock of modern data communication. The **Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** in the Internet protocol suite is a direct descendant of Selective Repeat ARQ. It governs every web page, video stream, and email you transmit across the Internet. Without sliding window flow control, satellite communication, high-speed LANs, and 4G/5G data channels would collapse into single-frame-per-RTT throughput.

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
- **Maximum Window Size ($W_{max}$):** $2^k - 1$ (GBN) and $2^{k-1}$ (SR)
- **Channel Utilization ($U$):** Measured as a fraction between $0$ and $1$
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product ($B \times T_p$):** Measured in **bits** or **bytes**
- **Parameter $a$:** Dimensionless ratio defined as $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ (Propagation Time / Transmission Time)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The Sliding Window protocol operates on three synchronized mechanisms working in concert:

### Mechanism A: The Send Window (Sender Side)
- The sender maintains a buffer of $W_s$ frames whose sequence numbers fall within the current send window range.
- The **lower edge** of the window = the oldest sent-but-unacknowledged frame (e.g., `S_base`).
- The **upper edge** of the window = `S_base + W_s - 1` (the highest sequence number the sender may transmit).
- When a valid ACK for `S_base` arrives, the window slides: $S_{base} \leftarrow S_{base} + 1$, and a new frame (if any) is pushed into the newly vacated slot.

### Mechanism B: The Receive Window (Receiver Side)
- The receiver maintains a buffer of $W_r$ frames it is currently willing to accept.
- For **Go-Back-N**: $W_r = 1$. The receiver ONLY accepts the in-order next frame; out-of-order frames are silently discarded and the cumulative ACK for the last correctly received in-order frame is re-issued.
- For **Selective Repeat**: $W_r > 1$. The receiver buffers all correctly received frames (whether in-order or not) and issues an ACK for **each** individual frame (selective ACK). Out-of-order frames are held in the receive buffer until the missing predecessor is filled.

### Mechanism C: The Acknowledgment & Retransmission Strategy
- **Go-Back-N (GBN)**: On timeout of frame $i$, the sender retransmits frame $i$ AND all subsequently sent frames (frames $i+1, i+2, \ldots, i+W_s-1$). This is the "Go Back N" — the pipeline is **rewound and replayed**.
- **Selective Repeat (SR)**: On timeout of frame $i$, the sender retransmits **ONLY frame $i$**. Other in-flight frames are not disturbed. The receiver must then re-sequence the buffered frames into the proper order before delivering to the upper layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** The KTU board examiner expects students to know WHY the **Selective Repeat window** must be $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$. This inequality is the mathematical proof that prevents a stale (delayed) duplicate frame from being mistakenly accepted as new — a classic exam question.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** you must memorize for solving numerical problems on Sliding Window protocols in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Range | Applicable To |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Transmission Time ($T_t$) | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | All protocols |
| 2 | Propagation Time ($T_p$) | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | All protocols |
| 3 | Dimensionless parameter ($a$) | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{d \cdot R}{L \cdot v}$ | unitless | All protocols |
| 4 | Bandwidth-Delay Product ($BDP$) | $BDP = R \cdot T_p$ | bits | All protocols |
| 5 | Stop-and-Wait Efficiency | $U = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | $0 < U \leq 1$ | Stop-and-Wait only |
| 6 | Go-Back-N Efficiency ($W_s \geq 2a+1$) | $U = 1$ (or $100\%$) | $U = 1$ | Go-Back-N |
| 7 | Go-Back-N Efficiency ($W_s < 2a+1$) | $U = \dfrac{W_s}{1+2a}$ | $0 < U < 1$ | Go-Back-N |
| 8 | Selective Repeat Efficiency ($W_s \geq 2a+1$) | $U = 1$ | $U = 1$ | Selective Repeat |
| 9 | Selective Repeat Efficiency ($W_s < 2a+1$) | $U = \dfrac{W_s}{1+2a}$ | $0 < U < 1$ | Selective Repeat |
| 10 | GBN Max Window | $W_{s,max} = 2^k - 1$ | sequence numbers | Go-Back-N |
| 11 | SR Max Window (each side) | $W_{s,max} = W_{r,max} = 2^{k-1}$ | sequence numbers | Selective Repeat |
| 12 | SR Constraint | $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$ | — | Selective Repeat |
| 13 | Total Time (GBN, frame lost) | $T_{total} = T_t(2a+1)$ for $N=1$ variant | seconds | Go-Back-N |
| 14 | Throughput ($S$) | $S = U \cdot R$ | frames/sec or bits/sec | All protocols |

**Notation Glossary (KTU Board Examiner Approved):**
- $L$ = Frame length in **bits**
- $R$ = Channel bit rate in **bits/second**
- $d$ = One-way link distance in **meters**
- $v$ = Signal propagation speed in **m/s** (typically $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber)
- $N$ or $W_s$ = Send window size
- $k$ = Number of bits in the sequence number field

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TCP Tahoe/Reno/NewReno congestion control** uses a sliding congestion window (`cwnd`) that opens additively and contracts multiplicatively — directly inspired by Sliding Window flow control.
- **Satellite link communication** (e.g., VSAT, GPS telemetry) heavily relies on Selective Repeat ARQ because of the very high $T_p$ values (≈ 250 ms to GEO satellite) — Stop-and-Wait would be catastrophic.
- **High-speed LAN backbones (10 Gbps Ethernet, Fiber Channel)** require Sliding Window with large sequence number fields ($k = 32$ bits in modern TCP).
- **Storage Area Networks (SANs)** and **RAID controllers** use sliding window ARQ for reliable block-level data transfer between disks and hosts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stop-and-Wait Efficiency (Foundation for Sliding Window)

> **Problem Setup:** Consider a sender transmitting a frame of length $L$ bits over a link of bit rate $R$ bps at distance $d$ meters, with signal speed $v$ m/s. The ACK is instantaneous (negligible length). Find channel utilization.

**Step 1 — Define Transmission Time:**
This is the time required to push all $L$ bits of the frame onto the physical medium.

$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Define Propagation Time:**
This is the time for the last bit of the frame to travel the one-way link distance to the receiver.

$$T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Sketch the Timing Diagram (logical analysis):**
The sender transmits for $T_t$ seconds. The last bit arrives at the receiver after $T_t + T_p$ seconds. The ACK (assumed zero-length, zero delay) returns instantly. The ACK arrives back at the sender after $T_t + 2T_p$ seconds total. Hence, the **total cycle time** is:

$$T_{cycle} = T_t + 2T_p$$

**Step 4 — Define Channel Utilization:**
Channel utilization $U$ is the fraction of total cycle time spent transmitting **useful data**.

$$U = \dfrac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the dimensionless parameter $a$:**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_t$:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

where $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Behavior:** If $a \to 0$ (very fast link / very long frame), $U \to 1$. If $a \to \infty$ (very long propagation delay), $U \to 0$ — meaning the link sits **idle** most of the time. This is the fundamental inefficiency that Sliding Window protocols overcome.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Go-Back-N ARQ Efficiency for Small Window ($W_s < 2a + 1$)

> **Problem Setup:** A sender uses a window size $W_s$ that is **insufficient** to keep the pipe full. Derive the channel utilization.

**Step 1 — Identify the Pipelining Constraint:**
The sender can transmit $W_s$ frames back-to-back. The total transmission time for all $W_s$ frames is $W_s \cdot T_t$. After the last bit of the $W_s$-th frame enters the channel, it takes $T_p$ to arrive. The ACK for the first frame returns $T_p$ after the receiver finishes processing it. Total time for the cycle of $W_s$ frames:

$$T_{cycle,GBN} = W_s \cdot T_t + 2T_p$$

**Step 2 — Useful Transmission Time:**
The useful time spent transmitting payload (assuming no error) is $W_s \cdot T_t$.

**Step 3 — Compute Utilization:**

$$U_{GBN} = \dfrac{W_s \cdot T_t}{W_s \cdot T_t + 2T_p}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by dividing by $T_t$:**

$$U_{GBN} = \dfrac{W_s}{W_s + 2a}$$

**Step 5 — Critical Threshold:**
If the window is large enough that the ACK returns BEFORE the sender exhausts the window, i.e., $W_s \geq 2a + 1$, then the sender never has to wait, and $U_{GBN} = 1$. Combining both cases:

$$U_{GBN} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{W_s}{1 + 2a}, & W_s < 2a + 1 \\ 1, & W_s \geq 2a + 1 \end{cases}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Selective Repeat ARQ Constraint $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$

> **Problem Setup:** Prove that for Selective Repeat to be free of ambiguity between old and new frames, the sum of send and receive window sizes must not exceed the sequence number space $2^k$.

**Step 1 — Define the Sequence Number Space:**
Sequence numbers cycle modulo $2^k$. A frame with sequence number $x$ and another with sequence number $x + 2^k$ (mod $2^k$) are **indistinguishable** in the sequence number field — both look like $x$.

**Step 2 — Define the "Window Overlap" Condition:**
A confusing situation arises if the **receive window** overlaps with the **send window** of a previous round. Consider:
- Old send window: $[a, a + W_s - 1]$ (mod $2^k$)
- New send window (after ACKs): $[b, b + W_s - 1]$ (mod $2^k$)
- Receiver window: $[c, c + W_r - 1]$ (mod $2^k$)

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Ambiguity:**
The most dangerous case: receiver expects frames in $[0, W_r - 1]$ while the sender's next round sends frames in $[W_r, W_r + W_s - 1]$. A duplicate of frame $0$ from the **old** cycle arriving at this moment could be misinterpreted as a new frame with sequence number $0$.

**Step 4 — Derive the Constraint:**
For no overlap and no ambiguity:

$$W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$$

When the windows are equal-sized (typical symmetric design), $W_s = W_r = W$, giving:

$$2W \leq 2^k \implies W \leq 2^{k-1}$$

This proves the **selective repeat maximum window** is $W_{max} = 2^{k-1}$ for each side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Go-Back-N special case:** For GBN, $W_r = 1$ (receiver accepts only one in-order frame), so the constraint reduces to $W_s + 1 \leq 2^k$, i.e., $W_s \leq 2^k - 1$. This is why the GBN max window is $2^k - 1$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Question:** Consider a satellite link at $R = 1$ Mbps, $d = 36{,}000$ km, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Frame length $L = 1000$ bits. ACK length is negligible. Find (a) the value of $a$, (b) the maximum channel utilization under Stop-and-Wait, and (c) the minimum send window size for Go-Back-N to achieve 100% utilization.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_p$:**

$$T_p = \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_t$:**

$$T_t = \dfrac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a$:**

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{120 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 120$$

**Step 4 — Stop-and-Wait Utilization:**

$$U_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 240} = \dfrac{1}{241} \approx 0.00415 = 0.415\%$$

**Step 5 — Minimum GBN Window for 100% Utilization:**

$$W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(120) + 1 = 241$$

This is an enormous window, indicating that satellite links are **inherently pipelined** problems — and motivates the use of Selective Repeat with large $k$-bit sequence numbers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sliding Window Protocol Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Upper Layer Application Data] --> B[Sender Buffer Queue]
    B --> C{Send Window Logic}
    C --> D[Frame Assembler with Seq Number]
    D --> E[Physical Channel Tx]
    E --> F[Physical Channel Rx]
    F --> G[Receiver Window Logic]
    G --> H{In-Order Frame}
    H -- Yes --> I[Deliver to Upper Layer and Slide Rx Window]
    H -- No --> J[GBN: Discard and Re-send Cumulative ACK]
    H -- No --> K[SR: Buffer Out-of-Order and Send Selective ACK]
    I --> L[Generate ACK for Sender]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[ACK Transmitter]
    M --> E
    C --> N{ACK Arrived}
    N -- Yes --> O[Slide Send Window Forward]
    N -- No --> P{Timeout Occurred}
    P -- Yes GBN --> Q[Retransmit N Frames from Base]
    P -- Yes SR --> R[Retransmit Only Timed-Out Frame]
    P -- No --> S[Wait for ACK or Timeout]
    O --> C
    S --> C
```

## 4.2 Go-Back-N ARQ — Sequence Number State Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Sender State S base equals i] -- Transmit frames i to i plus W minus 1 --> S1[Window Slides on ACK for i]
    S1 -- ACK for i plus 1 --> S2[Window Slides Further]
    S2 -- Timeout on frame j --> S3[Retransmit j to j plus W minus 1]
    S3 -- ACK Received --> S0
    R0[Receiver State Expects frame i] -- Correct i Received --> R1[Send ACK for i and Slide to i plus 1]
    R1 -- Correct i plus 1 Received --> R2[Send ACK for i plus 1]
    R0 -- Out of Order Frame --> R3[Discard and Re-send ACK for i minus 1]
```

## 4.3 Selective Repeat ARQ — Window Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SWIN [Sender Window Ws equals 4]
        S0[Frame 4]
        S1[Frame 5]
        S2[Frame 6]
        S3[Frame 7]
    end
    subgraph RWIN [Receiver Window Wr equals 4]
        R0[Expecting 4]
        R1[Got 5 Buffered]
        R2[Got 6 Buffered]
        R3[Got 7 Buffered]
    end
    SWIN -- Data Packets --> RWIN
    RWIN -- Selective ACKs --> SWIN
    S0 -- ACK 4 --> SNEW[Slide to 8]
    SNEW[Slide to 8] --> SNew[New window 5 6 7 8]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Flow — GBN vs SR Error Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START([Frame Loss Detected]) --> Q{Protocol Type}
    Q -- GBN --> GBNA[Discard All Subsequent Buffered Frames]
    GBNA --> GBNB[Retransmit Lost Frame and All Successors]
    GBNB --> GBNC[Wait for Cumulative ACK to Resume]
    Q -- SR --> SRA[Retain All Subsequent Correctly Received Frames in Buffer]
    SRA --> SRB[Retransmit ONLY the Lost Frame]
    SRB --> SRC[Receiver Re-sequences Buffered Frames and Delivers to Upper Layer]
    GBNC --> END([Pipeline Resumed])
    SRC --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical state in the protocol finite state machine (FSM). The arrows denote state transitions triggered by events such as ACK arrival, timeout expiry, or out-of-order frame reception. The `Ws` and `Wr` parameters are the algebraic window sizes used in Section 3 derivations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Sliding Window Protocol. What are the two major categories of sliding window protocols?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Sliding Window Protocol** is a data link layer flow and error control technique in which the sender is permitted to transmit multiple data frames (a "window" of them) before receiving an acknowledgment for the earliest frame. The window "slides" forward over the sequence number space as acknowledgments are received.

The two major categories are:
1. **Go-Back-N ARQ (GBN)** — The receiver accepts only in-order frames; on error, all subsequent frames are retransmitted.
2. **Selective Repeat ARQ (SR)** — The receiver accepts and buffers out-of-order frames; only the specific lost frame is retransmitted.

*(Examiner's Note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for naming and describing the two categories.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is the Selective Repeat window size limited to $2^{k-1}$ while Go-Back-N can use $2^k - 1$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The constraint arises from avoiding ambiguity between newly transmitted frames and duplicate (stale) frames from a previous round. For **Selective Repeat**, both sender and receiver maintain independent sliding windows of size $W_s$ and $W_r$. To prevent the receiver from confusing a duplicate frame with a new one, the condition is $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$. With symmetric design ($W_s = W_r = W$), this gives $W \leq 2^{k-1}$.

For **Go-Back-N**, the receive window is $W_r = 1$ (only the next in-order frame is accepted), so the constraint becomes $W_s + 1 \leq 2^k$, giving $W_s \leq 2^k - 1$.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for the core constraint, 1 mark for the SR case, 1 mark for the GBN case with reasoning.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** In KTU ESE, students must answer ONE full 14-mark question out of two choices. Both choices are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Explain the operation of the **Go-Back-N ARQ** protocol with a suitable sender and receiver sliding window diagram. Clearly state the protocol rules and the maximum window size formula. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A channel has a bit rate of **$R = 64$ kbps** and a one-way propagation delay of **$T_p = 20$ ms**. Frame size is **$L = 1000$ bits** and the ACK is negligible. Find:
   (i) The value of $a$
   (ii) Stop-and-Wait efficiency
   (iii) Minimum send window size for Go-Back-N to achieve 100% utilization
   (iv) Selective Repeat efficiency if $W_s = 5$ and $W_r = 5$ with $k = 3$ bits. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Go-Back-N ARQ Operation (7 Marks):**

**Protocol Rules (3 Marks):**
1. The send window is of size $W_s$ (maximum $2^k - 1$ where $k$ = sequence number bits).
2. The sender can transmit up to $W_s$ frames without ACK.
3. The receiver accepts frames **only in order**. Out-of-order frames are **discarded**.
4. On receiving an in-order frame, the receiver sends a **cumulative ACK** for the highest in-order frame received.
5. On **timeout** for frame $i$, the sender retransmits frame $i$ and **all subsequently transmitted frames** (i.e., frames $i, i+1, \ldots, i+W_s-1$).

**Sender and Receiver Window Behavior (3 Marks):**
- *Sender:* Maintains a window over sequence numbers $[S_{base}, S_{base} + W_s - 1]$. Each new ACK for $S_{base}$ slides the window by 1.
- *Receiver:* Maintains a window of size **1**. It expects exactly $R_{next}$ and discards anything else while re-issuing the ACK for $R_{next} - 1$.

**Maximum Window Size (1 Mark):**
The maximum send window for Go-Back-N is $W_{s,max} = 2^k - 1$ to prevent sequence number ambiguity.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Compute $a$ (2 Marks):**

$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1000}{64000} = 15.625 \text{ ms}$$

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{20 \text{ ms}}{15.625 \text{ ms}} = 1.28$$

**(ii) Stop-and-Wait Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$U_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2(1.28)} = \dfrac{1}{3.56} \approx 0.2809 \approx 28.09\%$$

**(iii) Minimum GBN Window for 100% Utilization (1 Mark):**

$$W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(1.28) + 1 = 3.56$$

Since $W_s$ must be an integer, the minimum integer window is $W_s = 4$.

**(iv) Selective Repeat Efficiency (2 Marks):**

Since $W_s = 5 \geq 2a + 1 = 3.56$, the window is sufficient to keep the pipe full.

$$U_{SR} = 1 = 100\%$$

(Equivalently, since $5 \geq 3.56$, the protocol can fill the channel completely.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Selective Repeat ARQ** protocol. Explain with diagrams how it differs from Go-Back-N. State and prove the constraint $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Consider a $1$ Mbps satellite link with $T_p = 270$ ms. Frame length $L = 4000$ bits$.
   (i) Compute $a$ and Stop-and-Wait utilization.
   (ii) If Go-Back-N is used with $W_s = 7$, what is the efficiency?
   (iii) If Selective Repeat is used with $W_s = W_r = 4$ and $k = 3$, what is the efficiency? Justify whether this configuration is valid. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Selective Repeat ARQ (7 Marks):**

**Description (3 Marks):**
- The sender transmits up to $W_s$ frames without waiting.
- The receiver has a window of size $W_r > 1$ and **accepts frames out of order**, buffering them.
- A **selective ACK** is sent for every correctly received frame (in-order or out-of-order).
- On timeout, the sender retransmits **only the specific lost frame** (not the whole pipeline).

**Difference from Go-Back-N (2 Marks):**
| Aspect | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Receive window | $W_r = 1$ | $W_r > 1$ |
| Out-of-order frames | Discarded | Buffered |
| Retransmission | All subsequent frames | Only lost frame |
| Max send window | $2^k - 1$ | $2^{k-1}$ |
| Efficiency on noisy links | Lower | Higher |

**Proof of $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$ (2 Marks):**
Assume $W_s + W_r > 2^k$. Then the receive window and the next-cycle send window overlap. A delayed duplicate of frame $x$ from the old cycle (still in transit) would arrive when the receiver is now expecting frames whose sequence numbers coincide with $x$ in the new cycle. The receiver cannot distinguish old from new — leading to corrupted data delivery. To prevent this overlap: $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Compute $a$ and Stop-and-Wait Efficiency (3 Marks):**

$$T_t = \dfrac{4000}{10^6} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

$$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{270}{4} = 67.5$$

$$U_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2(67.5)} = \dfrac{1}{136} \approx 0.00735 = 0.735\%$$

**(ii) Go-Back-N Efficiency with $W_s = 7$ (2 Marks):**

Since $W_s = 7 < 2a + 1 = 136$, the window is too small to keep the pipe full.

$$U_{GBN} = \dfrac{W_s}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{7}{136} \approx 0.0515 = 5.15\%$$

**(iii) Selective Repeat with $W_s = W_r = 4$, $k = 3$ (2 Marks):**

Check constraint: $W_s + W_r = 4 + 4 = 8 \leq 2^3 = 8$ ✓ (Valid — boundary case.)

Since $W_s = 4 < 2a + 1 = 136$:

$$U_{SR} = \dfrac{W_s}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{4}{136} \approx 0.0294 = 2.94\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the integer constraint** on $W_s$ in the $W_{min} = 2a + 1$ calculation — students often write $W_{min} = 3.56$ when the answer should be $4$.
> 2. **Confusing $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$ with equality** — many students write $W_s = W_r = 2^{k-1}$ without justifying the symmetric design assumption.
> 3. **Using $2a$ instead of $2a + 1$** in the $100\%$ utilization threshold — the $+1$ comes from the fact that the sender needs to send at least one frame AFTER receiving the ACK to maintain a non-stop pipeline.
> 4. **Ignoring ACK transmission time** when ACKs are non-negligible — the $T_t$ of the ACK must be added to the cycle time.
> 5. **Mixing up GBN and SR window sizes** — saying GBN max is $2^{k-1}$ or SR max is $2^k - 1$ will cost full marks.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in $T_t$, $T_p$, and $a$ — KTU examiners explicitly check for unit consistency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Fire Revision — Pin This in Your Brain Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Sliding Window Protocol** = Pipelined ARQ for Data Link Layer flow and error control.
- ✅ **Send Window** = sender's outstanding frame limit; **Receive Window** = receiver's acceptance range.
- ✅ **Stop-and-Wait** is Sliding Window with $W_s = 1$.
- ✅ **Go-Back-N ARQ:** $W_r = 1$, retransmits entire pipeline on error. Max $W_s = 2^k - 1$.
- ✅ **Selective Repeat ARQ:** $W_r > 1$, retransmits only lost frame. Max $W_s = 2^{k-1}$.
- ✅ **Constraint:** $W_s + W_r \leq 2^k$ (for SR) — prevents old/new frame ambiguity.
- ✅ **$a = T_p / T_t$** — the dimensionless parameter that controls pipelining needs.
- ✅ **$100\%$ Utilization Condition:** $W_s \geq 2a + 1$ for both GBN and SR.
- ✅ **Sub-optimal Efficiency:** $U = W_s / (1 + 2a)$ when $W_s < 2a + 1$.
- ✅ **Stop-and-Wait Efficiency:** $U = 1 / (1 + 2a)$ — fundamentally limited.
- ✅ **Throughput** $S = U \times R$ in frames/sec or bits/sec.
- ✅ **$T_t = L/R$** and **$T_p = d/v$** — these are the two foundational time units.
- ✅ **Propagation speed** $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber and $\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum/air.
- ✅ **TCP congestion window** is a direct engineering application of the Sliding Window paradigm.
- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always draw a timeline showing $T_t$ and $T_p$ before solving numerical problems; the KTU examiner values diagram-aided reasoning.
- ✅ **The "Go Back N"** name literally means the sender "goes back" to the lost frame and retransmits N frames (where N = window size).
- ✅ **The "Selective Repeat"** name means the sender selectively retransmits and the receiver repeats (delivers) the buffered frames in correct sequence.
- ✅ **Pipeline flushing** in GBN wastes bandwidth; SR's selective retransmission preserves bandwidth on noisy links.
- ✅ **Receiver never sends a NACK in GBN** — it only re-issues cumulative ACKs. (Some variants do use NACK; default KTU answer: No NACK.)
- ✅ **Sequence number space** cycles modulo $2^k$; this is the mathematical root of all window size constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** MAC Protocols: CSMA/CD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** is the Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol standardized by the IEEE 802.3 working group for classical Ethernet networks (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T). It is a contention-based, random-access protocol that governs how multiple stations share a single, broadcast, half-duplex channel.

In CSMA/CD, a station wishing to transmit first *listens* to the medium (carrier sense). If the medium is idle, it begins transmission. **Simultaneously**, the station continues to monitor the medium to *detect* a collision (collision detection). If a collision is detected, the station aborts transmission, transmits a 32-bit *jam signal* to reinforce the collision, and then schedules a retransmission attempt after a random *backoff* interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** CSMA/CD is examined as part of the *Data Link Layer Sub-layer — MAC Sublayer* unit. Students are expected to know the full operational cycle, frame size constraints, backoff algorithm, and efficiency derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two distinct sensing phases must not be confused:**
> 1. **Carrier Sense** → Done *before* transmission (is the wire free?).
> 2. **Collision Detection** → Done *during* transmission (did two signals collide?).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Polite Dinner Party

Imagine a long dinner table in a dimly lit hall where **5 friends** want to talk at the same time, but only one microphone is being passed around (the shared medium).

| Real-World Element | CSMA/CD Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Single shared microphone | Shared half-duplex Ethernet bus/cable |
| Listening before speaking | **Carrier Sense** |
| Speaking and hearing your own voice | **Collision Detection** while transmitting |
| Two people starting to speak together | **Signal collision** on the wire |
| "Sorry, you go first!" apology | **Jam signal** + **Binary Exponential Backoff** |
| Waiting a random time before retrying | Random backoff in slot times |

**The intuition:** Just as polite speakers listen first and immediately stop if they hear someone else talking, a CSMA/CD station senses the wire first and aborts the instant it senses a voltage spike above the nominal signal level. The "stop immediately" is the critical difference from pure CSMA — it preserves bandwidth by not transmitting a corrupted full-length frame.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this hierarchy:** Pure ALOHA → Slotted ALOHA → **CSMA** → **CSMA/CD** → **CSMA/CA**. Each layer adds intelligence. CSMA/CD is the first protocol that *catches its own mistake* mid-transmission.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Logical Constants

The following values are **standardized** in IEEE 802.3 and are high-yield for KTU problems:

- **Slot Time (τ):** **51.2 µs** for 10 Mbps Ethernet (equals 512 bit times).
- **Jam Signal:** **32 bits** of alternating 1010... pattern.
- **Maximum Retransmission Attempts:** **16**, after which the frame is dropped and the upper layer is notified.
- **Minimum Frame Size:** **64 bytes (512 bits)** for 10BASE-T.
- **Maximum Network Length (10BASE5):** **2500 meters** of coax between repeaters.
- **Maximum Number of Repeaters:** **4** between any two stations (5-segment / 4-repeater rule).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of CSMA/CD

The protocol can be broken down into **6 sequential phases**. Examiners frequently ask students to enumerate these phases in order.

### Phase 1 — Carrier Sense (Listen Before Talk)
- The station samples the physical medium (typically by detecting a voltage threshold or a carrier frequency on the wire).
- If the medium is **busy** → the station defers its transmission and continues to monitor.
- If the medium is **idle** for **96 bit times** (the *Inter-Frame Gap*, IFG ≈ 9.6 µs) → it proceeds to Phase 2.

### Phase 2 — Frame Transmission Begins
- The station starts transmitting the frame bit-by-bit.
- Transmission is **serial** at the line rate R (e.g., 10 Mbps).

### Phase 3 — Concurrent Collision Detection
- Throughout transmission, the station compares the *voltage level on the wire* with the *signal it is currently sending*.
- A voltage difference exceeding a threshold indicates a collision.
- Detection is purely **analog** — it relies on the fact that two superimposed signals produce a DC offset nearly double the normal amplitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of Collision Detection:** A station cannot detect a collision *instantly*. The earliest moment of detection is when the collision signal propagates *back* to the sender. This round-trip propagation delay **t_p** is the bottleneck of CSMA/CD.

### Phase 4 — Jam Signal Transmission
- Upon detecting a collision, the sender transmits a **32-bit jam sequence**.
- This ensures that *all other stations* on the segment also detect the collision (so they too can back off).

### Phase 5 — Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)
- The colliding stations each select a random integer **k** of slot times to wait.
- The slot count is drawn from $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$ where **i = min(n, 10)**, and **n** is the number of collisions experienced by that particular frame.
- After **16 collisions** → the frame is discarded (error reported to LLC).

### Phase 6 — Retransmission Attempt
- After the backoff counter expires, the station returns to **Phase 1** and re-listens.
- The cycle repeats until the frame is successfully delivered or the 16-attempt limit is reached.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Constraint Notice:** All absolute-value bars and "such that" symbols inside the table use `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent markdown table column breaking.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission time of a frame | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length in bits, $R$ = line rate | seconds |
| 2 | One-way propagation delay | $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = cable length, $v$ ≈ $2 \times 10^8$ m/s (in coax) | seconds |
| 3 | Round-trip propagation | $2 t_p$ | Maximum collision-detection window | seconds |
| 4 | Parameter **a** (ratio) | $a = \dfrac{t_p}{T_{tx}} = \dfrac{t_p \cdot R}{L}$ | Normalized propagation delay | dimensionless |
| 5 | Minimum frame length | $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_p \cdot R$ | Must satisfy $L \geq 2 \cdot t_p \cdot R$ | bits |
| 6 | Pure CSMA/CD efficiency (1-persistent) | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 a}$ | Empirical formula for IEEE 802.3 efficiency | fraction |
| 7 | Maximum throughput | $S_{max} = E \cdot R$ | Practical upper-bound on channel utilization | bps |
| 8 | BEB slot count range | $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$ | $i = \min(n, 10)$, $n$ = collision count | slot times |
| 9 | Slot time (10 Mbps) | $\tau = 51.2 \,\mu s = 512 \text{ bit times}$ | $= 2 \cdot t_{p,\max}$ for 2500 m cable | µs |
| 10 | Jam duration | $T_{jam} = \dfrac{32}{R}$ | 32 bits at line rate | seconds |
| 11 | Throughput with collisions | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{G(e^{-G} + 1)}$ | General slotted contention (theoretical) | fraction |
| 12 | Maximum cable length | $d_{max} = \dfrac{v \cdot \tau}{2}$ | From $\tau = 2 t_{p,\max}$ | meters |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSMA/CD is not merely a textbook curiosity — it is the **historical and conceptual foundation** of all modern Ethernet:

- **Original Thick Ethernet (10BASE5)** and **Thin Ethernet (10BASE2)** used CSMA/CD over a single coaxial bus.
- **10BASE-T (twisted pair)** retained CSMA/CD in half-duplex mode, although full-duplex switches now dominate.
- **Gigabit Ethernet (1000BASE-T)** still implements CSMA/CD for backward compatibility, but the *minimum frame size* was extended to **512 bytes** using *carrier extension* to keep the 2500 m legacy reach.
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** could not use CSMA/CD because wireless stations cannot sense during transmission; it adopted **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance) instead. Understanding CSMA/CD is therefore a *prerequisite* to understanding CSMA/CA.
- **Industrial control & automotive CAN bus** borrow the CSMA/CD + backoff paradigm for deterministic, low-cost networking.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents the three derivations that are most frequently asked in KTU university examinations: **(A)** Minimum frame size, **(B)** CSMA/CD Efficiency, and **(C)** Binary Exponential Backoff slot count.

---

## 3.1 Derivation (A): Minimum Frame Size for Collision Detection

### 3.1.1 The Fundamental Problem
A station must be certain that if a collision occurs, it will *still be transmitting* when the collision signal propagates back to it. If the station finishes transmission before hearing the collision, the corrupted frame would be incorrectly accepted.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the worst-case scenario.**
Consider station **A** at one extreme end of the cable and station **B** at the other extreme. They begin transmitting *almost simultaneously* — A just after the signal from B has passed it but before B's signal has terminated.

**Step 2 — Time for the collision to occur.**
The first bit of B's signal must travel the full cable length $d$ before reaching A. This takes one propagation delay:

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{v}
$$

The two frames collide at a point near B at time $t_p$.

**Step 3 — Time for the collision signal to return.**
The resulting collision signature (anomalous voltage) must propagate *back* to A, taking another $t_p$.

**Step 4 — Total collision-detection window.**
The sender A therefore needs to be **still transmitting** at time:

$$
t_{collision\_detect} = 2 \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 5 — Frame transmission time must exceed this window.**
For A to be still transmitting when the collision news arrives:

$$
T_{tx} \geq 2 \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the transmission time formula.**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{L}{R} \geq 2 \cdot t_p
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the minimum frame size in bits.**

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_p \cdot R}
$$

### 3.1.3 Numerical Verification (10BASE5 Ethernet)
Given $R = 10$ Mbps, $d_{max} = 2500$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$
t_p = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^8} = 12.5 \,\mu s
$$

$$
2 \cdot t_p = 25 \,\mu s
$$

$$
L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_p \cdot R = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^6 = 250 \text{ bits}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IEEE 802.3 specifies 512 bits, not 250 bits:** The standard adds a safety margin to account for repeater delays, signal rise/fall times, and the jam signal. Thus $L_{min}$ is fixed at **64 bytes (512 bits)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation (B): Efficiency of CSMA/CD

### 3.2.1 Setup
The efficiency $E$ is defined as the ratio of *useful transmission time* to the *total channel busy time*:

$$
E = \frac{\text{Frame transmission time}}{\text{Frame transmission time} + \text{Wasted time}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Detailed Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the wasted time.**
In CSMA/CD, the channel is occupied for the entire duration the sender is on the air. The "wasted" time per attempt is dominated by the round-trip propagation delay $2 t_p$ (during which, if a collision occurs, valid data is not being passed).

**Step 2 — Express efficiency in terms of $a$.**
Define $a = \dfrac{t_p}{T_{tx}}$. Then:

$$
E = \frac{T_{tx}}{T_{tx} + 2 t_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

This is the *ideal* (collision-free) CSMA/CD efficiency. It assumes no collisions occur, which is unrealistic.

**Step 3 — Account for collision probability (Empirical refinement).**
Studies by Metcalfe and Boggs, later refined by the IEEE 802.3 committee, showed that collisions and backoff reduce efficiency. The empirically validated formula for 802.3 Ethernet is:

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \, a}}
$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases.**

- As $a \to 0$ (very long frames or very short cable): $E \to 1$ → near-perfect utilization.
- As $a \to \infty$ (very short frames or very long cable): $E \to 0$ → protocol collapses.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example
For a 10 Mbps network with $t_p = 25 \,\mu s$ and a 1500-byte (12000-bit) frame:

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{12000}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.2 \, ms
$$

$$
a = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0208
$$

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.0208} \approx \frac{1}{1.134} \approx 0.882
$$

Thus about **88.2% of channel capacity is used for useful data**, with ~11.8% lost to propagation and collisions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation (C): Binary Exponential Backoff Slot Count

### 3.3.1 The Algorithm

**Step 1 — Define the collision counter.**
For any given frame, maintain a counter $n$ of how many times it has collided. Initialize $n = 0$.

**Step 2 — Compute the slot-window exponent.**
The exponent is capped at 10 to bound the maximum wait:

$$
i = \min(n, 10)
$$

**Step 3 — Draw a random integer $k$.**
The number of slot times to wait is uniformly distributed over:

$$
k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^i - 1\}
$$

The probability mass function is therefore:

$$
P(k) = \frac{1}{2^i}, \quad \text{for} \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, 2^i - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Increment and retry.**
The station waits $k \cdot \tau$ seconds, then attempts retransmission. On failure, $n \leftarrow n + 1$ and the process repeats.

**Step 5 — Abort threshold.**
If $n = 16$ → discard the frame and signal a failure to the upper layer.

### 3.3.2 Worked Table (for examination answers)

| Collision # $n$ | $i = \min(n, 10)$ | Slot window $2^i$ | Random pick $k$ range | Max wait (in $\tau$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | $\{0, 1\}$ | 1 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 | $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ | 3 |
| 3 | 3 | 8 | $\{0, \dots, 7\}$ | 7 |
| 4 | 4 | 16 | $\{0, \dots, 15\}$ | 15 |
| 5 | 5 | 32 | $\{0, \dots, 31\}$ | 31 |
| 6 | 6 | 64 | $\{0, \dots, 63\}$ | 63 |
| 7 | 7 | 128 | $\{0, \dots, 127\}$ | 127 |
| 8 | 8 | 256 | $\{0, \dots, 255\}$ | 255 |
| 9 | 9 | 512 | $\{0, \dots, 511\}$ | 511 |
| 10 | 10 | 1024 | $\{0, \dots, 1023\}$ | 1023 |
| 11..15 | 10 (capped) | 1024 | $\{0, \dots, 1023\}$ | 1023 |
| 16 | — | — | Frame discarded | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Why cap the exponent at 10?** Beyond 10 collisions, the network is almost certainly broken or overloaded. The cap keeps the worst-case backoff at ~52 ms ($1023 \times 51.2 \,\mu s$), preventing indefinite suspension of a frame.

---

## 3.4 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

This matrix ties every formula to a question pattern, helping students identify the right equation at a glance during the exam.

| Problem Pattern | Required Formula | Key Knowns |
|---|---|---|
| "Find minimum frame size for cable length L" | $L_{min} = 2 t_p R$ | $d$, $v$, $R$ |
| "Find maximum throughput" | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 a} \cdot R$ | $R$, $L$, $t_p$ |
| "Find backoff range after $n$ collisions" | $k \in [0, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1]$ | $n$ |
| "How long does a sender wait?" | $T_{wait} = k \cdot \tau$ | $k$, $\tau$ |
| "Find propagation velocity" | $v = \dfrac{d}{t_p}$ | $d$, $t_p$ |
| "Efficiency drops below X% — find max length" | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44 a}$ | Solve for $a$, then $t_p$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides three Mermaid diagrams that visualize the CSMA/CD architecture and decision flow. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with the Mermaid syntax rules.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — CSMA/CD Operational State Machine

This flowchart shows the complete decision tree a station walks through for every frame it wishes to send.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Station has frame to send] --> A1{Carrier Sense: medium idle for 96 bit times?}
    A1 -- No --> A1b[Defer and continue sensing] --> A1
    A1 -- Yes --> A2[Begin frame transmission]
    A2 --> A3{Monitor medium during transmission}
    A3 -- Voltage within normal range --> A4[Continue transmission]
    A4 --> A5{All bits sent?}
    A5 -- No --> A3
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Frame sent successfully]
    A3 -- Collision signature detected --> B1[Abort transmission immediately]
    B1 --> B2[Transmit 32 bit jam signal]
    B2 --> B3{Collision count n equals 16?}
    B3 -- Yes --> B4[Discard frame and report error to upper layer]
    B3 -- No --> B5[Increment n by 1]
    B5 --> B6[Compute i = min of n and 10]
    B6 --> B7[Pick random k from 0 to 2 power i minus 1]
    B7 --> B8[Wait for k slot times]
    B8 --> A1
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Collision Detection Timing Scenario (Worst Case)

This block diagram explains *when* and *where* a collision happens, and how the timing constraint forces the minimum frame size.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Station A at left end] -- t_p --> C1[Signal traveling rightward]
    C1 -- collides with --> C2[Signal from Station B at right end]
    C2 -- reflected collision signal travels back --> C3[Station A receives collision news]
    C3 -- total round trip = 2 t_p --> C4{A still transmitting?}
    C4 -- Yes --> C5[Detects collision and backs off]
    C4 -- No --> C6[Frame would be wrongly accepted]
    C7[Requirement L min equals 2 t_p times R] --> C4
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Block Architecture of a CSMA/CD Node

This block diagram maps the internal modules of a station that participate in CSMA/CD, replacing the need for a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Upper Layer LLC] --> D2[MAC Frame Assembly]
    D2 --> D3[Carrier Sense Module]
    D3 -- medium busy --> D4[Defer Queue]
    D4 --> D3
    D3 -- medium idle --> D5[Transmit Engine]
    D5 --> D6[Collision Detector]
    D6 -- no collision --> D7[Frame completed to Physical Layer]
    D6 -- collision detected --> D8[Jam Signal Generator]
    D8 --> D9[Binary Exponential Backoff Unit]
    D9 --> D10[Backoff Timer and Slot Counter]
    D10 --> D3
    D6 -- 16th collision --> D11[Frame Discard and Error Report to LLC]
    D11 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Examiners:** When asked to "draw the CSMA/CD process", the most efficient answer is the state machine (Diagram 4.1). For a "collision timing" question, use Diagram 4.2. For an "architecture" or "station block" question, use Diagram 4.3.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the concept of Collision Detection in CSMA/CD. Why is it preferred over pure CSMA?**

**Model Answer:**

In CSMA/CD, while a station is transmitting a frame, it simultaneously monitors the medium to detect any anomalous voltage level that would indicate another station's signal is also on the wire. As soon as a collision is detected, the station aborts transmission and sends a jam signal.

This is preferred over pure CSMA for two reasons:
1. **Bandwidth conservation:** In pure CSMA, two colliding stations would continue transmitting the entire frame despite the corruption. CSMA/CD stops almost immediately, freeing the medium sooner.
2. **Earlier recovery:** The jam signal ensures all stations become aware of the collision, allowing them to back off in a coordinated (though probabilistic) way.

**[Marks Distribution: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for advantages]**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the term "minimum frame size" in CSMA/CD? Why is it fixed at 64 bytes for 10 Mbps Ethernet?**

**Model Answer:**

The minimum frame size is the smallest legal Ethernet frame, fixed to ensure that a transmitting station is still on the air when the collision signal returns from the farthest point on the network. It is derived from the relation $L_{min} = 2 \cdot t_p \cdot R$.

For 10 Mbps Ethernet with a maximum segment length of 2500 m, the round-trip propagation delay $2 t_p$ equals 25 µs. Theoretically this requires $L_{min} = 25 \,\mu s \times 10 \text{ Mbps} = 250$ bits. The IEEE 802.3 standard adds a safety margin for repeater delays, signal rise times, and the jam signal, fixing the minimum at **64 bytes (512 bits)**.

**[Marks Distribution: 1 for definition, 1 for derivation reference, 1 for the 64-byte value with reason]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B are provided; the student answers one.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**A.** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat timing diagram, explain how CSMA/CD handles a collision between two stations. Define *slot time* and state its value for 10 Mbps Ethernet.

**A.** **(b) [7 Marks]** A 10 Mbps Ethernet network uses a 1000-meter cable with propagation velocity $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Frames are 1500 bytes long.
   (i) Calculate the parameter $a$.
   (ii) Calculate the efficiency using the 802.3 formula.
   (iii) Calculate the maximum throughput in Mbps.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Timing Diagram (in text form):**

| Time (µs) | Station A | Medium | Station B |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Begins transmission | Signal propagates right | Idle, no carrier yet |
| 5 | Transmitting bits 1–50 | First bit reaches B's end | Begins transmission (collision imminent) |
| 10 | Transmitting bits 51–100 | Two signals overlap at B | Detects collision, sends jam |
| 15 | Detects collision via voltage spike | Collision signature travels left | Jam sent |
| 20 | Stops, sends jam signal | All stations detect | Waiting |
| 25 | Starts BEB backoff | Medium goes idle | Starts BEB backoff |

**Slot Time Definition:**
The slot time is the time taken for a signal to travel from one extreme end of the maximum-length network to the other and back again, multiplied by 2 (i.e., $2 \cdot t_{p,\max}$). For 10 Mbps Ethernet, $\tau = 51.2 \,\mu s = 512$ bit times.

**[Marks: 4 for diagram, 2 for slot time definition, 1 for numerical value]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $R = 10$ Mbps $= 10^7$ bits/s
- $d = 1000$ m
- $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 12000$ bits

**Step (i): Compute $a$.**

$$
t_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5 \,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^7} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1200 \,\mu s
$$

$$
a = \frac{t_p}{T_{tx}} = \frac{5}{1200} = 0.00417
$$

**Step (ii): Compute efficiency.**

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \cdot a} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.44 \times 0.00417}
$$

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 + 0.02685} = \frac{1}{1.02685} \approx 0.9738 \text{ or } 97.38\%
$$

**Step (iii): Compute maximum throughput.**

$$
S_{max} = E \cdot R = 0.9738 \times 10 \text{ Mbps} \approx 9.738 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**[Marks: 1 for each sub-step, plus 1 for the final boxed answer]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**B.** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) algorithm used in CSMA/CD. A frame experiences 4 collisions. What is the range of slot times the station will wait? What is the maximum wait if the 11th collision occurs?

**B.** **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a *jam signal*. What is its length and why is it transmitted? Why is the maximum number of retransmissions fixed at 16?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**BEB Algorithm Description (5 points):**

1. After a collision, each colliding station increments a per-frame collision counter $n$ (initially 0).
2. The exponent $i$ is computed as $i = \min(n, 10)$.
3. A random integer $k$ is drawn uniformly from $\{0, 1, \dots, 2^i - 1\}$.
4. The station waits $k \cdot \tau$ seconds, where $\tau$ is the slot time (51.2 µs for 10 Mbps).
5. The station then re-listens to the medium and retransmits if idle.
6. If $n = 16$ → frame is dropped.

**Range after 4 collisions (1 point):**
$n = 4 \Rightarrow i = 4 \Rightarrow 2^4 = 16$ slots
Range: $k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 15\}$

**Maximum wait after 11th collision (1 point):**
$n = 11 \Rightarrow i = \min(11, 10) = 10 \Rightarrow 2^{10} = 1024$ slots
Maximum wait $= 1023 \times 51.2 \,\mu s = 52.43$ ms

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Jam Signal Concept (2 points):**
The jam signal is a 32-bit pattern of alternating 1s and 0s (typically `1010...`) that a station transmits immediately after detecting a collision. Its purpose is twofold:
- To ensure that the collision is detected by all other stations on the segment (including those that may have just started receiving).
- To reinforce the collision signature so it cannot be mistaken for a valid frame preamble.

**Length and Justification (2 points):**
Length = **32 bits** (transmitted in 3.2 µs at 10 Mbps). This is short enough to minimize wasted bandwidth but long enough to traverse the entire network and reach all stations within the collision-detection window.

**Why the 16-attempt limit? (3 points):**
1. **Defensive bound:** Beyond 16 collisions, the probability of success is negligible; the network is almost certainly broken, overloaded, or has a physical fault.
2. **Bounded worst-case delay:** The total worst-case wait is bounded — the protocol can guarantee a deterministic *upper limit* on the time a frame may spend in retransmission.
3. **Prevents indefinite starvation:** Without a cap, a station could wait almost forever in extreme congestion, violating QoS requirements.
4. **Signals upper layer:** Discarding the frame forces the LLC/Network layer to take corrective action (e.g., reduce offered load or reroute).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on CSMA/CD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the propagation delay in minimum frame size.** Many students write $L_{min} = t_p \cdot R$ instead of $L_{min} = 2 t_p \cdot R$. The factor of **2** (round trip) is non-negotiable — losing 1–2 marks per error.
>
> 2. **Confusing slot time with transmission time.** Slot time $\tau$ is **not** the time to send one bit; it is **$2 \cdot t_{p,\max}$** (or 512 bit times for 10 Mbps).
>
> 3. **Using $E = 1/(1+2a)$ instead of the IEEE 802.3 formula.** The $1/(1+2a)$ expression is the *collision-free ideal*. The 802.3 standard mandates $1/(1+6.44a)$. Examiners expect the latter.
>
> 4. **Not stating units.** Always write µs, ms, Mbps alongside numerical answers. A correct number without units loses 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **Skipping the BEB cap of 10.** The exponent is $\min(n, 10)$, **not** simply $n$. This is a frequently tested trap.
>
> 6. **Mixing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA.** CSMA/CA is for wireless (802.11), uses **ACKs** and **RTS/CTS**, and *avoids* collisions rather than detecting them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

Use this dense bullet list for last-minute revision the night before the exam:

- **CSMA/CD = Carrier Sense + Multiple Access + Collision Detection** — all three phases are mandatory for full marks.
- **Three timing windows to memorize:** $t_p$ (one-way), $2 t_p$ (collision window), $\tau = 2 t_{p,\max}$ (slot time).
- **For 10 Mbps Ethernet, the magic numbers are: 64 bytes minimum frame, 1518 bytes maximum frame, 51.2 µs slot time, 32-bit jam signal, 16 retransmission cap, 2500 m max segment, 4 max repeaters.**
- **Minimum frame size formula:** $L_{min} = 2 t_p R$ — round trip propagation times bit rate.
- **The "a" parameter:** $a = t_p / T_{tx}$ — small $a$ means good efficiency, large $a$ means poor efficiency.
- **IEEE 802.3 efficiency formula:** $E = 1/(1 + 6.44a)$ — the 6.44 is empirical, not derivable from first principles in an exam.
- **BEB slot count:** After $n$ collisions, pick $k$ uniformly from $[0, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1]$.
- **After 16 collisions → frame is dropped → upper layer is notified.**
- **Jam signal** = 32 bits, transmitted to *all* stations, ensures the collision is universally recognized.
- **IFG (Inter-Frame Gap)** = 96 bit times (9.6 µs at 10 Mbps) — the minimum idle time between two consecutive frames on the wire.
- **CSMA/CD is half-duplex only**; full-duplex switched Ethernet disables CSMA/CD because there is no contention.
- **Modern Gigabit Ethernet** uses *carrier extension* to artificially pad short frames to 512 bytes, preserving CSMA/CD compatibility at higher speeds.
- **CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA:** CD for wired (detect mid-flight), CA for wireless (avoid before transmission via ACK/RTS-CTS).
- **History:** Invented by Robert Metcalfe and David Boggs at Xerox PARC in 1973; standardized as IEEE 802.3 in 1983.
- **Killer exam line:** *"A station must be still transmitting when the collision signal returns — therefore $L \geq 2 t_p R$."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSMA/CA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** CSMA/CA ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is a **Medium Access Control (MAC) protocol** used primarily in **wireless networks** (IEEE 802.11/Wi-Fi, IEEE 802.15) where stations **sense the carrier** before transmitting and then use deliberate **collision avoidance** mechanisms (waiting, backoff, and handshake frames) to minimize the probability of simultaneous transmissions. In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, CSMA/CA belongs to the family of **random-access protocols** at the **MAC sublayer of the Data Link Layer**, designed for **half-duplex, shared-medium wireless broadcast channels** where collision **detection is infeasible** due to: - The **hidden station problem** - The **exposed station problem** - The **fading and signal strength variation** of radio channels - Practical impossibility of a station to receive and transmit on the same frequency simultaneously. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polite Hallway Conversation" Imagine a **dark, narrow hallway** where many people are walking and want to talk. - Each person **cannot see** everyone else (hidden stations). - Each person **cannot listen and speak at the same time** (no collision detection). - A person **cannot shout loudly enough to reach the far end** (signal attenuation). How do they converse politely? 1. They **listen first** (Carrier Sense) — if they hear silence, the channel might be free. 2. They **wait for a respectful pause** (Inter Frame Space / IFS) — to give priority to important messages. 3. They **wait an additional random time** (Backoff) — so two people who both heard silence don't speak at the same instant. 4. Optionally, they **exchange polite "may I speak?" and "go ahead" handshakes** (RTS/CTS) — to reserve the floor. 5. Then they **speak** (transmit) and wait for an **acknowledgment** (ACK) — to confirm the listener heard them. This polite, *defensive* dance is exactly what CSMA/CA enforces in wireless networks. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction — CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA** > - **CSMA/CD (Ethernet/wired):** Detects collisions *after* they occur, then backs off and retransmits. > - **CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi/wireless):** *Avoids* collisions *before* they occur through controlled waiting and handshakes, because detection itself is impossible in wireless media. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted > [!IMPORTANT] > - **IFS** — Inter Frame Space (a mandatory silence period) > - **CW (Contention Window)** — the range of random slot choices, denoted by an integer $W$ > - **Slot Time** — a discrete time unit, denoted $\sigma$ (e.g., **20 µs in IEEE 802.11 DSSS**) > - **DIFS, SIFS, PIFS, EIFS** — different IFS durations > - **CWmin, CWmax** — minimum and maximum contention window sizes (e.g., **31** and **1023** in IEEE 802.11b) > - **ACK timeout** — typical default value of about **300 µs** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of CSMA/CA When a station (say, Station A) has a MAC frame ready to transmit, it executes the following sequence: 1. **Carrier Sense Phase** - The station monitors the wireless medium for a duration equal to the **DIFS (DCF Inter Frame Space)**. - If the medium is found **idle** for the entire DIFS period, the station proceeds. - If the medium is found **busy** at any instant, the station **defers** and continues monitoring. 2. **Backoff Phase (Contention Window)** - The station selects a **random integer** $k$ (the backoff counter) from the range $\left[0, CW\right]$, where $CW$ is the current contention window size. - The station then waits for $k \times \text{Slot Time}$ additional idle slots, decrementing $k$ only when the medium remains idle. - If the medium becomes busy during this backoff, the counter is **frozen** (paused) and resumed after the next DIFS idle period. 3. **Transmission Phase** - When the backoff counter reaches zero, the station transmits the entire MAC frame. - In **basic CSMA/CA**, no handshake is exchanged — the station simply transmits. 4. **Acknowledgment Phase (in IEEE 802.11 / DCF mode)** - The receiver waits for a **SIFS** interval (shorter than DIFS, giving the ACK priority) and replies with an **ACK** frame. - If the sender does not receive the ACK within the **ACK timeout**, it assumes the frame was lost (due to collision or channel error) and schedules a **retransmission**. 5. **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) on Failure** - On every retransmission attempt, the contention window is **doubled** (up to a maximum $CW_{max}$). - The backoff counter range evolves as: $CW = 2^i \times CW_{min} - 1$ where $i$ is the retry count. 6. **Optional RTS/CTS Handshake (Optional 4-way mechanism)** - Before data transmission, the sender transmits a **Request-To-Send (RTS)** mini-frame. - The receiver replies with **Clear-To-Send (CTS)** after a SIFS. - All other stations hearing either RTS or CTS update their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** — a virtual carrier sense timer — and remain silent for the duration of the upcoming transmission. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step - **Why sense the carrier?** Because radio is a shared medium — you must check if someone else is talking. - **Why wait for DIFS, not transmit immediately on idle?** DIFS gives **priority** to ACK, CTS, and other control frames which use the shorter **SIFS**. This avoids a race for the channel. - **Why random backoff?** Because all stations that heard the channel go idle will sense the silence at the *same* instant. Without randomness, they would all transmit in lockstep and collide every time. - **Why double the contention window on collision?** Because a collision is a statistical indicator of **high load**; widening the window spreads future attempts over a larger range, reducing the probability of re-collision. This is the **BEB (Binary Exponential Backoff)** algorithm. - **Why RTS/CTS?** To combat the **hidden station problem** — station A and station C may both be in range of station B but not of each other. RTS/CTS lets station B coordinate the channel for them. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **must-memorize** formula set for any CSMA/CA numerical in KTU exams. | **Quantity** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Description & Units** | |---|---|---| | Slot Time | $\sigma$ | Fundamental time unit. For IEEE 802.11b DSSS: **$\sigma = 20\ \mu s$** | | Inter Frame Spaces | SIFS $<$ PIFS $<$ DIFS $<$ EIFS | Priority ordering — shorter IFS = higher priority | | SIFS | $SIFS = 10\ \mu s$ (802.11b) | Used for ACK, CTS, fragmentation | | DIFS | $DIFS = SIFS + 2\sigma$ | Standard DCF deferral time | | Contention Window (after $i$ retries) | $CW_i = \min\!\left(2^i (CW_{min}+1) - 1,\ CW_{max}\right)$ | Standard BEB formula | | Backoff Counter | $k = \text{Random integer in } [0, CW_i]$ | Uniformly distributed | | Backoff Time | $T_{backoff} = k \times \sigma$ | In seconds or slot-times | | Total Deferral | $T_{defer} = DIFS + T_{backoff}$ | Before data frame | | CW min (802.11b) | $CW_{min} = 31$ | Initial backoff range $[0, 31]$ | | CW max (802.11b) | $CW_{max} = 1023$ | Saturation value | | Max retry limit | $m$ (e.g., 6 or 7) | Beyond this, frame is dropped | | Throughput (Saturation) | $S = \dfrac{P_s \cdot E[L]}{(1-P_{tr})\sigma + P_{tr} P_s T_s + P_{tr}(1-P_s) T_c}$ | Bianchi's saturation model | | Frame Transmit Time | $T_s = H + E[L] + SIFS + \delta + ACK + DIFS + \delta$ | Successful slot time | | Collision/Error Time | $T_c = H + E[L] + DIFS + \delta$ (approx.) | Wasted slot time | | Propagation Delay | $\delta$ | Tiny (order of $1\ \mu s$ indoor) | | RTS/CTS Reservation | $T_{RTS/CTS} = RTS + SIFS + \delta + CTS + SIFS + \delta$ | Reservation overhead | > [!WARNING] > In the table above, the value of $CW_i$ is an *integer* representing the *number of slots minus one*. For example, the **initial range** is $[0, 31]$, meaning the backoff counter $k$ takes 32 possible values, hence $CW_0 = 31$. Always write the formula with the explicit **$-1$** correction. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** — every home and enterprise wireless router uses CSMA/CA as the DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) baseline. - **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee, Thread, 6LoWPAN)** — used in IoT and low-power sensor networks with **slotted CSMA/CA** synchronized to a coordinator's beacon. - **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication** — IEEE 802.11p WAVE uses CSMA/CA with EDCA (Enhanced Distributed Channel Access) for prioritized safety messages. - **Underwater Acoustic Networks** — extremely long propagation delays make CSMA/CD impossible; CSMA/CA with handshake variants is the standard approach. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Evolution of the Contention Window under Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) **Problem Setup:** A station starts with $CW_{min} = 31$ and uses BEB. After each unsuccessful transmission (timeout without ACK), the contention window is doubled. Find the backoff range after $i$ retries. **Standard Formula (KFU):** $$ CW_i = \min\!\left(2^{i}\,(CW_{min}+1) - 1,\ CW_{max}\right) $$ **Exhaustive Step-by-Step Evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Retry } i = 0 \;&:\; CW_0 = 2^0 \cdot 32 - 1 = 32 - 1 = 31 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 1 \;&:\; CW_1 = 2^1 \cdot 32 - 1 = 64 - 1 = 63 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 2 \;&:\; CW_2 = 2^2 \cdot 32 - 1 = 128 - 1 = 127 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 3 \;&:\; CW_3 = 2^3 \cdot 32 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 4 \;&:\; CW_4 = 2^4 \cdot 32 - 1 = 512 - 1 = 511 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 5 \;&:\; CW_5 = 2^5 \cdot 32 - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023 \\[4pt] \text{Retry } i = 6 \;&:\; CW_6 = \min(2047,\ 1023) = 1023 \quad \text{(capped at } CW_{max}\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logical reasoning for the formula:** > - The +1 converts "$CW_{min}$" from the *number of slots minus one* notation into the *number of slot values* $N = CW_{min}+1$. > - Each retry **doubles the number of slot values**. > - Subtracting 1 returns to the standard "max backoff counter" convention. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Backoff Time Given a Contention Window **Problem Setup:** Suppose the current contention window is $CW = 31$. The slot time is $\sigma = 20\ \mu s$. The backoff counter $k$ is chosen uniformly from $\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, CW\}$.
