---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A

**(a) Hierarchical DNS Namespace & Server Types [7 marks]:**

The DNS namespace is an **inverted tree** with the **root label `.`** at the apex. Each node is a **domain**; each edge is a **label**. The path from any leaf to the root gives its **FQDN**.

**Hierarchy (top-down):**

1. **Root Zone `.`** — administered by **ICANN**; served by **13 logical root server clusters** (A–M), physically replicated via anycast at over 1,300+ locations.
2. **Top-Level Domain (TLD)** — categorized as:
   - **gTLD** (generic): `.com`, `.org`, `.net`, `.edu`
   - **ccTLD** (country-code): `.in`, `.uk`, `.jp`
3. **Second-Level Domain (SLD)** — registered by organizations, e.g., `ktu.edu.in`.
4. **Sub-Domains** — administrative partitions, e.g., `mail.ktu.edu.in`, `erp.ktu.edu.in`.
5. **Host Names** — leaf nodes mapped to A/AAAA records.

**DNS Server Types [four of the following required for full marks]:**

| Server Type | Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Root Name Server** | Returns referrals to TLD servers; 13 logical clusters globally. |
| **TLD Name Server** | Maintains records for all second-level domains under one TLD. |
| **Authoritative Name Server** | Holds the master/slave copy of a zone's data; returns the final answer. |
| **Caching / Recursive Resolver** | Performs recursive lookups on behalf of clients; caches answers with TTL. |
| **Stub Resolver** | Minimal client-side code that forwards queries to a recursive resolver. |
| **Forwarding Server** | Passes queries to an upstream resolver; maintains its own smaller cache. |

**Diagram (refer to §4.1 for the full Mermaid rendering):** The tree is rooted at `.`, branches into `.com / .in / .org / .edu`, further branches into registered names, and terminates at hostnames.

**Marking distribution:** Tree diagram + 3 levels labeled — 2 marks; Four server types — 4 marks; One example per level — 1 mark.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step DNS Resolution (Cache Miss) [7 marks]:**

Refer to the exhaustive 10-step derivation in **§3.1** of these notes for the full walkthrough. The examiner-facing summary is:

1. **Browser** invokes `getaddrinfo("www.google.com")`.
2. **Stub resolver** sends a recursive UDP query to the local resolver (e.g., 8.8.8.8).
3. **Resolver cache check** → miss.
4. **Root server query** (iterative) → returns referral to `.com` TLD server.
5. **TLD server query** (iterative) → returns referral to `google.com`'s authoritative NS.
6. **Authoritative server query** (iterative) → returns `A record: 142.250.195.68, TTL=300`.
7. **Resolver caches** all answers/referrals.
8. **Final answer** returned to stub resolver.
9. **Browser** initiates TCP three-way handshake to `142.250.195.68:80`.
10. **HTTP GET** is sent and the page loads.

**Recursive vs. Iterative Distinction:**

- The **stub resolver** issues **one recursive query** and waits.
- The **recursive resolver** issues **three iterative queries** (root → TLD → authoritative) on the client's behalf.
- A total of **4 UDP packets** traverse the network (1 in, 3 out, 1 back) for a cache miss.

**Marking distribution:** Step 1–3 — 2 marks; Steps 4–6 (iterative chain) — 3 marks; Distinction between query types — 1 mark; Final TCP/HTTP link — 1 mark.

---

#### Solution to Question B

**(a) DNS Message Format (RFC 1035) [7 marks]:**

Refer to the Mermaid diagram in **§4.3** and the byte-level breakdown in **§3.3**.

**Structure:** `Header (12 B)` → `Question` → `Answer` → `Authority` → `Additional`.

**Header Fields (12 bytes total):**

| Offset | Field | Size | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | **ID** | 16 bits | Transaction ID; matches response to query |
| 2 | **QR** | 1 bit | 0 = query, 1 = response |
| 2 | **Opcode** | 4 bits | 0 = standard query, 5 = update |
| 2 | **AA** | 1 bit | Authoritative Answer |
| 2 | **TC** | 1 bit | Truncated (use TCP) |
| 2 | **RD** | 1 bit | Recursion Desired |
| 2 | **RA** | 1 bit | Recursion Available |
| 2 | **Z** | 3 bits | Reserved (zero) |
| 2 | **RCODE** | 4 bits | 0 = NOERROR, 3 = NXDOMAIN |
| 4 | **QDCOUNT** | 16 bits | Number of questions |
| 6 | **ANCOUNT** | 16 bits | Number of answer RRs |
| 8 | **NSCOUNT** | 16 bits | Number of authority RRs |
| 10 | **ARCOUNT** | 16 bits | Number of additional RRs |

**Question Section:** `QNAME` (length-prefixed labels, e.g., `\003 w w w \006 g o o g l e \003 c o m \000`) + `QTYPE` (2 B) + `QCLASS` (2 B, always `IN` = 1 for Internet).

**Answer / Authority / Additional Sections:** Each is a sequence of **Resource Records** with the format: `NAME (with compression) \vert TYPE (2B) \vert CLASS (2B) \vert TTL (4B) \vert RDLENGTH (2B) \vert RDATA`.

**Name Compression:** RFC 1035 allows a label to be replaced by a 2-byte pointer `0xC0 xx` pointing to an earlier occurrence in the same message, reducing packet size by up to 70%.

**Marking distribution:** Header field table — 3 marks; Question section — 1 mark; Answer/Authority/Additional explanation — 2 marks; Name compression mention — 1 mark.

---

**(b)(i) Resource Records in a Zone File [3.5 marks]:**

```
$TTL 86400
@   IN  SOA  ns1.ktu.edu.in. admin.ktu.edu.in. (
            2024010101  ; Serial
            3600        ; Refresh
            1800        ; Retry
            1209600     ; Expire
            86400 )     ; Minimum TTL
@   IN  NS   ns1.ktu.edu.in.
@   IN  NS   ns2.ktu.edu.in.
@   IN  MX   10 mail.ktu.edu.in.
www IN  CNAME  server1.ktu.edu.in.
```

- **SOA** — defines zone metadata; the very first record of any zone.
- **NS** — declares authoritative name servers for the zone.
- **MX** — directs email to `mail.ktu.edu.in` (priority 10).
- **CNAME** — `www` is an alias for `server1.ktu.edu.in`; useful for hosting migrations.

**Marking distribution:** SOA + NS — 1.5 marks; MX — 1 mark; CNAME — 1 mark.

---

**(b)(ii) Reverse DNS Lookup [3.5 marks]:**

**Definition:** Reverse DNS (rDNS) maps an **IP address back to a hostname**, the inverse of the normal A/AAAA record lookup.

**Mechanism:** The IPv4 address `a.b.c.d` is **reversed** and appended to the special domain `.in-addr.arpa` (for IPv4) or `.ip6.arpa` (for IPv6). The resulting PTR query is:

$$
\text{PTR query for } 45.197.24.103.\text{in-addr.arpa} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{PTR record} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ktu.edu.in.}
$$

**Delegation:** IP block owners (e.g., APNIC for APAC) delegate the corresponding `.in-addr.arpa` zone to the organization that owns the address block. The organization publishes PTR records.

**Anti-Spam Use:** Mail servers (e.g., Gmail, Yahoo) **reject** incoming SMTP connections from IPs that have **no valid PTR record** matching their claimed HELO/EHLO hostname. This is a baseline anti-spam heuristic (RFC 5782). Other consumers of rDNS include:
- **Logging systems** (resolving attacker IPs in security incident reports).
- **SpamAssassin / Postfix** (PTR-based reputation scoring).
- **Troubleshooting tools** (verifying the human-readable identity of a server).

**Marking distribution:** Definition + `.in-addr.arpa` mechanism — 1.5 marks; Delegation chain — 0.5 mark; Anti-spam use case — 1.5 marks.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on DNS Questions (Based on Past Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing authoritative and recursive servers.** A server is "authoritative" only for zones it holds master data for; "recursive" describes a behavior, not a hardware box. Most ISP resolvers are **caching/recursive but non-authoritative**.
> 2. **Forgetting the port number (53).** Examiners often deduct 0.5–1 mark for omitting the UDP/TCP port.
> 3. **Mixing up the query direction.** Recursive queries flow **client → resolver**; iterative queries flow **resolver → root/TLD/auth**. Drawing the arrows backwards is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Omitting TTL and caching.** Whenever you write the resolution steps, **always state that the resolver caches the answer with a TTL** — it is a free 1-mark addition.
> 5. **Treating DNS as a "transport-layer" protocol.** DNS is **application layer**; it uses UDP/TCP as a transport, but the *service* it provides is application-layer directory lookup.
> 6. **Skipping the SOA/NS distinction.** NS records delegate; SOA records carry zone metadata. Many students use them interchangeably.
> 7. **Forgetting DNSSEC / security context.** If a 14-mark question asks for "challenges of DNS," mentioning **DNS cache poisoning, DNS spoofing, and DNSSEC** is highly valued by examiners.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DNS — One-Page Board Revision Sheet:**

- **DNS** = hierarchical, distributed naming service; **RFC 1034/1035**; **Application Layer**.
- **Default Port = 53** (UDP primarily; TCP for large responses, AXFR, DNSSEC).
- **Header = 12 bytes** (fixed); ID, Flags, QDCOUNT, ANCOUNT, NSCOUNT, ARCOUNT.
- **13 logical root servers** (A–M); ~1,300+ anycast instances worldwide.
- **Recursive query** = server does all the work (client → resolver).
- **Iterative query** = server returns best info / referral (resolver → root/TLD/auth).
- **Cache miss** = 4 round trips; **Cache hit** = 1 round trip.
- **FQDN** ends with a dot (`.`); max length 253 octets; max label 63 octets.
- **Resource Records to memorize:** A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, SOA, TXT, SRV.
- **MX priority** = lower number = higher preference (10 beats 20).
- **PTR queries** use `.in-addr.arpa` (IPv4) and `.ip6.arpa` (IPv6).
- **Zone transfer:** AXFR (full) / IXFR (incremental) — TCP, port 53.
- **Mean latency formula:** $T_{avg} = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{remote}$.
- **DNSSEC** = adds cryptographic authentication (chain of trust from root).
- **Public resolvers:** Google 8.8.8.8 / 8.8.4.4, Cloudflare 1.1.1.1, Quad9 9.9.9.9, OpenDNS 208.67.222.222.
- **Security threats:** cache poisoning, DNS spoofing, DDoS amplification (Smurf-style) — mitigated by **DNSSEC, rate-limiting, and anycast**.
- **Caching time = TTL** of the record; honor it strictly to maintain consistency.
- **Authoritative server** = has the zone file (master or slave); **Recursive resolver** = does not own the zone but resolves on behalf of clients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DHCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DHCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol)** is an application-layer client–server network management protocol standardized in **RFC 2131** (and updated by **RFC 2132**, **RFC 3315** for IPv6) that **dynamically assigns IP addresses** and disseminates other essential network configuration parameters—such as the **subnet mask**, **default gateway**, **DNS server addresses**, and **lease duration**—to IP-enabled devices in an automated, centralized, and scalable manner. DHCP operates directly over the **User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** using the well-known **port 67** on the server side and **port 68** on the client side, making it a connectionless, lightweight, and broadcast-friendly configuration mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU PECCT751 Module 4**
> DHCP is treated as a core **application-layer service** that showcases how TCP/IP hosts obtain their identity (IP address) and bootstrap configuration. KTU questions frequently test the *4-message handshake* (DORA), the *lease lifecycle*, and the *DHCP message format*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Check-In Desk"

Imagine you arrive at a large hotel where you have no pre-booked room (a **DHCP client** with no IP address). The hotel's front-desk manager (the **DHCP server**) maintains a pool of available rooms (the **IP address pool**). Here is how your stay unfolds:

1. You walk into the lobby and **shout your request** to the front desk (**DHCP DISCOVER** broadcast).
2. The front desk responds with an **offer of a specific room** for a defined period (**DHCP OFFER**).
3. You **formally accept** the offered room by signing the register (**DHCP REQUEST**).
4. The front desk **confirms the allocation** and hands you the key card (**DHCP ACK**), and you are officially checked in for the **lease duration**.

If you wish to extend your stay past half the original time, you ask politely (**DHCP REQUEST — renewal at T1**). If the front desk is busy, you escalate to the assistant manager at 7/8ths of the lease (**DHCP REQUEST — rebinding at T2**). If everything fails, you **return the key** (**lease expiration**) and the room returns to the pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> DHCP eliminates the manual, error-prone process of statically assigning IP addresses. In enterprise networks, an **ISP**, a **campus LAN**, or a **Wi-Fi router** at home uses DHCP to serve hundreds or thousands of clients with zero manual intervention—**scaling to IoT** and **BYOD** environments effortlessly.

## 1.3 Why DHCP is Needed — The Manual Configuration Problem

Without DHCP, every device would need an administrator to manually type in:
- A unique **IPv4/IPv6 address**
- The correct **subnet mask**
- The **default gateway** IP
- The **DNS resolver** addresses

This is the dreaded **"static IP configuration"**—labor-intensive, prone to **duplicate IP conflicts**, and unscalable beyond a handful of devices. DHCP solves all of this through automation.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Cheat-Sheet

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **DHCP Server** | Host that holds the IP address pool and leases addresses to clients |
| **DHCP Client** | Any device requesting configuration (PC, phone, IoT) |
| **DHCP Relay Agent** | Router/host that forwards DHCP broadcasts across subnets as unicasts |
| **Lease** | The finite duration for which an IP address is assigned to a client |
| **Scope / Pool** | The range of IP addresses the server is allowed to assign |
| **Reservation** | A permanent lease tied to a client's MAC address |
| **T1 Timer** | Time at which the client attempts first renewal (typically **50% of lease time**) |
| **T2 Timer** | Time at which the client rebinds to any available server (**87.5% of lease time**) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DHCP Architecture — The Three Logical Entities

DHCP is built around **three logical roles** that may reside on the same physical machine or on completely separate ones:

1. **DHCP Server** — Maintains the address pool and configuration database. Listens on **UDP port 67**.
2. **DHCP Client** — Initiates the request and listens on **UDP port 68**. Most modern operating systems ship with a built-in DHCP client.
3. **DHCP Relay Agent** (RFC 951 / 1542) — A router or layer-3 device that **forwards DHCP broadcast packets across subnet boundaries** as IP unicasts to a known DHCP server, since broadcasts do not traverse routers by default.

## 2.2 The DHCP Lease Lifecycle — A State Machine

The DHCP lease moves through a **finite-state machine**. KTU frequently asks students to label the states and the timer values that drive state transitions.

| State | Description | Trigger / Timer |
|---|---|---|
| **INIT** | Client has no IP and is initializing | Power-on / NIC up |
| **SELECTING** | Client waiting for OFFER messages from servers | DHCPDISCOVER sent |
| **REQUESTING** | Client has chosen an offer and is sending REQUEST | DHCPOFFER received |
| **BOUND** | Client owns the lease and may use the IP normally | DHCPACK received |
| **RENEWING** | Client attempts unicast renewal with originating server | **T1 = 0.5 × lease\_time** |
| **REBINDING** | Client broadcasts renewal to any server | **T2 = 0.875 × lease\_time** |
| **INIT-REBOOT** | Client restarts and tries to verify its last lease | Local reboot |
| **DECLINE** | Client detected IP conflict (e.g., via ARP) | ARP probe fails |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following numerical relationships and standard port values are **exam-favorite** facts. Memorize them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{T1 (first renewal)} &= 0.5 \times \text{Lease Duration} \\
\text{T2 (rebinding)} &= 0.875 \times \text{Lease Duration} \\
\text{Lease Duration Range} &= 1 \text{ second} \;\text{to}\; \infty \\
\text{Default Home Router Lease} &= 24 \text{ hours} \\
\text{UDP Server Port} &= 67 \\
\text{UDP Client Port} &= 68 \\
\text{All-zeros Client Source IP} &= 0.0.0.0 \\
\text{Broadcast Destination IP} &= 255.255.255.255 \\
\text{IP Header TTL (client broadcast)} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Magic Cookie** in DHCP options field = decimal **99.130.83.99** (or hex **0x63825363**). This is a fixed 4-byte value that separates the options field from any padding.

**Chaddr** field holds the client's **MAC (hardware) address**, which is the only stable identifier the server sees in the very first broadcast.

## 2.4 DHCP vs. BOOTP — The Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> DHCP is a **superset of BOOTP** (Bootstrap Protocol, RFC 951). The two are interoperable: a DHCP client can request configuration from a BOOTP server and vice-versa. However, DHCP adds:
> - Automatic allocation (no manual entry per client)
> - Lease-based temporary assignment
> - Full vendor-class option support
> - Dynamic DNS updates (RFC 4702)

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Each Operational Step

| Step | Why it happens |
|---|---|
| Client broadcasts DISCOVER | Client has no IP → cannot unicast → must broadcast to **0.0.0.0 → 255.255.255.255** |
| Server offers IP from pool | Multiple servers may exist; client must be able to **choose** |
| Client broadcasts REQUEST (not unicast) | Other clients must also hear it and **cancel their selections** |
| Server replies with ACK | Confirms binding; client transitions to **BOUND** state |
| Client renews at T1 | Proactive renewal prevents service interruption at lease expiry |
| Client rebinds at T2 | If original server is down, **any DHCP server** may extend the lease |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise LANs**: A single pair of redundant DHCP servers can serve **tens of thousands** of corporate endpoints.
- **ISP Broadband**: Cable and DSL ISPs issue dynamic IPs from a shared pool, drastically reducing IPv4 wastage.
- **Wi-Fi Access Points**: Home routers embed a DHCP server; guests get temporary IPs the moment they connect.
- **Container Orchestration**: Modern data centers use **DHCP relays** to integrate with overlay networks.
- **Disaster Recovery**: A laptop moved to a new subnet automatically receives a new configuration—no manual intervention.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The 4-Message DORA Handshake — Exhaustive Walk-Through

The **D**iscover / **O**ffer / **R**equest / **A**cknowledgment exchange is the **central exam topic** for DHCP. Below is the complete, step-by-step trace.

### Step 1: DHCPDISCOVER (Client → Broadcast)

The freshly booted client has **no IP address**. It constructs a DHCP message and **broadcasts** it on the local subnet.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source IP} &= 0.0.0.0 \\
\text{Destination IP} &= 255.255.255.255 \\
\text{Source UDP Port} &= 68 \\
\text{Destination UDP Port} &= 67 \\
\text{Transaction ID (xid)} &= \text{random 32-bit nonce} \\
\text{Message Type Option} &= 1 \text{ (DHCPDISCOVER)} \\
\text{Client MAC (chaddr)} &= \text{device hardware address} \\
\text{GIADDR} &= 0 \;(\text{no relay yet})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Because the client has no IP, the source IP must be **0.0.0.0**. Because it doesn't know the server's IP either, the destination is the **limited broadcast address 255.255.255.255**. The router must not forward this packet; therefore the IP header **TTL is set to 1**.

### Step 2: DHCPOFFER (Server → Client)

Each DHCP server on the subnet (or reachable via relay) that has a free lease responds with an OFFER.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source IP} &= \text{Server IP} \\
\text{Destination IP} &= 255.255.255.255 \\
\text{Your IP (yiaddr)} &= 192.168.1.42 \;(\text{offered address}) \\
\text{Server IP (siaddr)} &= 192.168.1.1 \\
\text{Lease Time option} &= 86400 \text{ seconds} \\
\text{Subnet Mask option (1)} &= 255.255.255.0 \\
\text{Router option (3)} &= 192.168.1.1 \\
\text{DNS option (6)} &= 8.8.8.8, \;8.8.4.4 \\
\text{Message Type Option} &= 2 \text{ (DHCPOFFER)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The server picks an unused address from its pool (often verified via ICMP echo or ARP probing) and reserves it. The destination is still broadcast because the client still has no IP.

### Step 3: DHCPREQUEST (Client → Broadcast)

The client selects **one** OFFER (typically the first received) and broadcasts a REQUEST. *Crucial*: the REQUEST is broadcast so that **all other OFFER-ing servers hear it and withdraw their offers**, returning those addresses to the pool.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source IP} &= 0.0.0.0 \\
\text{Destination IP} &= 255.255.255.255 \\
\text{Server Identifier option (54)} &= 192.168.1.1 \\
\text{Requested IP option (50)} &= 192.168.1.42 \\
\text{Message Type Option} &= 3 \text{ (DHCPREQUEST)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The `Server Identifier` option tells every other server "I picked THIS one, not you." The `Requested IP` option asks the chosen server to confirm the previously offered IP.

### Step 4: DHCPACK (Server → Client)

The chosen server commits the binding and sends a final acknowledgment. The client is now in the **BOUND** state and may use the IP.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source IP} &= 192.168.1.1 \\
\text{Destination IP} &= 255.255.255.255 \\
\text{Your IP (yiaddr)} &= 192.168.1.42 \\
\text{Lease Time} &= 86400 \; \text{seconds} \\
\text{Message Type Option} &= 5 \text{ (DHCPACK)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Upon receiving the ACK, the client performs a **gratuitous ARP** to ensure no other device on the LAN uses the same IP. If a conflict is detected, the client sends a **DHCPDECLINE** and retries the whole process.

## 3.2 DHCP Message Format — Field-by-Field Analysis

The DHCP message rides **inside a UDP datagram** which is **inside an IP datagram**. The DHCP payload is **316 bytes fixed** plus a variable-length options field up to **312 bytes** (totaling a 576-byte minimum IP datagram per RFC 791).

| Offset | Field | Size (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **op** | 1 | 1 = BOOTREQUEST, 2 = BOOTREPLY |
| 1 | **htype** | 1 | Hardware address type (1 = Ethernet) |
| 2 | **hlen** | 1 | Hardware address length (6 for MAC) |
| 3 | **hops** | 1 | Incremented by relay agents |
| 4 | **xid** | 4 | Transaction ID (matches DISCOVER) |
| 8 | **secs** | 2 | Seconds elapsed since client began |
| 10 | **flags** | 2 | Bit 15 = broadcast flag |
| 12 | **ciaddr** | 4 | Client IP (only valid in BOUND/RENEWING) |
| 16 | **yiaddr** | 4 | "Your" IP — what the server is offering/assigning |
| 20 | **siaddr** | 4 | Next server IP (e.g., TFTP for PXE boot) |
| 24 | **giaddr** | 4 | Relay agent IP |
| 28 | **chaddr** | 16 | Client hardware (MAC) address |
| 34 | **sname** | 64 | Optional server hostname |
| 98 | **file** | 128 | Boot filename |
| 226 | **options** | variable | Starts with magic cookie **0x63825363** |

> [!NOTE]
> The `chaddr` field is **16 bytes** wide even though Ethernet MACs are only 6 bytes—the extra room accommodates other link-layer addresses (e.g., Token Ring or IEEE 802.15.4).

## 3.3 Lease Renewal — Mathematical Derivation

Suppose the server grants a lease of duration $L$ seconds. The renewal timers are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= \frac{L}{2} = 0.5L \\
T_2 &= \frac{7L}{8} = 0.875L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

A DHCP server issues a lease of $L = 8 \; \text{hours}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \text{ seconds} \\
T_1 &= 0.5 \times 28800 = 14400 \text{ seconds} = 4 \text{ hours} \\
T_2 &= 0.875 \times 28800 = 25200 \text{ seconds} = 7 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- At **t = 4 hours**, the client sends a unicast DHCPREQUEST directly to the original server.
- If no response, at **t = 7 hours**, the client broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST to **any** DHCP server.
- If still no response, at **t = 8 hours** the lease **expires**; the client must release the IP and return to INIT state.

## 3.4 DHCP Relay Agent — Across-Subnet Bootstrapping

When a client on **Subnet A** (say 192.168.1.0/24) boots, it cannot reach a DHCP server on **Subnet B** (192.168.2.0/24) because broadcasts are not forwarded. The **DHCP relay agent** running on the local router fixes this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} &\; \text{Client broadcasts DISCOVER} \\
\text{Step 2:} &\; \text{Local router receives it, sets } giaddr = \text{its own IP} \\
&\; \text{and unicast-forwards the packet to the remote DHCP server} \\
\text{Step 3:} &\; \text{Server unicasts OFFER back to the router's IP} \\
\text{Step 4:} &\; \text{Router broadcasts OFFER onto Subnet A}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is called **BOOTP Relay** or **DHCP Relay** and is essential for centralized DHCP deployment in enterprise networks.

## 3.5 Summary Table of All DHCP Message Types (RFC 2131)

| Code | Message | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | DHCPDISCOVER | Client → Server |
| 2 | DHCPOFFER | Server → Client |
| 3 | DHCPREQUEST | Client → Server |
| 4 | DHCPDECLINE | Client → Server |
| 5 | DHCPACK | Server → Client |
| 6 | DHCPNAK | Server → Client |
| 7 | DHCPRELEASE | Client → Server |
| 8 | DHCPINFORM | Client → Server |

**DHCPDECLINE (4)** — client detected IP conflict.
**DHCPNAK (6)** — server rejects the REQUEST (e.g., lease expired or bad address).
**DHCPRELEASE (7)** — client gracefully returns the IP.
**DHCPINFORM (8)** — client already has an IP (manually configured) and only needs other parameters.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The DORA Handshake — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant ClientA as DHCP Client
    participant ServerA as DHCP Server
    participant NetworkA as Local Subnet Broadcast Domain

    ClientA->>NetworkA: DHCPDISCOVER (src=0.0.0.0, dst=255.255.255.255)
    Note over ClientA: State: INIT to SELECTING
    NetworkA->>ServerA: DISCOVER reaches all servers
    ServerA-->>NetworkA: DHCPOFFER (yiaddr=192.168.1.42, lease=86400s)
    Note over ClientA: Receives one or more offers
    ClientA->>NetworkA: DHCPREQUEST (server-id=192.168.1.1, requested=192.168.1.42)
    Note over ServerA: Other servers withdraw offers
    ServerA-->>NetworkA: DHCPACK (yiaddr=192.168.1.42, lease confirmed)
    Note over ClientA: State: BOUND, performs gratuitous ARP
```

## 4.2 DHCP Message Format — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IP Header - 20 bytes] --> B[UDP Header - 8 bytes src 68 dst 67]
    B --> C[OP - 1 byte]
    C --> D[HTYPE - 1 byte]
    D --> E[HLEN - 1 byte]
    E --> F[HOPS - 1 byte]
    F --> G[XID - 4 bytes]
    G --> H[SECS - 2 bytes]
    H --> I[FLAGS - 2 bytes]
    I --> J[CIADDR - 4 bytes]
    J --> K[YIADDR - 4 bytes]
    K --> L[SIADDR - 4 bytes]
    L --> M[GIADDR - 4 bytes]
    M --> N[CHADDR - 16 bytes]
    N --> O[SNAME - 64 bytes]
    O --> P[FILE - 128 bytes]
    P --> Q[MAGIC COOKIE - 4 bytes 0x63825363]
    Q --> R[OPTIONS - variable up to 312 bytes]
```

## 4.3 Lease State Machine — Renewal Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> SELECTING : DHCPDISCOVER sent
    SELECTING --> REQUESTING : DHCPOFFER received
    REQUESTING --> BOUND : DHCPACK received
    REQUESTING --> INIT : DHCPNAK received
    BOUND --> RENEWING : Timer T1 = 0.5L elapsed
    RENEWING --> BOUND : DHCPACK from original server
    RENEWING --> REBINDING : Timer T2 = 0.875L elapsed
    REBINDING --> BOUND : DHCPACK from any server
    REBINDING --> INIT : Lease L expires
    BOUND --> INIT : DHCPRELEASE sent
```

## 4.4 DHCP Relay Agent — Cross-Subnet Operation

```mermaid
graph LR
    SubA[Subnet A - 192.168.1.0/24] -->|DHCPDISCOVER broadcast| RouterX[Router - DHCP Relay Agent]
    RouterX -->|Unicast DISCOVER with giaddr set| SubB[Subnet B - 192.168.2.0/24]
    SubB --> ServerB[Centralized DHCP Server 192.168.2.10]
    ServerB -->|Unicast OFFER back to giaddr| RouterX
    RouterX -->|Broadcast OFFER on Subnet A| SubA
    SubA -->|Unicast DHCPREQUEST| RouterX
    RouterX -->|Unicast REQUEST| ServerB
    ServerB -->|Unicast DHCPACK| RouterX
    RouterX -->|Broadcast ACK| SubA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any four advantages of using DHCP over manual (static) IP configuration.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Automation**: IP addresses are assigned automatically, eliminating manual configuration errors.
2. **Centralized Management**: A single DHCP server can manage the entire network, simplifying administration.
3. **Scalability**: Easily supports networks with thousands of clients, including transient devices (guests, IoT).
4. **Avoidance of IP Conflicts**: The server ensures each leased address is unique, preventing duplicate-IP problems.
5. **Efficient Address Utilization**: Addresses can be reclaimed from inactive clients (lease expiry), reducing IPv4 wastage.
6. **Mobility Support**: Devices moving between subnets automatically obtain new valid configurations.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of a DHCP relay agent. Why is it required?**

**Model Answer:**

A **DHCP relay agent** is a router or layer-3 device that **forwards DHCP broadcast packets across subnet boundaries** as IP unicast packets to a remote DHCP server. It is required because:

- DHCP clients initially use IP address **0.0.0.0** and broadcast to **255.255.255.255**.
- IP routers **do not forward broadcast packets** by default, isolating the client's DISCOVER within its local subnet.
- By setting the **giaddr** field to its own IP and unicast-forwarding the packet, the relay agent enables a centralized DHCP server in a different subnet to serve clients.

This eliminates the need to deploy a DHCP server in every subnet, reducing cost and simplifying administration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Don't confuse yiaddr with ciaddr.** `yiaddr` is what the server *offers/assigns* to the client; `ciaddr` is the IP the client *already has* (only valid in BOUND or RENEWING state).
> - **Don't write "DHCP uses TCP."** DHCP is **UDP-based** (ports 67/68). Examiners deduct marks for this.
> - **Don't say the DISCOVER is unicasted.** The very first packet from a client with no IP **must be broadcast** (255.255.255.255) with source 0.0.0.0.
> - **T1 and T2 are 50% and 87.5%** of the lease time, not "half" and "three-quarters." Exact decimals matter.
> - The **magic cookie 0x63825363** is a frequent trick question — know it.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### (a) Explain the DHCP message format in detail with a neat diagram. List all the message types defined by RFC 2131. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The DHCP message is encapsulated inside a **UDP datagram (ports 67/68)**, which in turn rides inside an **IP datagram**. The DHCP payload has a fixed 236-byte header plus a variable-length options field.

**Key Fields:**

| Field | Bytes | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `op` | 1 | 1 = BOOTREQUEST (client), 2 = BOOTREPLY (server) |
| `htype` | 1 | Hardware type (1 for Ethernet) |
| `hlen` | 1 | Hardware address length (6 for 48-bit MAC) |
| `hops` | 1 | Set to 0 by client; incremented by relays |
| `xid` | 4 | Random transaction ID — matches DISCOVER → ACK |
| `secs` | 2 | Seconds elapsed since client began boot process |
| `flags` | 2 | Bit 15 = broadcast flag; others reserved |
| `ciaddr` | 4 | Client's current IP (only in BOUND/RENEWING) |
| `yiaddr` | 4 | "Your" IP — assigned by server |
| `siaddr` | 4 | IP of next server (e.g., TFTP for PXE) |
| `giaddr` | 4 | Relay agent IP |
| `chaddr` | 16 | Client MAC address |
| `sname` | 64 | Server hostname (optional) |
| `file` | 128 | Boot filename |
| `options` | ≥4 | Begins with magic cookie **0x63825363** |

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

**Message Types (RFC 2131):**

1. DHCPDISCOVER
2. DHCPOFFER
3. DHCPREQUEST
4. DHCPDECLINE
5. DHCPACK
6. DHCPNAK
7. DHCPRELEASE
8. DHCPINFORM

---

#### (b) Describe the complete lease lifecycle of a DHCP client with reference to the T1 and T2 timers. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A DHCP lease progresses through a well-defined sequence of states:

1. **INIT → SELECTING**: On boot, the client broadcasts a DHCPDISCOVER.
2. **SELECTING → REQUESTING**: Client chooses an offer and broadcasts DHCPREQUEST.
3. **REQUESTING → BOUND**: Server replies with DHCPACK; client now owns the IP for the lease duration.
4. **BOUND → RENEWING (at T1)**: After **T1 = 0.5 × lease\_time** seconds, the client unicasts a DHCPREQUEST to the *original* server to extend the lease.
5. **RENEWING → BOUND (successful)**: Server replies with DHCPACK, lease is extended.
6. **RENEWING → REBINDING (at T2)**: If no ACK, at **T2 = 0.875 × lease\_time** the client broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST to *any* server.
7. **REBINDING → BOUND**: Any server may acknowledge.
8. **REBINDING → INIT (failure)**: If lease expires with no ACK, the client must release the IP and restart the entire process.

**Numerical Example:** For a 24-hour lease,
- T1 = 12 hours, T2 = 21 hours, expiry = 24 hours.

**Real-world note:** This hierarchical renewal (unicast → broadcast) minimizes broadcast traffic on the LAN, scaling DHCP to large enterprise networks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the DORA process in DHCP with a sequence diagram. Why must the DHCPREQUEST be broadcast and not unicast? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **DORA** acronym stands for **Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge** — the four-step handshake used when a client first joins a network.

| Step | Message | Source → Destination | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DHCPDISCOVER | Client → Broadcast (255.255.255.255) | Locate any DHCP server |
| 2 | DHCPOFFER | Server → Client (broadcast) | Reserve an IP and offer it |
| 3 | DHCPREQUEST | Client → Broadcast | Formally request the chosen IP |
| 4 | DHCPACK | Server → Client (broadcast) | Confirm the binding |

**Sequence Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Why must the DHCPREQUEST be broadcast (not unicast)?**

The client sends the REQUEST as a **broadcast** for two critical reasons:

1. **Telling other servers to release their offers**: After the client picks one OFFER, all the *other* DHCP servers that had offered addresses need to know they were *not* chosen so they can return those addresses to their free pool. A unicast REQUEST would not reach them.
2. **The client still has no IP**: At this point, the client is still in the REQUESTING state and has not yet been bound to an IP. A unicast requires a routable source IP, which the client does not have yet.

Once the ACK arrives, the client enters the **BOUND** state and may use the IP for unicast communication.

---

#### (b) What is a DHCP relay agent? Explain how it enables a centralized DHCP server to serve multiple subnets. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **DHCP relay agent** is a router (or any IP host) configured to **forward DHCP broadcast packets across subnets** by encapsulating them in IP unicasts addressed to a specific remote DHCP server. It is formally defined in **RFC 951** and **RFC 1542**.

**Working — Step by Step:**

1. **Client on Subnet A** boots and broadcasts a DHCPDISCOVER (destination 255.255.255.255).
2. The **local router**, acting as the relay agent, receives this broadcast (giaddr field is currently 0).
3. The relay agent **sets the giaddr field to its own IP** (the IP facing Subnet A) and **unicast-forwards** the packet to the **centralized DHCP server** on Subnet B.
4. The DHCP server examines the giaddr, identifies the originating subnet, and **selects an IP from the appropriate scope/pool**.
5. The server **unicasts a DHCPOFFER** back to the relay agent's IP.
6. The relay agent **broadcasts the OFFER** on Subnet A so the client (which still has no IP) can receive it.
7. The same flow repeats for REQUEST and ACK.

**Advantages:**

- **Centralized Management**: One server handles all subnets.
- **Cost-Efficient**: No need to deploy a DHCP server on every subnet.
- **High Availability**: Easy to configure failover between multiple servers.
- **Security**: DHCP packets can be inspected/filtered at the relay agent.

This is the standard enterprise deployment model and is the reason every router in a corporate network can act as a DHCP relay.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — DHCP (KTU Module 4)**

- **Protocol Type**: Application-layer protocol, **UDP-based**, ports **67 (server)** and **68 (client)**.
- **RFC**: Defined in **RFC 2131** (IPv4) and **RFC 3315** (DHCPv6). Options in **RFC 2132**.
- **Address Family**: Primarily IPv4; for IPv6 use **DHCPv6** or **SLAAC**.
- **DORA Process**: **D**iscover → **O**ffer → **R**equest → **A**ck — the 4-message exchange.
- **Initial Packet Headers**: Client source IP = **0.0.0.0**, destination = **255.255.255.255**, TTL = **1**.
- **Key Fields**: `yiaddr` (assigned IP), `chaddr` (client MAC), `giaddr` (relay IP), `xid` (transaction ID), `op` (1=REQUEST, 2=REPLY).
- **Magic Cookie**: **0x63825363** (decimal 99.130.83.99) — marks the start of options.
- **T1 Timer**: **0.5 × lease time** — client unicast-renews with original server.
- **T2 Timer**: **0.875 × lease time** — client broadcast-rebinds to any server.
- **8 DHCP Message Types**: DISCOVER, OFFER, REQUEST, DECLINE, ACK, NAK, RELEASE, INFORM.
- **DHCP Relay Agent**: Forwards client broadcasts across subnets as unicasts; sets `giaddr`.
- **Conflict Detection**: Client performs **gratuitous ARP** after receiving ACK; on conflict, sends **DHCPDECLINE**.
- **Lease States**: INIT → SELECTING → REQUESTING → BOUND → (RENEWING → REBINDING) → INIT.
- **DHCP vs BOOTP**: DHCP is a **superset** of BOOTP — interoperable, but DHCP adds leases and full options.
- **PXE Boot**: Uses DHCP to deliver `siaddr` (TFTP server) and `file` (boot filename) — fundamental for diskless clients.
- **Default Home Lease**: Typically **24 hours (86400 seconds)**.
- **Common Pitfall**: Never write that DHCP uses TCP — it is **always UDP**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HTTP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HTTP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of HTTP

> [!NOTE]
> **HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer, request-response, stateless protocol standardized under **RFC 7230 – RFC 7235** (HTTP/1.1) and **RFC 9110** (HTTP semantics), used for transmitting hypermedia documents such as HTML, images, video, JSON, and XML over the Internet. It operates as the foundation of data communication for the **World Wide Web (WWW)** and relies on **TCP** at the transport layer (typically **port 80** for unencrypted HTTP and **port 443** for HTTPS).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, HTTP is the application-layer protocol that governs how web clients (browsers) and web servers exchange resources. It is a **text-based protocol** in versions 1.0 and 1.1 (binary framing in HTTP/2 and QUIC-based HTTP/3), where each interaction is initiated by the client through a well-defined *request message*, and concluded by the server with a *response message*.

### Key KTU Terminology

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Resource** | Any identifiable entity on the web (HTML page, image, JSON, video) |
| **URI / URL** | Uniform Resource Identifier / Locator — address of a resource |
| **Method** | The action verb (GET, POST, etc.) the client wishes to perform |
| **Stateless** | Each request is independent; server retains no client state between requests |
| **Proxy** | Intermediate entity forwarding HTTP messages |
| **User-Agent** | Software (browser) initiating the request |
| **Origin Server** | The definitive source of a resource |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Menu

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: HTTP is like ordering food at a restaurant.**

Imagine you walk into a restaurant (the **client**) and a waiter (**HTTP**) takes your order from the menu. The waiter does not know you personally, has no memory of what you ordered yesterday, and treats every new order independently — this is the **stateless** nature of HTTP. You specify the dish (the **URL**), the type of preparation (the **HTTP method**), and any special instructions (the **HTTP headers**). The kitchen (**web server**) prepares the dish and the waiter returns with the plate along with a printed bill that says either *"Order ready"* (status code **200 OK**) or *"Item unavailable"* (status code **404 Not Found**). The bill itself is the **HTTP response status line**.

This stateless, request-response, simple-text nature is what makes HTTP easy to debug, scalable across millions of servers, and the universal language of the web.

## 1.3 Why HTTP Matters in Computer Communication

HTTP is the **dominant application-layer protocol** in modern networks, accounting for the majority of internet traffic. It is:

- The protocol of REST APIs powering mobile apps, IoT, and microservices.
- The transport for nearly all web-based services (email clients, streaming, e-commerce).
- The basis for understanding **caching, cookies, security (HTTPS/TLS), and CDN** operations.
- A core topic in KTU's Module 4 covering the **Application Layer** of the TCP/IP stack.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember the layered placement: HTTP sits on top of TCP, which sits on top of IP. Examiners frequently test the relationship between HTTP and TCP, and how the **Three-Way Handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK)** underlies every HTTP connection.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HTTP Architecture

HTTP follows a classic **client-server model**:

1. **Client** (browser, mobile app, API caller) opens a TCP connection to the server.
2. Client formats and sends an **HTTP Request Message**.
3. Server processes the request, generates an **HTTP Response Message**.
4. Server returns the response and (in HTTP/1.1) keeps the connection open for reuse.
5. Connection is closed after timeout or explicitly by either side.

## 2.2 HTTP Connection Types

| Connection Type | Behavior | Default Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-persistent** | One TCP connection per object. Server closes after one response. | HTTP/1.0 |
| **Persistent** | Multiple objects transferred over one TCP connection. | HTTP/1.1 default |
| **Pipelined** | Multiple requests sent without waiting for responses. | Optional in HTTP/1.1 |
| **Multiplexed** | Many streams over a single TCP connection, binary framed. | HTTP/2 |

### Response Time Derivation (Non-Persistent HTTP)

The total time to fetch an HTML page with **N** referenced objects is:

$$
T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT + \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 \cdot RTT_{i}
$$

### Response Time Derivation (Persistent HTTP)

For persistent connections with pipelining:

$$
T_{total} = 2 \cdot RTT + \sum_{i=1}^{N} RTT_{i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of 2 represents one RTT for TCP connection establishment (three-way handshake) and one RTT for the HTTP request-response exchange. For persistent connections, the TCP handshake RTT is paid only once.

## 2.3 HTTP Methods (Request Verbs)

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent | Safe | Body Allowed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GET** | Retrieve a resource | Yes | Yes | No |
| **POST** | Submit data / create resource | No | No | Yes |
| **PUT** | Replace/Update resource at URI | Yes | No | Yes |
| **DELETE** | Remove resource | Yes | No | No |
| **HEAD** | Same as GET but no body | Yes | Yes | No |
| **OPTIONS** | Discover allowed methods | Yes | Yes | No |
| **PATCH** | Partial update | No | No | Yes |
| **TRACE** | Echo request for debugging | Yes | Yes | No |
| **CONNECT** | Establish tunnel (for HTTPS proxy) | No | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Idempotent** = executing the same method multiple times yields the same result on the server. **Safe** = the method does not modify server state. Examiners love testing these distinctions.

## 2.4 HTTP Status Codes — The Five Classes

| Class | Range | Meaning | KTU-Favorite Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Provisional response | 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request accepted | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action required | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified, 307 Temporary Redirect |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Client fault | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server fault | 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable |

## 2.5 HTTP Message Format

### HTTP Request Message

```
<Request Line>          (e.g., GET /index.html HTTP/1.1)
<Header Section>        (Host, User-Agent, Accept, ...)
<Blank Line (CRLF)>
<Optional Message Body>
```

### HTTP Response Message

```
<Status Line>           (e.g., HTTP/1.1 200 OK)
<Header Section>        (Content-Type, Content-Length, ...)
<Blank Line (CRLF)>
<Message Body>
```

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Non-persistent total time | $T_{np} = (2N+1) \cdot RTT$ | For *N* objects with constant RTT |
| 2 | Persistent total time | $T_{p} = (N+1) \cdot RTT$ | Pipelined persistent connection |
| 3 | Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Object Size}}{\text{Time per Object}}$ | Bytes per second |
| 4 | HTTP/2 Streams | Up to $\mathbf{1000^{+}}$ concurrent streams | Single TCP connection |
| 5 | HTTP/2 HPACK compression | Header size reduction up to $\mathbf{80\%-90\%}$ | Header compression |
| 6 | TLS Handshake RTTs | $2 \cdot RTT$ for full handshake | HTTPS connection setup |
| 7 | HTTP/3 (QUIC) RTTs | $1 \cdot RTT$ for 0-RTT | Faster connection setup |
| 8 | Cache hit ratio | $HR = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Total Requests}}$ | Web caching efficiency |
| 9 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | Influences TCP window |
| 10 | Date header format | `RFC 1123` (e.g., `Sun, 06 Nov 1994 08:49:37 GMT`) | HTTP date format |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web development & REST APIs** — HTTP is the lingua franca of modern distributed systems.
- **Microservices** — Services communicate via HTTP/REST or gRPC (over HTTP/2).
- **CDNs (Cloudflare, Akamai)** — Use HTTP caching, ETag, and Last-Modified headers to reduce origin load.
- **Edge computing & IoT** — Lightweight HTTP (e.g., CoAP adapts HTTP for constrained devices).
- **Security (WAFs, Proxies)** — Inspect HTTP headers for threat detection.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern CDNs like Cloudflare and AWS CloudFront reduce global HTTP latency to <50 ms by using HTTP/2 multiplexing, TLS 1.3 0-RTT, and edge caching — directly extending the principles in this module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Non-Persistent vs Persistent HTTP Response Time

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a KTU-favorite. Mastering it guarantees marks on any application-layer timing question.

### Step 1: Define Assumptions
- One HTML base file of negligible transmission time.
- $N$ referenced objects, each requiring its own transfer.
- One-way propagation delay approximated by $\dfrac{RTT}{2}$.
- TCP three-way handshake costs one $RTT$.
- HTTP request/response exchange for one object costs one $RTT$ (transmission time assumed negligible).

### Step 2: Non-Persistent HTTP Timing

For each of the $N$ objects in non-persistent mode:
- One $RTT$ for TCP connection establishment.
- One $RTT$ for HTTP request-response.

Thus per object: $2 \cdot RTT$. For $N$ objects plus the base HTML:

$$
T_{np} = 2 \cdot RTT_{\text{base}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(2 \cdot RTT_i\right)
$$

If we assume $RTT_i = RTT$ for all $i$ (LAN or uniform RTT):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{np} &= 2 \cdot RTT + N \cdot 2 \cdot RTT \\
T_{np} &= 2 \cdot RTT \cdot (N + 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Persistent HTTP Timing

For persistent connections, the TCP handshake is performed once for all $N+1$ objects, and HTTP request-response still costs one $RTT$ per object:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{p} &= 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{TCP}} + (N+1) \cdot RTT_{\text{HTTP}} \\
T_{p} &= RTT + (N+1) \cdot RTT \\
T_{p} &= (N + 2) \cdot RTT
\end{aligned}
$$

> This assumes pipelining (HTTP/1.1 default) — otherwise the constant is $1 + 2(N+1)$ RTTs for serial persistent.

### Step 4: Speed-up Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} \\
S &= \dfrac{2 \cdot (N+1) \cdot RTT}{(N+2) \cdot RTT} \\
S &= \dfrac{2(N+1)}{N+2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $N$, $S \to 2$, i.e., persistent HTTP halves the time. For small $N$, the speed-up is more dramatic.

### Step 5: Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> Suppose a webpage references $N = 10$ images and $RTT = 100$ ms. Compute the total time for non-persistent and persistent HTTP.

**Non-persistent:**

$$
T_{np} = 2 \cdot (10 + 1) \cdot 0.1 \text{ s} = 22 \cdot 0.1 = 2.2 \text{ s}
$$

**Persistent (pipelined):**

$$
T_{p} = (10 + 2) \cdot 0.1 = 1.2 \text{ s}
$$

> **Speed-up:** $\dfrac{2.2}{1.2} \approx 1.83 \times$ faster. **Savings:** 1.0 second.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conditional GET and Web Cache Coherence

A **conditional GET** uses `If-Modified-Since` or `If-None-Match` headers to ask the server: *"Send the object only if it has changed."*

The cache stores:
- $C_{cached}$: the cached object.
- $L_{cached}$: its Last-Modified date or ETag.
- $D_{current}$: the current date.

**Logical flow:**

$$
\text{If } D_{file} > L_{cached} \implies \text{Server returns 200 OK with new object.}
$$

$$
\text{If } D_{file} \leq L_{cached} \implies \text{Server returns 304 Not Modified, no body.}
$$

**Bandwidth saved per request:**

$$
\text{Savings} = \text{Object Size (bytes)}
$$

For a cache hit ratio $h$ over $R$ total requests:

$$
\text{Bytes saved} = h \cdot R \cdot \text{AvgObjSize}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput for HTTP/1.1 Pipelined vs HTTP/2 Multiplexed

For HTTP/1.1 pipelined, **Head-of-Line (HOL) blocking** at the TCP layer means a single lost packet stalls all subsequent streams:

$$
\text{Utilization}_{\text{HTTP/1.1}} \leq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{p}}
$$

where $p$ is the packet loss probability (Mathis formula approximation). For $p = 0.01$, utilization $\leq 10\times$ degraded.

HTTP/2 solves HOL blocking at the application layer using **binary framing** and **multiplexed streams**, recovering throughput:

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{HTTP/2}} = \dfrac{\text{Window Size}}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \text{Streams}_{\text{parallel}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — HTTP Version Comparison

| Feature | HTTP/1.0 | HTTP/1.1 | HTTP/2 | HTTP/3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Year | 1996 | 1997 (RFC 2068) → 2014 (RFC 7230) | 2015 (RFC 7540) | 2022 (RFC 9114) |
| Connection | Non-persistent default | Persistent default | Single multiplexed | QUIC (UDP-based) |
| Format | Text | Text | Binary framing | Binary over QUIC |
| Methods | GET, POST, HEAD | + PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, TRACE | All HTTP/1.1 | All HTTP/1.1 |
| Headers | Plain | Plain | HPACK compressed | QPACK compressed |
| Server Push | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| HOL Blocking | TCP | TCP | Reduced (app-level) | Eliminated (per stream) |
| Encryption | Optional | Optional | Not required (but recommended) | Mandatory (TLS 1.3) |
| Status | Legacy | Widely used | Dominant | Growing adoption |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — HTTPS Handshake Cost (TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3)

**TLS 1.2 (over TCP, used in HTTPS):**

$$
T_{\text{HTTPS-1.2}} = 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{TCP}} + 2 \cdot RTT_{\text{TLS}} + 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{HTTP}}
$$

For a $100$ ms RTT link:

$$
T_{\text{HTTPS-1.2}} = 100 + 200 + 100 = 400 \text{ ms}
$$

**TLS 1.3 (1-RTT handshake, used in HTTP/2 and HTTP/3):**

$$
T_{\text{HTTPS-1.3}} = 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{TCP/TLS}} + 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{HTTP}} = 200 \text{ ms}
$$

**TLS 1.3 with 0-RTT (QUIC / HTTP/3):**

$$
T_{\text{HTTPS-0RTT}} \approx 1 \cdot RTT_{\text{HTTP}} = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The migration from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 to HTTP/3 is fundamentally a race to reduce $RTT$ overhead. Each version halves the number of round-trips required before application data flows.

## 3.6 Detailed Walkthrough — Anatomy of an HTTP Transaction

### Step 1: DNS Resolution
Client resolves the host to an IP address via DNS (1 RTT typically).

### Step 2: TCP Three-Way Handshake
Client $\to$ Server: `SYN` (1 RTT half).
Server $\to$ Client: `SYN-ACK`.
Client $\to$ Server: `ACK` (1 RTT complete).

### Step 3: HTTP Request
Client sends:
```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive
```

### Step 4: HTTP Response
Server sends:
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1234
Date: Sun, 06 Nov 1994 08:49:37 GMT
Server: Apache/2.4

<HTML>...</HTML>
```

### Step 5: Connection Management
- HTTP/1.0: `Connection: close` terminates after this response.
- HTTP/1.1: `Connection: keep-alive` reuses for subsequent requests.

### Step 6: Resource Cleanup
If no further requests within the keep-alive timeout (typically **15–60 s**), TCP FIN closes the connection.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HTTP Request-Response Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Browser Initiates Request] --> B[DNS Resolution]
    B --> C[TCP Three-Way Handshake]
    C --> D[Client Sends HTTP Request Message]
    D --> E[Server Processes Request]
    E --> F{Request Valid?}
    F -- No --> G[Server Returns 4xx or 5xx Response]
    F -- Yes --> H[Server Generates 2xx Response with Resource]
    G --> I[Connection Closed or Kept Alive]
    H --> I
    I --> J{More Resources Needed?}
    J -- Yes --> D
    J -- No --> K[Close TCP Connection FIN]
    K --> L[End of HTTP Session]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#7B8A8B,stroke:#3A4A4A,color:#FFFFFF
    style F fill:#D0021B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style H fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style G fill:#BD1E1E,stroke:#7A0F0F,color:#FFFFFF
    style L fill:#9013FE,stroke:#5C0AB0,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 HTTP Version Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HTTP/0.9 1991 Raw HTML] --> B[HTTP/1.0 1996 RFC 1945]
    B --> C[HTTP/1.1 1997 RFC 2068]
    C --> D[HTTP/2 2015 RFC 7540 Binary Framing]
    D --> E[HTTP/3 2022 RFC 9114 QUIC over UDP]

    A1[Features GET Only Plain HTML] -.-> A
    B1[Features Headers GET POST HEAD Non Persistent] -.-> B
    C1[Features Persistent Connections Pipelining PUT DELETE] -.-> C
    D1[Features Multiplexing HPACK Server Push Binary] -.-> D
    E1[Features 0 RTT Handshake QUIC UDP Based Mandatory TLS] -.-> E

    style A fill:#BDC3C7,stroke:#7F8C8D,color:#000000
    style B fill:#7B8A8B,stroke:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF
    style C fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#9013FE,stroke:#5C0AB0,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 HTTP Status Code Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[HTTP Response Status Code] --> S1{First Digit}
    S1 -- 1xx --> N1[1xx Informational Provisional 100 Continue]
    S1 -- 2xx --> N2[2xx Success Request Accepted 200 OK 201 Created 204 No Content]
    S1 -- 3xx --> N3[3xx Redirection Further Action 301 Moved Permanently 304 Not Modified 307 Temporary Redirect]
    S1 -- 4xx --> N4[4xx Client Error 400 Bad Request 401 Unauthorized 403 Forbidden 404 Not Found]
    S1 -- 5xx --> N5[5xx Server Error 500 Internal 502 Bad Gateway 503 Unavailable]

    style Start fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style N1 fill:#7B8A8B,stroke:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF
    style N2 fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style N3 fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style N4 fill:#D0021B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style N5 fill:#BD1E1E,stroke:#7A0F0F,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.4 Non-Persistent vs Persistent Connection Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NONPERSISTENT[Non Persistent HTTP 1 0 Default]
        C1[Client] -->|SYN| S1[Server]
        S1 -->|SYN ACK| C1
        C1 -->|ACK| S1
        C1 -->|HTTP Request| S1
        S1 -->|Response Obj 1| C1
        S1 -->|FIN Close| C1
        C1 -.->|New TCP Connection for Obj 2| S1
    end

    subgraph PERSISTENT[Persistent HTTP 1 1 Default]
        C2[Client] -->|SYN| S2[Server]
        S2 -->|SYN ACK| C2
        C2 -->|ACK| S2
        C2 -->|HTTP Request 1| S2
        S2 -->|Response 1| C2
        C2 -->|HTTP Request 2| S2
        S2 -->|Response 2| C2
        C2 -->|HTTP Request N| S2
        S2 -->|Response N| C2
        S2 -->|FIN Idle Timeout| C2
    end

    style NONPERSISTENT fill:#FDEBD0,stroke:#E67E22
    style PERSISTENT fill:#D5F5E3,stroke:#27AE60
    style C1 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style C2 fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style S1 fill:#7B8A8B,stroke:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF
    style S2 fill:#7B8A8B,stroke:#34495E,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.5 HTTP Message Structure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ[HTTP Request Message]
    REQ --> RL[Request Line]
    REQ --> RH[Header Section]
    REQ --> BLANK[Blank Line CRLF]
    REQ --> RB[Optional Body]

    RL --> RL1[METHOD GET POST PUT DELETE etc]
    RL --> RL2[URI Path /index.html]
    RL --> RL3[VERSION HTTP/1.1]

    RH --> RH1[Host example.com]
    RH --> RH2[User Agent Browser]
    RH --> RH3[Accept Content Types]
    RH --> RH4[Cookie Session]
    RH --> RH5[Authorization Token]

    RB --> RB1[Form Data JSON XML Binary]

    RES[HTTP Response Message]
    RES --> SL[Status Line]
    RES --> SH[Header Section]
    RES --> BLANK2[Blank Line CRLF]
    RES --> MB[Message Body]

    SL --> SL1[VERSION HTTP/1.1]
    SL --> SL2[STATUS CODE 200 404 500]
    SL --> SL3[REASON PHRASE OK Not Found]

    SH --> SH1[Content Type]
    SH --> SH2[Content Length]
    SH --> SH3[Date]
    SH --> SH4[Server]
    SH --> SH5[Set Cookie]

    MB --> MB1[HTML JSON XML Image]

    style REQ fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style RES fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style BLANK fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
    style BLANK2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000
```

## 4.6 Caching and Conditional GET Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Needs Resource] --> B{Is Resource in Cache?}
    B -- No --> C[Client Sends GET Request to Server]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is Cache Fresh?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Serve from Cache]
    C --> F[Server Returns Response]
    F --> G[Client Stores in Cache and Uses]
    E --> H[Client Sends Conditional GET If Modified Since]
    H --> I{File Modified?}
    I -- No --> J[Server Returns 304 Not Modified]
    I -- Yes --> K[Server Returns 200 OK with New Resource]
    J --> L[Client Uses Cached Version]
    K --> G
    G --> M[Done]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#2C5F8D,color:#FFFFFF
    style B fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#F5A623,stroke:#C77A00,color:#FFFFFF
    style E fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style H fill:#9013FE,stroke:#5C0AB0,color:#FFFFFF
    style J fill:#417505,stroke:#2C5004,color:#FFFFFF
    style K fill:#D0021B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
```
