## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Google Doc of Trust"

Imagine a **shared Google Spreadsheet**:
- The spreadsheet is **publicly visible** to every participant in a group.
- Once a row of data is added, **no one can silently delete or modify** an old row.
- Every **10 minutes**, the spreadsheet is "frozen" into a *page* (a **block**) and a new page is started.
- Each new page **references the unique fingerprint (hash) of the previous page**, so any tampering would immediately break the chain.
- No single person owns it; thousands of computers worldwide keep an **identical copy**.

That is essentially how a **Blockchain** works — except it is cryptographically enforced, mathematically tamper-evident, and distributed across the globe.

---

## 1.3 Block — The Atomic Unit of Blockchain

A *Block* is a container data structure that bundles multiple transactions. In the Bitcoin protocol (the canonical reference for academic study), a block is roughly **1 MB** in size and contains:

```
Block #N
├── Block Header (80 bytes)
│   ├── Version Number      (4 bytes)
│   ├── Previous Block Hash (32 bytes)  ← SHA-256
│   ├── Merkle Root         (32 bytes)
│   ├── Timestamp           (4 bytes)
│   ├── Difficulty Target   (4 bytes)
│   └── Nonce               (4 bytes)
└── Block Body
    └── Transaction Counter + Transactions
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Hash Algorithm:** **SHA-256 (Secure Hash Algorithm – 256 bits)** is the de-facto standard used in Bitcoin and many KTU-curriculum blockchain models. Output length = **256 bits** (i.e., **32 bytes**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition

The blockchain ecosystem is best understood as a **four-layer architecture**:

### Layer 1 — Application Layer
End-user applications: wallets, smart contracts, DApps.

### Layer 2 — Contract / Logic Layer
Business rules and scripts (e.g., Ethereum's Solidity, Hyperledger's Chaincode).

### Layer 3 — Data & Consensus Layer ⭐ (Most Exam-Relevant)
- **Data Structure:** Linked list of blocks via hash pointers
- **Consensus Engine:** Proof of Work, Proof of Stake, PBFT, Raft

### Layer 4 — Network Layer
P2P gossip protocol (typically using **TCP port 8333** in Bitcoin), transaction and block propagation.

---

## 2.2 Cryptographic Foundations

### A. Hash Function Properties
A cryptographic hash function $H(x)$ must satisfy:

| Property | Mathematical Statement |
|----------|----------------------|
| Determinism | $H(x) = H(x)$ always |
| One-Way | Given $y = H(x)$, finding $x$ is computationally infeasible |
| Avalanche Effect | Flipping 1 bit in $x$ changes ~50% of bits in $H(x)$ |
| Collision Resistance | Finding $x_1 \neq x_2$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ is infeasible |
| Fixed Output | $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$ (for SHA-256) |

### B. Block Hash Computation
The hash of Block $N$ is computed as:

$$H_N = \text{SHA-256}\Big(\text{SHA-256}(\text{Header}_N)\Big)$$

> **Why double hashing?** Bitcoin applies SHA-256 twice to mitigate the *length-extension attack* — a frequently asked 3-mark conceptual question.

### C. Digital Signatures (ECDSA — Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm)
A transaction is signed as:

$$\sigma = \text{Sign}(d_A, \, H(m))$$

and verified as:

$$\text{Verify}(Q_A, \, m, \, \sigma) = \text{True} \iff Q_A = d_A \cdot G$$

where:
- $d_A$ = private key of sender Alice
- $Q_A$ = public key of sender Alice
- $G$ = generator point on the elliptic curve
- $H(m)$ = hash of the message/transaction
- $\cdot$ = elliptic curve scalar multiplication

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---------|--------------------|--------------|------|
| 1 | SHA-256 Output Length | $L_{\text{out}} = 256$ | 256 | bits |
| 2 | Block Hash | $H_N = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_N))$ | 256 | bits |
| 3 | Mining Target | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{expected}}}$ | Computed | — |
| 4 | Expected Mining Time | $E[t] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{\text{rate}}}$ | 600 | seconds (Bitcoin) |
| 5 | Block Reward (Bitcoin) | $R = 50 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ | 3.125 (2024) | BTC |
| 6 | Max Bitcoin Supply | $S_{\max} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 210000 \cdot 50 \cdot 2^{-n} = 21{,}000{,}000$ | 21,000,000 | BTC |
| 7 | ECDSA Signature Size | $ \vert \sigma \vert = 512$ | 512 | bits |
| 8 | Merkle Tree Levels | $L = \lceil \log_2(N_t) \rceil$ | $\log_2$ | levels |
| 9 | Hash Pointer Size | $ \vert H \vert = 256$ | 256 | bits |
| 10 | Block Size Limit | $ \vert B \vert \leq 1\,\text{MB}$ (Bitcoin) | 1,000,000 | bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU ESE, examiners love converting the mining-time formula into a numerical problem. Memorize $E[t] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{\text{rate}}}$ thoroughly.

---

## 2.4 Consensus Mechanisms — The Heart of Blockchain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *consensus mechanism* is the protocol by which **distributed, untrusting nodes agree on a single valid state** of the ledger, despite the presence of malicious actors (Byzantine Generals' Problem).

### A. Proof of Work (PoW) — Bitcoin's Approach
Miners must find a *nonce* $n$ such that:

$$H(\text{Header} \,\vert\vert\, n) < T_{\text{difficulty}}$$

- **Probabilistic Finality:** A block is considered *final* after **6 confirmations** (≈ 60 minutes in Bitcoin).

### B. Proof of Stake (PoS) — Ethereum's Approach
Validators are *pseudo-randomly* selected to propose blocks, weighted by their *stake* (locked crypto-coins).

$$P(\text{selection}) = \dfrac{\text{Stake}_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \text{Stake}_j}$$

### C. Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)
Tolerates up to $f$ faulty nodes out of a total of $N$:

$$N \geq 3f + 1$$

> **Engineering Utility:** PoW → public cryptocurrencies; PoS → modern Ethereum (since "The Merge", Sept 2022); PBFT → enterprise blockchains like *Hyperledger Fabric*.

---

## 2.5 Types of Blockchain

| Type | Access | Read | Write | Consensus | Example |
|------|--------|------|-------|-----------|---------|
| **Public** | Permissionless | Anyone | Anyone | PoW / PoS | Bitcoin, Ethereum |
| **Private** | Permissioned | Restricted | Authorized nodes | Raft / PBFT | Hyperledger |
| **Consortium** | Semi-permissioned | Restricted | Pre-selected | Multi-party PBFT | R3 Corda |
| **Hybrid** | Mixed | Mixed | Mixed | Custom | Dragonchain |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Cryptocurrency:** Decentralized digital cash (Bitcoin, Litecoin).
2. **Smart Contracts:** Self-executing code on Ethereum (Solidity, Vyper).
3. **Supply Chain:** IBM Food Trust — tracks produce from farm to fork.
4. **Healthcare:** Secure, interoperable Electronic Health Records (EHR).
5. **Voting Systems:** Tamper-proof digital democracy.
6. **IoT Security:** Device-to-device authentication without central CA.
7. **Cross-Border Payments:** Ripple (XRP) for SWIFT replacement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Merkle Root from Transactions

The **Merkle Tree** is a binary hash tree that compresses all transactions in a block into a single 256-bit fingerprint — the *Merkle Root*.

### Given:
A block contains $N_t = 4$ transactions:
$$T_1, \, T_2, \, T_3, \, T_4$$

Each transaction is first hashed:
$$H_1 = \text{SHA256}(T_1), \quad H_2 = \text{SHA256}(T_2)$$
$$H_3 = \text{SHA256}(T_3), \quad H_4 = \text{SHA256}(T_4)$$

### Step-by-Step Computation:

**Level 1 (Leaves):** Hash each transaction individually.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_1 &= \text{SHA256}(T_1) \\
H_2 &= \text{SHA256}(T_2) \\
H_3 &= \text{SHA256}(T_3) \\
H_4 &= \text{SHA256}(T_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each transaction is converted into a fixed 256-bit fingerprint to standardize input lengths and ensure cryptographic uniformity.

---

**Level 2 (Internal Nodes):** Concatenate pairs and hash.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{12} &= \text{SHA256}(H_1 \,\vert\vert\, H_2) \\
H_{34} &= \text{SHA256}(H_3 \,\vert\vert\, H_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Concatenation $\vert\vert$ is **bit-level**, producing a 512-bit input to SHA-256, which yields a 256-bit output.

---

**Level 3 (Root):** Final hash to obtain the Merkle Root.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{root}} &= \text{SHA256}(H_{12} \,\vert\vert\, H_{34})
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This 256-bit value is included in the block header. **Any modification to any transaction propagates up the tree, changing the root**, making tampering instantly detectable.

> **Final Compact Form:**
> $$M_{\text{root}} = \text{SHA256}\big(\text{SHA256}(H_1 \,\vert\vert\, H_2) \,\vert\vert\, \text{SHA256}(H_3 \,\vert\vert\, H_4)\big)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bitcoin's Difficulty Adjustment Algorithm

Bitcoin re-calibrates mining difficulty every **2016 blocks** (≈ 2 weeks at 10-min block time).

### Given:
- Old difficulty $D_{\text{old}}$
- Old target $T_{\text{old}}$
- Time taken for last 2016 blocks: $T_{\text{actual}}$
- Expected time: $T_{\text{expected}} = 2016 \times 10 \text{ min} = 20160 \text{ min}$

### Derivation:

The relationship between difficulty and target is *inversely proportional*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{old}} \cdot T_{\text{old}} &= D_{\text{new}} \cdot T_{\text{new}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the proportionality condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{new}} &= T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{expected}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example:
Suppose $T_{\text{old}} = 2^{224}$, $T_{\text{actual}} = 25{,}200$ min (slower than expected).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{new}} &= 2^{224} \cdot \frac{25{,}200}{20{,}160} \\
T_{\text{new}} &= 2^{224} \cdot 1.25 \\
T_{\text{new}} &\approx 2^{224.32}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Since the network was *too slow*, the target is **raised** (made easier) to bring block time back to 10 minutes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expected Time to Mine a Block (PoW)

### Given:
- Network hash rate $H_{\text{rate}}$ (in hashes/sec)
- Difficulty $D$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The probability of a single hash being valid (i.e., meeting the target) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success per hash}) &= \frac{1}{D \cdot 2^{32}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* A higher difficulty $D$ means the target $T$ is smaller, exponentially reducing the probability of success.

Expected number of hashes to find a valid block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[H] &= D \cdot 2^{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the mean of a geometric distribution with success probability $p = \dfrac{1}{D \cdot 2^{32}}$.

Time required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[t] &= \frac{E[H]}{H_{\text{rate}}} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{\text{rate}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example:
If $D = 20{,}000{,}000$ and $H_{\text{rate}} = 1.2 \times 10^{20}$ H/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[t] &= \frac{2 \times 10^7 \cdot 4.295 \times 10^9}{1.2 \times 10^{20}} \\
E[t] &= \frac{8.59 \times 10^{16}}{1.2 \times 10^{20}} \\
E[t] &\approx 7.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds per hash attempt cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for block discovery time (after $E[H]$ hashes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[t_{\text{block}}] &= \frac{2 \times 10^7 \cdot 2^{32}}{1.2 \times 10^{20}} \approx 71{,}580 \text{ s} \approx 19.88 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Engineering Insight:* Bitcoin's network is designed to keep $E[t_{\text{block}}] \approx 600$ seconds by dynamically adjusting $D$ as miners join or leave.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — ECDSA Signature Verification

A user Alice signs a transaction $m$ using her private key $d_A$.

### Signing:
$$
\begin{aligned}
k &\leftarrow \text{random in } [1, n-1] \\
R &= k \cdot G = (x_R, y_R) \\
r &= x_R \mod n \\
s &= k^{-1}(H(m) + d_A \cdot r) \mod n \\
\sigma &= (r, s)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Verification by Bob:
$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= H(m) \cdot s^{-1} \mod n \\
u_2 &= r \cdot s^{-1} \mod n \\
P &= u_1 \cdot G + u_2 \cdot Q_A \\
\text{Valid} &\iff P_x \equiv r \pmod{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This works due to the **homomorphic property of elliptic curves** — scalar multiplication is commutative. The verification reconstructs the original point $R$ without revealing $d_A$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pros vs. Cons of Blockchain

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Trust** | Trustless — no central authority | 51% attack risk in PoW |
| **Transparency** | All transactions are auditable | Privacy concerns in public chains |
| **Security** | Cryptographically tamper-evident | Quantum vulnerability (Shor's algorithm) |
| **Speed** | N/A | Slow throughput (~7 TPS Bitcoin vs. Visa ~65,000 TPS) |
| **Cost** | Removes intermediaries | High energy consumption (PoW) |
| **Immutability** | Permanent record | Cannot correct erroneous transactions |
| **Decentralization** | No single point of failure | Storage bloat (~500 GB Bitcoin ledger) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Block Anatomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Block Number N] --> B[Block Header]
    A --> C[Block Body]
    B --> D1[Version: 4 bytes]
    B --> D2[Previous Block Hash: 32 bytes]
    B --> D3[Merkle Root: 32 bytes]
    B --> D4[Timestamp: 4 bytes]
    B --> D5[Difficulty Target: 4 bytes]
    B --> D6[Nonce: 4 bytes]
    C --> E1[Transaction Counter]
    C --> E2[Transaction 1]
    C --> E3[Transaction 2]
    C --> E4[Transaction N]
    E2 --> F1[Sender Public Key]
    E2 --> F2[Receiver Public Key]
    E2 --> F3[Amount + Signature]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Linked Blockchain Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    genesis[Block 0 Genesis] -->|prev_hash| b1[Block 1]
    b1 -->|prev_hash| b2[Block 2]
    b2 -->|prev_hash| b3[Block 3]
    b3 -->|prev_hash| b4[Block N]
    genesis -.->|Hash Pointer| hgen[256-bit SHA-256]
    b1 -.->|Hash Pointer| hb1[256-bit SHA-256]
    b2 -.->|Hash Pointer| hb2[256-bit SHA-256]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each arrow `prev_hash` represents the cryptographic pointer connecting consecutive blocks. Tampering with Block 1 invalidates Blocks 2, 3, ..., N.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Merkle Tree Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    t1[Tx 1] --> h1[H1]
    t2[Tx 2] --> h2[H2]
    t3[Tx 3] --> h3[H3]
    t4[Tx 4] --> h4[H4]
    h1 --> h12[H12]
    h2 --> h12
    h3 --> h34[H34]
    h4 --> h34
    h12 --> mroot[Merkle Root in Block Header]
    h34 --> mroot
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Proof of Work Mining Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[New Transactions Arrived] --> collect[Node Collects Tx into Block]
    collect --> header[Construct Block Header]
    header --> nonce[Try Nonce 0 to 2^32]
    nonce --> hash{HASH less than Target}
    hash -->|No| nonce
    hash -->|Yes| broadcast[Broadcast Block to Network]
    broadcast --> verify[Other Nodes Verify PoW and Transactions]
    verify --> accept[Block Appended to Chain]
    accept --> reward[Miner Receives Block Reward plus Fees]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Transaction Lifecycle in P2P Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    alice[Alice Signs Transaction] --> broadcast[Tx Broadcast to P2P Network]
    broadcast --> mempool[Nodes Validate and Add to Mempool]
    mempool --> mine[Miner Selects Tx from Mempool]
    mine --> block[Tx Included in New Block]
    block --> confirm[Block Confirmed after 6 Confirmations]
    confirm --> final[Transaction Finalized in Ledger]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Diagram 6 — Consensus Mechanism Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    cons[Consensus Mechanism] --> pow[Proof of Work]
    cons --> pos[Proof of Stake]
    cons --> pbft[PBFT]
    pow --> pow1[High Energy Use]
    pow --> pow2[Probabilistic Finality]
    pos --> pos1[Energy Efficient]
    pos --> pos2[Wealth Centralization Risk]
    pbft --> pbft1[Fast Finality]
    pbft --> pbft2[Permissioned Network]
```

## 4.7 Mermaid Diagram 7 — 51 Percent Attack Threat Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    attacker[Malicious Miner] --> pool[Acquire 51 percent Hash Power]
    pool --> doublespend[Double Spend Attack]
    pool --> censor[Censor Valid Transactions]
    pool --> fork[Create Longer Forks]
    fork --> reject[Honest Nodes Reject Fork]
    doublespend --> loss[Economic Loss to Network]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Blockchain. List any four of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Definition (1.5 marks):* A blockchain is a distributed, decentralized, append-only data structure that maintains a continuously growing list of cryptographically linked blocks, replicated across a peer-to-peer network and validated through a consensus mechanism.

*Four Key Properties (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks):*
1. **Decentralization** — No single point of control.
2. **Immutability** — Past records cannot be altered.
3. **Transparency** — All participants can verify transactions.
4. **Consensus** — All nodes agree on the ledger state.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is a Merkle Tree? Why is it used in blockchain?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Merkle Tree Definition (1.5 marks):* A Merkle Tree (or binary hash tree) is a tree data structure in which every leaf node is labelled with the cryptographic hash of a data block, and every non-leaf node is labelled with the hash of the labels of its child nodes. The topmost hash is called the *Merkle Root*, which is stored in the block header.

*Why Used (1.5 marks):*
1. **Efficient Verification** — A node can verify that a transaction is part of a block using only $O(\log N)$ hashes (Merkle Proof), without downloading the full block.
2. **Data Integrity** — Any tampering with a transaction propagates up the tree, altering the root and immediately revealing the inconsistency.
3. **Storage Efficiency** — Compresses thousands of transactions into a single 256-bit fingerprint.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks may have internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both options are provided below for parallel preparation.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
#### (a) Explain the architecture of a blockchain system in detail. Discuss the roles of hash pointers, Merkle trees, and consensus mechanisms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Blockchain Architecture (2 Marks):**
A blockchain system operates on a **four-layer architecture**:
- **Network Layer** — P2P gossip protocol over TCP.
- **Data Layer** — Linked blocks with hash pointers.
- **Consensus Layer** — Validates new blocks (PoW, PoS, PBFT).
- **Application Layer** — Wallets, smart contracts, DApps.

**2. Hash Pointers (2 Marks):**
A hash pointer is a data structure $(d, h)$ where $d$ is the data and $h = \text{SHA256}(d)$. Each block header stores the hash of the previous block, creating a tamper-evident chain. Modification of any historical block changes its hash, breaking all subsequent pointers.

**3. Merkle Trees (1.5 Marks):**
As derived in Section 3.1, Merkle trees compress all transactions in a block into a single 256-bit Merkle Root stored in the block header. This enables **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)** — lightweight clients can verify transaction inclusion with logarithmic complexity.

**4. Consensus Mechanisms (1.5 Marks):**
Consensus ensures that all honest nodes agree on a single state of the ledger despite adversarial actors. The most common mechanisms are:
- **Proof of Work (PoW):** Miners solve cryptographic puzzles (find a valid nonce).
- **Proof of Stake (PoS):** Validators chosen weighted by their staked coins.
- **PBFT:** Tolerates $f$ faulty nodes if $N \geq 3f + 1$.

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of Proof of Work (PoW) consensus algorithm. Mention its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working of PoW (3 Marks):**

The mining process in PoW proceeds as follows:

**Step 1:** A miner collects pending transactions from the mempool and forms a candidate block.

**Step 2:** The miner constructs the block header containing:
- Previous block hash $H_{N-1}$
- Merkle root $M_{\text{root}}$ of the transactions
- Timestamp $t$
- Difficulty target $T$
- Version number

**Step 3:** The miner iterates over nonce values $n \in [0, 2^{32})$:
- Compute $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header} \,\vert\vert\, n))$
- Check whether $H < T$ (the difficulty target)

**Step 4:** If the condition is satisfied, the miner broadcasts the valid block to the network. Other nodes verify the PoW and accept the block, appending it to their local copy of the chain.

**Step 5:** The miner receives a block reward (currently 3.125 BTC in 2024) plus transaction fees.

**2. Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
[Start] → [Collect Tx] → [Build Header] → [Set Nonce = 0]
                                                  ↓
                                    ┌──[H < Target?]──Yes──→ [Broadcast Block] → [End]
                                    │             │
                                    │             No
                                    │             ↓
                                    │      [Increment Nonce]
                                    │             │
                                    └─────────────┘
```

**3. Advantages (1 Mark):**
- **Sybil-resistant** — Attacking the network requires real computational power.
- **Probabilistic Finality** — Security increases exponentially with block depth.

**4. Disadvantages (1 Mark):**
- **Energy Intensive** — Bitcoin's network consumes ~150 TWh annually.
- **Slow Throughput** — Block time of 10 minutes limits transaction rate to ~7 TPS.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
#### (a) What is a hash function? Explain SHA-256 with its key properties. How is it used to link blocks in a blockchain? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Hash Function Definition (1 Mark):**
A cryptographic hash function is a mathematical algorithm that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size output (256 bits for SHA-256), satisfying determinism, pre-image resistance, and collision resistance.

**2. SHA-256 Algorithm (3 Marks):**

SHA-256 processes input in 512-bit blocks through 64 rounds of operations:
- **Step 1: Padding** — Append a '1' bit, then zeros, then the 64-bit message length.
- **Step 2: Initialize Hash Values** — 8 working variables $H_0$ to $H_7$ (initial square roots of first 8 primes).
- **Step 3: Message Schedule** — Expand each 512-bit block into 64 32-bit words $W_0$ to $W_{63}$.
- **Step 4: Compression Function** — Apply 64 rounds of bitwise operations (AND, OR, XOR, ROTR, SHR) using 64 round constants $K_0$ to $K_{63}$.
- **Step 5: Output** — Final 256-bit hash.

**Key Properties of SHA-256 (1.5 Marks):**
- Output Length: **256 bits**
- Block Size: 512 bits
- Avalanche Effect: ≥ 50% bit difference on 1-bit input change
- Collision Resistance: $2^{128}$ operations to find a collision

**3. Block Linking in Blockchain (1.5 Marks):**

Each block $N$ stores the hash of block $N-1$ in its header:
$$H_{N-1} = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_{N-1}))$$

The hash of the current block is:
$$H_N = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_N))$$

Modification of any transaction in block $N-1$ changes $H_{N-1}$, which propagates to invalidate all subsequent block hashes $H_N, H_{N+1}, \ldots, H_{\text{tip}}$.

---

#### (b) Compare Proof of Work (PoW) and Proof of Stake (PoS) consensus mechanisms. Discuss the 51% attack and its prevention. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Resource** | Computational power (hash rate) | Coin ownership (stake) |
| **Block Proposer** | First miner to find valid nonce | Randomly selected weighted by stake |
| **Energy Use** | Very High (~150 TWh/year Bitcoin) | Minimal |
| **Attack Cost** | Acquire >50% hash power | Acquire >50% stake |
| **Finality** | Probabilistic | Often deterministic (e.g., Casper FFG) |
| **Penalty for Misbehavior** | Wasted energy | Slashing of staked coins |
| **Decentralization Risk** | Mining pool centralization | Wealth centralization (rich get richer) |
| **Example** | Bitcoin | Ethereum (post-Merge, 2022) |

**2. The 51% Attack (2.5 Marks):**

If a single entity controls more than 50% of the network's hash power (PoW) or stake (PoS), they can:
- **Double Spend:** Reverse their own transactions to defraud merchants.
- **Censor Transactions:** Exclude specific transactions from blocks.
- **Fork the Chain:** Create an alternative longer chain that eventually overtakes the honest chain.

**Mathematical Probability of Attack Success (PoW):**
For an attacker with hash fraction $q$ and honest nodes with $p = 1 - q$:

$$P_{\text{attack}} = \left(\frac{q}{p}\right)^z$$

where $z$ is the number of confirmations. For $q = 0.4$ (below 50%) and $z = 6$:

$$P_{\text{attack}} = \left(\frac{0.4}{0.6}\right)^6 \approx 0.0877 \approx 8.77\%$$

This drops exponentially as $z$ increases.

**3. Prevention (1.5 Marks):**
- **Long Confirmation Delays** — Require $z \geq 6$ for high-value transactions.
- **Checkpointing** — Finalize blocks periodically (e.g., Ethereum's Casper).
- **Slashing (PoS)** — Confiscate attacker stake.
- **Decentralized Mining** — Discourage large mining pool monopolies.
- **Algorand's Sortition** — Cryptographic leader selection prevents targeted attacks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Blockchain Questions:**
>
> 1. **Hash Confusion** — Writing "SHA-256 outputs 128 bits" or "block hash uses MD5" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Always state **256 bits** and **SHA-256**.
>
> 2. **Nonce Confusion** — Many students write that the *nonce* is the transaction number. The nonce is a **32-bit counter used in mining** to vary the header hash. Do not confuse with Ethereum's account nonce.
>
> 3. **Merkle Root Source** — Examiners expect students to state that the **Merkle Root is stored in the BLOCK HEADER, not the block body**.
>
> 4. **Consensus Confusion** — Do not write "PoS is faster than PoW" without justification. Always support claims with **mathematical or engineering reasoning**.
>
> 5. **Missing Diagram in 14-Mark Questions** — KTU mandates a diagram for full 14-mark questions. Skipping it = **loss of 2 marks minimum**.
>
> 6. **Immutability ≠ Unchangeable** — A block is computationally immutable under normal conditions but *theoretically* alterable with >50% hash power. Mention this nuance for full marks.
>
> 7. **Public vs Private Key Mix-up** — In ECDSA, the **private key** signs, the **public key** verifies. Reversing them = -1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Blockchain**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- Blockchain = **Distributed + Decentralized + Append-Only + Cryptographically Linked** ledger
- Each block contains: **Previous Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Nonce, Difficulty Target**
- Block hash = **SHA256(SHA256(Header))** — *double hashing*
- Block time target (Bitcoin): **600 seconds** (10 minutes)
- Block size limit (Bitcoin): **1 MB**
- Block reward halving: every **210,000 blocks** (≈ 4 years)

### 📌 Cryptographic Primitives
- **SHA-256:** 256-bit output, 512-bit block, 64 rounds
- **Merkle Tree:** Binary hash tree, root in block header
- **ECDSA:** Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm, key size 256 bits, signature 512 bits
- **Hash Pointer:** Combines data + its hash, used for block linking

### 📌 Consensus Mechanisms
- **PoW:** Probabilistic, energy-intensive, Sybil-resistant → Bitcoin
- **PoS:** Energy-efficient, slashing penalties, wealth centralization risk → Ethereum
- **PBFT:** Deterministic, tolerates $f$ faults if $N \geq 3f + 1$ → Hyperledger

### 📌 Formulas to Memorize
- $H_N = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{Header}_N))$
- $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{expected}}}$
- $E[t_{\text{block}}] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{\text{rate}}}$
- $P_{\text{attack}} = (q/p)^z$ for 51% attack
- $S_{\max}^{\text{Bitcoin}} = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC
- $M_{\text{root}}$ = final hash of all transactions in a block

### 📌 Security Threats (High-Yield ESE Topics)
- **51% Attack** — Majority control
- **Double Spend** — Reusing same coins
- **Sybil Attack** — Multiple fake identities
- **Eclipse Attack** — Isolating a node from the network
- **Routing Attack** — BGP hijacking of blockchain traffic
- **Quantum Threat** — Shor's algorithm breaks ECDSA

### 📌 Real-World Applications (Quick Mentions for Marks)
- Bitcoin (2008, Satoshi Nakamoto) — first blockchain
- Ethereum (2015, Vitalik Buterin) — smart contracts
- Hyperledger Fabric (2015, Linux Foundation) — enterprise blockchain
- IBM Food Trust — supply chain
- Ripple (XRP) — cross-border payments
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantum Networking (Basic Concepts Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT751 - DATA & COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantum Networking (Basic Concepts Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantum Networking (QN)** is a specialized branch of data communication that leverages the principles of **quantum mechanics** — specifically **quantum superposition**, **quantum entanglement**, and the **no-cloning theorem** — to transmit, route, and process information encoded in *quantum states* (qubits) across a physical medium (optical fiber, free-space photonic links, or satellite channels).

In classical computer communication (covered in Modules 1–3 of PECCT751), a bit is a deterministic binary value $\in \{0, 1\}$. In quantum networking, the fundamental information carrier is the **quantum bit (qubit)**, whose state $\vert \psi \rangle$ lives in a two-dimensional complex Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}_2 \cong \mathbb{C}^2$ :

$$\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$$

where the complex amplitudes satisfy the **normalization (Born rule) condition**:

$$\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$$

Here, $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are *probability amplitudes*, and $\vert \alpha \vert^2$ gives the probability of measuring the qubit in state $\vert 0 \rangle$, while $\vert \beta \vert^2$ gives the probability of measuring it as $\vert 1 \rangle$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magic Coin in a Sealed Envelope"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spinning Coin:** Imagine a coin spinning on a table. While it is spinning, it is neither heads nor tails — it is in a **superposition of both**. The moment you slap the table (i.e., **measure** it), the coin "collapses" into either heads or tails. Similarly, a qubit exists in a *superposed* state of $\vert 0 \rangle$ and $\vert 1 \rangle$ until it is measured. Furthermore, if you entangle two such coins and place them in sealed envelopes, sending one to Alice and one to Bob halfway across the world — the *instant* Alice opens hers, Bob's coin *simultaneously* decides its face. This is the essence of **quantum entanglement**, the resource that powers quantum networking.

### 1.3 Foundational Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (CODATA 2018) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** |
| $\hbar$ | Reduced Planck's constant $\tfrac{h}{2\pi}$ | $1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics in Quantum Networking:**
> - **QBER (Quantum Bit Error Rate):** Ratio of erroneous to total detected bits, typically tolerated up to ~**11%** in BB84.
> - **Fidelity ($F$):** Measure of how close a transmitted state is to the original; $F = \vert \langle \psi_{\text{ideal}} \vert \psi_{\text{actual}} \rangle \vert^2$, with $F \in [0, 1]$.
> - **Entanglement Distribution Rate:** Photons entangled per second (**ebits/s**).
> - **Quantum Channel Length:** Currently limited to ~**100–500 km** of optical fiber before quantum repeaters become essential.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Quantum Networking

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Superposition:** A qubit can simultaneously represent 0 and 1, enabling massive parallel state space.
> 2. **Entanglement:** Two (or more) qubits can be *non-classically correlated* such that the state of one instantaneously influences the other, regardless of distance (spooky action at a distance).
> 3. **No-Cloning Theorem (Wootters & Zurek, 1982):** It is physically impossible to create an *identical copy* of an arbitrary unknown quantum state. This single principle is what makes quantum communication *unconditionally secure*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How a Quantum Network Functions

A quantum network is *not* a replacement for the classical Internet; rather, it operates as a **parallel, physically distinct substrate** that is tightly coupled to a classical control channel. The operational flow is decomposed as follows:

- **Step 1 — Qubit Generation:** Photons (or atoms, ions, superconducting circuits) are prepared in well-defined quantum states using sources such as *Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC)* crystals or *single-photon emitters* (quantum dots, NV centers).
- **Step 2 — Quantum State Encoding:** Information is encoded onto physical degrees of freedom of the photon — **polarization**, **phase**, **time-bin**, or **frequency** — which act as the qubit basis.
- **Step 3 — Quantum Channel Transmission:** The encoded photon travels through an *optical fiber* (telecom C-band ~1550 nm) or *free-space link*. Critical fact: the photon **cannot be amplified** (no-cloning forbids it), so signal power decays exponentially as $P(L) = P_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$, where $\alpha \approx 0.2$ **dB/km** for telecom fiber.
- **Step 4 — Detection:** At the receiver, single-photon detectors (avalanche photodiodes — APDs, or superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors — SNSPDs) record arrivals.
- **Step 5 — Classical Post-Processing:** A classical authenticated channel is used for *sifting*, *error correction*, and *privacy amplification* (e.g., via the Cascade or LDPC protocols).

### 2.2 Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) — The Flagship Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QKD** allows two parties, conventionally named **Alice** (sender) and **Bob** (receiver), to generate a shared cryptographic key whose security is guaranteed by the *laws of physics* rather than computational hardness.

#### 2.2.1 BB84 Protocol (Bennett & Brassard, 1984)

1. Alice randomly chooses a bit (0 or 1) **and** a basis ($\{+, \times\}$ — rectilinear or diagonal).
2. She sends a polarized photon to Bob.
3. Bob randomly chooses a basis and measures.
4. Over a classical channel, they **discard** measurements where bases mismatched (*sifting*), keeping ~**50%** of raw bits.
5. They estimate QBER; if QBER < 11%, they proceed with error correction + privacy amplification → **final secret key**.

#### 2.2.2 B92 Protocol (Bennett, 1992)

A simplified 2-state protocol using only two non-orthogonal states $\vert 0 \rangle$ and $\vert + \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle)$.

### 2.3 Quantum Teleportation Protocol (Bennett et al., 1993)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the protocol that *transfers an unknown quantum state* $\vert \psi \rangle$ from Alice to Bob using a shared entangled pair **plus** 2 classical bits of communication. It is **not** faster-than-light communication because of the classical channel bottleneck.

The four **Bell states** (maximally entangled two-qubit basis) are:

$$\vert \Phi^{\pm} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle \pm \vert 11 \rangle), \quad \vert \Psi^{\pm} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 01 \rangle \pm \vert 10 \rangle)$$

### 2.4 Quantum Repeaters — Beating the Exponential Loss

Direct fiber transmission is bounded by the **Piria–Spencer limit** ($\sim 500$ km for BB84). **Quantum repeaters** segment the channel into shorter links, perform *entanglement swapping* at intermediate nodes, and use *quantum memory* to buffer photons.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Use `\vert` not `|` inside any table cell to avoid breaking markdown syntax.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Qubit state | $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ | Fundamental information unit |
| 2 | Normalization | $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$ | Valid probability amplitudes |
| 3 | Born rule (measurement) | $P(0) = \vert \alpha \vert^2, \; P(1) = \vert \beta \vert^2$ | Probabilistic outcome |
| 4 | Fiber attenuation | $P(L) = P_0 \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ | Channel loss in dB |
| 5 | Fidelity | $F = \vert \langle \psi_{\text{ideal}} \vert \psi_{\text{actual}} \rangle \vert^2$ | State transfer quality |
| 6 | BB84 sifting efficiency | $\eta_{\text{sift}} = 0.5$ | Raw key reduction |
| 7 | QBER threshold (BB84) | $Q_{\text{th}} \approx 0.1100$ | Security limit |
| 8 | BB84 key rate (asymptotic) | $R \geq 1 - 2H_2(Q)$ bits/pulse | Secret bits per signal |
| 9 | Binary entropy | $H_2(x) = -x\log_2 x - (1-x)\log_2(1-x)$ | Information content |
| 10 | No-cloning constraint | $U \vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle = \vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \psi \rangle$ | Impossible for unknown $\vert \psi \rangle$ |
| 11 | Heisenberg uncertainty | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar / 2$ | Limits joint measurement |
| 12 | Photon energy | $E = h \nu = \hbar \omega$ | Telecom band freq mapping |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Unhackable cryptography** for banking, defense, and government.
- **Quantum-secured backbone** for the next-generation Internet (the EU's *Quantum Internet Alliance*, China's *Micius* satellite, India's *National Quantum Mission*).
- **Distributed quantum computing** — linking quantum processors via entangled links to scale beyond single-chip qubit counts.
- **Quantum clock synchronization** with precision beyond classical GPS limits.
- **Secure voting and identity protocols** based on physical-law guarantees rather than mathematical assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Proof of the No-Cloning Theorem (Wootters & Zurek, 1982)

> **Goal:** Show that no unitary operator $U$ can copy an arbitrary unknown quantum state $\vert \psi \rangle$.

Assume, for contradiction, that a universal quantum copier exists as a unitary $U$ acting on system + ancilla:

$$U \big( \vert \psi \rangle_A \otimes \vert 0 \rangle_B \big) = \vert \psi \rangle_A \otimes \vert \psi \rangle_B$$

Apply this to two arbitrary orthogonal states $\vert 0 \rangle$ and $\vert 1 \rangle$:

$$U \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle = \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle$$

$$U \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle = \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 1 \rangle$$

Now consider a superposition $\vert \psi \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle)$ (linearity of $U$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle &= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\big( U \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle + U \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle \big) \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\big( \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 1 \rangle \big)
\end{aligned}
$$

But a true cloner would require:

$$\vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \psi \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2}\big( \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle + \vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 1 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle + \vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 1 \rangle \big)$$

The two results **differ in cross terms** $\vert 0 \rangle \otimes \vert 1 \rangle$ and $\vert 1 \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle$. Contradiction $\Rightarrow$ no universal cloner exists. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Because cloning is forbidden, any *eavesdropper* (Eve) tapping the quantum channel must *measure* the photon, which *disturbs* the state and reveals her presence via elevated QBER. This is the bedrock of QKD security.

### 3.2 Derivation — Sifting Efficiency of BB84

Each photon is sent by Alice in a random basis ($\{+\}$ or $\{\times\}$, 50–50). Bob independently chooses his measurement basis with the same 50–50 distribution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{bases match}) &= P(\text{Alice} = +) \cdot P(\text{Bob} = +) + P(\text{Alice} = \times) \cdot P(\text{Bob} = \times) \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \tfrac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **sifting efficiency** $\eta_{\text{sift}} = 0.5$, meaning half the raw bits are discarded as *useless* (but verified publicly).

### 3.3 Derivation — BB84 Secret Key Rate (Shor–Preskill, 2000)

After error correction and privacy amplification, the asymptotic secret key fraction is:

$$r = 1 - 2 H_2(Q)$$

where $Q$ is the QBER and $H_2$ is the binary Shannon entropy. Derivation sketch:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_2(Q) &= -Q \log_2 Q - (1 - Q) \log_2 (1 - Q) \\[4pt]
\text{If } Q &= 0 \Rightarrow H_2 = 0 \Rightarrow r = 1 \text{ (perfect channel)} \\[4pt]
\text{If } Q &= 0.11 \Rightarrow H_2 \approx 0.5 \Rightarrow r = 0 \text{ (security break-even)} \\[4pt]
\text{If } Q &> 0.11 \Rightarrow r < 0 \text{ (no secret key can be extracted)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This justifies the famous **11% QBER threshold** of BB84.

### 3.4 Derivation — Quantum Teleportation Resource Cost

For teleporting 1 unknown qubit $\vert \psi \rangle$:

- **Pre-shared entanglement:** 1 ebit (maximally entangled pair $\vert \Phi^{+} \rangle$).
- **Classical communication:** 2 classical bits (to convey the outcome of Alice's Bell-State Measurement, BSM).
- **Total:** 1 ebit + 2 cbits $\rightarrow$ 1 teleported qubit.

The **no-communication theorem** is preserved: the 2 classical bits must travel at $\leq c$, so the protocol cannot transmit information faster than light.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of QKD Protocols (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Protocol | Year | States Used | Basis Structure | QBER Threshold | Detector Required |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| BB84 | 1984 | 4 (2 bases × 2 bits) | Rectilinear + Diagonal | 11.0% | Single-photon |
| B92 | 1992 | 2 (non-orthogonal) | Single | ~6.5% | Single-photon |
| E91 (Ekert) | 1991 | 4 (entangled) | CHSH inequality test | ~14.6% | Entangled source |
| SARG04 | 2004 | 4 | Weak coherent | 2.05% (decoy) | Single-photon |
| MDI-QKD | 2012 | 4 | Measurement-device-indep. | ~11% | Bell-state meas. |
| TF-QKD | 2018 | — | Twin-field | ~14.6% (asymptotic) | Single-photon |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — QBER Calculation

> **Problem:** Alice sends 10,000 photons. Bob detects 9,200 correctly and 800 incorrectly. Compute the QBER and determine if the BB84 key is secure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total detected bits} &= N_{\text{correct}} + N_{\text{error}} = 9200 + 800 = 10000 \\[4pt]
Q &= \dfrac{N_{\text{error}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{800}{10000} = 0.08 = 8\% \\[4pt]
\text{Compare to threshold: } Q &= 0.08 < 0.11 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SECURE.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Secret key rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_2(0.08) &= -0.08 \log_2(0.08) - 0.92 \log_2(0.92) \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.08 \times 3.644 + 0.92 \times 0.120 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.2915 + 0.1104 \approx 0.4019 \text{ bits/symbol} \\[4pt]
r &= 1 - 2(0.4019) = 1 - 0.8038 = 0.1962
\end{aligned}
$$

So roughly **19.6%** of sifted bits remain as final secret key $\Rightarrow$ ~**903 secret bits** from this 10,000-pulse run (after $\times 0.5$ sifting).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Quantum Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Alice QKD Transmitter]:::tx -->|Polarized Photons| nodeF[Quantum Channel Optical Fiber 1550 nm]
    nodeF -->|Loss alpha 0.2 dB per km| nodeB[Bob QKD Receiver Single Photon Detector]
    nodeB -->|Raw Key Bits| nodeC[Classical Authenticated Channel]
    nodeA -->|Basis Choices| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Sifting and QBER Estimation| nodeP[Post Processing Error Correction and Privacy Amplification]
    nodeP -->|Final Secret Key K| nodeApp[Application Layer Secure Messaging and Crypto]

    classDef tx fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#e2f0fd,stroke:#36c,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2a7,color:#000
    classDef nodeA tx
    classDef nodeB rx
    classDef nodeApp app
```

### 4.2 Quantum Teleportation Protocol Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice
    participant EPR as Entangled Pair Source
    participant BSM as Bell State Measurement
    participant Cls as Classical Channel
    participant B as Bob

    Note over EPR: Prepare Phi plus = 1 over sqrt2 times 00 plus 11
    EPR->>A: Send qubit 1
    EPR->>B: Send qubit 2
    A->>BSM: Combine unknown psi with qubit 1
    BSM->>Cls: 2 classical bits outcome
    Cls->>B: Transmit outcome
    B->>B: Apply Pauli correction I X Z XZ
    B-->>B: Reconstruct psi at remote site
```

```mermaid
    nodeL1 --> nodeN1[Repeater Node 1 Entanglement Swapping]
    nodeN1 --> nodeL2[Entanglement Link Segment 2]
    nodeL2 --> nodeN2[Repeater Node 2 Entanglement Swapping]
    nodeN2 --> nodeL3[Entanglement Link Segment 3]
    nodeL3 --> nodeB1[Bob Station Qubit Memory]

    nodeQM -.-> nodeN2

```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    BitGen --> BasisSel

    BasisSel --> PhotonEmit

    PhotonEmit --> ChanTrx
    ChanTrx: Quantum Channel Transmission
    ChanTrx --> BobMeas

    BobMeas --> Sift

    QBERest: QBER Estimation
    QBERest --> Secure

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q1.** Define a *qubit*. How does it differ from a classical bit in terms of state representation and measurement?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** A qubit is a two-state quantum-mechanical system whose state is represented as a unit vector in a 2-D Hilbert space: $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$ with $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.
> - **Difference — State Representation (1 mark):** A classical bit is deterministic and takes exactly one of $\{0, 1\}$. A qubit exists in a *superposition* of both basis states simultaneously, parameterized by continuous complex amplitudes $\alpha, \beta \in \mathbb{C}$.
> - **Difference — Measurement (1 mark):** Measuring a classical bit yields its value deterministically. Measuring a qubit collapses it probabilistically to $\vert 0 \rangle$ with probability $\vert \alpha \vert^2$ or $\vert 1 \rangle$ with probability $\vert \beta \vert^2$ (Born rule). The pre-measurement superposition is irreversibly destroyed.

> **`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q2.** State the **No-Cloning Theorem** and explain in one sentence why it is important for quantum cryptography.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Statement (2 marks):** The No-Cloning Theorem (Wootters & Zurek, 1982) states that it is impossible to construct a universal quantum device that produces identical copies of an arbitrary unknown quantum state. Formally, no unitary $U$ exists such that $U \vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert 0 \rangle = \vert \psi \rangle \otimes \vert \psi \rangle$ for every $\vert \psi \rangle$.
> - **Importance (1 mark):** Since eavesdroppers cannot copy qubits in transit, any interception *disturbs* the quantum state, making attacks detectable via QBER — guaranteeing *unconditional security* of QKD.

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Solution to A(a): BB84 Protocol

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Setup:** Alice and Bob are connected by two channels — a *quantum channel* (single-photon transmission) and a *classical authenticated channel*.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Encoding:** Alice generates a random bit string and a random basis string. She uses two bases:
- **Rectilinear ($+$):** $\vert 0 \rangle$ = horizontal (0°), $\vert 1 \rangle$ = vertical (90°).
- **Diagonal ($\times$):** $\vert 0 \rangle$ = +45°, $\vert 1 \rangle$ = –45°.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Transmission:** A polarized photon is sent to Bob per bit.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Measurement:** Bob randomly chooses a basis and measures each incoming photon with a single-photon detector.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Sifting:** Over the classical channel, they publicly announce their *basis choices* (not results) and keep only bits where bases matched — this is ~50% of the raw key.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — QBER Estimation:** A random subset is publicly compared; if QBER > 11%, the run is aborted. Otherwise, the subset is discarded.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Post-Processing:** Error correction (e.g., Cascade) and privacy amplification (e.g., universal hashing) yield the final secret key.

| Basis Type | State for bit 0 | State for bit 1 | Measurement Angle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rectilinear ($+$) | $\vert 0 \rangle$ = $\vert \leftrightarrow \rangle$ | $\vert 1 \rangle$ = $\vert \updownarrow \rangle$ | 0° or 90° |
| Diagonal ($\times$) | $\vert 0 \rangle$ = $\vert \nearrow \rangle$ | $\vert 1 \rangle$ = $\vert \searrow \rangle$ | 45° or 135° |

#### Solution to A(b): Sifting & QBER Derivation

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Sifting Efficiency:**
With Alice and Bob independently choosing bases with probability $\tfrac{1}{2}$ each:
$$P(\text{match}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}$$
Hence, sifting efficiency $\eta_{\text{sift}} = 0.5$.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — 11% Threshold:** From Shor–Preskill, the secret key rate is
$$r = 1 - 2 H_2(Q)$$
Setting $r = 0$ for security break-even: $H_2(Q) = 0.5$. Solving $H_2(Q) = 0.5$ yields $Q = 0.1100$ (i.e., 11%).

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Compute for Q = 5%:**
$$H_2(0.05) = -0.05 \log_2 0.05 - 0.95 \log_2 0.95 \approx 0.2864$$
$$r = 1 - 2(0.2864) = 0.4272 \approx 42.72\%$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation:** Out of 10,000 sifted bits, approximately **4,272 secret bits** are extractable after privacy amplification.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Solution to B(a): Quantum Teleportation

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Goal:** Transfer an unknown state $\vert \psi \rangle_C = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle_C + \beta \vert 1 \rangle_C$ from Alice to Bob without physically sending the qubit.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Pre-shared Resource:** A maximally entangled pair (an *ebit*) $\vert \Phi^{+} \rangle_{AB} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 0 \rangle_A \vert 0 \rangle_B + \vert 1 \rangle_A \vert 1 \rangle_B)$ is distributed: qubit $A$ to Alice, qubit $B$ to Bob.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Composite System:** Total state is $\vert \psi \rangle_C \otimes \vert \Phi^+ \rangle_{AB}$, a 4-D Hilbert space.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Bell-State Measurement (BSM):** Alice performs a joint measurement on qubits $C$ and $A$ in the Bell basis $\{\vert \Phi^+ \rangle, \vert \Phi^- \rangle, \vert \Psi^+ \rangle, \vert \Psi^- \rangle\}$. She obtains one of 4 outcomes (each with probability $\tfrac{1}{4}$).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Classical Communication:** Alice sends 2 classical bits to Bob encoding her measurement outcome.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Pauli Correction:** Bob applies one of $\{I, X, Z, XZ\}$ on his qubit $B$ based on the received 2 bits, recovering $\vert \psi \rangle_B$.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Why No FTL Communication:** The 2 classical bits travel at $\leq c$. Without them, Bob's qubit is in a *classical mixture* from which no information about $\alpha, \beta$ can be extracted. Hence teleportation respects the **no-communication theorem**.

#### Solution to B(b): Entanglement & Quantum Repeaters

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Entanglement:** Two qubits are entangled if their joint state $\vert \psi \rangle_{AB}$ **cannot** be written as a tensor product of individual states, e.g., $\vert \Phi^+ \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle + \vert 11 \rangle)$. Measuring $A$ instantly determines $B$'s outcome — the **EPR paradox** in modern form.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Entanglement Swapping:** Two independent entangled pairs $(\vert \Phi^+ \rangle_{12}$ and $\vert \Phi^+ \rangle_{34})$ are joined by a Bell-state measurement on the middle qubits (2, 3). This projects qubits 1 and 4 into an entangled state $\vert \Phi^+ \rangle_{14}$ *even though they never directly interacted*. This is the operational primitive of a quantum repeater.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Piria–Spencer Limit:** Without repeaters, the secret key rate over a lossy channel scales as $R \sim -\log_2(1 - \eta)$, where $\eta = 10^{-\alpha L/10}$ is the transmittance. The maximum secure distance is ~**500 km** (assuming $\alpha = 0.2$ dB/km, 11% QBER, perfect detectors).

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Quantum Repeater Solution:** The channel is divided into $N$ segments of length $L/N$. Each segment generates an entangled pair *independently*. Entanglement swapping is performed hierarchically across nodes, scaling loss as $N \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / (10N)}$ — much more favorable. **Quantum memories** (e.g., rare-earth-doped crystals, atomic ensembles) buffer the photons until both segments are ready, enabling *deterministic* swapping.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Practical Status:** As of 2024, three-node proof-of-principle repeaters have been demonstrated in Delft (Netherlands) and Hefei (China). Worldwide efforts (EuroQCI, Quantum Internet Alliance) target continent-scale quantum networks by 2030.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in Quantum Networking questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization constraint** $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$ in any state-vector question — automatic 1 mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing *superposition* with *entanglement*** — superposition is a *single* qubit property; entanglement requires *two or more* qubits and a non-separable joint state.
> 3. **Writing "BB84 uses entanglement"** — false! BB84 is a *prepare-and-measure* protocol. Only E91 (Ekert 1991) explicitly uses entanglement.
> 4. **Stating teleportation is faster-than-light** — guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Always emphasize the **2 classical bits over a classical channel**.
> 5. **Skipping the `sifting` step** in BB84 derivations — examiners explicitly test for sifting efficiency (50%) and QBER threshold (11%).
> 6. **Mixing up which basis is rectilinear vs. diagonal** — clearly state angles: 0°/90° ($+$) and 45°/135° ($\times$).
> 7. **Omitting units** in physical quantities (Hz, ebits/s, dB/km) — KTU is strict on unit notation.
> 8. **Forgetting to multiply by sifting efficiency** when computing final secret bit count from raw bits.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quantum Networking (Basic Concepts)**

- **Qubit:** $\vert \psi \rangle = \alpha \vert 0 \rangle + \beta \vert 1 \rangle$, with $\vert \alpha \vert^2 + \vert \beta \vert^2 = 1$.
- **Born Rule:** Measurement probabilities are $|\text{amplitude}|^2$.
- **Three Pillars:** Superposition, Entanglement, No-Cloning.
- **No-Cloning Theorem (1982):** Unknown quantum states cannot be copied; proved by linearity contradiction.
- **BB84 (1984):** 4 states, 2 bases, $\eta_{\text{sift}} = 0.5$, $Q_{\text{th}} = 11\%$.
- **Shor–Preskill bound:** $r = 1 - 2H_2(Q)$; secure iff $Q < 11\%$.
- **B92 (1992):** 2 non-orthogonal states, single basis.
- **E91 (1991):** Uses entanglement + Bell inequality (CHSH) test.
- **Teleportation (1993):** 1 ebit + 2 cbits $\rightarrow$ 1 qubit transferred; no FTL signal.
- **Bell States:** $\vert \Phi^{\pm} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 00 \rangle \pm \vert 11 \rangle)$; $\vert \Psi^{\pm} \rangle = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vert 01 \rangle \pm \vert 10 \rangle)$.
- **Quantum Repeaters:** Defeat exponential fiber loss via entanglement swapping + quantum memories.
- **Piria–Spencer Limit:** ~500 km max BB84 distance on telecom fiber without repeaters.
- **Photon Sources:** SPDC, quantum dots, NV centers.
- **Detectors:** APDs, SNSPDs (superconducting nanowire).
- **Telecom Wavelength:** 1550 nm (lowest fiber attenuation window).
- **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; $\hbar = h/2\pi$.
- **Heisenberg:** $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$.
- **Real Systems (2024):** Micius satellite, Delft network, Boston Quantum Net, EuroQCI, India's NQM.
- **Fidelity:** $F = \vert \langle \psi_{\text{ideal}} \vert \psi_{\text{actual}} \rangle \vert^2 \in [0, 1]$.
- **Binary Entropy:** $H_2(x) = -x \log_2 x - (1-x) \log_2 (1-x)$.
- **Fiber Loss:** $\sim 0.2$ dB/km @ 1550 nm; classical optical amplifiers (EDFA) are **forbidden** in quantum channels.
